topics.title: Definition and History of Cryptography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and History of Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: DEFINITION AND HISTORY OF CRYPTOGRAPHY

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cryptography** is the discipline that embodies the principles, means, and methods for the transformation of data in order to hide its semantic content, prevent its unauthorized use, or establish its authenticity. Formally, it is the science of securing information by converting intelligible plaintext (P) into unintelligible ciphertext (C) and back again using mathematical algorithms and secret keys (K).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT501)** syllabus, Applied Cryptography is defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Official Definition (PCCCT501):**
> *Cryptography is the practice of designing secure communication protocols that prevent unauthorized third parties from accessing or altering transmitted information. It encompasses encryption, decryption, key generation, digital signatures, authentication, and cryptographic hash functions.*

The etymological roots of the word lie in **Greek**:
- **"Kryptos"** (κρυπτός) — meaning *hidden* or *secret*
- **"Graphein"** (γράφειν) — meaning *to write* or *to study*

Thus, literally, cryptography means **"hidden writing"**.

### 1.2 Core Objectives — The CIA Triad in Cryptography

Modern cryptographic systems aim to satisfy three primary security goals, often referred to as the **CIA Triad**:

| Objective | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Confidentiality** | C | Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized entities. |
| **Integrity** | I | Guarantees that data has not been altered or tampered with during transit. |
| **Authentication** | A | Verifies the identity of the sender and/or the recipient of a message. |

Extended goals include **Non-Repudiation** (preventing a sender from denying they sent a message) and **Access Control**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The Locked Treasure Chest**
> Imagine you wish to send a precious diamond (your **message**) to a friend living in another city. You place the diamond inside a sturdy iron chest (**encryption algorithm**), and you lock it using a complex mechanical combination lock (**cryptographic key**). You then dispatch the locked chest via a public courier (**insecure channel**). Even if a thief intercepts the chest, he cannot open it because he lacks the combination. Upon arrival, your friend uses his matching copy of the combination (**shared key**) to unlock the chest and retrieve the diamond (**decryption**).

In this analogy:
- The **diamond** = Plaintext (original readable message)
- The **iron chest** = Encryption algorithm (cipher)
- The **combination lock** = Key (K)
- The **locked chest** = Ciphertext (encrypted output)
- The **courier** = Communication channel (possibly insecure)
- The **matching combination** = Decryption key

This simple model captures the essence of all cryptographic systems, from ancient Caesar ciphers to modern AES-256 algorithms.

### 1.4 Basic Cryptographic Terminology (KTU Must-Know Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Notation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Plaintext (P)** | $P$ | The original, human-readable message or data before encryption. |
| **Ciphertext (C)** | $C$ | The encrypted, unreadable output produced by the cipher. |
| **Encryption (E)** | $E_K(P) \rightarrow C$ | The transformation process converting plaintext into ciphertext using key $K$. |
| **Decryption (D)** | $D_K(C) \rightarrow P$ | The reverse transformation, recovering plaintext from ciphertext using key $K$. |
| **Key (K)** | $K$ | A secret parameter (number, string, or mathematical value) used by the cipher. |
| **Cipher** | $E$ | The mathematical algorithm performing encryption. |
| **Cryptosystem** | $\{P, C, K, E, D\}$ | The complete set of five components defining a working cryptographic system. |
| **Cryptanalysis** | — | The art and science of breaking ciphers (recovering P or K from C). |
| **Cryptology** | — | The umbrella discipline encompassing both cryptography and cryptanalysis. |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Cryptography**, **Cryptanalysis**, and **Cryptology**. Remember: *Cryptology = Cryptography + Cryptanalysis*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Fundamental Cryptographic Equation

Every cryptographic operation, regardless of complexity, can be expressed in its most reduced mathematical form. The two core transformations are:

**Encryption Transformation:**

$$
E_K(P) = C
$$

**Decryption Transformation:**

$$
D_K(C) = P
$$

**Composition Property (Correctness Criterion):**

$$
D_K(E_K(P)) = P
$$

This is the **mandatory correctness criterion** for every valid cryptosystem. A cipher is considered *correct* if and only if applying the decryption function to the ciphertext produced by encryption always yields the original plaintext.

### 2.2 Kerckhoffs's Principle (The Golden Rule of Cryptography)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auguste Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883):**
> *A cryptosystem should be secure even if everything about the system — except the key — is public knowledge.*

In other words: **Security must reside solely in the secrecy of the key, not in the secrecy of the algorithm.** This is the cornerstone principle that distinguishes amateur cipher designers from professional cryptographers.

Formal Mathematical Statement:

$$
\text{Security}(E, D, P, C) \;\propto\; \text{Secrecy}(K) \quad \text{only}
$$

Modern algorithms like **AES, RSA, and SHA-256** are openly published and analyzed — their security depends entirely on the keys, which are typically **128-bit, 256-bit, or 2048-bit** values.

### 2.3 Shannon's Maxim (The Enemy Knows the System)

Claude Shannon, the father of information theory, refined Kerckhoffs's principle into what is now called **Shannon's Maxim**:

> *"The enemy knows the system."* — Claude Shannon (1949)

This means a cryptographer must assume the attacker has full knowledge of the cipher's algorithm and only needs to find the key.

### 2.4 Computational Security vs. Information-Theoretic Security

| Security Type | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Computational Security** | The cipher is secure against any adversary with **bounded computational resources** within a reasonable time. | AES-128, RSA-2048 |
| **Information-Theoretic Security** | The cipher is secure even against an adversary with **unlimited computational power**. | One-Time Pad (OTP) |

The **One-Time Pad (OTP)** is the only provably unbreakable cipher, provided the key is truly random, as long as the message, never reused, and kept secret.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_K(P) = C$ | Encryption function | Transforming plaintext |
| $D_K(C) = P$ | Decryption function | Recovering plaintext |
| $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ | Correctness criterion | Validates any cryptosystem |
| $C \equiv P + K \pmod{26}$ | Additive (Caesar) cipher | Classical substitution |
| $C_i \equiv (P_i + K_i) \pmod{26}$ | Vigenère cipher | Polyalphabetic cipher |
| $2^{128}$ operations | Brute-force work for 128-bit key | AES-128 security bound |
| $2^{256}$ operations | Brute-force work for 256-bit key | AES-256 security bound |
| $n = pq$ | RSA modulus | Public key cryptosystem |
| $H(M) = \text{SHA-256}(M)$ | Cryptographic hash | Integrity verification |

### 2.6 Classification of Cryptographic Systems

Cryptosystems are broadly classified along three independent axes:

1. **By Key Type**
   - **Symmetric Key Cryptography** — Same key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
     - Example: AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish
   - **Asymmetric Key Cryptography** — A public key $K_{pub}$ encrypts, a private key $K_{priv}$ decrypts.
     - Example: RSA, ECC, ElGamal

2. **By Operation Type**
   - **Substitution Ciphers** — Replace plaintext symbols with ciphertext symbols.
   - **Transposition Ciphers** — Rearrange the order of symbols in the plaintext.

3. **By Plaintext Processing**
   - **Block Ciphers** — Process input in fixed-size blocks (e.g., 128 bits for AES).
   - **Stream Ciphers** — Process input one bit or byte at a time (e.g., RC4).

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

Cryptography is foundational to nearly every modern digital system:

- **HTTPS / TLS** — Secures web browsing using RSA + AES + SHA.
- **Banking (ATM, UPI)** — Uses 3DES/AES for PIN encryption.
- **Mobile Networks (4G/5G)** — Uses SNOW 3G and ZUC stream ciphers.
- **Blockchain (Bitcoin)** — Uses SHA-256 hashing and ECDSA signatures.
- **Digital Signatures** — Used in e-passports, Aadhaar, and legal contracts.
- **Disk Encryption** — BitLocker, FileVault use AES-128/256.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Caesar Cipher Mathematical Model

The **Caesar Cipher**, attributed to Julius Caesar (~100 BC), is the canonical example for understanding the encryption–decryption principle. It is a *monoalphabetic substitution cipher* where each letter is shifted by a fixed number of positions in the alphabet.

### Step 1: Assign Numeric Values to Letters

We map each letter $A, B, C, \dots, Z$ to a numeric value from $0$ to $25$:

$$
A \rightarrow 0, \; B \rightarrow 1, \; C \rightarrow 2, \dots, \; Z \rightarrow 25
$$

For a given letter $P$ (as a number), the Caesar cipher with key $K$ performs the following encryption:

$$
C \equiv P + K \pmod{26}
$$

### Step 2: Encryption of the Word "HELLO"

Let the key $K = 3$ (Caesar's traditional shift).

| Plaintext Letter | $P$ (numeric) | $C \equiv P + 3 \pmod{26}$ | Ciphertext Letter |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| H | 7 | $(7+3) \bmod 26 = 10$ | K |
| E | 4 | $(4+3) \bmod 26 = 7$ | H |
| L | 11 | $(11+3) \bmod 26 = 14$ | O |
| L | 11 | $(11+3) \bmod 26 = 14$ | O |
| O | 14 | $(14+3) \bmod 26 = 17$ | R |

**Result:** Plaintext "HELLO" $\rightarrow$ Ciphertext "KHOOR"

### Step 3: Decryption Verification

Decryption must invert the encryption:

$$
P \equiv C - K \pmod{26}
$$

Testing on the first letter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &\equiv C - K \pmod{26} \\
P &\equiv 10 - 3 \pmod{26} \\
P &\equiv 7 \pmod{26} \\
P &= 7 \;\Rightarrow\; H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of correctness criterion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_K(E_K(P)) &= (P + K) - K \pmod{26} \\
&= P \pmod{26}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the correctness criterion $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ is satisfied. $\blacksquare$

### Step 4: Keyspace Analysis

The Caesar cipher has only $K \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 25\}$, i.e., a **keyspace of size 26**. An attacker need only try at most **26 possibilities** to break it — a trivial brute-force attack. This demonstrates why modern ciphers require keys of $2^{128}$ or more possibilities.

$$
\text{Brute-force work (Caesar)} = 26 \;\text{trials}
$$

$$
\text{Brute-force work (AES-128)} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \;\text{trials}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Caesar vs. Modern AES

| Property | Caesar Cipher | AES-128 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Algorithm Type** | Monoalphabetic substitution | Substitution-Permutation Network |
| **Key Size** | 5 bits (K ∈ [0,25]) | 128 bits |
| **Block Size** | 1 character (8 bits) | 128 bits (16 bytes) |
| **Keyspace** | $26$ | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| **Rounds** | 1 | 10 |
| **Year** | ~50 BC | 2001 AD |
| **Vulnerable to** | Brute force, frequency analysis | Theoretically none known |

## 3.3 Historical Evolution — Mathematical Maturity

The progression of ciphers can be mapped against their mathematical complexity and keyspace growth:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Caesar (50 BC)} &: \text{Keyspace} = 26 \\
\text{Affine (600 AD)} &: \text{Keyspace} \approx 312 \\
\text{Vigenère (1553)} &: \text{Keyspace} \approx 26^k \;\text{(}k = \text{key length)} \\
\text{Enigma (1939)} &: \text{Keyspace} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{23} \\
\text{DES (1977)} &: \text{Keyspace} = 2^{56} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{16} \\
\text{AES-128 (2001)} &: \text{Keyspace} = 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \\
\text{AES-256 (2001)} &: \text{Keyspace} = 2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}
\end{aligned}
$$

This exponential growth in keyspace reflects the corresponding rise in computational power available to attackers, fulfilling **Shannon's** requirement of computational security.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: A Generic Cryptosystem

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow of a symmetric-key cryptosystem — from plaintext generation at the sender to plaintext recovery at the receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PLAINTEXT P"]:::in
    E["ENCRYPTION ALGORITHM E"]:::algo
    K1["SECRET KEY K"]:::key
    C["CIPHERTEXT C"]:::out
    CH["INSECURE CHANNEL"]:::chan
    D["DECRYPTION ALGORITHM D"]:::algo
    K2["SECRET KEY K"]:::key
    P2["PLAINTEXT P"]:::in
    ADV["ADVERSARY EAVESDROPPER"]:::adv

    P1 --> E
    K1 --> E
    E --> C
    C --> CH
    CH --> D
    D --> P2
    K2 --> D
    ADV -.->|intercepts| C

    classDef in fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef algo fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef key fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chan fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef adv fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Sender side (left):** Plaintext $P$ is fed into the encryption algorithm $E$, together with secret key $K$, producing ciphertext $C$.
- **Channel (middle):** $C$ traverses an insecure channel where an adversary may attempt to intercept or tamper with it.
- **Receiver side (right):** The same secret key $K$ is fed into the decryption algorithm $D$, which inverts $E$ to recover $P$.

## 4.2 Timeline Diagram: Historical Evolution of Cryptography

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["1900 BC EGYPTIAN HIEROGLYPHICS Non-standard carvings"]:::ancient
    A2["600 BC SCYTALE Spartans wrap parchment on cylinder"]:::ancient
    A3["100 BC CAESAR CIPHER Shift cipher key equals 3"]:::ancient
    A4["800 AD AL-KINDI Frequency analysis treatise"]:::ancient
    A5["1553 VIGENERE CIPHER Polyalphabetic cipher"]:::classical
    A6["1854 BACONIAN CIPHER Binary encoding"]:::classical
    A7["1918 ENIGMA ROTOR MACHINE Electromechanical cipher"]:::modern
    A8["1949 SHANNON THEORY Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems"]:::modern
    A9["1976 DIFFIE-HELLMAN Public key cryptography introduced"]:::modern
    A10["1977 RSA Rivest Shamir Adleman algorithm"]:::modern
    A11["2001 AES Advanced Encryption Standard adopted by NIST"]:::modern

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11

    classDef ancient fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef classical fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef modern fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Classification Tree: Cryptographic Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["CRYPTOLOGY THE STUDY OF SECURE COMMUNICATION"]:::root
    CG["CRYPTOGRAPHY DESIGNING SECURE SYSTEMS"]:::crypto
    CA["CRYPTANALYSIS BREAKING SECURE SYSTEMS"]:::attack

    ROOT --> CG
    ROOT --> CA

    CG --> SK["SYMMETRIC KEY SAME KEY K FOR BOTH ENCRYPT AND DECRYPT"]:::sym
    CG --> AK["ASYMMETRIC KEY PUBLIC KEY ENCRYPTS PRIVATE KEY DECRYPTS"]:::asym
    CG --> H["HASH FUNCTIONS ONE WAY MAPPING NO KEY USED"]:::hash

    SK --> BC["BLOCK CIPHERS FIXED SIZE BLOCKS e.g. AES DES"]:::sub
    SK --> SC["STREAM CIPHERS BIT OR BYTE AT A TIME e.g. RC4"]:::sub

    AK --> RSA["RSA BASED ON INTEGER FACTORING"]:::sub2
    AK --> ECC["ECC BASED ON ELLIPTIC CURVES"]:::sub2

    H --> SHA["SHA FAMILY SHA 1 SHA 256 SHA 3"]:::sub3

    classDef root fill:#1e293b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef crypto fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef attack fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sym fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef asym fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hash fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub2 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note for KTU Students:** This classification tree maps directly to Module 2 (Symmetric Ciphers), Module 3 (Asymmetric Ciphers), and Module 4 (Hash Functions) of the PCCCT501 syllabus. Memorizing this hierarchy earns you at least 3 marks in any descriptive question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse the terms *Cryptography*, *Cryptanalysis*, and *Cryptology*. This costs 1 mark instantly.
> 2. Do NOT credit Kerckhoffs's principle to Shannon; it was published in 1883 by Auguste Kerckhoffs.
> 3. When asked for the "security goal", always list all three: Confidentiality, Integrity, **and** Authentication. Skipping any one loses the third mark.
> 4. Spelling "ciphertext" as "cyphertext" or "cipher text" is penalized under KTU strict marking rules.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define cryptography. List and briefly explain the three security goals it aims to achieve.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cryptography is the science and art of transforming intelligible information (plaintext) into an unintelligible form (ciphertext) using mathematical algorithms and secret keys, so that only authorized parties can retrieve the original message.

**Three Security Goals:**

1. **Confidentiality:** Ensures that the message contents are accessible only to parties who possess the correct decryption key. It prevents unauthorized disclosure of information. Example: HTTPS encrypts credit card numbers during online transactions.

2. **Integrity:** Guarantees that the message has not been altered, modified, or tampered with during transmission. Achieved using cryptographic hash functions like SHA-256 or MAC (Message Authentication Codes).

3. **Authentication:** Verifies the claimed identity of the sender and the legitimacy of the message. Digital signatures (e.g., RSA, ECDSA) provide strong authentication.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming goals + 1 mark for brief explanation)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series 2]
**State and explain Kerckhoffs's Principle. Why is it important in modern cryptography?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement (1883):** *Auguste Kerckhoffs's Principle asserts that the security of a cryptosystem must not depend on keeping the algorithm secret; it must depend solely on keeping the key secret.*

**Explanation:** A cryptographic system $S$ consists of algorithm $E$, algorithm $D$, and key $K$. Kerckhoffs argued that $E$ and $D$ should be publicly published and subjected to intense scrutiny by the cryptographic community, while $K$ remains the sole secret.

**Importance in Modern Cryptography:**

1. **Enables Peer Review:** Open algorithms (AES, RSA) have been attacked by thousands of researchers for over two decades and remain unbroken, providing high confidence.

2. **Standardization:** Open algorithms enable global interoperability (e.g., TLS uses the same AES everywhere).

3. **Prevents Security Through Obscurity (STO):** Hidden algorithms have historically failed repeatedly — examples include the compromised A5/1 algorithm in GSM mobile networks and the proprietary Crypto-1 cipher in MIFARE Classic RFID cards.

4. **Supports Long-Term Trust:** Algorithms like DES and AES went through rigorous open competitions by NIST before adoption.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement + 1 for explanation + 1 for importance)*

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Mandate:** Per KTU ESE regulation 2024, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generic model of a symmetric-key cryptosystem. List the five essential components of a cryptosystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a detailed note on the historical evolution of cryptography from ancient times to the modern era, highlighting at least five landmark contributions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Generic Symmetric-Key Cryptosystem Block Diagram:**

```
+----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+
|   Plaintext    |     |   Encryption    |     |   Ciphertext    |
|       P        | --> |  Algorithm E_K  | --> |       C        |
+----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+
                              ^                       |
                              | K                     | (Insecure Channel)
                              v                       v
+----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+
|   Plaintext    |     |   Decryption    |     |   Ciphertext    |
|       P        | <-- |  Algorithm D_K  | <-- |       C        |
+----------------+     +-----------------+     +-----------------+
```

**Five Essential Components of a Cryptosystem:**

A cryptosystem is formally defined as a 5-tuple $\{P, C, K, E, D\}$:

1. **P (Plaintext Space):** The set of all possible plaintext messages.

2. **C (Ciphertext Space):** The set of all possible ciphertext outputs.

3. **K (Key Space):** The set of all possible keys that the algorithm accepts.

4. **E (Encryption Algorithm):** The set of encryption transformations $E_K : P \rightarrow C$ for each $K \in K$.

5. **D (Decryption Algorithm):** The set of decryption transformations $D_K : C \rightarrow P$ for each $K \in K$, satisfying $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$.

**Working Principle:**
The sender applies the encryption transformation $E_K$ to plaintext $P$ using the agreed secret key $K$, producing ciphertext $C = E_K(P)$. This ciphertext is transmitted through an insecure channel where an adversary may intercept it. The authorized receiver applies the inverse decryption transformation $D_K$ using the same key $K$, recovering $P = D_K(C) = D_K(E_K(P))$.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 3 marks for five components + 2 marks for working principle explanation)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Historical Evolution of Cryptography:**

**1. Ancient Era (~1900 BC – 500 BC):**

- **Egyptian Hieroglyphics (~1900 BC):** The earliest known cryptographic inscriptions appear in the tomb of Khnumhotep II, where scribes used non-standard hieroglyphic symbols to obscure meaning. This was *not* true cryptography but rather a form of *misdirection*.

- **Scytale Cipher (~600 BC, Sparta):** The Spartans wrapped a strip of parchment around a cylindrical rod of fixed diameter and wrote their message along the rod. When unwound, the message appeared scrambled. The receiver re-wrapped it on a rod of the same diameter to read it. This is the earliest known *transposition cipher*.

**2. Classical Era (~100 BC – 1900 AD):**

- **Caesar Cipher (~100 BC):** Julius Caesar used a monoalphabetic substitution cipher with a fixed shift of $K = 3$ to communicate with his generals. Each letter was shifted forward by 3 positions in the Latin alphabet (A → D, B → E, etc.).

- **Al-Kindi's Frequency Analysis (~800 AD):** Arab polymath Abu Yusuf Al-Kindi published the first known treatise on **cryptanalysis**, demonstrating that substitution ciphers can be broken by analyzing the statistical frequency of letters in ciphertext. This single discovery rendered all monoalphabetic substitution ciphers obsolete.

- **Vigenère Cipher (1553):** Invented by Giovan Battista Bellaso but misattributed to Blaise de Vigenère, this polyalphabetic cipher uses a keyword to vary the shift for each letter, thwarting simple frequency analysis. It was called *"le chiffre indéchiffrable"* (the indecipherable cipher) for nearly 300 years until Charles Babbage and Friedrich Kasiski independently broke it in the 1860s.

**3. Mechanical Era (1900 – 1949):**

- **Enigma Machine (1918 – WWII):** Invented by Arthur Scherbius, the German Enigma was a rotor-based electromechanical cipher machine producing $1.59 \times 10^{20}$ possible configurations. It was famously broken by **Alan Turing**, **Gordon Welchman**, and the team at **Bletchley Park** using the *Bombe* electromechanical decoder, shortening WWII by an estimated two years.

**4. Mathematical Era (1949 – Present):**

- **Shannon's Information Theory (1949):** Claude Shannon's landmark paper *"Communication Theory of Secrecy Systems"* established the mathematical foundations of cryptography, introducing concepts of *entropy*, *confusion*, and *diffusion* that still govern modern cipher design.

- **DES and Public-Key Cryptography (1976–1977):** The **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** was adopted by NIST in 1977. Simultaneously, **Diffie-Hellman** (1976) and **RSA** (1977) revolutionized the field by introducing **public-key (asymmetric) cryptography**, solving the previously intractable key-distribution problem.

- **AES (2001):** The **Advanced Encryption Standard** was adopted as a replacement for DES, using 128-bit blocks and keys of 128/192/256 bits. AES remains the global standard for symmetric encryption.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per era introduction + 1 mark for each major cipher explanation across the 5+ landmarks)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the differences between symmetric-key and asymmetric-key cryptography. Provide two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the Caesar cipher, demonstrate the encryption and decryption of the message **"KHOOR"** with key $K = 3$, and comment on its security weaknesses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Symmetric-Key Cryptography | Asymmetric-Key Cryptography |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Keys** | One shared secret key $K$ | Two keys: Public $K_{pub}$ and Private $K_{priv}$ |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult — must be shared via secure channel | Easy — public key can be openly distributed |
| **Speed** | Very fast (suitable for bulk data) | Slower (100–1000× slower than symmetric) |
| **Scalability** | Poor — $n$ users need $n(n-1)/2$ keys | Excellent — only $n$ key pairs needed |
| **Primary Use** | Encryption of bulk data | Digital signatures, key exchange |
| **Key Size** | 128–256 bits typically | 2048–4096 bits (RSA) or 256 bits (ECC) |
| **Examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, Blowfish | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, DSA |
| **Founders** | Ancient (Caesar) | Diffie-Hellman (1976) |
| **Mathematical Basis** | Substitution-permutation networks | Integer factorization (RSA), discrete log (ECC) |

**Symmetric Key Examples:**

1. **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard):** Block cipher with 128-bit blocks, key sizes of 128/192/256 bits. Adopted by NIST in 2001 after an open competition.

2. **DES (Data Encryption Standard):** Block cipher with 64-bit blocks and 56-bit effective key. Now considered insecure due to its small keyspace ($2^{56}$).

**Asymmetric Key Examples:**

1. **RSA (1977):** Security based on the computational difficulty of factoring the product of two large primes. Standard key sizes: 2048 or 4096 bits.

2. **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography, 1985):** Security based on the difficulty of the *Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)*. Offers equivalent security to RSA at much smaller key sizes (e.g., 256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA).

*(7 marks: 4 marks for comparison table + 2 marks for symmetric examples + 1 mark for asymmetric examples)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:**
- Ciphertext: **"KHOOR"**
- Key: $K = 3$
- We must apply decryption: $P \equiv C - K \pmod{26}$

**Step-by-Step Decryption:**

| Ciphertext Letter | $C$ (numeric) | $P \equiv C - 3 \pmod{26}$ | Plaintext Letter |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| K | 10 | $(10-3) \bmod 26 = 7$ | H |
| H | 7 | $(7-3) \bmod 26 = 4$ | E |
| O | 14 | $(14-3) \bmod 26 = 11$ | L |
| O | 14 | $(14-3) \bmod 26 = 11$ | L |
| R | 17 | $(17-3) \bmod 26 = 14$ | O |

**Result:** Decrypted plaintext = **"HELLO"**

**Verification of Encryption Round-Trip:**

To confirm the correctness, let us re-encrypt "HELLO" with $K = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H: &\quad 7 + 3 \pmod{26} = 10 \rightarrow K \\
E: &\quad 4 + 3 \pmod{26} = 7 \rightarrow H \\
L: &\quad 11 + 3 \pmod{26} = 14 \rightarrow O \\
L: &\quad 11 + 3 \pmod{26} = 14 \rightarrow O \\
O: &\quad 14 + 3 \pmod{26} = 17 \rightarrow R
\end{aligned}
$$

Encryption of "HELLO" yields "KHOOR" ✓ — this confirms $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$.

**Security Weaknesses of the Caesar Cipher:**

1. **Tiny Keyspace:** Only 25 non-trivial keys exist. An attacker can test all 25 in milliseconds using brute force.

2. **Frequency Analysis Attack:** In any sufficiently long English text, the most frequent letter is **E (~12.7%)**, followed by T, A, O, I, N, S. By comparing the most frequent ciphertext letter to 'E', the attacker instantly deduces the shift $K$.

3. **No Diffusion:** Each plaintext letter maps to a *single* fixed ciphertext letter. Patterns in the plaintext (e.g., double letters like "LL" in "HELLO") are preserved in ciphertext ("OO" in "KHOOR"), leaking structural information.

4. **No Confusion with Key Variation:** Because $K$ is fixed, the entire ciphertext is a simple shifted alphabet, making it trivially solvable by trial-and-error.

5. **Vulnerable to Known-Plaintext Attack:** If the attacker knows even one plaintext–ciphertext pair, $K$ is immediately computed as $K = C - P \pmod{26}$.

**Conclusion:** The Caesar cipher, while historically significant, is unsuitable for any modern security requirement. It serves today only as an educational illustration of substitution principles.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for decryption math + 2 marks for verification + 2 marks for security weaknesses)*

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Module 1: Definition & History of Cryptography**

- [ ] **Definition:** Cryptography = "hidden writing" (Greek *kryptos* + *graphein*). It is the science of securing communication against adversaries.
- [ ] **Three Core Goals:** **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication. Add **Non-Repudiation** for extended model.
- [ ] **Cryptosystem 5-Tuple:** $\{P, C, K, E, D\}$. Memorize the formal notation $E_K(P) = C$ and $D_K(C) = P$.
- [ ] **Correctness Criterion:** $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ — must hold for every valid cipher.
- [ ] **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883):** Security through key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy.
- [ ] **Shannon's Maxim (1949):** "The enemy knows the system."
- [ ] **One-Time Pad (OTP):** The only *information-theoretically secure* cipher.
- [ ] **Historical Landmarks:**
   - ~1900 BC — Egyptian non-standard hieroglyphics
   - ~600 BC — Scytale (Sparta)
   - ~100 BC — Caesar cipher (shift = 3)
   - ~800 AD — Al-Kindi's frequency analysis
   - 1553 — Vigenère cipher
   - 1918 — Enigma machine
   - 1949 — Shannon's theory
   - 1976 — Diffie-Hellman (public key)
   - 1977 — RSA algorithm
   - 2001 — AES adoption
- [ ] **Classification:** Symmetric vs. Asymmetric; Block vs. Stream; Substitution vs. Transposition.
- [ ] **Caesar Math:** $C \equiv P + K \pmod{26}$ and $P \equiv C - K \pmod{26}$; keyspace = 26.
- [ ] **Cryptology ≠ Cryptography ≠ Cryptanalysis:** Cryptology = Cryptography + Cryptanalysis.
- [ ] **Modern Examples:** AES (symmetric), RSA (asymmetric), SHA-256 (hash).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Definition and History of Cryptography**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT501 — Applied Cryptography)*

# MODULE 1 — IMPORTANCE AND APPLICATIONS OF APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Applied Cryptography** is the disciplined study and engineering deployment of mathematical techniques, computational algorithms, and protocol-level mechanisms designed to secure digital communication against adversarial behaviour. The discipline guarantees four foundational properties collectively known as the **CIA Triad plus Non-Repudiation**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Security Services (CIA + NR)**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized entities (achieved primarily through *encryption*).
> 2. **Integrity** — Guarantees that data has not been altered or tampered with during transit or storage (achieved through *hash functions* and *MACs*).
> 3. **Authentication** — Verifies the identity of a communicating party (achieved through *digital signatures*, *certificates*, and *challenge-response protocols*).
> 4. **Non-Repudiation** — Prevents an entity from denying that it performed a particular action (achieved via *digital signatures* with trusted timestamping).

The **"Importance and Applications"** topic positions cryptography not as an abstract mathematical exercise, but as the **backbone of modern digital infrastructure** — governing how money moves, how identities are verified, and how nations defend their cyberspace.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Letter & Wax Seal" Model

Imagine you are a merchant in 1500 AD sending a price quote to a buyer in another city. You have three worries:

| Real-World Problem | Cryptographic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The messenger might **read** the letter | **Confidentiality** (Encryption) |
| The messenger might **change** the price | **Integrity** (Hashing / MAC) |
| The buyer might later **deny** receiving the quote | **Non-Repudiation** (Digital Signature) |
| You are not sure if the letter is **truly from the buyer** | **Authentication** (Certificates) |

In the 1500s, you solved this with a **wax seal stamped with your unique signet ring**. In the 21st century, the "wax seal" is a **cryptographic hash + digital signature** produced by a private key. The underlying principle is identical — the seal binds the *identity of the sender* to the *content of the message* in a way that cannot be forged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**: Every modern security service you study in this course (AES, RSA, SHA-256, TLS, etc.) is simply a digital reincarnation of a physical-world trust problem that humans have been solving for centuries.

---

### Why Cryptography is **Important** in the Modern Era

The importance of applied cryptography can be categorized along three axes — **Technological**, **Economic**, and **Societal**:

#### A. Technological Importance
- **Foundation of Cybersecurity**: Every secure protocol (HTTPS, SSH, IPsec, WPA3, TLS 1.3) is built on cryptographic primitives.
- **Protection against evolving threats**: Quantum-safe cryptography is now critical because future quantum computers can break RSA and ECC using **Shor's algorithm**.
- **Secure software development**: APIs, mobile apps, and IoT devices rely on cryptographic libraries (OpenSSL, libsodium, Bouncy Castle).

#### B. Economic Importance
- **E-Commerce Enablement**: Without TLS/SSL, online banking, UPI, credit card transactions, and stock trading would be impossible.
- **Global digital economy**: According to industry estimates, the global cryptographic solutions market exceeds **USD 15+ billion annually**, with projected growth driven by cloud, blockchain, and zero-trust architectures.
- **Intellectual Property Protection**: DRM (Digital Rights Management) protects films, music, and software.

#### C. Societal & National Security Importance
- **Defense & Intelligence**: Military-grade encryption (e.g., **AES-256**, **Suite B Cryptography**) protects classified communications.
- **Privacy & Civil Liberties**: GDPR, India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023 mandate cryptographic protection of personal data.
- **Critical Infrastructure**: Power grids, water supply, healthcare systems (HIPAA compliance) all depend on cryptographic controls.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Knowledge Sheet

### The 5 Pillars of Cryptographic Application

Applied cryptography is not a monolith — it is a layered discipline where each primitive serves a specific purpose. Below is the operational breakdown:

#### Pillar 1 — **Symmetric Key Cryptography (SKC)**
- **Operational Logic**: A single shared secret key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
- **Equation**: $C = E_K(P)$ and $P = D_K(C)$.
- **Speed**: Very fast (suitable for bulk data).
- **Examples**: **AES (128/192/256-bit)**, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20.
- **Limitation**: Key distribution problem — how to safely share $K$?

#### Pillar 2 — **Asymmetric Key Cryptography (AKC)**
- **Operational Logic**: A mathematically linked key pair $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ where $K_{pub}$ encrypts and only $K_{priv}$ decrypts (or vice-versa for signatures).
- **Equation**: $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$, $P = D_{K_{priv}}(C)$.
- **Examples**: **RSA**, **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**, Diffie-Hellman, ElGamal.
- **Advantage**: Solves key distribution. **Disadvantage**: 100–1000x slower than SKC.

#### Pillar 3 — **Hash Functions**
- **Operational Logic**: A one-way function that maps arbitrary input to a fixed-size digest.
- **Equation**: $h = H(M)$ where $h$ is a fixed length (e.g., **256 bits** for SHA-256).
- **Properties**: Pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance.
- **Examples**: **SHA-256**, SHA-3, BLAKE2.

#### Pillar 4 — **Digital Signatures & Authentication**
- **Operational Logic**: Sign with private key, verify with public key.
- **Equation**: $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{K_{priv}}(H(M))$; verify with $\text{Verify}_{K_{pub}}(M, \sigma)$.
- **Examples**: RSA-PSS, **ECDSA**, EdDSA (Ed25519).

#### Pillar 5 — **Key Management & Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**
- **Operational Logic**: Trusted third-party **Certificate Authorities (CAs)** bind identities to public keys via X.509 digital certificates.
- **Examples**: Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, eMudhra (India's national CA).

---

### 📋 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Cryptographic Applications Matrix

| Application Domain | Cryptographic Primitive Used | Security Service Provided |
|---|---|---|
| HTTPS / Web Browsing | TLS 1.3 (RSA/ECC + AES-GCM + SHA-256) | Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication |
| Email Security (PGP, S/MIME) | Hybrid (RSA/AES), Digital Signatures | Confidentiality, Non-Repudiation |
| Wireless Networks (Wi-Fi) | WPA3 (SAE + AES-128) | Authentication, Confidentiality |
| Blockchain & Cryptocurrency | ECDSA, SHA-256, Merkle Trees | Integrity, Non-Repudiation, Authentication |
| Mobile Banking / UPI | AES + RSA + HMAC + PKI Certificates | All four CIA+NR services |
| Smart Cards / Aadhaar | ECC, RSA, SHA-256 | Authentication, Integrity |
| Disk Encryption (BitLocker) | AES-128/256 (XTS mode) | Confidentiality at rest |
| VPNs (IPsec, OpenVPN) | IKEv2 (DH + RSA) + AES + HMAC-SHA | All four services for tunnel traffic |
| Digital Currency (CBDC, e₹) | ECDSA + Hash chains + HSMs | Authentication, Non-Repudiation |
| Cloud Storage (AWS S3 SSE) | AES-256 + KMS-managed keys | Confidentiality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: When you graduate and join a software or cybersecurity firm, you will rarely design new cryptographic algorithms. Instead, you will **select, integrate, and configure** the correct combination of primitives from the table above to solve a business problem. KTU exams test exactly this selection skill.

---

### The 3-Plane Model of Cryptographic Importance (Conceptual Framework)

Cryptography is critical because modern digital systems operate across three planes:

1. **Data Plane (Content Security)** — Protecting the payload: encryption at rest and in transit.
2. **Identity Plane (Trust Security)** — Proving who you are: authentication, PKI, biometrics + crypto.
3. **Control Plane (Protocol Security)** — Protecting the rules of communication: key exchange, session management, certificate validation.

A failure in any one plane collapses the entire system. For example, even perfect AES encryption is useless if the key is exchanged over an untrusted channel (failure in control plane).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Analytical Matrix — Quantitative Importance & Step-by-Step Deductions

### Worked Example 1: Computational Cost Comparison (Why Hybrid Cryptography Exists)

**Problem (KTU-Style 3-mark derivation):**  
Prove mathematically that hybrid cryptography (using both symmetric and asymmetric primitives) is computationally more efficient than pure asymmetric encryption for a 1 MB file.

---

**Step 1: Define the cost functions.**  
Let $T_{sym}$ = time to encrypt 1 byte with AES-256.  
Let $T_{asym}$ = time to encrypt 1 byte with RSA-2048.

**Step 2: Empirical benchmark values (from industry measurements).**

$$
T_{sym} \approx 0.5 \text{ ns/byte} \quad \text{(AES-NI hardware acceleration)}
$$

$$
T_{asym} \approx 50{,}000 \text{ ns/byte} \quad \text{(RSA-2048 software)}
$$

**Step 3: Time for pure asymmetric encryption of 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes.**

$$
T_{pure} = 2^{20} \times 50{,}000 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{pure} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 50{,}000 \text{ ns} = 5.243 \times 10^{10} \text{ ns} \approx 52.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4: Time for hybrid scheme.**  
- Generate a random 256-bit AES session key: $\approx 0.001$ seconds.  
- Encrypt the session key with RSA-2048: 256 bits = 32 bytes.

$$
T_{RSA-key} = 32 \times 50{,}000 \text{ ns} = 1.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ ns} = 0.0016 \text{ seconds}
$$

- Encrypt 1 MB file with AES:

$$
T_{AES} = 2^{20} \times 0.5 \text{ ns} = 5.24 \times 10^{5} \text{ ns} \approx 0.000524 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5: Total hybrid time.**

$$
T_{hybrid} = T_{RSA-key} + T_{AES} \approx 0.0016 + 0.000524 \approx 0.002124 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the speedup factor.**

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{pure}}{T_{hybrid}} = \frac{52.4}{0.002124} \approx 24{,}670\times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: Hybrid cryptography is roughly **24,000× faster** than pure asymmetric encryption for 1 MB files. This is exactly why every real protocol (TLS, PGP, SSH) uses **hybrid encryption** — asymmetric crypto only encrypts a tiny session key, and symmetric crypto encrypts the actual data.

---

### Worked Example 2: Brute-Force Resistance of AES-256 (Why Key Size Matters)

**Problem:** Show that AES-256 is computationally infeasible to brute-force using a mathematical argument based on the number of possible keys.

**Step 1:** AES-256 uses a key of length **256 bits**, so the total keyspace is:

$$
N = 2^{256}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to decimal for intuition.

$$
N = 2^{256} \approx 1.1579 \times 10^{77}
$$

**Step 3:** Assume the attacker can test **$10^{18}$ keys/second** (a powerful supercomputer like Frontier).

**Step 4:** Number of seconds in the age of the universe ($\approx 13.8$ billion years):

$$
t_{universe} = 13.8 \times 10^{9} \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \approx 4.35 \times 10^{17} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** Total keys tried in the entire age of the universe:

$$
N_{tested} = 10^{18} \times 4.35 \times 10^{17} = 4.35 \times 10^{35}
$$

**Step 6:** Probability of guessing the correct key:

$$
P = \frac{N_{tested}}{N} = \frac{4.35 \times 10^{35}}{1.1579 \times 10^{77}} \approx 3.76 \times 10^{-42}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: The probability of breaking AES-256 by brute force — even running a supercomputer for the entire age of the universe — is approximately **$3.76 \times 10^{-42}$**, effectively zero. This is the mathematical bedrock of *importance* of strong cryptography in protecting long-lived secrets (e.g., classified government data with a 50-year secrecy requirement).

---

### Analytical Matrix — Mapping Security Threats to Cryptographic Solutions

| Threat / Attack | Description | Cryptographic Countermeasure | Security Service Restored |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping (Passive)** | Attacker reads transmitted data | Symmetric encryption (AES) | Confidentiality |
| **Tampering (Active)** | Attacker modifies data in transit | Hash + Digital Signature | Integrity |
| **Impersonation** | Attacker pretends to be a legitimate user | Digital Certificates, PKI | Authentication |
| **Replay Attack** | Attacker resends a previously valid message | Timestamps + Nonces + MAC | Freshness, Authentication |
| **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** | Attacker intercepts and relays messages | TLS handshake with CA verification | Authentication + Key Exchange |
| **Repudiation** | Sender denies sending a message | Digital Signature (non-forgeable) | Non-Repudiation |
| **Data at Rest Theft** | Stolen hard drive / database | Disk encryption (AES-XTS) | Confidentiality |
| **Password Database Breach** | Plaintext passwords leaked | bcrypt / Argon2 (hashing + salt) | Authentication storage |
| **Quantum Attack** | Shor's algorithm on RSA/ECC | Post-Quantum Cryptography (Kyber, Dilithium) | Long-term Confidentiality |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: The 4-Security-Services Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Cryptographic System] --> B1[Confidentiality Service]
    A0 --> B2[Integrity Service]
    A0 --> B3[Authentication Service]
    A0 --> B4[Non-Repudiation Service]

    B1 --> C1[Symmetric Encryption - AES]
    B1 --> C2[Asymmetric Encryption - RSA/ECC]

    B2 --> D1[Hash Functions - SHA-256]
    B2 --> D2[Message Authentication Codes - HMAC]

    B3 --> E1[Digital Certificates - X.509]
    B3 --> E2[Challenge Response Protocols]

    B4 --> F1[Digital Signatures - RSA/ECDSA]
    B4 --> F2[Trusted Timestamping]
```

---

### Diagram 2: Hybrid Cryptography Workflow (How TLS Actually Works)

```mermaid

    S0[Start - Sender has plaintext M] --> S1[Generate random 256-bit AES session key K]

    S3 --> S4[Transmit C and W over network]
    S4 --> R1[Receiver unwraps K using RSA private key]
    R1 --> R2[Receiver decrypts C using K to recover M]
    R2 --> R3[End - Receiver has plaintext M]

    S1:::cryptonode
    S2:::cryptonode

    S4:::networknode
```

### Diagram 4: The 3-Plane Security Model (Conceptual Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PLANE1[Plane 1 - Data Plane] --> T1[Encryption at Rest and In Transit]
```
