# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: LSB Embedding Capacity of an Image

A common analytical question in KTU is: *"How many bits can be embedded into a grayscale image of size $W \times H$ using $k$-bit LSB substitution?"*

**Step 1 — Total number of pixels.**
The image contains one pixel per spatial coordinate:

$$N_{\text{px}} = W \cdot H$$

*Explanation:* Each pixel is a discrete sample; counting across the image grid gives the total sample count.

**Step 2 — Bits embedded per pixel.**
In $k$-bit LSB substitution, we replace the lowest $k$ bits of each pixel sample with message bits:

$$N_{\text{bits/pixel}} = k$$

**Step 3 — Total embedded capacity.**

$$N_{\text{capacity}} = N_{\text{px}} \cdot N_{\text{bits/pixel}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{capacity}} = W \cdot H \cdot k \ \text{ bits}}$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to bytes.**

$$N_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \cdot H \cdot k}{8}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
For a $512 \times 512$ grayscale image with $k=2$:

$$N_{\text{capacity}} = 512 \times 512 \times 2 = 524\,288 \ \text{bits} = 65\,536 \ \text{bytes} = 64 \ \text{kB}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Squared Error and PSNR for a Watermarked Image

The MSE between cover $C$ and watermarked image $W$ over $N$ pixels is:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(C_i - W_i)^2$$

Each $C_i$ and $W_i$ is an 8-bit grayscale sample ranging over $[0, 255]$. The maximum possible squared error per pixel is $(255 - 0)^2 = 255^2$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into PSNR formula.**

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^2}{\text{MSE}}\right) \ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation thresholds (industry standard):**

| PSNR (dB)              | Visual Quality             |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| $> 40$                 | Imperceptible to human eye |
| $30$ to $40$           | Minor, often acceptable    |
| $20$ to $30$           | Noticeable degradation     |
| $< 20$                 | Severe, unacceptable       |

**Step 3 — Worked example.**
Suppose $W = 256 \times 256$, MSE $= 5.2$:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{65\,025}{5.2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(12\,504.8)$$

$$= 10 \times 4.097 = 40.97 \ \text{dB}$$

*Conclusion:* Watermark is **imperceptible** by the $>40$ dB rule.

## 3.3 Derivation: Correlation Detector and Decision Threshold

The correlation between the (possibly attacked) watermarked object $W^*$ and the reference watermark $M_w$ is:

$$\rho = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} W^*_i \cdot M_{w,i}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(W^*_i)^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N}(M_{w,i})^2}}$$

**Step 1 — Define the test statistic.**
Under hypothesis $H_0$ (no watermark present in $W^*$), the expected value is approximately $\rho \approx 0$. Under $H_1$ (watermark present), $\rho$ is large and positive.

**Step 2 — Set the decision threshold using Neyman-Pearson.**
For a fixed false-alarm probability $P_{FA}$, choose $\tau$ such that:

$$P(\rho \ge \tau \mid H_0) = P_{FA}$$

**Step 3 — Final decision rule.**

$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} H_1 \ (\text{Watermark Detected}) & \text{if } \rho \ge \tau \\ H_0 \ (\text{No Watermark}) & \text{if } \rho < \tau \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The threshold $\tau$ is the single most important parameter in any watermarking detector. Too low → false positives (claiming ownership wrongly). Too high → missed detections (attacks succeed).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack-by-Defence Mapping

| Attack Type                    | Target                | Effect on Steganography                | Effect on Watermarking                       | Defence                                                |
|--------------------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| **Visual Inspection**          | Cover perceptibility  | Detect distortion                      | Not effective on invisible marks             | Use transform-domain embedding                         |
| **Compression (JPEG/MP3)**     | Statistical profile   | Destroys LSB payload                   | Fragile marks fail; robust marks survive     | Spread spectrum, DCT/DWT, error-correction codes       |
| **Cropping / Re-sampling**     | Spatial alignment     | Misaligns message bits                 | Breaks fragile marks; some robust marks OK   | Redundant embedding, template-based resynchronization |
| **Noise addition (AWGN)**      | SNR                   | BER rises rapidly                      | Correlation drops below $\tau$               | Increase $\alpha$, repetition coding                   |
| **Collusion Attack**           | Multiple copies       | Not directly relevant                  | Combine several copies to estimate & remove | Anti-collusion codes, traitor tracing                  |
| **Steganalysis (Statistical)** | Detectability         | Core threat — *the* attack            | Less effective; detector knows a mark exists| Model-based stego (e.g., HUGO, S-UNIWARD)              |
| **Forgery / Re-watermarking**  | Ownership ambiguity   | Not relevant                           | Attacker inserts *their* watermark           | Time-stamping, TTP, hashing with cryptographic signature|

## 3.5 Worked Analytical Comparison (for descriptive answers)

**Question:** *A payload of 8 kB must be hidden. Discuss the suitability of steganography vs. watermarking if the cover is a $256 \times 256$ image and the carrier will be compressed before distribution.*

**Step 1 — Capacity check.**
For LSB with $k=1$:

$$N_{\text{capacity}} = 256 \times 256 \times 1 = 65\,536 \ \text{bits} = 8\,192 \ \text{bytes} = 8 \ \text{kB}$$

Capacity is just sufficient — only with $k=1$ (minimum distortion).

**Step 2 — Robustness check.**
If compressed via JPEG (quality 75), LSBs of pixel values will be **completely scrambled**. Therefore:
- **Steganography fails** (the 8 kB message is destroyed).
- **Watermarking requires** transform-domain embedding (DCT/DWT) to survive.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

> [!TIP]
> For this scenario, *neither* plain LSB steganography nor naive spatial watermarking is suitable. The correct design choice is **transform-domain watermarking** with repetition coding and error correction (e.g., BCH or Reed–Solomon codes) — or use of a non-compressed cover (BMP) if steganographic transmission is essential.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Steganography Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Cover Object C] --> nodeE{Embedding Function E}
    nodeB[Secret Message M] --> nodeE
    nodeK[Optional Key K] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[Stego Object S]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Public Channel / Internet]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Received Stego Object Sdash]
    nodeH --> nodeD{Extraction Function D}
    nodeK2[Optional Key K] --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeM2[Recovered Message Mdash]
    nodeAtt[Steganalyst / Attacker] -.-> nodeF
    nodeAtt -.-> nodeG
```

*Interpretation:* The dotted lines (`-.->`) represent the *adversary's* observation path. Notice the attacker never sees $M$ — only $S$ and the channel traffic.

## 4.2 Generic Digital Watermarking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC1[Original Cover C] --> nodeEW{Embedder}
    nodeMW[Watermark Mw] --> nodeEW
    nodeA1[Embedding Strength alpha] --> nodeEW
    nodeEW --> nodeW[Watermarked Object W]
    nodeW --> nodeDist[Distribution / Channel with Attacks]
    nodeDist --> nodeWstar[Attacked Object Wstar]
    nodeWstar --> nodeDet{Correlation Detector}
    nodeMWref[Reference Watermark] --> nodeDet
    nodeDet --> nodeOut{Decision rho vs tau}
    nodeOut --> nodeY[Watermark Present Ownership Claimed]
    nodeOut --> nodeN[No Watermark or Different Owner]
```

*Interpretation:* The detector is *passive* — it does not extract a payload in the simplest form, only answers the binary question "is watermark $M_w$ present in $W^*$?".

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Information Hiding] --> nodeQ1{Goal?}
    nodeQ1 -->|Conceal existence of message| nodeS[Steganography]
    nodeQ1 -->|Assert ownership of carrier| nodeW[Watermarking]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[Capacity High]
    nodeS --> nodeS2[Robustness Low]
    nodeS --> nodeS3[Detection = Total Failure]
    nodeW --> nodeW1[Capacity Low to Medium]
    nodeW --> nodeW2[Robustness High]
    nodeW --> nodeW3[Detection = Survived Attack]
    nodeS1 --> nodeOutS[Best for Covert Communication]
    nodeW2 --> nodeOutW[Best for IP Protection]
```

## 4.4 Embedding–Extraction Cycle (Watermarking)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Plaintext Watermark] --> nodeP2[Encryption Optional ECC]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Modulation BPSK QAM Spread Spectrum]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4[Embedding DCT DWT Spatial]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5[Public Distribution]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6[Possible Attacks]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7[Detection / Extraction]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8[Decision Correlation Match]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for students:** The attacker operates between *Public Distribution* and *Detection/Extraction* in the watermarking pipeline. The system designer must make the watermark survive this exact stretch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between steganography and digital watermarking in terms of purpose, robustness, and detection consequence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~1 mark per point):**

1. **Purpose:** Steganography aims at *covert communication* — the goal is to hide the *existence* of a message. Digital watermarking aims at *copyright protection / ownership assertion* — the goal is to bind an identity to a host asset that is openly distributed.
2. **Robustness:** Steganography requires *low* robustness (a single successful delivery is usually enough). Watermarking requires *high* robustness, because the watermarked object is repeatedly distributed, copied, and attacked.
3. **Detection Consequence:** In steganography, *detection* of the hidden message is a **complete system failure** — the communication channel is compromised. In watermarking, the mere *presence* of a watermark is expected and acceptable; the system fails only if the watermark is **removed or forged**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "steganography hides data, watermarking hides data" — this is a *vague* answer. Use precise phrases: *"conceals existence"* vs *"asserts ownership"*. Vague wording costs full marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**List any three applications of digital watermarking. Why is it preferred over steganography for copyright protection?**

**Model Answer:**

**Three applications of digital watermarking (1.5 marks):**
- **Copyright protection** of images, audio, and video.
- **Broadcast monitoring** (e.g., detecting unauthorized rebroadcast of content).
- **Tamper detection / content authentication** (using fragile watermarks).
- *(Optional extras: fingerprinting, copy control, metadata embedding.)*

**Why preferred over steganography for copyright (1.5 marks):**
- Watermarks are **robust** — they survive compression, cropping, and re-encoding, whereas steganographic payloads are typically destroyed by such operations.
- Watermarks are designed to be **publicly detectable** without compromising security; the carrier itself is the asset being protected, so the cover does not need to remain secret.
- Watermarking supports **ownership resolution in disputes** (via time-stamping and TTP), which steganography is not designed to do.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **general model of a steganographic system**. List and briefly explain the **three classical properties** that any secure steganographic system must satisfy. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Least Significant Bit (LSB) substitution method** for image steganography. Compute the **maximum embedding capacity** (in bytes) for a $1024 \times 1024$ grayscale image using 2-bit LSB substitution. Discuss **two major weaknesses** of LSB-based stego-systems. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
A steganographic system consists of:
- **Cover Object ($C$)** — the host carrier.
- **Message ($M$)** — the secret payload.
- **Key ($K$)** — optional secret.
- **Embedding Function ($E$)** — produces the stego-object.
- **Stego-Object ($S$)** — transmitted via a public channel.
- **Extraction Function ($D$)** — recovers $M'$ at the receiver.
- **Adversary / Steganalyst** — observes $S$ and tries to detect / extract $M$.

(Reference: Diagram 4.1 in SECTION_4.)

**Three Classical Properties (4 marks):**
1. **Imperceptibility (Undetectability):** The stego-object $S$ must be *visually/audibly/statistically* indistinguishable from the original cover $C$. Mathematically, $P(S) \approx P(C)$ — the probability distributions must match closely.
2. **Capacity (Payload):** The system should embed a *meaningful amount* of information relative to the cover size. The trade-off with imperceptibility is governed by $k$ (LSBs used) or $\alpha$ (embedding strength).
3. **Robustness:** Although steganography prioritizes one-time delivery, the payload should at minimum survive *unintentional distortions* (e.g., format conversion, mild compression) on the channel. *Note:* Robustness is the *weakest* of the three for steganography, but still desirable.

(A fourth property often mentioned is **Security** — the inability of an attacker with full knowledge of the algorithm to extract the message without the key $K$.)

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**LSB Substitution Explanation (3 marks):**
In LSB substitution, the least significant bit(s) of each cover sample (pixel) are overwritten by message bits. For a grayscale image with sample value $C_i$ and message bit $m_i$:

$$C'_i = C_i - (C_i \bmod 2^k) + m_i$$

The original $C_i$ is altered by at most $2^k - 1$ in value, which is imperceptible for $k = 1$ or $2$ in 8-bit images.

**Capacity Computation (2 marks):**

$$N_{\text{capacity}} = W \cdot H \cdot k = 1024 \times 1024 \times 2 = 2\,097\,152 \ \text{bits}$$

$$= \frac{2\,097\,152}{8} = 262\,144 \ \text{bytes} = 256 \ \text{kB}$$

**Two Weaknesses (2 marks):**
1. **Statistical Detectability:** Histogram-based steganalysis (e.g., *chi-square attack*, *RS analysis*) can detect LSB embedding with high confidence because the pixel-value histogram exhibits a characteristic "pair-of-values" pattern after embedding.
2. **Fragility to Compression:** Any lossy compression (JPEG) destroys the LSBs entirely, causing total payload loss. Hence LSB stego is unsuitable for images that will be re-encoded before reaching the receiver.
3. *(Optional third: Sequential scanning order is predictable; secret-key ordering / randomization is needed to resist visual attacks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]*

**(a)** Explain the **three key requirements** of a digital watermarking system with examples. State the **trade-off triangle** and describe how the embedding strength $\alpha$ affects it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **classification of digital watermarking** based on (i) perceptibility, (ii) robustness, and (iii) the need for the original cover during detection. For each class, give **one real-world example**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**Three Key Requirements (4 marks):**
1. **Imperceptibility:** The watermark must be invisible/inaudible. Example: A photograph with a copyright watermark should look identical to a casual viewer.
2. **Robustness:** The watermark must survive common signal-processing operations. Example: A logo embedded in a music file must still be detectable after MP3 compression at 128 kbps.
3. **Capacity:** The watermark must carry sufficient information for the application. Example: A fingerprinting system embeds the buyer's ID (a few hundred bits), whereas a logo-only system needs only 1–100 bits.

**The Trade-off Triangle (3 marks):**
The three properties are mutually competing. Increasing any one of them typically *decreases* the other two:
- Higher $\alpha$ → stronger watermark, **better robustness**, **worse imperceptibility**.
- Lower $\alpha$ → near-invisible mark, **better imperceptibility**, **worse robustness**.
- Embedding more bits per sample → **higher capacity**, but if it requires $\alpha \uparrow$, it reduces imperceptibility/robustness.

Designers must balance the triangle based on application priority:
- Copyright protection → prioritize *robustness*.
- Covert communication (note: this is stego, not WM) → prioritize *imperceptibility*.
- Fingerprinting → prioritize *capacity* + *imperceptibility*.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**Classification — Three Axes (5 marks + 2 marks for examples):**

**(i) Perceptibility:**
- **Visible Watermark:** Semi-transparent logo overlaid on image. *Example:* TV channel logo in the corner of broadcast video.
- **Invisible Watermark:** Detected only algorithmically. *Example:* Copyright watermark in stock photographs sold online.

**(ii) Robustness:**
- **Robust Watermark:** Survives compression, cropping, noise. *Example:* Copyright notice in a song distributed via streaming.
- **Fragile Watermark:** Breaks on the slightest modification. *Example:* Integrity seal on a forensic image — any edit breaks it, revealing tampering.
- *(Semi-fragile also acceptable with an example.)*

**(iii) Need for Original Cover (during detection):**
- **Private / Non-blind Watermarking:** Requires the original cover for detection. *Example:* Copy-control systems in DVD/Blu-ray players that compare against a master.
- **Public / Blind Watermarking:** Detects the watermark without the original. *Example:* Broadcast monitoring systems that scan TV signals for embedded station IDs in real time.
- **Semi-blind:** Requires only the watermark key/sequence, not the full original. *Example:* Many modern image-watermarking standards (e.g., Digimarc).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students list watermarking types but **forget to give real-world examples**. Each classification entry *must* be paired with at least one example — otherwise 1–2 marks are lost per sub-part. Also avoid mixing up "fragile" with "invisible" — they are *orthogonal* properties (a watermark can be both visible and fragile, or invisible and robust).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

- **Steganography** = *covert communication*; protects the **existence** of a message. Carrier is incidental.
- **Digital Watermarking** = *ownership assertion / authentication*; protects the **identity** of the host. Carrier is essential.
- Both fall under the broader umbrella of **Information Hiding**, alongside **Covert Channels** and **Fingerprinting**.
- Core watermarking equation: $W = C + \alpha \cdot M_w$, with $\alpha$ controlling the trade-off.
- Core detection metric: **Correlation coefficient** $\rho$ compared to threshold $\tau$.
- The **Imperceptibility–Robustness–Capacity** trade-off is the single most-tested concept.
- LSB substitution is the *simplest* spatial-domain method, but is **statistically detectable** and **fragile under compression**.
- Transform-domain methods (DCT, DWT, DFT) provide **better robustness** for watermarking.
- Quality metrics to remember: **PSNR** (imperceptibility), **BER** (extraction fidelity), **Correlation** (detection confidence).
- **Steganalysis** is the *primary attack* on steganography; **Removal / Forgery / Collusion** are the *primary attacks* on watermarking.
- In KTU answers, always state **three explicit differences** (goal, robustness, detection consequence) — vague statements lose marks.
- Distinguish clearly: **fragile ≠ invisible**; **robust ≠ public**; these pairs are orthogonal.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook for the exam:**
> *"Stego hides the **letter**; Watermarking hides the **signature** on the letter's envelope."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Digital Watermarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Digital Watermarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Applied Cryptography syllabus**, **Digital Watermarking** is formally defined as the science and art of embedding an imperceptible or perceptible auxiliary signal (called a *watermark*) into a host digital media object (such as an image, audio, video, or text document) using a secret key, such that the watermark can later be detected or extracted to assert ownership, verify authenticity, trace provenance, or deter unauthorized redistribution.

The classification into **Types of Digital Watermarking** is therefore driven by:
- **Visibility** (perceptibility to the human visual/auditory system),
- **Robustness** (survivability against attacks),
- **Detection methodology** (whether the original host is needed),
- **Embedding domain** (spatial vs. transform), and
- **Application semantics** (ownership, fingerprinting, tamper detection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition (Memorize this verbatim):**
> "A digital watermark is a perceptually transparent or deliberately visible code, embedded directly into the cover content via a cryptographic key, that remains bound to the host even after format conversions, compression, or malicious signal-processing operations, enabling robust identity assertion and content authentication."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a Renaissance painter commissioned to create an original masterpiece. To prevent art forgers from duplicating your work and selling fake copies, you decide to **scratch your signature in a very specific, almost-invisible spot on the canvas — perhaps within the folds of a painted robe**. Anyone who later examines the painting can tilt it under raking light and see the mark, but a casual observer never notices it. Even if a forger photocopies the painting, the scratched mark is lost, exposing the fake. That invisible signature is the **watermark**.

In the digital world, instead of scratching a canvas, a cryptographic algorithm imperceptibly perturbs pixel values, audio samples, or transform coefficients. The perturbation encodes the owner's identity, a transaction ID, or a hash of the content itself. The original work can be compressed, cropped, filtered, or printed — yet the embedded signature survives (in **robust** watermarks) or breaks deterministically (in **fragile** watermarks), thereby revealing tampering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Watermarking is essentially a *covert-channel communication problem* embedded inside a *steganographic* framework, but with the critical addition of *robustness against active adversaries* — which distinguishes it from pure steganography.

### 1.3 Taxonomy at a Glance — Five Primary Classification Axes

| Axis | Primary Types | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Perceptibility | Visible / Invisible | High |
| Robustness | Robust / Fragile / Semi-Fragile | Very High |
| Detection Method | Public / Private / Asymmetric | High |
| Embedding Domain | Spatial / Frequency (DCT, DWT, DFT) | Medium |
| Content Medium | Image / Audio / Video / Text | Medium |

### 1.4 Key Distinguishing Metric Definitions (with constants in bold)

- **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** — measured in **decibels (dB)**; values **> 36 dB** generally imply acceptable imperceptibility.
- **MSE (Mean Squared Error)** — measured in squared intensity units.
- **NC (Normalized Correlation)** — dimensionless, range **[0, 1]**; **> 0.7** typically indicates successful watermark recovery.
- **BER (Bit Error Rate)** — dimensionless ratio in **[0, 1]**; **< 0.1** is the standard acceptance threshold.
- **SSIM (Structural Similarity Index)** — dimensionless, range **[-1, 1]**; target value **≈ 1** for transparent embedding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Type 1 — Based on Perceptibility (Visibility)

This is the **most fundamental** classification axis in KTU Module 1 and carries direct ESE (End Semester Examination) weightage.

#### 2.1.1 Visible Watermarking
- **Definition:** The watermark is intentionally rendered *conspicuous* to human observers. Common examples include TV channel logos overlaid on broadcast video, copyright stamps on stock photographs, and newspaper-style translucent identifiers.
- **Why use it:** Provides *immediate, on-sight* ownership assertion. Serves as a *psychological deterrent* against unauthorized use.
- **How it works:** A logo or text is alpha-blended onto the host image using:
$$I_w(i,j) = \alpha \cdot W(i,j) + (1 - \alpha) \cdot I(i,j)$$
  where $I_w$ is the watermarked image, $I$ is the original, $W$ is the watermark logo, and $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is the blending (strength) factor.
- **Limitations:** Easily removed via cropping or inpainting; degrades aesthetic value; cannot survive geometric distortion attacks.

#### 2.1.2 Invisible (Imperceptible / Transparent) Watermarking
- **Definition:** The watermark is embedded *below the human perception threshold*, leaving the host perceptually identical to the original.
- **Why use it:** Required for covert tracking, broadcast monitoring, fingerprinting, and legal evidence — where the watermark must remain undetected by pirates to actually catch them.
- **How it works:** Embedding modifies low-order bits or mid-band transform coefficients such that the perturbation is masked by spatial/temporal/frequency masking properties of human perception.
- **Sub-classification:** Further split into **imperceptible** (statistically detectable only by algorithms) and **perceptible-only-after-processing** (visually masked but recoverable via extraction).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Visible = Visible, Invisible = Stealth"* — examiners love this binary distinction; quote both definitions together in 2-mark questions.

---

### 2.2 Type 2 — Based on Robustness (Resistance to Attacks)

This is the **highest-weightage** classification in Module 1 per the 2024 Scheme outcomes.

#### 2.2.1 Robust Watermarking
- **Definition:** Designed to *survive* a wide range of legitimate and malicious signal-processing operations — JPEG compression, resizing, filtering, noise addition, cropping, rotation, format conversion.
- **Why:** Required for **ownership protection** and **copy control** applications.
- **How:** Embedding is performed in *perceptually significant* regions of the transform domain (e.g., mid-frequency DCT coefficients, low-frequency DWT sub-bands) where attackers cannot modify content without causing unacceptable visual degradation.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in IP protection for digital cinema (AACS, HDCP), satellite imagery, and forensic fingerprinting.

#### 2.2.2 Fragile Watermarking
- **Definition:** Designed to *break deterministically* upon *any* modification of the host content. The watermark is essentially a *content-sensitive hash* spread across the signal.
- **Why:** Required for **tamper detection** and **integrity verification**.
- **How:** The watermark encodes a cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) of each block of the image. If even one pixel changes, the block's watermark mismatches, and the tampering is localized to a precise spatial region.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used in surveillance footage integrity (chain-of-custody), medical imaging (legal admissibility), and legal evidence.

#### 2.2.3 Semi-Fragile Watermarking
- **Definition:** A *hybrid* class that survives **benign** operations (JPEG compression, mild noise) but breaks under **malicious** manipulations (cropping, recompression at very low bitrates, object removal).
- **Why:** Permits distinction between *acceptable* and *malicious* alterations — essential for content-authentication scenarios where lossy compression is unavoidable.
- **How:** Uses *content-adaptive* thresholds and embeds in coefficients whose modification signatures allow classification of the attack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Robust** = survives *intentional* attacks → ownership.
> - **Fragile** = survives *nothing* → authentication.
> - **Semi-fragile** = survives *content-preserving* operations → authenticity-with-compression-tolerance.

---

### 2.3 Type 3 — Based on Detection Methodology

#### 2.3.1 Private Watermarking (Secret-Key / Non-Blind Detection)
- **Definition:** Detection requires *both* the original host image $I$ *and* the secret embedding key $K$.
- **Why:** Highest detection reliability; minimal false positives.
- **How:** Detector correlates $W_{extracted}$ with $(I_w - I)$ using the secret key.
- **Limitation:** Original host is often unavailable at the detector (e.g., consumer DVDR players).

#### 2.3.2 Public Watermarking (Blind Detection)
- **Definition:** Detection requires *only* the watermarked content and the secret key — *no* original host needed.
- **Why:** Practical for distributed environments (web crawlers, broadcast monitors, YouTube content ID systems).
- **How:** Detection uses *informed coding* or *spread-spectrum* techniques robust to host-signal interference.

#### 2.3.3 Asymmetric Watermarking (Public-Key Watermarking)
- **Definition:** Embedding uses a *private* key; detection uses a *publicly known* key. Conceptually analogous to public-key cryptography.
- **Why:** Allows anyone to verify the watermark (read-only access) but only the owner can embed (write access).
- **How:** Typically constructed via one-way trapdoor functions; computationally expensive.
- **Reality Check:** Asymmetric schemes are *theoretically elegant* but *practically rare* due to detection reliability issues — examiners frequently ask about this trade-off.

---

### 2.4 Type 4 — Based on Embedding Domain

| Domain | Embedding Location | Robustness | Speed | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial (LSB) | Pixel LSBs | Low | Very High | Common |
| DCT | Mid-frequency coefficients | High | Medium | Very Common |
| DWT (Wavelet) | LL/LH/HL/HH sub-bands | High | Medium-High | Very Common |
| DFT (Fourier) | Magnitude/Phase | Rotation-scale invariant | Low | Less Common |

- **Spatial Domain (LSB):** Simple replacement of least-significant bits. Vulnerable to any compression.
- **Frequency Domain (DCT/DWT):** Embedding in transform coefficients aligns with compression algorithm internals (e.g., JPEG's $8\times 8$ DCT), giving natural robustness.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready Reference)

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LSB Embedding** | $I_w(i,j) = I(i,j) - I(i,j) \bmod 2^k + b_i$ | $k$ = bit-depth, $b_i$ = watermark bit | Spatial-domain Q&A |
| **Additive Spread-Spectrum** | $I_w = I + \alpha \cdot W$ | $\alpha$ = strength, $W$ = PN sequence | Frequency-domain proofs |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\mathrm{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i=1}^{M}\sum_{j=1}^{N}\bigl[I(i,j) - I_w(i,j)\bigr]^{2}$ | $M,N$ = image dimensions | Im perceptibility test |
| **Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR)** | $\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\mathrm{MAX}_I^{\,2}}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right)$ | $\mathrm{MAX}_I = 255$ for 8-bit | Im perceptibility test |
| **Normalized Correlation (NC)** | $\mathrm{NC} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} W_i W^{\prime}_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i W_i^{2}}\,\sqrt{\sum_i W^{\prime}_i^{2}}}$ | $W$ = original, $W^{\prime}$ = extracted | Robustness test |
| **Bit Error Rate (BER)** | $\mathrm{BER} = \dfrac{\text{Number of incorrect bits}}{\text{Total extracted bits}}$ | Ratio, dimensionless | Robustness test |
| **Structural Similarity (SSIM)** | $\mathrm{SSIM}(x,y) = \dfrac{(2\mu_x\mu_y + C_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + C_2)}{(\mu_x^{2}+\mu_y^{2}+C_1)(\sigma_x^{2}+\sigma_y^{2}+C_2)}$ | $C_1,C_2$ = stabilization constants | Modern perceptibility |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Discipline:** In the above table, all mathematical absolute values have been deliberately written as $\lvert x \rvert$ or excluded entirely to avoid corrupting the markdown pipe-delimiter syntax. Never write `|x|` inside a markdown table row — it breaks the renderer.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Broadcast Monitoring:** Robust + public watermarks embedded by advertising agencies; automated web crawlers scan YouTube for unauthorized reuse of TV commercials.
- **Copyright Litigation:** Robust watermarks in stock photography act as forensic evidence in court (invisible, yet extractable by the owner).
- **Medical Imaging Integrity:** Fragile + private watermarks in MRI/CT scans prove the image has not been doctored for legal/adversarial proceedings.
- **Digital Rights Management (DRM):** Robust + public watermarks in HDCP-protected video streams prevent unauthorized redistribution.
- **Covert Communication (Intelligence):** Invisible watermarks embedded in news broadcast audio can carry authentication watermarks for field operatives.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the PSNR Imperceptibility Metric (Exhaustive)

The PSNR metric is the **most frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU ESE Module 1. We derive it from first principles.

**Starting Point — Define the per-pixel error:**
For a grayscale image $I$ of dimensions $M \times N$, after watermark embedding we obtain the watermarked image $I_w$. The *per-pixel error* (residual) is:
$$e(i,j) = I(i,j) - I_w(i,j)$$

**Step 1 — Define total squared error energy:**
Sum the squared residuals over all pixels:
$$E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\bigl[I(i,j) - I_w(i,j)\bigr]^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize to obtain the Mean Squared Error:**
Divide by the total number of pixels $M \cdot N$ to obtain an *average* per-pixel squared error:
$$\mathrm{MSE} = \frac{1}{M \cdot N}\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\bigl[I(i,j) - I_w(i,j)\bigr]^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Define the maximum possible signal power:**
For a $k$-bit grayscale image, the peak intensity is:
$$\mathrm{MAX}_I = 2^{k} - 1$$
For the standard $8$-bit case, $\mathrm{MAX}_I = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$. The peak signal power (relative to MSE) is therefore:
$$P_{\text{signal}} = \mathrm{MAX}_I^{\,2}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Ratio:**
By definition, SNR (linear) is signal power divided by noise power:
$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{\mathrm{MSE}} = \frac{\mathrm{MAX}_I^{\,2}}{\mathrm{MSE}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels:**
The engineering standard uses the base-10 logarithmic decibel scale, multiplied by $10$ (since SNR is a *power* ratio):
$$\mathrm{PSNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{MAX}_I^{\,2}}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent alternate form:**
Using the identity $\log(a^{2}) = 2\log(a)$, the formula can also be written as:
$$\mathrm{PSNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{MAX}_I}{\sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}}\right)$$

> Both forms are **mathematically identical** and KTU accepts either. Most textbooks prefer the first form (cleaner inside LaTeX), but examiners frequently write the second form in question papers.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A $512 \times 512$, 8-bit grayscale image is watermarked. The total summed squared error between original and watermarked image equals $E_{\text{total}} = 1{,}048{,}576$. Compute MSE and PSNR.

**Step A — Compute MSE:**
$$M \cdot N = 512 \times 512 = 262{,}144$$

$$\mathrm{MSE} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{M \cdot N} = \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{262{,}144} = 4.0$$

**Step B — Compute PSNR:**
For an 8-bit image, $\mathrm{MAX}_I = 255$, hence $\mathrm{MAX}_I^{2} = 65{,}025$.

$$\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{65{,}025}{4.0}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(16{,}256.25)$$

$$\log_{10}(16{,}256.25) \approx 4.2111$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{PSNR} \approx 42.11\ \text{dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since PSNR = 42.11 dB **> 36 dB**, the watermark embedding is judged **imperceptibly transparent** by KTU rubric standards.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Normalized Correlation (NC) Robustness Metric

The NC coefficient measures the similarity between the original watermark $W$ and the extracted watermark $W^{\prime}$. It is derived from the cosine-similarity measure of two vectors.

**Step 1 — Define the inner-product form of correlation:**
The unnormalized correlation between two equal-length vectors $W$ and $W^{\prime}$ is:
$$C = \sum_{i=1}^{L} W_i \cdot W^{\prime}_i$$
where $L$ is the total number of watermark bits/samples.

**Step 2 — Normalize against vector norms:**
Divide by the product of the Euclidean norms $\Vert W \Vert_2$ and $\Vert W^{\prime} \Vert_2$:
$$\mathrm{NC} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{L} W_i W^{\prime}_i}{\Vert W \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert W^{\prime} \Vert_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{L} W_i W^{\prime}_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{L} W_i^{2}}\ \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{L} W^{\prime}_i^{2}}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify range:**
By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $\lvert \sum W_i W^{\prime}_i \rvert \le \Vert W \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert W^{\prime} \Vert_2$, hence $\mathrm{NC} \in [-1, +1]$. For non-negative pixel-based watermarks, the range reduces to $[0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Decision rule:**
$$\text{Watermark present} \iff \mathrm{NC} \ge T_{\mathrm{NC}}$$
Typical threshold $T_{\mathrm{NC}} = 0.7$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Bit Error Rate (BER)

For a binary watermark $W \in \{0,1\}^{L}$, define the indicator function:
$$\mathbb{1}_{W_i \neq W^{\prime}_i} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } W_i \neq W^{\prime}_i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The BER is simply the fraction of mismatched bits:
$$\mathrm{BER} = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{i=1}^{L}\mathbb{1}_{W_i \neq W^{\prime}_i}$$

Typical KTU acceptance threshold: $\mathrm{BER} \le 0.1$ (i.e., 90% of bits must survive).

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Watermark Type

| Application Scenario | Recommended Visibility | Recommended Robustness | Detection Method | Embedding Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TV channel logo on broadcast | Visible | Robust (redundant frames) | Private | Spatial / DCT |
| Copyright proof in court | Invisible | Robust | Private | DCT / DWT |
| Tamper detection in medical scan | Invisible | Fragile | Private | Spatial (LSB) |
| Authentication under compression | Invisible | Semi-fragile | Private/Public | DWT |
| Forensic transaction tracking | Invisible | Robust | Public | DCT |
| Broadcast commercial monitoring | Invisible | Robust | Public | DCT (spread spectrum) |
| News photo integrity | Invisible | Fragile | Private | DWT |
| DRM in streaming video | Invisible | Robust | Public | DCT |

> This matrix is a **direct, exam-ready answer** for KTU 14-mark scenario questions where the examiner asks: *"Select and justify the watermarking type for a given real-world application."*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Master Taxonomy — Types of Digital Watermarking

The following diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the hierarchical classification of all watermark types discussed in this module. This serves as a study-recall anchor and is safe to reproduce in answer scripts as a hand-drawn sketch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DIGITAL WATERMARKING] --> B[BY VISIBILITY]
    A --> C[BY ROBUSTNESS]
    A --> D[BY DETECTION METHOD]
    A --> E[BY EMBEDDING DOMAIN]

    B --> B1[VISIBLE WATERMARK]
    B --> B2[INVISIBLE WATERMARK]

    C --> C1[ROBUST WATERMARK]
    C --> C2[FRAGILE WATERMARK]
    C --> C3[SEMI FRAGILE WATERMARK]

    D --> D1[PRIVATE WATERMARK]
    D --> D2[PUBLIC WATERMARK]
    D --> D3[ASYMMETRIC WATERMARK]

    E --> E1[SPATIAL DOMAIN]
    E --> E2[FREQUENCY DOMAIN]
    E2 --> E2A[DCT DOMAIN]
    E2 --> E2B[DWT DOMAIN]
    E2 --> E2C[DFT DOMAIN]

    B1 --> F[APPLICATIONS]
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2A --> F
    E2B --> F
    E2C --> F

    F --> F1[BROADCAST MONITORING]
    F --> F2[COPYRIGHT PROTECTION]
    F --> F3[TAMPER DETECTION]
    F --> F4[CONTENT AUTHENTICATION]
    F --> F5[FINGERPRINTING]
    F --> F6[DRM AND COPY CONTROL]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Sketch Tip:** In answer scripts, you may draw this hierarchy as a tree diagram with **five branches**, each labeled with the classification axis. Examiners award partial marks even for partially correct hierarchies.

---

### 4.2 Generic Watermark Embedding and Extraction Pipeline

The diagram below models the **functional architecture** of a generic digital watermarking system, showing the data flow between the cover content, the watermark, the secret key, and the output channels.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUTS_BLOCK
        COVER[COVER IMAGE I]
        WMARK[WATERMARK MESSAGE W]
        KEY[SECRET KEY K]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING_BLOCK
        EMB[EMBEDDING ALGORITHM E]
        CHAN[TRANSMISSION CHANNEL AND ATTACKS]
        DET[DETECTION ALGORITHM D]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS_BLOCK
        OUT_IW[WATERMARKED CONTENT I W]
        OUT_WP[EXTRACTED WATERMARK W PRIME]
        OUT_DEC[DETECTION DECISION BIT]
    end

    COVER --> EMB
    WMARK --> EMB
    KEY --> EMB
    EMB --> OUT_IW
    OUT_IW --> CHAN
    CHAN --> DET
    KEY --> DET
    DET --> OUT_WP
    DET --> OUT_DEC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Pipeline (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Inputs:** Three independent inputs — cover image $I$, watermark $W$, secret key $K$.
> 2. **Embedding:** Function $E(I, W, K) \rightarrow I_w$.
> 3. **Channel:** Real-world lossy channel introduces noise/attacks (compression, cropping, etc.).
> 4. **Detection:** Function $D(I_w, K, I_{\text{optional}}) \rightarrow \{0,1\}$ — returns presence/absence decision and the recovered watermark.
> 5. **Public vs. Private:** The arrow from `COVER` to `DET` is *optional* — present in private schemes, absent in public/blind schemes.

---

### 4.3 Robustness-vs-Imperceptibility Trade-off (Block Decision Flow)

This decision flow helps students select the correct watermark configuration given application constraints:

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[START SELECT WATERMARK TYPE] --> Q1{IS COVER AVAILABLE AT DETECTOR?}
    Q1 -- YES --> PRIV[CHOOSE PRIVATE WATERMARK]
    Q1 -- NO --> PUB[CHOOSE PUBLIC WATERMARK]

    PRIV --> Q2{NEED TAMPER DETECTION?}
    PUB --> Q2

    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{IMAGE MAY UNDERGO COMPRESSION?}
    Q2 -- NO --> ROB[CHOOSE ROBUST WATERMARK]

    Q3 -- YES --> SEMI[CHOOSE SEMI FRAGILE WATERMARK]
    Q3 -- NO --> FRAG[CHOOSE FRAGILE WATERMARK]

    ROB --> DOM{DEGREE OF ATTACKS ANTICIPATED?}
    DOM -- HIGH --> FREQ[CHOOSE FREQUENCY DOMAIN EMBEDDING]
    DOM -- LOW --> SPAT[CHOOSE SPATIAL DOMAIN EMBEDDING]
```
