## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> Each Part-A question below is constructed to mirror the cognitive levels **L1 (Remember)** and **L2 (Understand)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy as mandated by the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

*Define digital watermarking. Differentiate between visible and invisible watermarking with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Digital Watermarking** is the technique of embedding auxiliary information (the watermark) into a host digital media object using a cryptographic key, such that the embedded data can later be extracted or detected for purposes of ownership proof, authentication, or content tracking. **(1 mark)**

**Visible Watermarking:** The watermark is *conspicuously perceptible* in the cover content. Example: TV channel logos (e.g., NDTV logo in the corner of a broadcast frame). **(1 mark)**

**Invisible Watermarking:** The watermark is embedded *below the human perception threshold*; the cover content appears identical to the original. Example: A photographer embedding a hidden transaction ID inside a stock photograph to detect unauthorized use. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

*List and briefly define the three categories of watermarking based on robustness. State one application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Robust Watermarking** — Designed to survive both benign and malicious signal-processing operations such as compression, filtering, and cropping. *Application:* Copyright protection in digital cinema. **(1 mark)**

2. **Fragile Watermarking** — Designed to break under *any* modification of the cover; used for tamper detection. *Application:* Integrity verification of medical MRI images. **(1 mark)**

3. **Semi-Fragile Watermarking** — Survives *benign* operations (e.g., JPEG compression) but breaks under *malicious* manipulations. *Application:* News photo authentication where lossy compression is acceptable but forgery is not. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer / ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Each Part-B question maps to KTU 2024 cognitive levels **L3 (Apply)**, **L4 (Analyze)**, and **L5 (Evaluate)**. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, totaling 14 marks. **TWO alternative choices** are provided — answer either.

---

#### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Robustness & Imperceptibility Analysis
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of digital watermarking techniques based on **robustness**, with suitable diagrams and examples. Discuss the trade-offs between robust, fragile, and semi-fragile watermarking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $256 \times 256$ grayscale image is watermarked. The mean squared error (MSE) between the original and watermarked image is computed as $4.5$. Compute the **PSNR** in decibels. Comment on whether the embedding is imperceptible, citing the relevant KTU threshold. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The classification of watermarking techniques based on **robustness** — the ability of the watermark to survive signal-processing operations — yields three principal categories:

**1. Robust Watermarking:**
Robust watermarks are engineered to survive a *deliberate and broad* class of attacks, including lossy compression (JPEG, MPEG), geometric distortions (rotation, scaling, translation), filtering (low-pass, median), noise injection (Gaussian, salt-and-pepper), and format conversions. They are embedded in perceptually significant regions of the transform domain — for instance, the **mid-frequency DCT coefficients** — because these regions coincide with the energy-preserving coefficients of natural images and cannot be modified without causing unacceptable visual degradation. **(2 marks)**

*Example:* A stock-photography agency embeds a robust watermark in DCT coefficients of every image sold. Even if the buyer crops, recompresses, or re-sizes the image, the agency can later extract the watermark to prove unauthorized redistribution. **(1 mark)**

**2. Fragile Watermarking:**
Fragile watermarks operate on the *opposite* principle — they are designed to *fail catastrophically* upon any modification. The watermark essentially encodes a cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-256) of each block of the cover image; if even a single pixel in a block is altered, the extracted hash mismatches the recomputed hash, and the tampering is localized to that block. **(1.5 marks)**

*Example:* A hospital embeds a fragile watermark in each MRI scan. If a clinician manipulates the scan to obscure a tumor, the watermark breaks and the tampering is spatially pinpointed. **(0.5 marks)**

**3. Semi-Fragile Watermarking:**
Semi-fragile watermarks occupy the middle ground — they *survive* content-preserving operations (JPEG compression at moderate bitrates, mild noise) but *break* under content-altering manipulations (heavy recompression, object removal, splicing). They typically employ a content-adaptive threshold and embed in wavelet sub-bands where benign and malicious distortions have distinguishable statistical signatures. **(1.5 marks)**

*Example:* A news agency embeds a semi-fragile watermark in photos published online. Routine web compression does not destroy the watermark, but if a malicious party alters the image (e.g., removes a person from a protest photo), the watermark breaks and authenticity is flagged. **(0.5 marks)**

**Trade-off Summary:** Robustness and imperceptibility are inherently in *tension* — increasing robustness typically requires stronger perturbation, which reduces imperceptibility. Fragile watermarks optimize imperceptibility but sacrifice robustness; robust watermarks do the reverse; semi-fragile schemes attempt to balance both via selective domain embedding. **(Included implicitly in the analysis above.)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** Image dimensions $M \times N = 256 \times 256$ (8-bit grayscale); $\mathrm{MSE} = 4.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute peak intensity:**
For an 8-bit image:
$$\mathrm{MAX}_I = 2^{8} - 1 = 255$$

**Step 2 — Apply the PSNR formula:**
$$\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\mathrm{MAX}_I^{\,2}}{\mathrm{MSE}}\right)$$

$$\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{255^{2}}{4.5}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{65{,}025}{4.5}\right)$$

$$\mathrm{PSNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(14{,}450)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**
$$\log_{10}(14{,}450) \approx 4.1599$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**
$$\boxed{\mathrm{PSNR} \approx 41.60\ \text{dB}}$$

**Step 5 — Imperceptibility verdict:**
According to the **KTU benchmark**, a PSNR value of $\ge 36$ dB is considered *imperceptibly transparent*. Since $41.60\ \text{dB} \gg 36\ \text{dB}$, the watermark embedding is judged **imperceptibly transparent** and passes the standard quality criterion. **(1 mark — for the verdict)** 

> **Marks Distribution Note:** Substituting into the formula = 2 marks; correct numerical evaluation = 3 marks; final numerical result = 1 mark; verdict + KTU threshold reference = 1 mark.

---

#### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Classification & Detection Methods
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify digital watermarking techniques based on **(i) visibility** and **(ii) detection methodology**. For each class, provide the principle of operation, one application, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **spatial-domain LSB watermarking** technique with the relevant embedding and extraction equations. Discuss two major vulnerabilities of this technique. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Visibility-based Classification:**

- **Visible Watermarking:** The watermark is rendered explicitly on the cover content. *Principle:* Alpha-blending of a logo/text onto the host. *Application:* TV channel logos overlaid on broadcast video. *Limitation:* Easily removed by cropping or inpainting; degrades host aesthetics. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Invisible Watermarking:** The watermark is embedded below the Just-Noticeable-Difference (JND) threshold. *Principle:* Subtle perturbation of pixel values or transform coefficients. *Application:* Covert forensic tracking of stock photographs. *Limitation:* Detection requires sophisticated extraction algorithms; can be destroyed by aggressive compression. **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Detection-methodology-based Classification:**

- **Private Watermarking:** Detector requires both the original host $I$ and the secret key $K$. *Principle:* Correlate $(I_w - I)$ with the watermark pattern. *Application:* Courtroom evidence extraction. *Limitation:* Original cover often unavailable at the detector. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Public Watermarking:** Detector requires only the watermarked content and the key. *Principle:* Blind detection via informed coding or spread-spectrum. *Application:* Web crawlers scanning YouTube for unauthorized commercial reuse. *Limitation:* Lower detection reliability and higher false-positive rates. **(1.5 marks)**

- **Asymmetric Watermarking:** Embedding uses a private key; detection uses a public key. *Principle:* Trapdoor functions analogous to public-key cryptography. *Application:* Theoretical broadcast-monitoring frameworks. *Limitation:* Computationally expensive; not widely deployed. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Principle of LSB Watermarking:**
The least-significant bit (or the lower $k$ bits) of each pixel carries visually negligible information. Replacing these bits with watermark data yields a watermarked image that is perceptually indistinguishable from the original.

**Embedding Equation:**
For an 8-bit pixel value $I(i,j)$ and a single watermark bit $b \in \{0,1\}$:
$$I_w(i,j) = \left\lfloor \frac{I(i,j)}{2^{k}} \right\rfloor \cdot 2^{k} + b$$

Equivalently, the operation clears the lowest $k$ bits of $I(i,j)$ and replaces them with the watermark bit $b$:
$$I_w(i,j) = \bigl[I(i,j) \;\&\; (2^{8} - 1 - (2^{k} - 1))\bigr] + b$$
where $\&$ denotes the bitwise AND operation.

**Extraction Equation:**
The embedded bit is recovered by reading the LSB of the watermarked pixel:
$$b' = I_w(i,j) \bmod 2^{k}$$

**Step-by-step procedure:**
1. Convert the original image to its bit planes.
2. Flatten the watermark message into a binary stream of length $L \le M \cdot N$.
3. Sequentially replace the LSB of each pixel with one watermark bit.
4. At the detector, read the LSBs of the watermarked image sequentially to reconstruct the message. **(3 marks — equations; 2 marks — procedure)**

**Vulnerability 1: Lossy Compression Destroys LSBs (2 marks):**
JPEG compression operates on $8 \times 8$ DCT blocks and quantizes the AC coefficients, effectively erasing low-order bit information. After JPEG compression at typical quality factor $Q = 75$, the LSBs of the pixels are statistically randomized, and the watermark cannot be recovered. This makes LSB watermarking entirely unsuitable for robust applications.

**Vulnerability 2: Trivial Removal by Bit-Plane Manipulation (2 marks):**
An attacker can simply zero out the lowest 1–2 bit planes of the watermarked image (a lossless operation requiring no key knowledge). This obliterates the watermark without any visible degradation to the host image. Furthermore, the *cover image itself can be statistically estimated* from the unwatermarked higher-order bit planes, allowing the attacker to forge a plausible "original."

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "Robustness" with "Visibility":** Students frequently write "robust watermarks are visible." This is wrong. Robustness and visibility are **independent** classification axes — a watermark can be invisible *and* robust simultaneously (the common case).
> 2. **Forgetting the PSNR threshold:** In numerical problems, students compute PSNR correctly but fail to comment on *whether it exceeds the 36 dB acceptance threshold*. Examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for the verdict.
> 3. **Skipping derivation steps:** Writing only the final PSNR formula without showing $\mathrm{MAX}_I = 255$ derivation or $\log_{10}$ evaluation loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing watermarking with steganography:** Watermarking emphasizes *robustness against active adversaries* and *binding to the host content*, whereas steganography emphasizes *undetectable communication*. Examiners will deduct marks if the distinction is not drawn.
> 5. **Mixing up private/public/asymmetric:** A common error is calling "asymmetric watermarking" the same as "public-key watermarking" without explaining the *embedding-vs-detection key separation*. Always state: *embedding uses private key, detection uses public key*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Digital watermarking** embeds an auxiliary signal into cover media for ownership, authentication, or tracking.
- ✅ **Five classification axes:** Visibility, Robustness, Detection Method, Embedding Domain, Content Medium.
- ✅ **Visible vs. Invisible:** Visible = conspicuous; Invisible = perceptually transparent.
- ✅ **Robust vs. Fragile vs. Semi-Fragile:** Robust survives attacks (ownership); Fragile breaks on any change (tamper detection); Semi-fragile survives benign operations only.
- ✅ **Private vs. Public vs. Asymmetric:** Private needs original cover; Public is blind; Asymmetric uses separate embed/detect keys.
- ✅ **Spatial domain** = LSB embedding, fast but fragile.
- ✅ **Frequency domain** = DCT/DWT/DFT, robust, common in JPEG-compression-resilient applications.
- ✅ **PSNR formula:** $10 \cdot \log_{10}(\mathrm{MAX}_I^{2}/\mathrm{MSE})$, threshold **> 36 dB** for imperceptibility.
- ✅ **NC formula:** Cosine-similarity between $W$ and $W^{\prime}$, threshold **> 0.7** for detection.
- ✅ **BER formula:** Ratio of incorrect extracted bits to total bits, threshold **< 0.1**.
- ✅ **Watermarking ≠ Steganography:** Watermarking = robust + host-bound; Steganography = covert + untraceable.
- ✅ **Examiners reward diagrams:** Always include a taxonomy tree or block pipeline in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Mention the KTU threshold** in every numerical answer — it is a guaranteed 1-mark bonus.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Goals of Digital Watermarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Goals of Digital Watermarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Digital Watermarking** is the science and art of embedding imperceptible, secret, and robust information (called a *watermark*) into a digital cover object (such as an image, audio, video, or text document) such that the watermark can be detected or extracted later to make an assertion about the **authenticity, ownership, integrity, or usage** of the host signal.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, watermarking is positioned as a **complementary security primitive to cryptography**. While cryptography *protects the content during transit*, watermarking *protects the content after delivery* — it remains embedded inside the asset itself and travels with the file wherever it goes.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **currency note with a hidden security thread woven into the paper**. To a casual observer, the note looks perfectly normal — they cannot see or feel the thread. But the Reserve Bank knows exactly where the thread is, and a special machine can extract the embedded code to verify:
- Whether the note is **genuine** (authentication),
- Which **batch / printing press** produced it (fingerprinting),
- Whether the note has been **tampered with** (integrity).

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** A digital watermark behaves exactly like that security thread — it is **hidden in plain sight** inside the digital file, survives ordinary file operations, and carries machine-readable evidence about the asset.

### Core Vocabulary (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cover Object** | The original digital file (host signal) into which the watermark is embedded. |
| **Embedder** | The algorithmic process that hides the watermark into the cover. |
| **Detector / Extractor** | The algorithm that recovers or tests for the presence of the watermark. |
| **Robustness** | The ability of the watermark to survive signal processing, compression, or attacks. |
| **Imperceptibility** | The watermark must be invisible/inaudible to human sensory systems. |
| **Capacity (Payload)** | The amount of information (in **bits**) that can be embedded reliably. |
| **False Positive** | Detector claims a watermark exists when the object is unmarked. |
| **False Negative** | Detector fails to find a watermark that is actually present. |

### The Three-Pillar Trade-off

A fundamental theorem of watermarking states that for any scheme, the following three properties are in **mutual tension** — improving one typically degrades the others:

- **Imperceptibility** (transparency to humans)
- **Robustness** (survival against attacks)
- **Capacity** (bits embedded per unit cover)

This is the famous *Imperceptibility–Robustness–Capacity (IRC) triangle*.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Know):**  
> - **PSNR ≥ 38 dB** is considered perceptually transparent for images.  
> - **BER ≤ 10⁻⁴** after common signal processing is considered acceptable robustness.  
> - **False Positive Probability ≤ 10⁻⁶** is the typical security benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Canonical Goals of Digital Watermarking

The KTU 2024 syllabus frames the *purpose* (the "why") of building a watermarking system through a set of well-defined goals. Each goal imposes a unique set of design constraints.

#### Goal 1: Copyright Protection (Ownership Assertion)

- **Why:** Settle disputes over *who owns* a piece of digital media.
- **How:** The rightful owner embeds a secret identifying mark (e.g., a hashed owner ID) before distributing the asset. If someone else claims ownership, the owner extracts the watermark and proves authorship.
- **Design Implication:** Must be **highly robust** against geometric distortions, compression, and malicious attacks.

#### Goal 2: Authentication (Content Integrity Verification)

- **Why:** Verify that the asset has *not been tampered with* after watermarking.
- **How:** A fragile or semi-fragile watermark is embedded; any modification to the asset breaks the watermark in a detectable way.
- **Design Implication:** Must be **fragile** (sensitive to changes) and **localized** so the tampered region can be pinpointed.

#### Goal 3: Fingerprinting / Traitor Tracing

- **Why:** Trace the **leaker** of a distributed copy.
- **How:** Each distributed copy receives a **unique watermark** (a fingerprint) identifying the buyer. If the file appears on a piracy site, the embedded fingerprint reveals the guilty recipient.
- **Design Implication:** Requires **collusion resistance** — multiple users must not be able to combine their copies to erase the unique trace.

#### Goal 4: Broadcast Monitoring

- **Why:** Detect when and where a copyrighted asset (e.g., a TV commercial, news clip, song) is broadcast.
- **How:** The broadcaster's station equipment embeds a real-time watermark; an automated monitoring station detects it and logs usage.
- **Design Implication:** Must be **real-time detectable** with low computational overhead.

#### Goal 5: Copy / Usage Control (DRM)

- **Why:** Enforce business rules like "copy once," "no copy," or "play on device X only."
- **How:** The watermark carries a **control bit** that compliant hardware reads before allowing recording/playback.
- **Design Implication:** Must be **readable by consumer-grade hardware** (cheap detectors).

#### Goal 6: Content Labeling / Annotation (Metadata Carriage)

- **Why:** Attach hidden descriptive metadata (caption, date, GPS, patient ID) directly inside the file rather than in a sidecar header.
- **How:** Watermark carries auxiliary information that survives even when headers are stripped.
- **Design Implication:** **High capacity** is more important than extreme robustness.

#### Goal 7: Covert Communication (Steganographic Goal)

- **Why:** Secretly transmit a message so that *even the existence of communication* is hidden.
- **How:** Embed the message in a cover so that an uninformed observer cannot tell communication took place.
- **Design Implication:** Maximum **imperceptibility** and **statistical undetectability** are paramount; robustness is secondary.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Goal-to-Requirement Mapping:** In a KTU exam answer, you will often be asked to "design a system for goal X" — your answer must explicitly list which of the three IRC properties you prioritize.

### 2.2 Mathematical Model of Watermarking

#### Embedding Equation (Additive Spread-Spectrum Form)

The most widely used model in KTU-level textbooks expresses watermarking as **additive embedding in a chosen transform domain**:

$$X_w = X + \alpha \cdot W$$

where:
- $X$ = original cover signal (vector form)
- $X_w$ = watermarked signal
- $W$ = watermark pattern (often $W \in \{-1, +1\}$ or pseudo-noise)
- $\alpha$ = **embedding strength / scaling factor** — the master knob controlling the imperceptibility–robustness trade-off.

#### Detection by Linear Correlation

The detector computes the **normalized correlation** between the test signal $\hat{X}$ and the candidate watermark $W$:

$$\rho(\hat{X}, W) = \frac{\langle \hat{X}, W \rangle}{\sqrt{\langle \hat{X}, \hat{X} \rangle \cdot \langle W, W \rangle}} = \frac{\sum_{i} \hat{X}_i W_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} \hat{X}_i^2 \; \sum_{i} W_i^2}}$$

Decision rule: declare watermark present if $\rho \geq T$, where $T$ is a threshold chosen to bound the false-positive probability.

#### Capacity (Shannon Channel Analogy)

Viewing the cover as a noisy communication channel, the **reliable payload** (in bits) is bounded by:

$$C \leq \frac{N}{2} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{\alpha^2 \sigma_W^2}{\sigma_N^2}\right)$$

where $N$ is the number of samples, $\sigma_W^2$ is watermark power, and $\sigma_N^2$ is attack-noise power.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $X_w = X + \alpha W$ | Additive embedding rule | — |
| $\alpha$ | Embedding strength | $0.1 \le \alpha \le 1.0$ |
| $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{MAX}^2}{\text{MSE}}\right)$ | Perceptual transparency (dB) | $\ge 38$ dB acceptable |
| $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{MN}\sum_{i,j}(X_{ij} - X^{w}_{ij})^2$ | Mean squared error (images) | pixels |
| $\rho = \dfrac{\sum W_i W'_i}{\sqrt{\sum W_i^2 \sum W'_i^2}}$ | Normalized correlation (detector) | $-1 \le \rho \le 1$ |
| $P_{FP} \le 10^{-6}$ | False-positive probability | benchmark |
| $\text{BER} = \dfrac{\text{erroneous bits}}{\text{total bits}}$ | Bit error rate (robustness test) | $\le 10^{-4}$ |
| $C \le \dfrac{N}{2}\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Capacity (Shannon bound) | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Pipe-character safety rule:** All vertical bars in this table have been rendered as $\mid$ to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Watermarking Goal Used |
|---|---|
| Hollywood / Streaming (Netflix, HBO) | Copy Control + Fingerprinting |
| News Agencies (AP, Reuters) | Authentication (forensic) |
| Pharmaceutical / Banking | Authentication of scanned documents |
| Satellite TV (DirecTV, Dish TV) | Broadcast Monitoring |
| Medical Imaging (DICOM) | Content Labeling (patient metadata) |
| Military / Intelligence | Covert Communication |
| Music Industry (RIAA) | Copyright Protection |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Embedding Equation to PSNR

**Step 1 — Define the cover and watermarked signal.**
Let $X(i,j)$ be the original image and $X_w(i,j)$ the watermarked image, both of size $M \times N$.

**Step 2 — Express the Mean Squared Error.**
The MSE is the average squared pixel-wise difference:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{M \cdot N} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \left[ X(i,j) - X_w(i,j) \right]^2$$

> Logic: This is simply the second moment of the perturbation $X - X_w$. It is the energy of the distortion.

**Step 3 — Express the perturbation using the embedding rule.**
From $X_w = X + \alpha W$ we have $X - X_w = -\alpha W$. Therefore:

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{M \cdot N} \sum_{i,j} \alpha^2 W(i,j)^2 = \alpha^2 \cdot \overline{W^2}$$

> Logic: The pixel difference is proportional to the watermark amplitude. Squaring removes the sign.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the PSNR formula.**
For 8-bit grayscale, $\text{MAX} = 2^8 - 1 = 255$. The PSNR is:

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{255^2}{\alpha^2 \cdot \overline{W^2}} \right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the embedding strength given a target PSNR.**
Rearranging for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{255}{10^{\,\text{PSNR}/20} \cdot \sqrt{\overline{W^2}}}$$

> **Engineering use:** This lets a designer pick $\alpha$ such that the resulting distortion is perceptually transparent (e.g., PSNR = 42 dB).

### 3.2 Derivation: Detector False-Positive Bound

**Step 1 — Model the test signal under no-watermark hypothesis ($H_0$).**
Under $H_0$, the correlation $\rho$ between a random test signal $\hat{X}$ and the secret watermark $W$ is approximately Gaussian by the Central Limit Theorem:

$$\rho \;\Big\vert_{H_0} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(0, \frac{1}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Set a detection threshold $T$.**
We choose $T$ such that $P(\rho \geq T \mid H_0) = P_{FP}$, the desired false-positive rate. For a Gaussian tail:

$$P_{FP} = \frac{1}{2}\operatorname{erfc}\!\left(\frac{T \sqrt{N}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required threshold.**
Using the inverse complementary error function:

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{N}}\,\operatorname{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(2 P_{FP}\right)$$

> **Interpretation:** Longer watermarks (larger $N$) allow tighter thresholds and thus *more secure* detection at the same false-positive rate.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Goal

| Application Scenario | Primary Goal | IRC Priority | Fragile / Robust | Public / Private Detector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Proving ownership of an image on the web | Copyright Protection | Robustness ≫ Capacity | Robust | Private |
| Verifying a medical scan was not edited | Authentication | Imperceptibility + Fragility | Fragile | Public |
| Tracing a leaked screener copy of a film | Fingerprinting | Robustness + Collusion-Resistance | Robust | Private |
| Logging radio play of a song | Broadcast Monitoring | Real-time, moderate robustness | Robust | Public |
| Preventing recording of a 4K Blu-ray | Copy Control | Robustness (must survive A/D) | Robust | Public |
| Hiding GPS data inside a drone photo | Content Labeling | Capacity + Imperceptibility | Semi-fragile | Private |
| Sending a covert message to a spy | Covert Communication | Imperceptibility (statistical) | Not required | Private |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Watermarking System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cover Object X] --> C[Watermark Embedder]
    W[Secret Key K] --> C
    M[Watermark Message M] --> C
    C --> D[Watermarked Object Xw]
    D --> E[Distribution Channel / Attacks]
    E --> F[Possibly Distorted Xw hat]
    F --> G[Detector / Extractor]
    W2[Same Secret Key K] --> G
    G --> H{Decision}
    H -->|rho greater than T| I[Watermark Detected M hat]
    H -->|rho less than T| J[No Watermark]
```

### 4.2 The IRC Trade-off Triangle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Im[Imperceptibility]
    Ro[Robustness]
    Ca[Capacity]

    Im --- Ro
    Ro --- Ca
    Ca --- Im

    Im -.->|degrades when alpha rises| Ro
    Ro -.->|more redundancy needed| Ca
    Ca -.->|more bits to hide| Im
```

### 4.3 Taxonomy of Watermarking Goals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G[Goals of Digital Watermarking]
    G --> G1[Copyright Protection]
    G --> G2[Authentication]
    G --> G3[Fingerprinting]
    G --> G4[Broadcast Monitoring]
    G --> G5[Copy and Usage Control]
    G --> G6[Content Labeling]
    G --> G7[Covert Communication]

    G1 --> P1[Robust + Private Detector]
    G2 --> P2[Fragile + Public Detector]
    G3 --> P3[Robust + Collusion Resistant]
    G4 --> P4[Real Time + Public Detector]
    G5 --> P5[Robust + Hardware Readable]
    G6 --> P6[High Capacity + Semi Fragile]
    G7 --> P7[Imperceptible + Statistically Undetectable]
```

### 4.4 Embedding-Detection Pipeline (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Cover Acquisition] --> S2[Step 2: Optional Transform DCT DWT]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Generate Watermark using PRNG seeded by Key K]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Scale by alpha for Strength Control]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Add to Coefficients]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Inverse Transform]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Output Watermarked File]

    S7 --> T1[Step A: Receive Test File]
    T1 --> T2[Step B: Apply Same Transform]
    T2 --> T3[Step C: Regenerate Watermark using Key K]
    T3 --> T4[Step D: Compute Correlation rho]
    T4 --> T5[Step E: Compare with Threshold T]
    T5 --> T6[Step F: Output Decision Bit and Extracted Message]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three goals of digital watermarking and state one real-world application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Copyright Protection** — Real-world use: Proving ownership of a stock photograph that has been reposted without license on a blog.
2. **Authentication** — Real-world use: Verifying the integrity of a forensic crime-scene photograph submitted as court evidence.
3. **Fingerprinting** — Real-world use: Identifying the specific cinema that recorded a movie using a handheld camera by tracing the unique watermark embedded in the screener copy distributed to that theatre.

> Award **1 mark per correctly stated goal + 1 mark for the correctly matched application**. Full 3 marks only if all three pairs are correct.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between a **fragile** watermark and a **robust** watermark. In which goal of watermarking is each typically used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **fragile watermark** is one that breaks (becomes undetectable) under even minor modification of the cover, and is therefore used for the **Authentication** goal — it acts as a tamper sensor.
- A **robust watermark** is one designed to survive signal-processing operations such as compression, cropping, or noise addition, and is used for **Copyright Protection, Fingerprinting, and Broadcast Monitoring**.
- **Key contrast:** A fragile watermark prioritizes sensitivity to change; a robust watermark prioritizes survival through change.

> Award **1 mark each** for correct definition of fragile and robust, **1 mark** for the goal association.

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Copyright Protection Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with suitable diagrams how digital watermarking achieves **copyright protection** for digital images. Describe the **embedder** and **detector** modules and the role of the secret key.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A 512 × 512 grayscale image ($M = N = 512$, 8 bits/pixel) is watermarked using an additive rule with embedding strength $\alpha = 0.4$ and a watermark of unit variance ($\overline{W^2} = 1$). Compute the **PSNR** of the watermarked image. Comment on whether the watermark is perceptually transparent, citing the standard benchmark.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

1. **Diagram (2 marks):** A clear block diagram showing Cover $X$, Key $K$, Embedder, Output $X_w$, Channel, Detector, Decision. (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)
2. **Embedder Module (2 marks):** The owner computes a pseudo-random watermark $W$ using a secret key $K$ as the seed of a PRNG, scales it by $\alpha$, and adds it to the cover (or to its transform coefficients): $X_w = X + \alpha W$.
3. **Detector Module (2 marks):** Upon dispute, the owner supplies $K$ to regenerate $W$. The detector computes the normalized correlation $\rho(\hat{X}, W)$ between the suspect file $\hat{X}$ and $W$, and compares it with threshold $T$. If $\rho \ge T$, ownership is asserted.
4. **Role of the Key (1 mark):** The key ensures that only the rightful owner can produce or detect the watermark, preventing forgery of ownership claims.

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

Given: $M = N = 512$, $\text{MAX} = 255$, $\alpha = 0.4$, $\overline{W^2} = 1$.

**Step 1 — Compute MSE.**

$$\text{MSE} = \alpha^2 \cdot \overline{W^2} = (0.4)^2 \times 1 = 0.16$$

**Step 2 — Compute PSNR.**

$$\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{255^2}{0.16} \right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{65025}{0.16} \right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left( 406406.25 \right)$$

$$= 10 \times 5.6091 \approx 56.09 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Verdict.**
Since $\text{PSNR} \approx 56.09 \text{ dB} \ge 38 \text{ dB}$ (the transparency benchmark), the watermark is **highly imperceptible / perceptually transparent**.

> Award **2 marks** for correct MSE, **3 marks** for correct PSNR with logs, **2 marks** for the correct benchmark comparison and conclusion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Authentication + Fingerprinting Focus**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare **authentication watermarking** and **fingerprinting watermarking** in terms of (i) purpose, (ii) robustness requirement, (iii) detector type, and (iv) collusion resistance.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A digital watermark of length $N = 1024$ bits is embedded into an image. If the desired false-positive probability is $P_{FP} = 10^{-6}$, calculate the **detection threshold** $T$ using the Gaussian approximation. Use the identity $\operatorname{erfc}^{-1}(2 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 3.39$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Authentication | Fingerprinting |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Purpose** | Verify the asset has not been modified after embedding. | Trace which recipient leaked a particular distributed copy. |
| (ii) **Robustness Requirement** | **Fragile / Semi-fragile** — must break on any edit. | **Robust** — must survive compression, recoding, and collusion. |
| (iii) **Detector Type** | **Public detector** (anyone with the key can verify integrity). | **Private detector** (only the distributor has the fingerprint database). |
| (iv) **Collusion Resistance** | **Not required** — a single shared watermark suffices. | **Critically required** — the scheme must resist averaging or combining attacks by a coalition of users. |

> Award **1.5 marks** per correctly addressed row, with **0.5 bonus mark** for a clear concluding sentence.

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the threshold formula.**

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{N}}\,\operatorname{erfc}^{-1}\!\left(2 P_{FP}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$T = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1024}} \times 3.39$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1024}} = \frac{1.4142}{32} = 0.04419$$

$$T = 0.04419 \times 3.39 \approx 0.1498$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
A detection threshold of $T \approx 0.15$ means the correlation between the test file and the secret watermark must exceed **0.15** before the detector declares the watermark present. This guarantees $P_{FP} \le 10^{-6}$.

> Award **2 marks** for the correct formula, **3 marks** for substitution, **2 marks** for the final numerical answer and interpretation.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Confusing Watermarking with Steganography** (–2 marks typical). Watermarking is about *asserting a property of the host*; steganography is about *hiding the existence of communication*. The goal of "covert communication" exists in watermarking, but examiners will deduct marks if you equate watermarking to steganography outright.  
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the secret key** (–1 mark). Every watermarking system has a key-based secret — answers that omit it are marked incomplete.  
> 3. **PSNR calculation errors** (–2 marks typical). Students frequently compute MSE first as a fraction but then forget to invert it in the log. Always write $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{MAX}^2 / \text{MSE})$, **not** $\log_{10}(\text{MSE} / \text{MAX}^2)$.  
> 4. **Skipping the IRC trade-off discussion** (–1 mark). KTU board examiners specifically award marks for acknowledging that improving robustness harms imperceptibility.  
> 5. **Writing "robust watermark" instead of "fragile watermark" for authentication** (–2 marks). This is one of the most frequently lost marks — memorize: **Authentication = Fragile; Copyright/Fingerprinting = Robust.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Digital watermarking** embeds a hidden, secret, recoverable signal *into* a digital asset to assert ownership, authenticity, integrity, or usage rights.
- The **seven canonical goals** are: **Copyright Protection, Authentication, Fingerprinting, Broadcast Monitoring, Copy/Usage Control, Content Labeling, and Covert Communication**.
- A watermarking system is described by the **embedder**, the **detector**, the **secret key**, and the **threshold**.
- The **embedding equation** in its canonical form is $X_w = X + \alpha W$.
- The **three fundamental properties** — Imperceptibility, Robustness, Capacity — are mutually constrained by the **IRC triangle**.
- **PSNR ≥ 38 dB** is the transparency benchmark for images.
- **Fragile watermarks** = used for **Authentication**.
- **Robust watermarks** = used for **Copyright Protection, Fingerprinting, Broadcast Monitoring, Copy Control**.
- **Fingerprinting** uniquely tags each distributed copy → demands **collusion resistance**.
- **Broadcast monitoring** requires **real-time detection** with cheap public detectors.
- **Covert communication** (watermarking cousin: steganography) prioritizes **statistical undetectability** over robustness.
- **Detection statistic** = normalized correlation $\rho$, with threshold chosen using $\operatorname{erfc}^{-1}$ to bound $P_{FP}$.
- Always mention the **secret key** in any design answer — it is what differentiates a true watermark from simple data hiding.
- In KTU answers, **frame every design** through the **goal → IRC priority → embedding choice** triad.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quick Review of Number Theory: Prime Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quick Review of Number Theory: Prime Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Prime Number

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCCT501 - Module 1)**
> A **prime number** is a positive integer $p > 1$ whose only positive divisors are exactly **1** and **p** itself. Equivalently, $p$ is prime if and only if it has exactly two distinct positive divisors. Any integer $n > 1$ that is not prime is called a **composite number**, satisfying $n = a \cdot b$ for some integers $a, b > 1$.

Formally, using the language of number theory:
$$p \in \mathbb{Z}^+, \ p > 1 \quad \text{is prime} \iff \forall\, a, b \in \mathbb{Z},\ \big(p = a \cdot b\big) \implies \big(a = 1 \lor b = 1\big)$$

The number **1** is *neither* prime nor composite by convention — this convention is essential for the uniqueness clause of the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

### Smallest Primes (foundational reference set)

| Index | $p_i$ |
|---|---|
| $p_1$ | **2** |
| $p_2$ | **3** |
| $p_3$ | **5** |
| $p_4$ | **7** |
| $p_5$ | **11** |
| $p_6$ | **13** |
| $p_7$ | **17** |

> [!NOTE]
> **2 is the only even prime.** Every other even integer is divisible by 2, hence composite. This seemingly trivial fact is the reason why 2 is treated as a *special case* in virtually every cryptographic algorithm (RSA, Diffie-Hellman, ECC).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Block of Numbers"

Imagine every positive integer as a **Lego sculpture**. Some sculptures are made of a single, indivisible piece — these are the primes. Other sculptures are built by snapping together two or more smaller Lego pieces — these are the composites.

The deep insight of number theory is this: **primes are the atomic, irreducible building blocks** out of which *every* integer can be constructed, and this construction is *unique* (up to the order of multiplication).

For example, $60$ decomposes as:
$$60 = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 5 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$$
No matter how hard you try, you will *always* end up with exactly the same pile of prime pieces: two 2s, one 3, and one 5.

---

## 1.3 The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic (FTA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic):** Every integer $n > 1$ can be written as a product of one or more primes, and this product is **unique** up to the order of the factors. Formally,
> $$n = p_1^{e_1} \cdot p_2^{e_2} \cdot \ldots \cdot p_k^{e_k}, \qquad p_1 < p_2 < \ldots < p_k,\ \ e_i \ge 1$$

This uniqueness is what makes primes the *true* atoms of arithmetic. It is also the foundation on which the **RSA cryptosystem** ultimately rests.

---

## 1.4 Engineering Relevance in Cryptography

In **public-key cryptography**, primes are not merely a curiosity — they are the **load-bearing pillars** of modern security:

- **RSA**: The modulus $n = p \cdot q$ is a product of two *large* (typically 1024-bit or 2048-bit) primes. The hardness of factoring $n$ into $p, q$ is what protects the secret key.
- **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange**: Operates inside a cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ whose order $p - 1$ must have a **large prime factor** to resist Pohlig-Hellman attacks.
- **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**: Security relies on the difficulty of the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP) over carefully chosen prime fields $\mathbb{F}_p$.

The engineering takeaway: **cryptographic security scales with prime size.** A 2048-bit RSA modulus is considered secure (circa 2024 standards) precisely because the primes embedded within it are astronomically large — far beyond the reach of any known factoring algorithm on classical hardware.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Properties of Prime Numbers

A working knowledge of the following properties is essential for the KTU board examination:

1. **Euclid's Theorem (Infinitude of Primes):** There are *infinitely many* primes. Proof sketch: assume only finitely many primes $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$ exist; then $N = p_1 p_2 \cdots p_k + 1$ has a prime divisor not in the list — contradiction.
2. **No largest prime:** As a corollary of (1), for every prime $p$ there exists another prime $q > p$.
3. **Divisibility property:** If $p$ is prime and $p \mid ab$, then $p \mid a$ or $p \mid b$. This is what makes primes behave like "irreducible atoms."
4. **Density of primes:** Primes become *sparser* as numbers grow. The Prime Number Theorem quantifies this.
5. **Trial division bound:** To test whether $n$ is prime, it suffices to try dividing $n$ by all primes $\le \sqrt{n}$.

---

## 2.2 The Prime Number Theorem (PNT)

The asymptotic density of primes is given by:
$$\pi(x) \sim \frac{x}{\ln x} \quad \text{as } x \to \infty$$

where $\pi(x)$ denotes the **prime-counting function** — the number of primes $\le x$.

The probability that a randomly chosen integer near $x$ is prime is approximately $\dfrac{1}{\ln x}$.

**Engineering interpretation:** Generating a 1024-bit prime by random sampling requires, on average, only about $\ln(2^{1024}) = 1024 \ln 2 \approx 710$ trials — surprisingly few!

---

## 2.3 Fermat's Little Theorem (FLT) — The Cryptographer's Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermat's Little Theorem:** Let $p$ be a prime and let $a$ be an integer with $\gcd(a, p) = 1$. Then
> $$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

A stronger corollary, often more useful in computation:
$$a^p \equiv a \pmod{p} \quad \text{for all integers } a$$

**Use in cryptography:** FLT is the foundation of primality testing (Fermat test), the discrete logarithm problem (DLP), and the construction of modular inverses via Fermat's method:
$$a^{-1} \equiv a^{p-2} \pmod{p}$$

---

## 2.4 Euler's Totient Function $\phi(n)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** $\phi(n)$ counts the number of integers in $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ that are **coprime** to $n$, i.e., $\gcd(k, n) = 1$.

For a prime $p$:
$$\phi(p) = p - 1$$

For a prime power $p^k$:
$$\phi(p^k) = p^k - p^{k-1} = p^{k-1}(p - 1)$$

For a general integer with prime factorization $n = p_1^{e_1} p_2^{e_2} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$:
$$\phi(n) = n \prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1 - \frac{1}{p_i}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\phi(n)$ is **multiplicative** — that is, if $\gcd(m, n) = 1$, then $\phi(mn) = \phi(m)\,\phi(n)$. This is what allows the formula above to combine cleanly across distinct primes.

---

## 2.5 Euler's Theorem (Generalization of FLT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Theorem:** If $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, then
> $$a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

Note that Fermat's Little Theorem is the special case $n = p$ (prime), since $\phi(p) = p - 1$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primality definition | $n>1$, only divisors $1,n$ | $1$ is neither prime nor composite |
| 2 | FTA | $n = \prod p_i^{e_i}$ | Uniqueness up to order |
| 3 | Prime Number Theorem | $\pi(x) \sim x / \ln x$ | Asymptotic density |
| 4 | Trial division limit | $p \le \sqrt{n}$ | Sufficient divisor test |
| 5 | Fermat's Little Theorem | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | Requires $\gcd(a,p)=1$, $p$ prime |
| 6 | FLT (corollary) | $a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}$ | For all $a$, $p$ prime |
| 7 | Modular inverse (Fermat) | $a^{-1} \equiv a^{p-2} \pmod{p}$ | Only when $p$ prime |
| 8 | Euler's totient | $\phi(n) = n \prod (1 - 1/p_i)$ | Over distinct prime divisors |
| 9 | $\phi(p)$ | $\phi(p) = p-1$ | $p$ prime |
| 10 | $\phi(p^k)$ | $\phi(p^k) = p^{k-1}(p-1)$ | Prime power |
| 11 | Multiplicativity | $\phi(mn) = \phi(m)\phi(n)$ | $\gcd(m,n)=1$ |
| 12 | Euler's Theorem | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | $\gcd(a,n)=1$ |
| 13 | Fermat-Euler link | FLT is $n=p$ case of Euler | $\phi(p) = p-1$ |
| 14 | Coprimality test | $\gcd(a,p)=1 \iff p \nmid a$ | For prime $p$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe safeguard:** All absolute-value / divisibility notations above use \vert-free phrasing in the table cells to preserve markdown rendering.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm / Protocol | Prime-Dependent Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **RSA** | $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| **Diffie-Hellman** | Public params: prime $p$ and generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ |
| **DSA / ElGamal** | Modular exponentiation in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ |
| **Miller-Rabin Primality Test** | Uses FLT-style witnesses |
| **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** | Exploits unique factorization into coprime prime-power moduli |

The chain of reasoning — *unique factorization → totient computation → modular exponentiation → one-way function* — is the conceptual spine of nearly all modern public-key cryptography.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Proof of Fermat's Little Theorem

**Statement:** If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

### Proof (Combinatorial / Group-Theoretic)

Consider the set of residues modulo $p$:
$$\mathbb{Z}_p^* = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, p-1\}$$

Since $p$ is prime, **every** nonzero element is coprime to $p$, so $\vert \mathbb{Z}_p^* \vert = p - 1$.

Now consider the $p - 1$ products obtained by multiplying each element by $a$:
$$S = \{a \cdot 1,\ a \cdot 2,\ a \cdot 3,\ \ldots,\ a \cdot (p-1)\} \pmod{p}$$

**Step 1 — No two are congruent mod $p$.** Suppose $a \cdot i \equiv a \cdot j \pmod{p}$. Then $p \mid a(i - j)$. Since $p \nmid a$ (as $\gcd(a, p) = 1$) and $p$ is prime, we get $p \mid (i - j)$. But $0 < \vert i - j \vert < p$, so $i = j$. Hence all elements of $S$ are **distinct** mod $p$.

**Step 2 — Every element of $S$ is a nonzero residue.** Since $\gcd(a, p) = 1$ and $i \in \{1, \ldots, p-1\}$, we have $p \nmid ai$.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.** Thus $S$ is a permutation of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Multiplying all elements:
$$\prod_{i=1}^{p-1}(a \cdot i) \equiv \prod_{i=1}^{p-1} i \pmod{p}$$

$$a^{p-1} \cdot (p-1)! \equiv (p-1)! \pmod{p}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $(p-1)!$.** Since $p \nmid (p-1)!$ (all factors are $< p$), it has a multiplicative inverse mod $p$. Cancelling:
$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Computing $\phi(n)$ for $n = p \cdot q$ (RSA Setup)

**Given:** $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are distinct primes.

**Step 1 — Apply the multiplicative formula:**
$$\phi(n) = n\left(1 - \frac{1}{p}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{q}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = pq$:**
$$\phi(pq) = pq \cdot \frac{p-1}{p} \cdot \frac{q-1}{q}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify term by term:**
$$\phi(pq) = pq \cdot \frac{p-1}{p} \cdot \frac{q-1}{q} = (p-1)(q-1)$$

**Final Result (RSA cornerstone):**
$$\boxed{\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Finding a Modular Inverse via Fermat

**Problem:** Find $7^{-1} \pmod{11}$.

**Step 1 — Verify $p = 11$ is prime and $\gcd(7, 11) = 1$.** ✓ Both true.

**Step 2 — Apply Fermat's modular inverse formula:**
$$a^{-1} \equiv a^{p-2} \pmod{p}$$

$$7^{-1} \equiv 7^{11 - 2} = 7^{9} \pmod{11}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $7^9 \pmod{11}$ via repeated squaring:**

$$7^{1} \equiv 7 \pmod{11}$$
$$7^{2} = 49 \equiv 49 - 44 = 5 \pmod{11}$$
$$7^{4} = (7^{2})^{2} \equiv 5^{2} = 25 \equiv 25 - 22 = 3 \pmod{11}$$
$$7^{8} = (7^{4})^{2} \equiv 3^{2} = 9 \pmod{11}$$

Now combine for $7^9 = 7^{8} \cdot 7^{1}$:
$$7^{9} \equiv 9 \cdot 7 = 63 \equiv 63 - 55 = 8 \pmod{11}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $7 \cdot 8 = 56 \equiv 56 - 55 = 1 \pmod{11}$. ✓

$$\boxed{7^{-1} \equiv 8 \pmod{11}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Fermat Primality Test

**Problem:** Test whether $n = 221$ is prime using base $a = 174$.

**Step 1 — Trial division bound:** $\sqrt{221} \approx 14.86$. We test $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13$. None divide 221, so $221$ is *not* ruled out by trial division.

**Step 2 — Apply Fermat test:**
$$174^{220} \pmod{221}$$

**Step 3 — Modular exponentiation via repeated squaring mod 221:**
$$174^1 \equiv 174 \pmod{221}$$
$$174^2 = 30276 \equiv 30276 - 137 \cdot 221 = 30276 - 30277 = -1 \equiv 220 \pmod{221}$$
$$174^4 \equiv 220^2 = 48400 \pmod{221}$$
$$48400 / 221 \approx 219.0,\ 219 \cdot 221 = 48399,\ \text{so } 174^4 \equiv 1 \pmod{221}$$

So $174^{220} = (174^4)^{55} \equiv 1^{55} = 1 \pmod{221}$.

**Step 4 — Fermat test result:** $174^{220} \equiv 1 \pmod{221}$. The test **passes** — but this does **not** prove primality. Indeed, $221 = 13 \cdot 17$ is **composite**, yet passes the Fermat test for base 174. Such numbers are called **Fermat pseudoprimes** (specifically, base-174 pseudoprimes).

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** A number that passes the Fermat test for a single base may still be composite. For cryptographic use, *many* random bases must be tested, or stronger tests (Miller-Rabin, AKS) must be employed.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — FLT vs Euler's Theorem

| Feature | Fermat's Little Theorem | Euler's Theorem |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus | Prime $p$ | Any $n$ with $\gcd(a,n)=1$ |
| Exponent | $p - 1$ | $\phi(n)$ |
| Special case? | Yes — Euler with $n$ prime | No — fully general |
| Cryptographic role | RSA, modular inverse | RSA key gen, group theory |
| Failure mode | If $p \nmid a$ fails | If $\gcd(a,n) \neq 1$ fails |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Primality Testing Methods

| Method | Type | Complexity | Deterministic? | Cryptographic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Trial division | Deterministic | $O(\sqrt{n})$ | Yes | Only small $n$ |
| Fermat test | Probabilistic | $O(k \log^3 n)$ | No (pseudoprimes fool it) | Heuristic filtering |
| Miller-Rabin | Probabilistic | $O(k \log^3 n)$ | No (with bounded error) | **Standard** for key gen |
| Solovay-Strassen | Probabilistic | $O(k \log^3 n)$ | No | Historical interest |
| AKS | Deterministic | $O(\log^6 n)$ | Yes | Theoretical importance |
| Elliptic Curve (ECPP) | Probabilistic | Heuristically $O(\log^5 n)$ | No (certifiable) | High-value certificates |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Map — Primes in the Cryptographic Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Positive Integers greater than 1] --> B{Prime or Composite?}
    B -->|Exactly 2 divisors| C[Prime Number]
    B -->|More than 2 divisors| D[Composite Number]
    C --> E[Fermats Little Theorem]
    C --> F[Eulers Totient Function]
    C --> G[Modular Inverse via Fermat]
    D --> H[Unique Prime Factorization via FTA]
    E --> I[RSA Encryption and Decryption]
    F --> J[Key Generation phi pq equals p-1 times q-1]
    G --> K[Discrete Logarithm Setting]
    H --> L[Chinese Remainder Theorem CRT]
    I --> M[Public Key Cryptosystem Security]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Architecture — Primality Testing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Input integer n greater than 1] --> TD[Trial division by small primes up to n cuberoot]
    TD -->|Divisor found| COMP[Output COMPOSITE]
    TD -->|No small divisor| FT[Fermat test on base a]
    FT -->|a power n-1 mod n equals 1| MR[Miller Rabin test with k rounds]
    FT -->|Not congruent to 1| COMP
    MR -->|All rounds pass| PROB[Output PROBABLY PRIME]
    MR -->|Any round fails| COMP
    PROB --> USE[Use as cryptographic prime]
    COMP --> RESEL[Reselect candidate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — RSA Key Generation (Prime-Dependent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PR1[Generate large random prime p] --> MUL[Compute n equals p times q]
    PR2[Generate large random prime q] --> MUL
    MUL --> TOT[Compute phi n equals p-1 times q-1]
    TOT --> EPK[Choose public exponent e coprime to phi n]
    EPK --> EDR[Compute d such that e times d is 1 mod phi n]
    EDR --> PUB[Public key: n and e]
    EDR --> PRV[Private key: n and d]
    PUB --> ENC[Encryption: c equals m power e mod n]
    PRV --> DEC[Decryption: m equals c power d mod n]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
