topics.title: 3DES
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 3DES # 3DES (Triple Data Encryption Standard) ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Triple Data Encryption Standard (3DES / TDEA)** is a symmetric-key block cipher that applies the Data Encryption Standard (DES) cipher algorithm three times to each data block. Formally standardized by **NIST** in **ANSI X9.52 (1998)** and later incorporated into the **FIPS PUB 46-3 (1999)**, 3DES was developed to overcome the fundamental weakness of single DES — its small **56-bit effective key** which had become vulnerable to brute-force exhaustive key search attacks as computational power grew through the 1990s. The cipher operates on **64-bit plaintext blocks** and uses either a **112-bit key (2-key 3DES)** or a **168-bit key (3-key 3DES)**, the latter providing an effective cryptographic strength significantly greater than plain DES. The core operation is an **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt (EDE)** sequence, which provides backward compatibility with single DES when all keys are made identical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** 3DES is a *direct evolution* of DES. Students must be able to (a) explain the EDE structure, (b) derive the effective key sizes, (c) justify why three operations are used instead of two, and (d) discuss its current security status (deprecated by NIST for new applications after 2023). > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box** > - **Full Name:** Triple Data Encryption Standard (3DES / TDEA — Triple Data Encryption Algorithm) > - **Block Size:** **64 bits** > - **Key Sizes:** **112 bits (K1=K2)** or **168 bits (K1, K2, K3 distinct)** > - **Structure:** Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN) iterated through DES three times in EDE mode > - **Standardization Body:** NIST (FIPS 46-3 withdrawn; SP 800-131A Rev. 2 governs transition) > - **Current Status:** **Deprecated for all new applications** by NIST (effective from January 1, 2024); allowed only for legacy decryption --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Triple-Locked Vault Imagine a bank vault that originally had a single mechanical lock with a 56-combination dial (this is plain DES — a strong lock, but the dial is small enough that a determined thief with modern tools can try every combination in a few hours). To strengthen the vault without ripping it out and installing a completely new vault (which would be expensive and incompatible with the old keys held by bank customers), the bank does the following: 1. **Add a second outer door with a different dial combination**, and 2. **Wrap the original vault in a third locked shell with yet another dial.** Now a thief has to defeat three different locks in sequence. Even though each individual lock is the same type as before, the combinations are different, so the thief can't just unlock one and be done — he has to do it three times. But here is the clever twist: the bank *intentionally* designs the middle door so that its "unlocking" mechanism works *backwards* compared to the others (this is the **Decrypt** step in the middle). Why? Because if all three keys ever happen to be set to the *same combination*, the three locks simply cancel out mathematically and behave exactly like the original single DES lock. This means old customers with the old single-DES key can still open the vault — **backward compatibility** is preserved automatically. This is exactly what 3DES achieves: a **3-stage cascade of DES operations in an Encrypt-Decrypt-Encrypt (EDE) pattern**, strengthening security enormously while remaining friendly to legacy single-DES systems. > [!TIP] > **Why not just "double DES" (two encryptions in a row)?** A clever attack called the **Meet-in-the-Middle attack** reduces double DES's effective security to almost the same as single DES (~57 bits), not ~112 bits as one would naively expect. Three operations (even with EDE) raise the effective security back up to about 112 bits for the 2-key variant, defeating the meet-in-the-middle attack. --- ## 1.3 Why Was 3DES Needed? (Historical Motivation) | Year | Event | Impact on DES | |------|-------|----------------| | 1977 | DES adopted as FIPS standard | 56-bit key considered secure | | 1990s | Rise of specialized hardware (EFF "Deep Crack," 1998) | DES brute-forced in < 3 days | | 1997 | NIST solicits proposals for AES | DES formally obsolete for new use | | 1998 | ANSI X9.52 standardizes 3DES | 3DES becomes the *interim* standard | | 1999 | FIPS 46-3 endorses 3DES | 3DES official US government standard | | 2001 | AES (Rijndael) selected | 3DES transitions to legacy role | | 2024 | NIST withdraws 3DES | Disallowed for new cryptographic applications | The **56-bit key** of DES, equivalent to approximately **7.2 × 10¹⁶** possible keys, was the fundamental flaw. Moore's Law made exhaustive search economically feasible by the late 1990s, necessitating a stopgap measure that preserved the existing DES infrastructure. --- ## 1.4 The Two Standardized Keying Options > [!IMPORTANT] > **Keying Option 1 — 2-Key 3DES (K1, K2, K1)** > - Three 56-bit keys are referenced, but only **two are independent** because K3 = K1. > - **Effective key length: 112 bits** (2 × 56). > - More compact; easier key management. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Keying Option 2 — 3-Key 3DES (K1, K2, K3)** > - Three **independent** 56-bit keys. > - **Effective key length: 168 bits** (3 × 56). > - Stronger security margin; recommended where 112 bits is borderline. A third theoretical option (**K1 = K2 = K3**, all keys identical) reduces 3DES to plain single DES, providing **backward compatibility** but no security improvement — this is *not* permitted under FIPS 46-3 except for backward compatibility use cases. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 3DES Encryption Equation The encryption operation of 3DES applies the DES function three times in an **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt (EDE)** cascade: $$ C = E_{K_3}\!\left(\,D_{K_2}\!\left(\,E_{K_1}(P)\,\right)\right) $$ Where: - $P$ = **64-bit plaintext block** - $C$ = **64-bit ciphertext block** - $E_{K}(\cdot)$ = **DES encryption** with 56-bit key $K$ - $D_{K}(\cdot)$ = **DES decryption** with 56-bit key $K$ - $K_1, K_2, K_3$ = three (possibly related) 56-bit DES keys The corresponding **decryption** is performed in reverse (Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt becomes Decrypt–Encrypt–Decrypt): $$ P = D_{K_1}\!\left(\,E_{K_2}\!\left(\,D_{K_3}(C)\,\right)\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Decrypt" in the middle?** Two reasons: > 1. **Backward compatibility:** If $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, then $C = E_K(D_K(E_K(P))) = E_K(P)$, recovering single DES exactly. > 2. **Implementation efficiency:** The same DES hardware can be reused for both encryption and decryption, simplifying chip design. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of 3DES Encryption The following bullet sequence describes the operational logic that the KTU examiner expects a student to write when explaining 3DES: 1. **Input Acceptance:** A 64-bit plaintext block $P$ is presented to the cipher. 2. **First DES Encryption:** $P$ is encrypted using the first key $K_1$ to produce an intermediate 64-bit block $I_1$: $$ I_1 = E_{K_1}(P) $$ 3. **DES Decryption (Middle Stage):** $I_1$ is *decrypted* using the second key $K_2$: $$ I_2 = D_{K_2}(I_1) = D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right) $$ 4. **Second DES Encryption (Final Stage):** $I_2$ is encrypted using the third key $K_3$ to yield the ciphertext: $$ C = E_{K_3}(I_2) = E_{K_3}\!\left(D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right)\right) $$ 5. **Output:** The 64-bit ciphertext $C$ is transmitted or stored. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic Device:** "E-D-E" reads as "Every Day Eats" — Encrypt, Decrypt, Encrypt. The reverse on the decryption side is "D-E-D" — "Decryption Eats Dust." --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Notes | |---|---------|---------------------|----------------------|-------| | 1 | 3DES Encryption | $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$ | $P$=plaintext, $C$=ciphertext, $K_i$ = 56-bit keys | EDE cascade | | 2 | 3DES Decryption | $P = D_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C)))$ | Symmetric reversal of EDE | DED cascade | | 3 | Plaintext Block Size | $n = 64 \text{ bits}$ | $n$ = block size in bits | Inherited from DES | | 4 | 2-Key 3DES Effective Key Length | $L_{\text{eff}} = 2 \times 56 = 112 \text{ bits}$ | $L_{\text{eff}}$ = effective key | Meet-in-the-middle limits this | | 5 | 3-Key 3DES Effective Key Length | $L_{\text{eff}} = 3 \times 56 = 168 \text{ bits}$ | All three keys independent | NIST-recommended until 2024 | | 6 | Backward Compatibility | If $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, then $C = E_K(P)$ | Reduces to single DES | Legacy support | | 7 | Meet-in-the-Middle Attack Complexity (2-key) | $W_{\text{MITM}} = 2^{56}$ encryptions + $2^{56}$ decryptions | Equivalent to $2^{57}$ operations | Defeated by using 3 keys | | 8 | Number of DES Rounds in 3DES | $R_{\text{total}} = 16 \times 3 = 48 \text{ rounds}$ | Each DES has 16 Feistel rounds | Triple application | | 9 | Key Schedule Expansion | $K_i$ expanded from 56 bits to 16 × 48-bit subkeys | $i = 1,2,3$ | Per DES key schedule | | 10 | Deprecated Status | NIST SP 800-131A Rev. 2 (2023) | Disallowed for new use after 2024 | AES recommended replacement | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 3DES found extensive deployment in industries where legacy infrastructure dominated and full migration to AES was cost-prohibitive: - **Financial Services:** Used in **EMV chip-card transactions** (Europay-Mastercard-Visa) and **ATM PIN block encryption** (e.g., ISO 9564) well into the 2010s. - **TLS/SSL Protocols:** 3DES cipher suites (`TLS_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA`) were supported in TLS 1.0–1.2 and finally removed in TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446, 2018). - **Microsoft Applications:** Widely used in legacy Windows systems (e.g., Kerberos, NTLM, SChannel). - **SSH Protocol:** Originally the default bulk encryption cipher in SSH-1. - **IPsec:** Used in early VPN deployments via ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload). > [!IMPORTANT] > Even though AES has largely replaced 3DES, understanding 3DES remains a **mandatory KTU syllabus topic** because (a) it is a direct application of DES theory, (b) it introduces the elegant concept of **block-cipher cascades**, and (c) it teaches the **meet-in-the-middle attack** which is a critical concept in modern cryptanalysis. --- ## 2.5 Security Analysis ### 2.5.1 Meet-in-the-Middle Attack on 2-Key 3DES The naive expectation is that two-key 3DES provides $2^{112}$ security. **This is wrong.** A known-plaintext attack can recover both keys in approximately $2^{56}$ operations using the meet-in-the-middle technique: 1. Attacker obtains a known pair $(P, C)$. 2. Encrypt $P$ with all $2^{56}$ possible values of $K_1$, storing results in a table indexed by intermediate value. 3. Decrypt $C$ with all $2^{56}$ possible values of $K_2$ and look for matches in the table. 4. The matching pair $(K_1, K_2)$ is the candidate key. This is why **3-key 3DES is preferred** when maximum security is needed — the meet-in-the-middle attack on three independent keys requires approximately $2^{112}$ operations (still infeasible). ### 2.5.2 Sweet32 Birthday Attack Disclosed in 2016, the **Sweet32** attack exploits 3DES's **64-bit block size**. When the same key encrypts approximately $2^{32}$ blocks (about **32 GB** of data), birthday-paradox collisions become probable, allowing plaintext recovery. This practical attack accelerated NIST's decision to deprecate 3DES. | Attack | Complexity | Year Disclosed | Practical? | |--------|-----------|----------------|------------| | Exhaustive key search (2-key) | $2^{112}$ | Theoretical | Infeasible | | Meet-in-the-middle (2-key) | $2^{56}$ work + $2^{56}$ storage | 1977 (Diffie-Hellman) | Borderline at best | | Sweet32 (block-collision) | $2^{32}$ blocks of data | 2016 | **Yes, demonstrated in TLS** | | Biclique attack (full 3DES) | $2^{80+}$ (3-key) | 2011 | Infeasible | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Showing 3DES Reduces to Single DES when K₁ = K₂ = K₃ We will prove the backward-compatibility property mathematically. **Given:** $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$ (all three keys are the same 56-bit DES key). **To Prove:** $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P))) = E_K(P)$. **Step 1:** Substitute the common key $K$ for each $K_i$: $$ C = E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P))) $$ **Step 2:** Recognize that $D_K$ and $E_K$ are inverse operations: for any block $X$, $D_K(E_K(X)) = X$. Apply this to the inner pair: $$ D_{K}\!\left(E_{K}(P)\right) = P $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the simplified inner result back: $$ C = E_{K}(P) $$ **Conclusion:** When the three keys coincide, the cascade collapses to a single DES encryption. Hence, an old single-DES user (with key $K$) can interoperate with a 3DES system by simply setting $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$, with no code change required on the encryption engine. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Key Length of 2-Key 3DES **Given:** 2-key 3DES uses keys $K_1, K_2$ with $K_3 = K_1$ (total key material = 112 bits). **Question:** Why is the *effective* security not $2^{112}$? **Step 1 — Number of candidate key combinations:** The attacker must consider all $(K_1, K_2)$ pairs. Each is 56 bits, so naive count is $2^{56} \times 2^{56} = 2^{112}$. **Step 2 — Apply the meet-in-the-middle logic:** For a known plaintext-ciphertext pair $(P, C)$, the attacker splits the cipher into two halves: $$ C = E_{K_1}\!\left(D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right)\right) $$ Define the intermediate state $I$ at the middle point: $$ I = D_{K_2}\!\left(E_{K_1}(P)\right) = E_{K_1}(C) \text{ (rearranged)} $$ **Step 3 — Build the two tables:** - **Forward table:** For all $2^{56}$ values of $K_1$, compute $F(K_1) = E_{K_1}(P)$. Store in a hash table indexed by $F$. - **Backward table:** For all $2^{56}$ values of $K_2$, compute $B(K_2) = D_{K_2}(C)$. Store in another hash table indexed by $B$. **Step 4 — Search for collisions:** A correct key pair $(K_1^*, K_2^*)$ satisfies: $$ F(K_1^*) = B(K_2^*) \iff E_{K_1^*}(P) = D_{K_2^*}(C) $$ The expected number of false matches is $\frac{2^{56} \times 2^{56}}{2^{64}} = 2^{48}$ (a manageable number, easily filtered with additional plaintext pairs). **Step 5 — Total work:** $$ W_{\text{total}} \approx 2^{56} + 2^{56} = 2 \cdot 2^{56} \approx 2^{57} $$ **Conclusion:** The effective security of 2-key 3DES is only $2^{57}$ operations for a known-plaintext attack — *not* $2^{112}$. The meet-in-the-middle attack breaks the naive expectation. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Why 3-Key 3DES is Immune to Meet-in-the-Middle **Given:** 3-key 3DES uses three independent keys $K_1, K_2, K_3$, each 56 bits. **Step 1:** The cipher is $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$. Define two intermediate states: $$ I_1 = E_{K_1}(P), \quad I_2 = D_{K_2}(I_1), \quad I_2 = E_{K_3}(C)^{-1} \text{ (rewritten)} $$ **Step 2:** A meet-in-the-middle attacker must build: - **Table A:** All $2^{56}$ values of $K_1$ → $A(K_1) = E_{K_1}(P)$. - **Table B:** All $2^{56}$ values of $K_3$ → $B(K_3) = D_{K_3}(C)$. **Step 3:** For every $(K_1, K_3)$ match producing a common value $V$, the attacker must still try all $2^{56}$ possible values of $K_2$ to confirm: $$ D_{K_2}(V) \stackrel{?}{=} V' $$ where $V'$ is the matched intermediate value. **Step 4 — Total work:** $$ W_{\text{MITM-3key}} \approx 2^{56} + 2^{56} + (2^{112} \text{ candidates}) \times 2^{56} $$ The dominant term is approximately $2^{168}$ (worst case) but with collisions reducing it. The standard cryptanalytic bound is $2^{112}$ work with $2^{56}$ memory — meaning the security is **bounded below by the 2-key meet-in-the-middle cost**, not improved by adding the third key alone. However, no better-than-brute-force attack on the full 3-key 3DES is currently known. **Conclusion:** 3-key 3DES provides about $2^{112}$ bits of security against the meet-in-the-middle attack, with brute force requiring $2^{168}$. The effective security is approximately $2^{112}$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of DES Rounds in 3DES Each DES application consists of 16 Feistel rounds. 3DES applies DES three times. $$ R_{\text{total}} = 3 \times R_{\text{DES}} = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ rounds} $$ Where $R_{\text{DES}} = 16$ is the standard DES round count. **Throughput Impact:** Because 3DES requires three times the DES work, its throughput is approximately **1/3** of single DES (in software; in hardware the overhead is smaller due to pipelining). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (DES vs 2-Key 3DES vs 3-Key 3DES vs AES) | Property | DES | 2-Key 3DES | 3-Key 3DES | AES-128 | |----------|-----|------------|------------|---------| | Block size (bits) | 64 | 64 | 64 | **128** | | Key size (bits) | 56 | 112 | 168 | 128, 192, 256 | | Effective security (bits) | 56 | ~80–112 | ~112 | 128+ | | Rounds | 16 | 48 | 48 | 10, 12, 14 | | Structure | Feistel | Feistel cascade | Feistel cascade | SPN | | Throughput (relative) | 1.0× | ~0.33× | ~0.33× | **~6×** | | Status (2024) | Withdrawn | **Deprecated** | **Deprecated** | Current standard | | Sweet32 vulnerable? | Yes | Yes | Yes | **No** | | MITM resistance | N/A | $2^{57}$ | $2^{112}$ | N/A | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** 3DES's 64-bit block size is its *fatal flaw* in the modern era. Even with a strong 168-bit key, encrypting petabytes of data on the same key causes Sweet32 collisions. This is why AES (128-bit blocks) is the only recommended block cipher for new systems. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — 2-Key 3DES Encryption Trace Let us trace a *conceptual* encryption with 2-key 3DES on a 64-bit block $P = \texttt{0x0123456789ABCDEF}$, with $K_1 = \texttt{0x133457799BBCDFF1}$ and $K_2 = \texttt{0x0E329232EA6D9D84}$ (note: $K_3 = K_1$). **Step 1: First DES Encryption with $K_1$** $$ I_1 = E_{K_1}(P) = \texttt{D5D44FF720683D0D} \quad \text{(hypothetical output)} $$ This involves the 16 Feistel rounds of DES with key schedule derived from $K_1$. **Step 2: DES Decryption with $K_2$** $$ I_2 = D_{K_2}(I_1) = D_{K_2}(\texttt{D5D44FF720683D0D}) = \texttt{8592D5E4A26B4F7C} $$ The decryption runs the DES Feistel rounds in reverse key order. **Step 3: Second DES Encryption with $K_1$ (since $K_3 = K_1$)** $$ C = E_{K_1}(I_2) = E_{K_1}(\texttt{8592D5E4A26B4F7C}) = \texttt{3F8C2A1B7D9E40F6} $$ **Final Ciphertext:** $C = \texttt{0x3F8C2A1B7D9E40F6}$ (hypothetical; actual DES outputs differ). > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** KTU rarely asks students to compute exact DES outputs by hand (the S-boxes are tedious). Instead, questions focus on the **structure, key sizes, number of rounds, and meet-in-the-middle analysis**. Memorize the EDE structure and the key sizes; that fetches full marks. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q: What is Triple DES? Mention its key sizes and block size.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Triple Data Encryption Standard (3DES or TDEA) is a symmetric-key block cipher that applies the DES algorithm **three times sequentially** in an **Encrypt-Decrypt-Encrypt (EDE)** configuration to overcome the small key size of plain DES. It operates on a **block size of 64 bits** (inherited from DES). It supports two standardized keying options: 1. **2-key 3DES** with effective key length **112 bits** (K1, K2 independent, K3 = K1). 2. **3-key 3DES** with effective key length **168 bits** (K1, K2, K3 all independent). The 3DES encryption equation is $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$, where the middle "decrypt" stage enables **backward compatibility** with single DES (when K1 = K2 = K3). *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for key sizes, 1 mark for EDE structure.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q: Briefly explain the meet-in-the-middle attack on double DES and how 3DES counters it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **meet-in-the-middle attack** is a known-plaintext cryptanalytic technique that breaks double DES's apparent $2^{112}$ key strength. The attacker: 1. **Encrypts** the known plaintext $P$ with all $2^{56}$ possible $K_1$ values, storing the $2^{56}$ intermediate results in a table. 2. **Decrypts** the known ciphertext $C$ with all $2^{56}$ possible $K_2$ values and looks for matches in the table. 3. The total work is approximately $2^{56} + 2^{56} = 2^{57}$ operations, recovering both keys. This reduces double DES to roughly the same effective security as single DES, making it useless. **3DES counters this attack** by introducing a **third independent key** in the EDE cascade. The 3-key variant requires approximately $2^{112}$ operations for a meet-in-the-middle attack (still infeasible) and $2^{168}$ for brute force. The structure $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$ with $K_1, K_2, K_3$ independent provides substantially higher security than double DES. *(Examiner's note: 1.5 marks for the attack mechanism, 1.5 marks for how 3DES defeats it.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. Students must write the full mathematical model, the diagram, and a clear justification. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper based on June 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the architecture of Triple DES (3DES) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the significance of the Encrypt-Decrypt-Encrypt (EDE) structure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between 2-key 3DES and 3-key 3DES. Analyze the security of 2-key 3DES against the meet-in-the-middle attack and justify why 3-key 3DES is preferred. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A ### Part (a) — 3DES Architecture and EDE Structure **Introduction (2 marks):** Triple DES (3DES / TDEA) is a symmetric block cipher that applies the single DES algorithm **three times in succession** to each 64-bit data block. The formal encryption function is: $$ C = E_{K_3}\!\left(\,D_{K_2}\!\left(\,E_{K_1}(P)\,\right)\right) $$ **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The architecture consists of three sequential DES stages: - **Stage 1:** DES encryption $E_{K_1}$ with 56-bit key $K_1$. - **Stage 2:** DES decryption $D_{K_2}$ with 56-bit key $K_2$. - **Stage 3:** DES encryption $E_{K_3}$ with 56-bit key $K_3$. Each stage internally performs **16 Feistel rounds**, yielding **48 total rounds** in 3DES. The intermediate 64-bit values between stages are not exposed externally. **Significance of EDE Structure (2 marks):** 1. **Backward Compatibility:** When $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$, the EDE structure reduces mathematically to $C = E_K(P)$, equivalent to single DES. This allows legacy single-DES users to interoperate with 3DES systems using their existing key. 2. **Implementation Efficiency:** The middle "decrypt" stage reuses the same DES hardware that performs encryption, eliminating the need for a separate inverse-DES module. 3. **Cryptographic Strength:** The "decrypt" in the middle does *not* weaken security — it is the inclusion of three independent keys that makes 3DES strong. --- ### Part (b) — Keying Options and Security Analysis **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Parameter | 2-Key 3DES | 3-Key 3DES | |-----------|------------|------------| | Key material | 112 bits | 168 bits | | Key relation | $K_3 = K_1$ | $K_1 \neq K_2 \neq K_3$ | | Effective security | ~112 bits (naive) / $2^{57}$ vs. MITM | ~112 bits (with MITM) / $2^{168}$ brute force | | Recommended use | Legacy systems | High-security legacy systems | **Meet-in-the-Middle Attack on 2-Key 3DES (3 marks):** For the cipher $C = E_{K_1}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$, an attacker with $(P, C)$ can: 1. Build a table of $E_{K_1}(P)$ for all $2^{56}$ values of $K_1$. 2. Compute $D_{K_2}(C)$ for all $2^{56}$ values of $K_2$. 3. Search for the unique match where $E_{K_1}(P) = D_{K_2}(C)$. 4. **Total work: $2^{57}$ operations + $2^{56}$ storage.** This is why **3-key 3DES is preferred**: the meet-in-the-middle cost on $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$ with three independent keys is $2^{112}$ operations, which is computationally infeasible. **Justification for 3-Key Preference (2 marks):** - Provides 50% more key material (168 bits vs 112 bits). - Resists the meet-in-the-middle attack ($2^{112}$ vs $2^{57}$). - Recommended by NIST under FIPS 46-3 for all new 3DES deployments (until the 2024 deprecation). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper based on Dec 2023 Scheme]** **(a)** Describe the steps of 3DES encryption and decryption. Show mathematically that 3DES is backward compatible with single DES. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the security vulnerabilities of 3DES, including the Sweet32 attack and the block-size limitation. Why has NIST deprecated 3DES, and what is the recommended replacement? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B ### Part (a) — Encryption/Decryption Steps and Backward Compatibility **Encryption Steps (3 marks):** 1. Take 64-bit plaintext $P$ and three 56-bit keys $(K_1, K_2, K_3)$. 2. Compute $I_1 = E_{K_1}(P)$ using standard DES encryption. 3. Compute $I_2 = D_{K_2}(I_1)$ using DES decryption. 4. Compute $C = E_{K_3}(I_2)$ using DES encryption. 5. Output the 64-bit ciphertext $C$. **Decryption Steps (1 mark):** Decryption is the inverse cascade $P = D_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C)))$, applied in reverse key order with inverted operations. **Backward Compatibility Proof (3 marks):** Assume $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$. Then: $$ C = E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P))) $$ Since $D_K(E_K(X)) = X$ for any block $X$, the inner pair simplifies: $$ D_{K}\!\left(E_{K}(P)\right) = P $$ Therefore: $$ C = E_{K}(P) $$ This is exactly the single DES encryption of $P$ with key $K$. Hence, 3DES is fully backward compatible. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Part (b) — Vulnerabilities and Deprecation **Block-Size Limitation (2 marks):** 3DES inherits the **64-bit block size** from DES. According to the birthday paradox, block collisions become probable after approximately $2^{32}$ encrypted blocks (≈ **32 GB** of data) under the same key. This allows attackers to detect ciphertext collisions and potentially recover plaintext. **Sweet32 Attack (2 marks):** Disclosed in 2016 by Bhargavan and Leurent, the **Sweet32** attack exploits the 64-bit block size in practical settings (e.g., TLS, OpenVPN). The attacker forces the server to encrypt enough data on the same key to induce a birthday collision, then uses the collision to recover plaintext (e.g., HTTP session cookies). Sweet32 was demonstrated against 3DES in real TLS sessions. **Other Vulnerabilities (1 mark):** - Slow throughput (1/3 of DES) due to triple operation. - NIST's 2017 quantum-resistance study indicated 3DES provides only **~112 bits** of security under Grover's algorithm, insufficient for post-quantum applications. **NIST Deprecation and Replacement (2 marks):** - **NIST SP 800-131A Rev. 2 (2023)** deprecated 3DES for **all new cryptographic applications** as of **January 1, 2024**. - Existing systems may use 3DES only for **decryption of legacy data**; new implementations must use **AES** (FIPS 197). - AES provides a 128-bit block (no Sweet32 vulnerability) and supports 128/192/256-bit keys with much higher throughput. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — 3DES Questions** > > 1. **Confusing key sizes with effective security:** Students often write "3DES uses 168-bit key" without noting that the *effective security* against the meet-in-the-middle attack is only ~112 bits. Always state both numbers. > 2. **Forgetting the "Decrypt" in EDE:** Many students write 3DES as "triple encryption" with three E's. The correct structure is **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt**. Marks are deducted for getting the EDE pattern wrong. > 3. **Missing the backward-compatibility justification:** The Decrypt in the middle is *not* an arbitrary choice — it specifically allows $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$ to reduce to single DES. Examiners look for this *why* explicitly. > 4. **Ignoring block-size vs key-size distinction:** Students sometimes say "3DES is secure because it has a 168-bit key," overlooking the 64-bit block. Modern attacks (Sweet32) target the block size, not the key. Always mention both. > 5. **Not drawing the block diagram:** For 7-mark questions, a labeled block diagram of the EDE cascade is **mandatory**. Skipping the diagram costs 1–2 marks even if the textual explanation is correct. > 6. **Confusing 2-key and 3-key 3DES:** Remember: 2-key 3DES has $K_3 = K_1$ (so 2 × 56 = 112 bits of key material, but $2^{57}$ meet-in-the-middle security). 3-key 3DES has all keys independent. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **3DES = Triple DES = TDEA** — applies DES three times in an **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt (EDE)** sequence. - **Block size:** **64 bits** (inherited from DES). - **Key sizes:** **112 bits (2-key)** or **168 bits (3-key)** key material. - **Effective security:** ~**112 bits** against the best known attacks for both variants. - **Number of rounds:** 16 × 3 = **48 Feistel rounds** total. - **Encryption equation:** $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$. - **Decryption equation:** $P = D_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C)))$. - **Backward compatibility:** When $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, 3DES reduces to single DES — $C = E_K(P)$. - **EDE purpose:** (1) Backward compatibility with single DES, (2) Hardware reuse, (3) Cryptographic strength. - **Meet-in-the-middle attack on 2-key 3DES:** Reduces effective security from $2^{112}$ to **$2^{57}$** operations. - **3-key 3DES MITM cost:** ~**$2^{112}$** operations (infeasible). - **Sweet32 attack (2016):** Exploits 64-bit block; practical after ~$2^{32}$ blocks of data. - **NIST status (as of 2024):** **Deprecated for new use**; AES is the recommended replacement. - **AES advantages over 3DES:** 128-bit block (no Sweet32), 4–6× faster throughput, larger key sizes. - **2-key 3DES is still encountered** in legacy financial, banking, and TLS 1.0–1.2 systems. - **Standardization:** FIPS 46-3 (withdrawn), ANSI X9.52, NIST SP 800-131A Rev. 2. - **Engineering takeaway:** Always use **AES** for new designs; treat 3DES strictly as a legacy decryption primitive. - **Mnemonic for EDE pattern:** **"E**ncrypt, **D**ecrypt, **E**ncrypt" = "Every Day Eats" — a useful exam-time memory aid. - **Most-tested KTU points:** (1) EDE structure, (2) key sizes, (3) meet-in-the-middle analysis, (4) backward compatibility derivation, (5) Sweet32 and deprecation rationale.

topics.title: AES (Block Level Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** AES (Block Level Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** is a symmetric-key, **substitution–permutation network (SPN)** block cipher standardized by **NIST** in **FIPS PUB 197** (2001). AES operates on a fixed **128-bit plaintext block** and supports three key lengths — **128, 192, and 256 bits** — using **10, 12, and 14 rounds**, respectively. It is based on the **Rijndael algorithm** designed by Joan Daemen and Vincent Rijmen.

The block-level view of AES concerns itself with the **overall cipher architecture, state representation, the four round transformations, the key schedule layout, and the inverse cipher** — *without diving into the bit-level construction of each S-box or the GF(2⁸) implementation details* (which are reserved for the round-level treatment).

## 1.2 Key Design Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Block size | $N_b$ | 4 words (128 bits) |
| Key size (words) | $N_k$ | 4, 6, or 8 |
| Number of rounds | $N_r$ | **10, 12, or 14** |
| Round key size | — | $N_b \cdot 32 = 128$ bits |
| Total round-key bits | — | $N_b(N_r+1) \cdot 32$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between the number of 32-bit key words $N_k$ and the number of rounds $N_r$ is fixed by the standard as:
> $$N_r = \max(N_b, N_k) + 6$$
> Since $N_b = 4$ for AES, this gives $N_r = N_k + 6$, i.e., $N_r \in \{10, 12, 14\}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "10-Lock Safe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a courier carrying a confidential 16-character document into a vault. Before the courier enters, the vault manager ("**AddRoundKey**") stamps the document with a one-time code from the master key. Inside the vault, the document passes through **10 identical transformation rooms**. In every room, four actions happen:
> 1. **SubBytes** — every character is replaced via a hidden substitution table.
> 2. **ShiftRows** — characters in each row are rotated by a different amount.
> 3. **MixColumns** — characters in each column are mathematically blended.
> 4. **AddRoundKey** — a fresh round-key segment is XORed in.
> The very last room skips the *MixColumns* step. To unlock the safe and recover the document, the courier must apply each transformation in **reverse order** using the **same round keys** (because XOR is its own inverse), and the **inverse** of the forward operations.

This layered design gives AES its celebrated **confusion** (SubBytes, AddRoundKey) and **diffusion** (ShiftRows, MixColumns) properties.

## 1.4 What "Block Level Only" Means in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus restricts the scope of this topic to:
> - The **AES state array** and its relationship to the input/output block.
> - The **sequence and purpose** of the four round transformations.
> - The **key schedule layout** (number of round keys, word boundaries).
> - The **inverse cipher structure**.
> - *Excluded*: bit-level construction of the S-box, GF(2⁸) polynomial arithmetic, finite-field inversion details.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AES State Array

The fundamental data structure inside AES is the **State**, a 4-row × $N_b$-column matrix of **bytes** (octets).

For AES, $N_b = 4$, so the State is a $4 \times 4$ matrix of 16 bytes.

### 2.1.1 Input-to-State Mapping

If the 128-bit input block is denoted as the byte sequence $in_0, in_1, \ldots, in_{15}$, then the State is filled **column-by-column**:

$$s[r, c] = in_{r + 4c}, \quad r, c \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$$

### 2.1.2 State-to-Output Mapping

After all round transformations, the State is read out column-by-column to produce the 128-bit ciphertext block:

$$out_{r + 4c} = s[r, c]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why column-major?** Column-major ordering matches the way **MixColumns** operates on each column independently, leading to efficient implementations.

## 2.2 The Round Transformations (Block-Level View)

The four AES round transformations are applied repeatedly. Each is a **bijection** on the 128-bit state, ensuring invertibility.

### 2.2.1 SubBytes (Byte Substitution)
- A **non-linear byte substitution** applied independently to every byte of the State.
- Implemented as a **16 × 16 lookup table (S-box)** in hardware/software.
- Provides **confusion** — destroys any algebraic relationship between key and ciphertext.

### 2.2.2 ShiftRows (Row Shifting)
- Row $r$ of the State is **cyclically shifted left by $r$ byte positions**.
- Row 0: no shift; Row 1: shift 1; Row 2: shift 2; Row 3: shift 3.
- Provides **inter-column diffusion**.

### 2.2.3 MixColumns (Column Mixing)
- Each column of the State is treated as a polynomial over **GF(2⁸)** and multiplied by a **fixed polynomial $a(x)$** modulo $x^4 + 1$.
- The matrix form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,c} \\ s'_{1,c} \\ s'_{2,c} \\ s'_{3,c} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,c} \\ s_{1,c} \\ s_{2,c} \\ s_{3,c} \end{bmatrix}$$

- Provides **intra-column diffusion** (each output byte depends on all four input bytes of that column).

### 2.2.4 AddRoundKey (Round-Key XOR)
- A **bitwise XOR** of the State with a 128-bit **round key** derived from the original cipher key via the key schedule.
- Provides **key-dependent diffusion**.

## 2.3 Overall Encryption Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complete AES encryption sequence is:
> 1. **Initial Round (Round 0):** AddRoundKey using round key $K_0$.
> 2. **Rounds 1 to $N_r - 1$:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey with $K_i$.
> 3. **Final Round (Round $N_r$):** SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey with $K_{N_r}$ (MixColumns is **omitted**).

The omission of MixColumns in the final round is a deliberate design choice that simplifies the inverse cipher without weakening security.

## 2.4 Key Schedule Layout

The key expansion converts the $N_k$-word cipher key into $N_b(N_r+1)$ words, grouped into $N_r + 1$ round keys of $N_b$ words each.

| AES Variant | $N_k$ | $N_r$ | Round-Key Words | Total Key-Schedule Words |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AES-128 | 4 | 10 | 4 | **44** |
| AES-192 | 6 | 12 | 4 | **52** |
| AES-256 | 8 | 14 | 4 | **60** |

The schedule is generated **word-by-word** $W[0], W[1], \ldots, W[N_b(N_r+1)-1]$ using:
- **RotWord** — cyclic left rotation of a 4-byte word by one byte.
- **SubWord** — S-box substitution applied to each of the 4 bytes.
- **Rcon[j]** — round constant, with $\text{RC}[1] = 1$ and $\text{RC}[j] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1]$ in GF(2⁸).

The key-expansion recurrence (for $N_k \le 6$, i.e., AES-128 / AES-192) is:

$$W[i] = \begin{cases} W[i - N_k] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i / N_k] & \text{if } i \equiv 0 \pmod{N_k} \\ W[i - N_k] \oplus W[i-1] & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 AES Decryption (Inverse Cipher)

> [!NOTE]
> The AES inverse cipher applies the **inverse of each round transformation in reverse order**:
> 1. **Initial Round:** AddRoundKey with $K_{N_r}$ (XOR is self-inverse).
> 2. **Rounds $N_r - 1$ down to 1:** InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey → InvMixColumns.
> 3. **Final Round:** InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey with $K_0$.

An **Equivalent Inverse Cipher** can be constructed by reordering the operations so that all AddRoundKey steps come first, allowing decryption to use the same key stream as encryption — useful in hardware.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Block size | $4 \cdot N_b \cdot 8 = 128$ bits | $N_b = 4$ for AES |
| Number of rounds | $N_r = 6 + \max(N_b, N_k)$ | $N_r \in \{10, 12, 14\}$ |
| Total round-key words | $N_b(N_r + 1)$ | 44, 52, or 60 |
| State filling | $s[r,c] = in_{r + 4c}$ | column-major |
| State reading | $out_{r + 4c} = s[r,c]$ | column-major |
| MixColumns matrix | fixed $4 \times 4$ over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ | entries $\{01, 02, 03\}$ |
| InvMixColumns matrix | fixed $4 \times 4$ over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ | entries $\{0E, 0B, 0D, 09\}$ |
| ShiftRows shift | row $r$ shifted left by $r$ bytes | $r \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ |
| Rcon recurrence | $\text{RC}[j] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1]$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$, $\text{RC}[1] = 1$ | with $\text{RC}[1] = \text{x}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **TLS 1.3 / HTTPS:** Every modern web session uses **AES-128-GCM** or **AES-256-GCM** for bulk data encryption.
> - **Disk Encryption:** BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS all default to AES-XTS.
> - **Wireless Security:** WPA3 uses **AES-128-GCMP** (mandatory).
> - **Database Encryption:** TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) in SQL Server, Oracle uses AES.
> - **Hardware Acceleration:** AES-NI instructions on Intel/AMD CPUs perform one round in ~1–2 clock cycles.
> - **IoT / Embedded:** Lightweight AES implementations dominate smart-card and RFID security.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Rounds $N_r$ in AES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AES defines the number of rounds in terms of the block size (in 32-bit words) and the key size (in 32-bit words). Derive the values used in standard AES.

**Step 1 — State the general Rijndael equation.**

The Rijndael design specifies:

$$N_r = 6 + \max(N_b, N_k)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the AES block size.**

For AES, the block is fixed at 128 bits = 4 words, so $N_b = 4$.

**Step 3 — Apply to each key length.**

For AES-128 ($N_k = 4$):

$$N_r = 6 + \max(4, 4) = 6 + 4 = 10$$

For AES-192 ($N_k = 6$):

$$N_r = 6 + \max(4, 6) = 6 + 6 = 12$$

For AES-256 ($N_k = 8$):

$$N_r = 6 + \max(4, 8) = 6 + 8 = 14$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

$$\boxed{N_r = 10 \text{ (AES-128)}, \quad 12 \text{ (AES-192)}, \quad 14 \text{ (AES-256)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Number of Round-Key Words

**Step 1 — Identify what one round key contains.**

Each round key is exactly **$N_b$ words** (= 128 bits = 16 bytes for AES).

**Step 2 — Identify the number of round keys.**

There is **one round key per round plus one for the initial AddRoundKey**, so the total number of round keys is $N_r + 1$.

**Step 3 — Multiply.**

$$\text{Total words} = N_b \cdot (N_r + 1)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the AES values.**

For AES-128:

$$4 \cdot (10 + 1) = 4 \cdot 11 = 44 \text{ words}$$

For AES-192:

$$4 \cdot (12 + 1) = 4 \cdot 13 = 52 \text{ words}$$

For AES-256:

$$4 \cdot (14 + 1) = 4 \cdot 15 = 60 \text{ words}$$

In bits, this equals:

$$\text{Total round-key bits} = N_b \cdot (N_r + 1) \cdot 32$$

For AES-128: $4 \cdot 11 \cdot 32 = 1408$ bits. Each round key consumes 128 bits, and 11 such round keys are needed.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State Array Construction from a 128-bit Block

**Step 1 — Write the input block as 16 bytes.**

The input block is the sequence:

$$\text{Input} = (in_0, in_1, in_2, \ldots, in_{15})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the column-major filling rule.**

The State $s$ is a $4 \times 4$ matrix filled column-by-column:

$$\begin{aligned}
s[0,0] &= in_0, & s[0,1] &= in_4, & s[0,2] &= in_8,  & s[0,3] &= in_{12} \\
s[1,0] &= in_1, & s[1,1] &= in_5, & s[1,2] &= in_9,  & s[1,3] &= in_{13} \\
s[2,0] &= in_2, & s[2,1] &= in_6, & s[2,2] &= in_{10}, & s[2,3] &= in_{14} \\
s[3,0] &= in_3, & s[3,1] &= in_7, & s[3,2] &= in_{11}, & s[3,3] &= in_{15}
\end{aligned}$$

The general formula $s[r, c] = in_{r + 4c}$ can be verified: for $r = 0, c = 0$: index 0. For $r = 3, c = 3$: index $3 + 4 \cdot 3 = 15$. ✓

**Step 3 — After all rounds, read out the State.**

The State is read out using the inverse mapping:

$$out_{r + 4c} = s[r, c]$$

So $out_0 = s[0,0]$, $out_1 = s[1,0]$, …, $out_{15} = s[3,3]$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MixColumns Matrix Operation (Block Level)

**Step 1 — Represent one column of the State as a polynomial.**

For column $c$:

$$s_c(x) = s_{3,c} \cdot x^3 + s_{2,c} \cdot x^2 + s_{1,c} \cdot x + s_{0,c}$$

**Step 2 — Define the fixed multiplier polynomial.**

$$a(x) = \{03\} \cdot x^3 + \{01\} \cdot x^2 + \{01\} \cdot x + \{02\}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply modulo $x^4 + 1$.**

The new column is given by:

$$s'_c(x) = a(x) \cdot s_c(x) \pmod{x^4 + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to matrix form.**

Each coefficient of the product yields one row of the matrix equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,c} \\ s'_{1,c} \\ s'_{2,c} \\ s'_{3,c} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,c} \\ s_{1,c} \\ s_{2,c} \\ s_{3,c} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Write the first row explicitly.**

$$s'_{0,c} = (\{02\} \cdot s_{0,c}) \oplus (\{03\} \cdot s_{1,c}) \oplus s_{2,c} \oplus s_{3,c}$$

The remaining rows are obtained by cyclic shift of the coefficient vector.

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $\{02\}$, $\{03\}$ refers to bytes in **hexadecimal** when used as AES constants, and arithmetic ($\cdot$, $\oplus$) is performed in **GF(2⁸)** with the AES irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — InvMixColumns Matrix

The inverse cipher multiplies by the polynomial:

$$a^{-1}(x) = \{0E\} \cdot x^3 + \{0B\} \cdot x^2 + \{0D\} \cdot x + \{09\}$$

This yields the inverse matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} 0E & 0B & 0D & 09 \\ 09 & 0E & 0B & 0D \\ 0D & 09 & 0E & 0B \\ 0B & 0D & 09 & 0E \end{bmatrix}$$

Verification: the determinant of the forward MixColumns matrix over GF(2⁸) is non-zero, guaranteeing a unique inverse.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Key Expansion Word Recurrence (AES-128, $N_k = 4$)

**Step 1 — Initialise the first $N_k$ words.**

$$W[0] = (k_0, k_1, k_2, k_3)$$
$$W[1] = (k_4, k_5, k_6, k_7)$$
$$W[2] = (k_8, k_9, k_{10}, k_{11})$$
$$W[3] = (k_{12}, k_{13}, k_{14}, k_{15})$$

**Step 2 — Generate the next word using the function $g$.**

For $i = 4$ (a multiple of $N_k = 4$):

$$W[4] = W[0] \oplus g(W[3])$$

where:

$$g(W[3]) = \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W[3])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[1]$$

Explicitly:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RotWord}(W[3]) &= (k_{13}, k_{14}, k_{15}, k_{12}) \\
\text{SubWord}(\cdot) &= (\text{S-box}(k_{13}), \text{S-box}(k_{14}), \text{S-box}(k_{15}), \text{S-box}(k_{12})) \\
\text{Rcon}[1] &= (\text{x}, 0, 0, 0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Generate a non-multiple-of-$N_k$ word.**

For $i = 5$ (not a multiple of $N_k$):

$$W[5] = W[1] \oplus W[4]$$

**Step 4 — Continue until 44 words are produced for AES-128.**

The total number of generated words is $N_b(N_r + 1) = 4 \cdot 11 = 44$.

> [!NOTE]
> For AES-256 ($N_k = 8$), an additional SubWord step is inserted for indices that are multiples of 4 but not of 8, making the schedule slightly more complex.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Operation Summary

| Transformation | Operates On | Algebraic Structure | Diffusion/Confusion | Invertible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SubBytes | Individual byte | S-box (non-linear) | **Confusion** | Yes (Inv S-box) |
| ShiftRows | Row-wise | Cyclic permutation | **Diffusion (inter-column)** | Yes (inverse shift right) |
| MixColumns | Column-wise | GF(2⁸) matrix mult. | **Diffusion (intra-column)** | Yes (InvMixColumns) |
| AddRoundKey | Entire State | XOR with round key | Key-dependent | Yes (XOR is self-inverse) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AES Encryption — Overall Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    inBlock["Input Block 128 bits"]
    stateIn["State 4x4 Bytes"]
    addKey0["AddRoundKey with K0"]
    roundLoop{"Round i = 1 to Nr-1"}
    subBytes["SubBytes"]
    shiftRows["ShiftRows"]
    mixCols["MixColumns"]
    addKeyI["AddRoundKey with Ki"]
    finalRound["Final Round Nr"]
    addKeyNr["AddRoundKey with KNr"]
    stateOut["State 4x4 Bytes"]
    outBlock["Output Block 128 bits Ciphertext"]

    inBlock --> stateIn
    stateIn --> addKey0
    addKey0 --> roundLoop
    roundLoop --> subBytes
    subBytes --> shiftRows
    shiftRows --> mixCols
    mixCols --> addKeyI
    addKeyI --> roundLoop
    roundLoop -->|Last normal round done| finalRound
    finalRound --> subBytes
    subBytes --> shiftRows
    shiftRows --> addKeyNr
    addKeyNr --> stateOut
    stateOut --> outBlock
```

## 4.2 AES Round Function — Internal Block Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    stateInR["State In"]
    sBox["SubBytes S-box Lookup"]
    shiftR["ShiftRows Cyclic Shift"]
    mixC["MixColumns GF2^8 Matrix Mult"]
    xorK["AddRoundKey XOR with Round Key"]
    stateOutR["State Out"]

    stateInR --> sBox
    sBox --> shiftR
    shiftR --> mixC
    mixC --> xorK
    xorK --> stateOutR
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **final round** simply omits the `mixC` node. So the final block passes through SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey only.

## 4.3 AES Key Schedule — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    cipherKey["Input Cipher Key Nk words"]
    wordGen{"Index i modulo Nk"}
    rotOp["RotWord 1-byte cyclic left shift"]
    subOp["SubWord S-box on 4 bytes"]
    rconXor["XOR with Rcon i over Nk"]
    xorPrev["XOR with W i minus Nk"]
    xorPrev1["XOR with W i minus 1"]
    wordOut["W i word 4 bytes"]

    cipherKey --> wordGen
    wordGen -->|i mod Nk equals 0| rotOp
    rotOp --> subOp
    subOp --> rconXor
    rconXor --> xorPrev1
    wordGen -->|otherwise| xorPrev
    xorPrev --> xorPrev1
    xorPrev1 --> wordOut
```

## 4.4 AES Decryption (Inverse Cipher) — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    cipherIn["Ciphertext Block 128 bits"]
    addKeyN["AddRoundKey with KNr"]
    loopInv{"Round i = Nr-1 down to 1"}
    invShift["InvShiftRows shift right"]
    invSub["InvSubBytes inverse S-box"]
    addKeyI2["AddRoundKey with Ki"]
    invMix["InvMixColumns"]
    finalInv["InvShiftRows then InvSubBytes"]
    addKey0Inv["AddRoundKey with K0"]
    plainOut["Plaintext Block 128 bits"]

    cipherIn --> addKeyN
    addKeyN --> loopInv
    loopInv --> invShift
    invShift --> invSub
    invSub --> addKeyI2
    addKeyI2 --> invMix
    invMix --> loopInv
    loopInv -->|i equals 1 done| finalInv
    finalInv --> addKey0Inv
    addKey0Inv --> plainOut
```

## 4.5 State Array — Memory Layout (Block Level)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph State_4x4 ["AES State 4 rows x 4 columns"]
        s00["s[0,0]"]
        s10["s[1,0]"]
        s20["s[2,0]"]
        s30["s[3,0]"]
        s01["s[0,1]"]
        s11["s[1,1]"]
        s21["s[2,1]"]
        s31["s[3,1]"]
        s02["s[0,2]"]
        s12["s[1,2]"]
        s22["s[2,2]"]
        s32["s[3,2]"]
        s03["s[0,3]"]
        s13["s[1,3]"]
        s23["s[2,3]"]
        s33["s[3,3]"]
    end
    in0["in_0"] -.-> s00
    in1["in_1"] -.-> s10
    in2["in_2"] -.-> s20
    in3["in_3"] -.-> s30
    in4["in_4"] -.-> s01
    in5["in_5"] -.-> s11
    in6["in_6"] -.-> s21
    in7["in_7"] -.-> s31
    in8["in_8"] -.-> s02
    in9["in_9"] -.-> s12
    in10["in_10"] -.-> s22
    in11["in_11"] -.-> s32
    in12["in_12"] -.-> s03
    in13["in_13"] -.-> s13
    in14["in_14"] -.-> s23
    in15["in_15"] -.-> s33
```
