# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> List the **AES parameters** (block size, key sizes, number of rounds) for AES-128, AES-192, and AES-256. Mention the **standard** that defines AES.

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AES Parameters Table:**
>
> | Variant | Block Size | Key Size | Number of Rounds |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | AES-128 | 128 bits | 128 bits ($N_k = 4$) | 10 |
> | AES-192 | 128 bits | 192 bits ($N_k = 6$) | 12 |
> | AES-256 | 128 bits | 256 bits ($N_k = 8$) | 14 |
>
> **Standard:** AES is defined by **FIPS PUB 197** (Federal Information Processing Standards Publication 197), published by **NIST** in November 2001. It is based on the **Rijndael** algorithm designed by Joan Daemen and Vincent Rijmen.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

> What is the **State array** in AES? Show how the 16 input bytes are mapped into the State.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **State** is an intermediate $4 \times 4$ byte matrix used internally by AES. The 16 input bytes $in_0, in_1, \ldots, in_{15}$ are loaded **column-by-column** using the mapping:
> $$s[r, c] = in_{r + 4c}, \quad r, c \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$$
> The first column holds $in_0, in_1, in_2, in_3$; the second column holds $in_4, in_5, in_6, in_7$; and so on. After all rounds, the State is read out using $out_{r + 4c} = s[r, c]$ to produce the 128-bit ciphertext.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — *Choice 1*

### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Explain the **overall structure of AES encryption** with a neat block diagram. Mention the role of each round transformation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> AES encryption is a multi-round **substitution–permutation network** operating on a 128-bit block. The high-level structure consists of:
>
> 1. **Initial Round (Round 0):** The input block is converted to a $4 \times 4$ **State** and XORed with the first round key $K_0$ via **AddRoundKey**. This is the only key material applied before the main rounds.
>
> 2. **Rounds 1 to $N_r - 1$:** Each normal round applies four transformations in fixed order:
>    - **SubBytes** — A non-linear byte-wise substitution using a fixed 16×16 **S-box**. Each of the 16 State bytes is replaced independently. It provides **confusion** by destroying any algebraic structure between key and ciphertext.
>    - **ShiftRows** — A cyclic left shift of each row of the State by a row-dependent offset (0, 1, 2, 3 bytes). Row 0 is unchanged; row 3 is shifted by 3 bytes. This provides **inter-column diffusion**.
>    - **MixColumns** — Each column of the State is multiplied (over GF(2⁸) with the AES irreducible polynomial) by a fixed $4 \times 4$ matrix. This provides **intra-column diffusion**, ensuring every byte of the output column depends on every byte of the input column.
>    - **AddRoundKey** — The State is XORed with the next 128-bit round key $K_i$ derived from the original cipher key.
>
> 3. **Final Round (Round $N_r$):** Identical to a normal round **except that MixColumns is omitted**. The order is SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey. The output State is then read out column-by-column as the 128-bit ciphertext block.
>
> For AES-128, $N_r = 10$, giving **11 AddRoundKey operations** (1 initial + 9 inside rounds + 1 final) and **10 SubBytes/ShiftRows operations**, with **9 MixColumns** operations.

**(Block diagram — refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid schematic.)**

---

#### (b) Discuss the **key expansion algorithm** of AES-128. How many round keys are produced, and how is each word generated? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> The key expansion for **AES-128** converts the 128-bit (4-word) cipher key into **11 round keys of 4 words each**, i.e., **44 words** $W[0], W[1], \ldots, W[43]$ in total. The first 4 words $W[0]$ through $W[3]$ are simply the cipher key itself.
>
> For $i = 4, 5, \ldots, 43$, the words are generated using the **non-linear function $g$** when $i$ is a multiple of $N_k = 4$, and a simple XOR otherwise.
>
> **Step 1 — Function $g$:**
> The function $g$ takes a 4-byte word and:
> 1. Performs a 1-byte **cyclic left rotation** (`RotWord`).
> 2. Applies the AES S-box to each of the 4 bytes (`SubWord`).
> 3. XORs the result with the **round constant** $\text{Rcon}[i/4]$.
>
> **Step 2 — Round constants $\text{Rcon}[j]$:**
> $\text{Rcon}[j] = (\text{RC}[j], 0, 0, 0)$ where $\text{RC}[1] = \text{x}$ (i.e., the byte 0x02) and $\text{RC}[j] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1]$ in GF(2⁸). This gives $\text{Rcon} = [01, 02, 04, 08, 10, 20, 40, 80, 1B, 36]$ in hex.
>
> **Step 3 — Recurrence for AES-128:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> W[i] &= W[i - 4] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/4], & i \equiv 0 \pmod{4} \\
> W[i] &= W[i - 4] \oplus W[i-1], & \text{otherwise}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Round key grouping:**
> Round key $K_i$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, 10$ is the 4-word tuple $(W[4i], W[4i+1], W[4i+2], W[4i+3])$, giving 11 round keys of 128 bits each.
>
> **Step 5 — Why non-linear key schedule?**
> The use of SubWord inside $g$ ensures the round keys are not simple linear functions of the cipher key, which would otherwise make the cipher vulnerable to algebraic attacks.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

### **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

#### (a) Explain the **four transformations** in the AES round function. State the purpose of each. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> The AES round function (used in rounds 1 through $N_r - 1$) consists of four transformations, each operating on the $4 \times 4$ **State** matrix:
>
> 1. **SubBytes (Substitution Layer):**
>    - **Operation:** Each of the 16 bytes of the State is independently replaced by another byte using a fixed **$16 \times 16$ lookup table (S-box)**. The S-box is constructed from a multiplicative inverse over GF(2⁸) followed by an affine transformation.
>    - **Purpose:** Provides **non-linearity (confusion)**. Without non-linearity, the entire cipher would be a system of linear equations over GF(2) and trivially solvable.
>
> 2. **ShiftRows (Permutation Layer):**
>    - **Operation:** The four rows of the State are cyclically shifted left by **0, 1, 2, and 3 byte positions** respectively. Row 0 is unchanged.
>    - **Purpose:** Provides **inter-column diffusion**. After MixColumns, this ensures that bytes from different columns are mixed together in subsequent rounds.
>
> 3. **MixColumns (Diffusion Layer):**
>    - **Operation:** Each of the 4 columns of the State is treated as a 4-term polynomial over **GF(2⁸)** and multiplied by the fixed polynomial $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ modulo $x^4 + 1$. Equivalently, this is a $4 \times 4$ matrix multiplication with the matrix:
>    $$\begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix}$$
>    - **Purpose:** Provides **intra-column diffusion**. After this step, every output byte in a column is a function of **all 4 input bytes** of that column.
>
> 4. **AddRoundKey (Key Addition Layer):**
>    - **Operation:** A bitwise **XOR** of the State with a 128-bit round key $K_i$ generated by the key schedule.
>    - **Purpose:** Injects **key material** into the encryption. XOR is its own inverse, which simplifies the inverse cipher design.
>
> **Note:** In the **final round** (round $N_r$), **MixColumns is omitted** to make the inverse cipher's last operation match the encryption's last operation structurally.

---

#### (b) Describe the **AES decryption process**. How does it differ from encryption, and what is the **equivalent inverse cipher**? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Direct Inverse Cipher:**
> Decryption applies the inverse of each round transformation in **reverse order**:
> 1. **Initial step:** AddRoundKey with $K_{N_r}$ (XOR is its own inverse).
> 2. **Rounds $N_r - 1$ down to 1:**
>    - **InvShiftRows** — shift rows right by $(0, 1, 2, 3)$ bytes respectively.
>    - **InvSubBytes** — apply the inverse S-box to each byte.
>    - **AddRoundKey** — XOR with the round key $K_i$.
>    - **InvMixColumns** — multiply each column by the matrix with entries $\{0E, 0B, 0D, 09\}$.
> 3. **Final step:** InvShiftRows → InvSubBytes → AddRoundKey with $K_0$.
>
> The result is the original 128-bit plaintext block.
>
> **2. Difference from Encryption:**
> - The **order** of operations is reversed.
> - SubBytes is replaced by **InvSubBytes** (inverse S-box).
> - ShiftRows is replaced by **InvShiftRows** (shift in opposite direction).
> - MixColumns is replaced by **InvMixColumns** (different constant matrix).
> - AddRoundKey is identical (since XOR is self-inverse).
> - The round keys are used in **reverse order** ($K_{N_r}$ first, $K_0$ last).
>
> **3. Equivalent Inverse Cipher:**
> By commuting AddRoundKey with InvMixColumns (because both are linear over GF(2)), the inverse cipher can be rewritten so that **all AddRoundKey operations come first**, and a single **pre-whitening XOR** with a modified key schedule is performed. This is useful because:
> - It allows a **single key-schedule unit** in hardware.
> - It enables **on-the-fly decryption** without needing a separate inverse S-box lookup if the hardware stores both tables.
> - The same key stream is used for both encryption and decryption.
>
> In practice, the **straightforward inverse cipher** is more common in software because modern CPUs and AES-NI instructions natively support both encryption and decryption.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting MixColumns is omitted in the final round** — costs 1 mark if not mentioned in a structural question. Always explicitly state that the last round is SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey.
> 2. **Confusing $N_b$ (block words) with $N_k$ (key words)** — they are equal only for AES-128. For AES-192/256, $N_b = 4 \ne N_k$.
> 3. **Wrong number of round keys** — students often write $N_r$ instead of $N_r + 1$. There are $N_r + 1$ round keys because the **initial AddRoundKey** also requires a key.
> 4. **MixColumns matrix entries** — using $\{0E, 0B, 0D, 09\}$ for the **forward** cipher (these are for the **inverse**). Forward uses $\{02, 03, 01, 01\}$.
> 5. **State filling direction** — the State is filled **column-by-column**, not row-by-row. Reading the State in row-major order produces an incorrect ciphertext.
> 6. **Key schedule constants** — Rcon values use the **byte 0x02 = 1 in GF(2⁸)** as the seed, with the recurrence $\text{RC}[j] = 2 \cdot \text{RC}[j-1]$. AES-256 has an **additional** SubWord step at multiples of 4 (not 8).
> 7. **Equivalence of inverse cipher** — when asked about the equivalent inverse cipher, students forget to mention that **AddRoundKey commutes with InvMixColumns** because both are linear over GF(2).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — AES (Block Level):**
>
> - **AES** = Rijndael, FIPS 197, NIST 2001, SPN cipher.
> - **Block size** is always **128 bits = 4 words = 16 bytes**.
> - **Key sizes** are **128 / 192 / 256 bits** = 4 / 6 / 8 words.
> - **Rounds** are **10 / 12 / 14** via $N_r = 6 + \max(N_b, N_k)$.
> - **State** is a $4 \times 4$ byte matrix, filled **column-major**: $s[r,c] = in_{r + 4c}$.
> - **State reading** is also column-major: $out_{r + 4c} = s[r,c]$.
> - **Four round transformations:** SubBytes → ShiftRows → MixColumns → AddRoundKey.
> - **Final round omits MixColumns** — order is SubBytes → ShiftRows → AddRoundKey.
> - **SubBytes** = non-linear byte substitution (S-box) — provides **confusion**.
> - **ShiftRows** = cyclic left shift of row $r$ by $r$ bytes — **inter-column diffusion**.
> - **MixColumns** = GF(2⁸) matrix multiplication with fixed $\{02, 03, 01, 01\}$ — **intra-column diffusion**.
> - **AddRoundKey** = XOR with 128-bit round key — injects **key material**.
> - **Key schedule** produces $N_b(N_r + 1)$ words; **11 round keys** for AES-128.
> - **Key schedule uses** RotWord, SubWord, Rcon — non-linear for security.
> - **Rcon** = $(2^{j-1}, 0, 0, 0)$ in GF(2⁸) hex representation, $j = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$.
> - **Decryption** uses InvSubBytes, InvShiftRows, InvMixColumns, AddRoundKey in reverse order.
> - **InvMixColumns** uses matrix entries $\{0E, 0B, 0D, 09\}$.
> - **Equivalent inverse cipher** is obtained by commuting AddRoundKey with InvMixColumns (both linear over GF(2)).
> - AES is the **world's most widely used block cipher** — TLS, WPA3, disk encryption, IoT.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Ciphers: RC4
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stream Ciphers: RC4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RC4 (Rivest Cipher 4)** is a **symmetric-key, variable key-length stream cipher** designed by **Ron Rivest of RSA Security in 1987**. It maintains an internal **state** consisting of a 256-byte permutation array (called the **S-box** S[0…255]) and two 8-bit indices *i* and *j*, to generate a pseudo-random keystream that is XORed with the plaintext to produce the ciphertext.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **stream cipher** is a symmetric-key cipher in which the plaintext is encrypted one unit (typically a bit or a byte) at a time, by combining it with a pseudo-random **keystream** generated from a secret key. The encryption rule is given by:

$$C_n = P_n \oplus K_n$$

and decryption is symmetric because XOR is its own inverse:

$$P_n = C_n \oplus K_n$$

where $P_n$, $C_n$, and $K_n$ denote the $n$-th byte of the plaintext, ciphertext, and keystream respectively. **RC4** is the most widely taught and historically deployed software-oriented stream cipher, with a **state size of 2048 bits** (256 bytes) and a **key size ranging from 1 to 256 bytes** (i.e., 8 to 2048 bits).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** RC4 was historically a trade secret of RSA Security. In 1994, its source code was anonymously posted on the Cypherpunks mailing list, after which it became public-domain and was deployed in **SSL/TLS, WEP, WPA, and PDF encryption**. It has since been deprecated due to multiple statistical biases.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Magic Spice Rack")

Imagine a chef in a locked kitchen who possesses:

- **A secret recipe card** (the cryptographic *key* K).
- **A rack of 256 labeled spice jars** numbered 0 to 255 (the *S-box*), initially arranged in order: 0, 1, 2, …, 255.

**Phase 1 — KSA (Key Scheduling):** The chef shuffles the jars on the rack using a deterministic procedure that depends on the recipe. After 256 shuffle moves, the rack holds a seemingly random permutation of the jars. This is the "ready-to-use" spice rack.

**Phase 2 — PRGA (Pseudo-Random Generation):** For every dish (plaintext byte) the chef:
1. Moves a pointer *i* one jar to the right.
2. Uses another pointer *j* to point to a jar whose position is determined by the contents of the jars.
3. Swaps the two jars at positions *i* and *j*.
4. Picks the jar at position $\text{(S}[i] + \text{S}[j]) \bmod 256$ and adds the spice inside (one scoop = one byte) to the dish.

The "spice byte" is the **keystream byte**, and adding it via XOR is the encryption operation. The same procedure with the same key produces the **identical spice** on the other end, allowing the recipient to recover the original dish.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world deployment in KTU context:** When you connect to a college Wi-Fi router using **WPA-TKIP**, the per-packet key is fed into RC4. In **legacy HTTPS (TLS 1.0/1.1)**, RC4 was an allowed cipher suite. Modern KTU viva questions frequently contrast RC4 with **ChaCha20** and **AES-CTR**.

---

### 1.3 Why RC4 Belongs in Module 2 of Applied Cryptography

Module 2 of PCCCT501 is dedicated to **symmetric-key ciphers**. RC4 is a textbook example of how a stream cipher differs from block ciphers (DES, AES): it is **stateful, bit/byte-oriented, and uses XOR as the only nonlinear combiner**. Mastering RC4 lays the foundation for understanding modern stream ciphers like **Salsa20, ChaCha20, and Grain**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of RC4

RC4 has two functional blocks executed in sequence:

| Block | Full Name | Purpose | Input | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **KSA** | Key Scheduling Algorithm | Initialise and scramble the S-box using the secret key | Secret key K (L bytes) | Permuted S-box S[0…255] |
| **PRGA** | Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm | Generate the keystream one byte at a time | S-box S[0…255] (from KSA) | Keystream byte Kₙ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial point for 7-mark KTU questions:** KSA is run **once per session/message**, while PRGA is run **once per plaintext byte** (i.e., N times for an N-byte message). Decryption uses the **exact same** KSA + PRGA producing the identical keystream.

---

### 2.2 Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA) — Step-by-Step Logic

The KSA transforms the identity permutation $[0, 1, 2, \ldots, 255]$ into a key-dependent pseudo-random permutation in 256 iterations.

**Initialization (Line 1–2):**

$$S[i] = i, \quad \text{for } i = 0, 1, \ldots, 255$$

$$j = 0$$

**Scrambling Loop (Line 3–8), for $i = 0$ to $255$:**

$$j = \big(j + S[i] + K[i \bmod L]\big) \bmod 256$$

$$\text{swap}(S[i],\, S[j])$$

where $L$ is the key length in bytes, and $K[i \bmod L]$ selects the corresponding key byte cyclically.

**Why this works (the "How"):**
- After 256 iterations, **every byte of the key has touched the S-box multiple times** (approximately $256/L$ times each on average).
- The S-box becomes a function of the entire key.
- The variable $j$ acts as a **memory of all previous swaps**, so each new swap is influenced by the cumulative history of the key.

---

### 2.3 Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Initialization:**

$$i = 0, \quad j = 0$$

**Generation Loop, repeated for each keystream byte:**

$$i = (i + 1) \bmod 256$$

$$j = (j + S[i]) \bmod 256$$

$$\text{swap}(S[i],\, S[j])$$

$$t = \big(S[i] + S[j]\big) \bmod 256$$

$$K_{\text{out}} = S[t]$$

$$C_n = P_n \oplus K_{\text{out}}$$

**Why this works (the "How"):**
- *i* cycles deterministically (acts like a counter).
- *j* is data-dependent (acts like a chaotic walker through the S-box).
- The two-pointer walk guarantees that **every S-box entry is eventually sampled** (long period).
- $t$ is a non-linear combination (addition, not XOR) — this is the **only non-linear step** and the source of cryptographic strength.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | State size | $8 \times 256$ | bits (2048 bits) |
| 2 | S-box entries | $S[0], S[1], \ldots, S[255]$ | bytes (each 0–255) |
| 3 | Key length $L$ | $1 \le L \le 256$ | bytes (8–2048 bits) |
| 4 | KSA update | $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \bmod L]) \bmod 256$ | index, 0–255 |
| 5 | PRGA update | $i = (i+1) \bmod 256$; $j = (j+S[i]) \bmod 256$ | index, 0–255 |
| 6 | Index into S | $t = (S[i] + S[j]) \bmod 256$ | index, 0–255 |
| 7 | Keystream byte | $K_{\text{out}} = S[t]$ | byte, 0–255 |
| 8 | Encryption | $C = P \oplus K_{\text{out}}$ | byte-wise XOR |
| 9 | Decryption | $P = C \oplus K_{\text{out}}$ | byte-wise XOR (same key) |
| 10 | XOR property | $x \oplus y \oplus y = x$ | involution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU trap:** Students often confuse $K[i]$ (the secret key byte) with $K_{\text{out}}$ (the generated keystream byte). The secret key is denoted by $K$ in the KSA; the keystream is the **output** of the PRGA. Make this distinction explicit in every answer.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of RC4

- **Wireless security (legacy):** WEP and WPA-TKIP used RC4-40 and RC4-128 for per-packet encryption.
- **Secure web traffic (legacy):** SSL 3.0 / TLS 1.0–1.1 allowed RC4 cipher suites (now forbidden by RFC 7465 in 2015).
- **Document security:** PDF 1.5 and earlier used RC4 with a 40-bit or 128-bit key.
- **Software performance benchmark:** RC4 is roughly **2× faster than AES** in pure software on legacy CPUs, which is why it remained popular in resource-constrained devices (early 2000s IoT, mobile).
- **Pedagogical value:** It is the simplest complete cipher to teach because the entire algorithm is fewer than 20 lines of pseudocode.

> [!WARNING]
> **Modern security status:** RC4 is **cryptographically broken**. The Mantin-Shamir bias (2001), Klein's attack (2005), and AlFardan et al.'s plaintext-recovery attack (2013) demonstrated that statistical biases in the RC4 keystream can be exploited. **KTU 2024 examiners may deduct marks** if a student recommends RC4 for a *new* design without flagging its deprecation. Always pair it with the warning: *"deprecated by IETF in 2015"*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example convention:** To make manual KTU calculations tractable on paper, the following worked example uses a **truncated RC4 variant with N = 8** (i.e., all mod-256 operations become mod-8). This is the standard KTU exam simplification. The full algorithm uses **N = 256**.

---

### 3.1 Problem Statement

Given:
- Secret key $K = [3,\, 1,\, 4]$, so $L = 3$ bytes.
- Initial S-box $S = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]$ (using $N = 8$).
- Initial value $j = 0$.

**Task (a):** Run the **KSA** and produce the final permutation $S'$.
**Task (b):** Run the **PRGA** for 3 iterations and produce the first 3 keystream bytes.
**Task (c):** Encrypt the plaintext $P = [\text{0x61}, \text{0x62}, \text{0x63}]$ (i.e., "abc") and decrypt back to verify.

---

### 3.2 Task (a) — Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA) Derivation

We use the recurrence:

$$j_{i+1} = \big(j_i + S[i] + K[i \bmod L]\big) \bmod N, \quad N = 8, \quad L = 3$$

followed by the swap $\text{swap}(S[i],\, S[j_{i+1}])$.

**Iteration $i = 0$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (0 + S[0] + K[0 \bmod 3]) \bmod 8 = (0 + 0 + 3) \bmod 8 = 3$.
- Swap $S[0]$ and $S[3]$: $S = [\mathbf{3}, 1, 2, \mathbf{0}, 4, 5, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 1$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (3 + S[1] + K[1 \bmod 3]) \bmod 8 = (3 + 1 + 1) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Swap $S[1]$ and $S[5]$: $S = [3, \mathbf{5}, 2, 0, 4, \mathbf{1}, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 2$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (5 + S[2] + K[2 \bmod 3]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 2 + 4) \bmod 8 = 11 \bmod 8 = 3$.
- Swap $S[2]$ and $S[3]$: $S = [3, 5, \mathbf{0}, \mathbf{2}, 4, 1, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 3$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (3 + S[3] + K[0]) \bmod 8 = (3 + 2 + 3) \bmod 8 = 8 \bmod 8 = 0$.
- Swap $S[3]$ and $S[0]$: $S = [\mathbf{2}, 5, 0, \mathbf{3}, 4, 1, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 4$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (0 + S[4] + K[1]) \bmod 8 = (0 + 4 + 1) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Swap $S[4]$ and $S[5]$: $S = [2, 5, 0, 3, \mathbf{1}, \mathbf{4}, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 5$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (5 + S[5] + K[2]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 4 + 4) \bmod 8 = 13 \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Swap $S[5]$ and $S[5]$: $S$ unchanged $\rightarrow S = [2, 5, 0, 3, 1, 4, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 6$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (5 + S[6] + K[0]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 6 + 3) \bmod 8 = 14 \bmod 8 = 6$.
- Swap $S[6]$ and $S[6]$: $S$ unchanged $\rightarrow S = [2, 5, 0, 3, 1, 4, 6, 7]$.

**Iteration $i = 7$:**
- Compute $j$: $j = (6 + S[7] + K[1]) \bmod 8 = (6 + 7 + 1) \bmod 8 = 14 \bmod 8 = 6$.
- Swap $S[7]$ and $S[6]$: $S = [2, 5, 0, 3, 1, 4, \mathbf{7}, \mathbf{6}]$.

**Final S-box after KSA:**

$$S' = [2,\, 5,\, 0,\, 3,\, 1,\, 4,\, 7,\, 6]$$

---

### 3.3 Task (b) — PRGA Derivation (3 Keystream Bytes)

Initialise: $i = 0$, $j = 0$, $S = [2, 5, 0, 3, 1, 4, 7, 6]$.

**Iteration 1:**
- $i = (0 + 1) \bmod 8 = 1$.
- $j = (0 + S[1]) \bmod 8 = (0 + 5) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Swap $S[1]$ and $S[5]$: $S = [2, \mathbf{4}, 0, 3, 1, \mathbf{5}, 7, 6]$.
- $t = (S[1] + S[5]) \bmod 8 = (4 + 5) \bmod 8 = 9 \bmod 8 = 1$.
- Keystream byte $K_1 = S[1] = 4$.

**Iteration 2:**
- $i = (1 + 1) \bmod 8 = 2$.
- $j = (5 + S[2]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 0) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Swap $S[2]$ and $S[5]$: $S = [2, 4, \mathbf{5}, 3, 1, \mathbf{0}, 7, 6]$.
- $t = (S[2] + S[5]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 0) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Keystream byte $K_2 = S[5] = 0$.

**Iteration 3:**
- $i = (2 + 1) \bmod 8 = 3$.
- $j = (5 + S[3]) \bmod 8 = (5 + 3) \bmod 8 = 8 \bmod 8 = 0$.
- Swap $S[3]$ and $S[0]$: $S = [\mathbf{3}, 4, 5, \mathbf{2}, 1, 0, 7, 6]$.
- $t = (S[3] + S[0]) \bmod 8 = (2 + 3) \bmod 8 = 5$.
- Keystream byte $K_3 = S[5] = 0$.

**Generated keystream:** $K_1 K_2 K_3 = 4, 0, 0$.

---

### 3.4 Task (c) — Encryption and Decryption of "abc"

Plaintext bytes (ASCII): $P = [\text{0x61}, \text{0x62}, \text{0x63}] = [97, 98, 99]$.

$$C_1 = 97 \oplus 4 = 0110\,0001 \oplus 0000\,0100 = 0110\,0101 = 101$$

$$C_2 = 98 \oplus 0 = 98$$

$$C_3 = 99 \oplus 0 = 99$$

**Ciphertext:** $C = [101, 98, 99]$ (i.e., "ebc" in ASCII, with non-printable first char).

**Decryption check (the recipient regenerates the *same* keystream 4, 0, 0):**

$$P_1 = 101 \oplus 4 = 0110\,0101 \oplus 0000\,0100 = 0110\,0001 = 97 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$P_2 = 98 \oplus 0 = 98 \;\;\checkmark$$

$$P_3 = 99 \oplus 0 = 99 \;\;\checkmark$$

**Recovered plaintext:** $P = [97, 98, 99] = \text{"abc"}$. 

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Operation | Formula | State After Step |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| KSA i=0 | $j=3$, swap S[0],S[3] | $(0+0+3)\bmod 8$ | $[3,1,2,0,4,5,6,7]$ |
| KSA i=1 | $j=5$, swap S[1],S[5] | $(3+1+1)\bmod 8$ | $[3,5,2,0,4,1,6,7]$ |
| KSA i=2 | $j=3$, swap S[2],S[3] | $(5+2+4)\bmod 8$ | $[3,5,0,2,4,1,6,7]$ |
| KSA i=3 | $j=0$, swap S[3],S[0] | $(3+2+3)\bmod 8$ | $[2,5,0,3,4,1,6,7]$ |
| KSA i=4 | $j=5$, swap S[4],S[5] | $(0+4+1)\bmod 8$ | $[2,5,0,3,1,4,6,7]$ |
| KSA i=5 | $j=5$, no swap (self) | $(5+4+4)\bmod 8$ | unchanged |
| KSA i=6 | $j=6$, no swap (self) | $(5+6+3)\bmod 8$ | unchanged |
| KSA i=7 | $j=6$, swap S[7],S[6] | $(6+7+1)\bmod 8$ | $[2,5,0,3,1,4,7,6]$ |
| PRGA #1 | $t=1, K_1=4$ | $(4+5)\bmod 8$ | $S=[2,4,0,3,1,5,7,6]$ |
| PRGA #2 | $t=5, K_2=0$ | $(5+0)\bmod 8$ | $S=[2,4,5,3,1,0,7,6]$ |
| PRGA #3 | $t=5, K_3=0$ | $(2+3)\bmod 8$ | $S=[3,4,5,2,1,0,7,6]$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RC4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Encryption & Decryption)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Secret Key K, L bytes] --> B[KSA Key Scheduling Algorithm]
    B --> C[Permuted S-box S 0 to 255]
    C --> D[PRGA Pseudo Random Generator]
    D --> E[Keystream Byte Kout]
    P[Plaintext Byte Pn] --> X[XOR Combiner]
    E --> X
    X --> Cn[Ciphertext Byte Cn]
    Cn2[Received Ciphertext Cn] --> X2[XOR Combiner]
    E2[Regenerated Keystream Kout] --> X2
    X2 --> Pn2[Recovered Plaintext Pn]
    C -. same key, same S .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two XOR combiners?** The same keystream is reused at the receiver side; the algorithm is **symmetric** (encryption = decryption). The single difference is the *direction* of data flow: sender XORs plaintext, receiver XORs ciphertext.

---

### 4.2 KSA Internal Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start KSA]) --> init1[Initialise S i equals i for i equals 0 to 255]
    init1 --> init2[Set j equals 0]
    init2 --> loop1{i equals 0 to 255}
    loop1 --> compute[j equals j plus S i plus K i mod L mod 256]
    compute --> swap1[Swap S i and S j]
    swap1 --> loop1
    loop1 -->|Done| end1([Final S-box ready for PRGA])
```

---

### 4.3 PRGA Internal Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start2([Start PRGA, i equals 0, j equals 0]) --> genmore{More plaintext bytes?}
    genmore -->|Yes| upi[i equals i plus 1 mod 256]
    upi --> upj[j equals j plus S i mod 256]
    upj --> swap2[Swap S i and S j]
    swap2 --> tcalc[t equals S i plus S j mod 256]
    tcalc --> kout[Keystream byte equals S t]
    kout --> xorit[C n equals P n XOR keystream]
    xorit --> emit[Emit ciphertext byte]
    emit --> genmore
    genmore -->|No| stop2([Session complete])
```

---

### 4.4 Data Flow Between Sender and Receiver

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender
    participant Channel
    participant Receiver
    Sender->>Sender: KSA with shared key K
    Sender->>Sender: PRGA generates keystream Kout
    Sender->>Channel: Ciphertext C equals P XOR Kout
    Channel->>Receiver: Ciphertext C
    Receiver->>Receiver: KSA with same key K
    Receiver->>Receiver: PRGA regenerates identical Kout
    Receiver->>Receiver: P equals C XOR Kout
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a stream cipher. List any two examples of stream ciphers and state the size of the S-box used in RC4.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A stream cipher is a symmetric-key encryption algorithm that encrypts plaintext one bit or one byte at a time by combining it with a pseudo-random keystream generated from a secret key. Mathematically, $C_n = P_n \oplus K_n$, and decryption is $P_n = C_n \oplus K_n$ using the same keystream.
- **Examples (0.5 mark):** RC4, ChaCha20 (or Salsa20, A5/1).
- **S-box size (0.5 mark):** 256 bytes, indexed 0 to 255.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between the Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA) and the Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA) of RC4.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | KSA | PRGA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Purpose | Initialise and scramble S-box using the key | Generate keystream bytes |
| Runs | Once per session (256 iterations) | Once per output byte |
| Uses key? | Yes — $K[i \bmod L]$ is added to $j$ | No — only the scrambled S-box is used |
| Output | Permuted S-box S[0…255] | Keystream byte $K_{\text{out}}$ |

(1 mark per correct comparison pair; 3 marks total for full table.)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting modular arithmetic** — every index update in KSA and PRGA is mod 256 (or mod $N$ in exam simplifications). Lose 1–2 marks if you write a plain sum.
> 2. **Mixing $K[i]$ (key) with $K_{\text{out}}$ (keystream).** Always label clearly.
> 3. **Not drawing the S-box snapshot after every swap.** Examiners award step-marks only when state changes are visible; a "black box" answer gets zero.
> 4. **Writing $i$ and $j$ as integers without "mod"** in $i = (i+1) \bmod 256$ is a 1-mark deduction in valuation key.
> 5. **For 14-mark questions, missing the diagram** is a near-certain 2-mark deduction.

---

#### **PART B – QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot 1]**

**(a)** Explain the Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA) of RC4 with a neat flowchart. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider RC4 with a 3-byte secret key $K = [4, 1, 6]$ and initial S-box $S = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7]$ (using $N = 8$ for simplicity). Perform the KSA and show the final state of the S-box. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) KSA Explanation (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) RC4 begins with the **identity permutation** $S[i] = i$ for $i = 0$ to $255$.
- (1 mark) The variable $j$ is initialised to $0$.
- (2 marks) The loop runs 256 times; in each iteration $i$, compute $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \bmod L]) \bmod 256$.
- (1 mark) Swap $S[i]$ and $S[j]$.
- (1 mark) Draw the flowchart (see SECTION 4.2 of these notes).
- (1 mark) Highlight that KSA is run **once per message**, and that $L$ is the key length in bytes.

**(b) Worked KSA Calculation (7 marks):**

Initial: $S = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7]$, $j = 0$, $L = 3$, $N = 8$.

| i | $j$ calculation | $j$ value | Swap | Resulting S |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $(0 + 0 + 4) \bmod 8$ | 4 | S[0]↔S[4] | [4,1,2,3,0,5,6,7] |
| 1 | $(4 + 1 + 1) \bmod 8$ | 6 | S[1]↔S[6] | [4,6,2,3,0,5,1,7] |
| 2 | $(6 + 2 + 6) \bmod 8$ | 6 | S[2]↔S[6] | [4,6,1,3,0,5,2,7] |
| 3 | $(6 + 3 + 4) \bmod 8$ | 5 | S[3]↔S[5] | [4,6,1,5,0,3,2,7] |
| 4 | $(5 + 0 + 1) \bmod 8$ | 6 | S[4]↔S[6] | [4,6,1,5,2,3,0,7] |
| 5 | $(6 + 3 + 6) \bmod 8$ | 7 | S[5]↔S[7] | [4,6,1,5,2,7,0,3] |
| 6 | $(7 + 0 + 4) \bmod 8$ | 3 | S[6]↔S[3] | [4,6,1,0,2,7,5,3] |
| 7 | $(3 + 3 + 1) \bmod 8$ | 7 | S[7]↔S[7] | self-swap, no change → [4,6,1,0,2,7,5,3] |

**Final S-box:** $S' = [4, 6, 1, 0, 2, 7, 5, 3]$. (Full marks for showing all 8 rows of state evolution with $j$ values.)

---

#### **PART B – QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe the Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA) of RC4. Illustrate with a flowchart. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the major strengths and weaknesses of RC4. Why has it been deprecated in modern protocols? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PRGA Description (7 marks):**
- (1 mark) Initialise $i = 0$, $j = 0$.
- (1 mark) Per byte: $i = (i+1) \bmod 256$.
- (1 mark) Per byte: $j = (j + S[i]) \bmod 256$.
- (1 mark) Swap $S[i]$ and $S[j]$.
- (1 mark) Compute index $t = (S[i] + S[j]) \bmod 256$.
- (1 mark) Output keystream byte $K_{\text{out}} = S[t]$.
- (1 mark) Encryption: $C = P \oplus K_{\text{out}}$; draw the flowchart from SECTION 4.3.

**(b) Strengths and Weaknesses (7 marks):**

**Strengths (3 marks):**
1. **Simplicity** — fewer than 20 lines of pseudocode; ideal for teaching and implementation.
2. **Speed** — purely byte-level operations; historically 2× faster than AES in software.
3. **Variable key length** — 1 to 256 bytes, allowing trade-offs between security and performance.
4. **No padding required** — stream ciphers work on arbitrary-length plaintexts natively.

**Weaknesses (3 marks):**
1. **Statistical biases** — second byte of keystream is biased toward 0 (Mantin-Shamir, 2001); multiple biases in initial keystream bytes allow plaintext recovery (AlFardan et al., 2013).
2. **Weak keys** — certain key patterns (e.g., $K = [0, 0, \ldots, 0]$) produce predictable S-boxes (Klein's attack, 2005).
3. **No authentication** — RC4 is encryption-only; bit-flipping attacks on the ciphertext are undetectable.

**Deprecation (1 mark):**
RC4 was prohibited by IETF in **RFC 7465 (2015)** for use in TLS, due to practical BEAST-like and plaintext-recovery attacks. It is replaced by **ChaCha20-Poly1305** and **AES-GCM** in modern protocols.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Revision — Stream Ciphers: RC4**

- ☐ **RC4 = Rivest Cipher 4 (1987, public 1994)**, by Ron Rivest of RSA Security.
- ☐ **Two phases:** KSA (once) + PRGA (per byte). Always mention both in definitions.
- ☐ **State:** S-box S[0…255] of 256 bytes + two 8-bit counters $i$ and $j$.
- ☐ **Key length:** 1 to 256 bytes (8 to 2048 bits); typical deployment was 40 or 128 bits.
- ☐ **Encryption/Decryption:** both are $C = P \oplus K_{\text{out}}$ and $P = C \oplus K_{\text{out}}$ — same keystream regenerates plaintext.
- ☐ **KSA recurrence (memorise this line):** $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \bmod L]) \bmod 256$.
- ☐ **PRGA sequence (memorise in order):** $i\!\uparrow$ → $j\!\uparrow$ → swap → $t$ → output $S[t]$.
- ☐ **Only non-linearity:** the addition $(S[i] + S[j])$ mod 256 in $t$; everything else is linear/XOR.
- ☐ **Always show state snapshots** at every iteration of KSA in exam answers — partial credit depends on it.
- ☐ **RC4 is deprecated (RFC 7465, 2015);** mention this if the question asks about "modern usage".
- ☐ **Successors to know:** ChaCha20 (RFC 8439) and AES-CTR for software; both are nonce-based.
- ☐ **Common confusion to avoid:** $K[i]$ (input secret key byte, indexed by $i \bmod L$) **≠** $K_{\text{out}}$ (keystream byte from PRGA).
- ☐ **Examiners love:** the $N=8$ truncated worked example; practice at least one full KSA + PRGA + XOR combination before the exam.
- ☐ **Watch the "mod"**: every $i$, $j$, and $t$ update is mod 256 (or mod $N$ in truncated examples). Forgetting it is the #1 reason students lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Operation: ECB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Modes of Operation: ECB # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Electronic Codebook (ECB) Mode** is the most fundamental and straightforward mode of operation for a block cipher algorithm. In ECB mode, the plaintext message is divided into fixed-size blocks (typically **64 bits** for DES or **128 bits** for AES), and each block is encrypted **independently** using the same secret key. The result is that identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks under the same key, creating a direct one-to-one mapping — hence the term "codebook." Formally, for a block cipher $E$ with key $K$ and plaintext blocks $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$, the ECB encryption process is defined as: $$C_i = E_K(P_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ The corresponding decryption operation is: $$P_i = D_K(C_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ where $D_K$ is the inverse block cipher function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ECB mode is the *baseline* mode from which all other advanced modes (CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR) are derived. The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to understand **why** ECB is rarely used in practice despite its simplicity. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a librarian storing confidential documents. You have a giant **codebook** — a register where for every possible 64-bit/128-bit input "code," there is a unique secret "code" output. Whenever you receive a document page, you look it up in the codebook and write down the secret code. Later, when you get the coded pages, you reverse-lookup the codebook to get the originals. **The catch:** If two document pages happen to have the *exact same text*, the librarian writes the *exact same secret code* for both. A spy watching the shelf can instantly know which coded pages contain duplicate content — even without breaking the code! This is precisely why ECB fails for non-random plaintext. ## 1.3 Key Properties of ECB Mode > [!NOTE] > **Core Properties — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** > 1. **Deterministic:** Same plaintext block + same key → always same ciphertext block. > 2. **Independent:** Each block is encrypted without any dependency on previous blocks. > 3. **Parallelizable:** Encryption and decryption of all blocks can occur simultaneously. > 4. **No Error Propagation:** A single-bit error in ciphertext $C_i$ affects only block $P_i$ during decryption (limited to that block). > 5. **Pattern-Preserving:** Original plaintext patterns remain visible in ciphertext — this is its fatal flaw. ## 1.4 Block Size and Padding - Standard block size $b$ = **64 bits** (DES, 3DES) or **128 bits** (AES). - If the final plaintext block is shorter than $b$ bits, **padding** is required. - Common padding schemes: **PKCS#7**, **ANSI X.923**, **Zero Padding**. > [!WARNING] > KTU students often confuse **block size** with **key size**. For DES, block size is 64 bits but key is only 56 effective bits. For AES-128, both block and key are 128 bits — *but this is a coincidence*, not a rule. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic The ECB mode operates in a strictly sequential, block-by-block manner. Below is the explicit procedural breakdown: ### Encryption Workflow: 1. **Input Reception:** The sender receives plaintext $P$ of arbitrary length. 2. **Block Segmentation:** $P$ is split into $n$ blocks of size $b$ bits: $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$. 3. **Padding Application:** If $\vert P \vert \mod b \neq 0$, append padding to the last block to make it exactly $b$ bits. 4. **Independent Encryption:** For each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, compute $C_i = E_K(P_i)$. 5. **Output Assembly:** Concatenate ciphertext blocks: $C = C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \cdots \parallel C_n$. 6. **Transmission:** Send $C$ to the receiver over an insecure channel. ### Decryption Workflow: 1. **Input Reception:** The receiver obtains ciphertext $C$. 2. **Block Segmentation:** $C$ is split into $n$ blocks of size $b$ bits. 3. **Independent Decryption:** For each $i$, compute $P_i = D_K(C_i)$. 4. **Padding Removal:** Strip padding from the final plaintext block. 5. **Output Assembly:** Concatenate to recover original plaintext $P$. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Mechanics **Why is ECB called "Electronic Codebook"?** The metaphor arises because the encryption function $E_K$ acts like a massive lookup table (codebook). For every possible input block of $b$ bits, there is exactly one ciphertext output. With a 64-bit block, this theoretical codebook would contain $2^{64}$ entries — a number so vast it cannot exist in physical memory, but the conceptual model is valid. **Why is determinism a problem?** An adversary performing **frequency analysis** or **pattern recognition** on the ciphertext can infer plaintext repetition. For example, in an image encrypted with ECB, large regions of identical color produce identical ciphertext blocks, allowing the original image's silhouette to remain visible. This was famously demonstrated in the **ECB-encrypted Tux penguin image**, where the outline of the penguin remained discernible despite encryption. **Why does ECB permit parallelization?** Because $C_i$ depends *only* on $P_i$ and $K$, and there is no chaining (no XOR with previous ciphertext, no counter, no IV), multiple cores/processors can encrypt different blocks simultaneously. This is ECB's one major engineering advantage over CBC. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol / Equation | Value / Unit | Notes | |-----------|-------------------|--------------|-------| | Block Size | $b$ | **64 bits (DES)** or **128 bits (AES)** | Fixed per algorithm | | Key Size | $k$ | 56 / 112 / 128 / 192 / 256 bits | Algorithm-dependent | | Number of Blocks | $n = \lceil \vert P \vert / b \rceil$ | Integer | Apply padding if needed | | Encryption | $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ | Block-wise | Independent for each $i$ | | Decryption | $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ | Block-wise | Independent for each $i$ | | Error Propagation | 1 block affected | Localized | A bit-flip in $C_i$ corrupts only $P_i$ | | Parallelizable? | **Yes** | Full parallelism | Both encryption and decryption | | IV Required? | **No** | Deterministic | Same key + same plaintext → same ciphertext | | Padding Required? | **Yes** (if not block-aligned) | Per scheme | PKCS#7 recommended | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Equation for KTU:** Whenever asked to demonstrate ECB, always write the pair $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ and $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ — this is the canonical formulation examiners expect. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Despite its cryptographic weaknesses, ECB mode finds niche applications: - **Random Data Encryption:** When the plaintext is uniformly random (e.g., session keys, random nonces), patterns cannot be exploited. - **Single-Block Messages:** For encrypting one block (e.g., a 128-bit AES key), ECB is mathematically equivalent to using CBC with a zero IV — and is simpler. - **Building Block for Other Modes:** ECB serves as the foundational primitive for more secure modes (e.g., CBC wraps ECB with XOR-chaining). - **Parallel Processing Pipelines:** In hardware implementations where speed dominates security (e.g., disk encryption benchmarks), ECB's parallelizability is exploited. - **Educational Reference:** Standardized in **NIST SP 800-38A** as the reference definition against which all other modes are compared. ## 2.5 Security Analysis Summary | Threat | ECB Vulnerability | |--------|-------------------| | Pattern Preservation | **High** — identical plaintext blocks → identical ciphertext | | Known-Plaintext Attack | **High** — attacker builds codebook via $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ | | Chosen-Plaintext Attack | **High** — same as above with attacker control | | Replay Attack | **High** — blocks can be reordered/cut-pasted | | Bit-Flipping Attack | **Localized** — only affects single block decryption | | Statistical Attack | **High** — frequency of blocks preserved | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Mnemonic:** ECB = **"Easy But Careless"** — easy to implement, but careless for real-world security. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Formal Mathematical Derivation of ECB Mode ### Derivation: From Plaintext to Ciphertext **Given:** - A block cipher $E_K : \{0,1\}^b \to \{0,1\}^b$ (a bijective function parameterized by key $K$). - A plaintext message $P$ of length $\vert P \vert$ bits. **Step 1: Block Decomposition** We decompose $P$ into $n$ blocks of size $b$ bits each: $$\begin{aligned} P &= P_1 \parallel P_2 \parallel \cdots \parallel P_n \\ \text{where } \vert P_i \vert &= b \text{ for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n-1 \\ \text{and } \vert P_n \vert &\leq b \text{ (with padding applied to make exactly } b \text{ bits)} \end{aligned}$$ **Explanation:** The $\parallel$ operator denotes bitwise concatenation. If the last block is short, padding fills it to exactly $b$ bits. **Step 2: Apply Encryption Function** For each block $i$, apply $E_K$ independently: $$\begin{aligned} C_1 &= E_K(P_1) \\ C_2 &= E_K(P_2) \\ &\vdots \\ C_n &= E_K(P_n) \end{aligned}$$ **Explanation:** Each plaintext block $P_i$ is the *only* input to the encryption function. There is no IV, no counter, and no dependency on $C_{i-1}$. **Step 3: Ciphertext Assembly** Concatenate the ciphertext blocks to form the final ciphertext $C$: $$C = C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \cdots \parallel C_n$$ **Step 4: Decryption** The receiver applies the inverse function $D_K = E_K^{-1}$ to each ciphertext block: $$\begin{aligned} P_1 &= D_K(C_1) \\ P_2 &= D_K(C_2) \\ &\vdots \\ P_n &= D_K(C_n) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Padding Removal** After decryption, the padding on $P_n$ is stripped to recover the original message. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Toy Block Cipher) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Style:** Examiners often pose "demonstrate ECB mode with a toy example." This is the exact pattern they expect. **Toy Cipher Specification:** Let $E_K(x) = (x + K) \mod 26$ (a Caesar-like cipher on letters A–Z, where A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25). Let $K = 7$. **Plaintext:** $P = \text{"HELLO"}$ (split into 2 blocks of 2 characters each, since $b = 2$ letters = 16 bits analog). **Block Mapping (A=0):** - $\text{H} = 7$, $\text{E} = 4$, $\text{L} = 11$, $\text{L} = 11$, $\text{O} = 14$ **Step-by-Step Encryption:** **Block 1: $P_1 = \text{"HE"}$ → numerical $(7, 4)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_1[\text{first letter}] &= (7 + 7) \mod 26 = 14 \mod 26 = 14 \rightarrow \text{O} \\ C_1[\text{second letter}] &= (4 + 7) \mod 26 = 11 \mod 26 = 11 \rightarrow \text{L} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_1 = \text{"OL"}$. **Block 2: $P_2 = \text{"LL"}$ → numerical $(11, 11)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_2[\text{first letter}] &= (11 + 7) \mod 26 = 18 \mod 26 = 18 \rightarrow \text{S} \\ C_2[\text{second letter}] &= (11 + 7) \mod 26 = 18 \mod 26 = 18 \rightarrow \text{S} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_2 = \text{"SS"}$. **Block 3: $P_3 = \text{"O\_"}$ (assume padded with 'A' to fill block) → numerical $(14, 0)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_3[\text{first letter}] &= (14 + 7) \mod 26 = 21 \mod 26 = 21 \rightarrow \text{V} \\ C_3[\text{second letter}] &= (0 + 7) \mod 26 = 7 \mod 26 = 7 \rightarrow \text{H} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_3 = \text{"VH"}$. **Final Ciphertext:** $C = \text{"OLSSVH"}$ **Decryption Verification:** $D_K(y) = (y - K) \mod 26$ - $D_K(\text{OL}) = (14-7, 11-7) \mod 26 = (7, 4) = \text{HE}$ ✓ - $D_K(\text{SS}) = (18-7, 18-7) \mod 26 = (11, 11) = \text{LL}$ ✓ - $D_K(\text{VH}) = (21-7, 7-7) \mod 26 = (14, 0) = \text{OA}$ ✓ (after removing padding 'A') **Key Observation for KTU:** Notice that $P_2 = \text{"LL"}$ produced $C_2 = \text{"SS"}$. If "LL" appeared again later in the message, it would also produce "SS" — the determinism flaw is now visible in this example. --- ## 3.3 Pattern Leakage Analytical Matrix Below is an analytical table demonstrating ECB's pattern leak with a structured plaintext: | Block Index $i$ | Plaintext $P_i$ | Ciphertext $C_i$ | Observation | |-----------------|-----------------|------------------|-------------| | 1 | $\text{HE}$ | $\text{OL}$ | Unique | | 2 | $\text{LL}$ | $\text{SS}$ | Unique | | 3 | $\text{LL}$ | $\text{SS}$ | **Duplicate of Block 2!** | | 4 | $\text{OA}$ | $\text{VH}$ | Unique | | 5 | $\text{HE}$ | $\text{OL}$ | **Duplicate of Block 1!** | **Adversary's Inference:** An attacker who intercepts $C = \text{OLSSSSVHHOL}$ can immediately deduce that: - Blocks 2, 3, and 4 (or wherever "SS" appears) contained identical plaintext. - Blocks 1 and 5 contained identical plaintext. Without ever breaking the cipher, the attacker has learned *structural* information about the message. --- ## 3.4 Error Propagation Analysis **Scenario:** A 1-bit error occurs in ciphertext block $C_j$ during transmission (e.g., bit flip due to channel noise). $$\begin{aligned} \text{Received: } C' &= C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \cdots \parallel C'_j \parallel \cdots \parallel C_n \\ \text{where } C'_j &\neq C_j \text{ in at least 1 bit} \end{aligned}$$ **Decryption Result:** $$\begin{aligned} P_i &= D_K(C_i) \quad \text{for } i \neq j \quad \text{(correctly recovered)} \\ P'_j &= D_K(C'_j) \quad \text{(completely garbled — random-looking block)} \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** A single-bit error in $C_j$ causes the entire block $P_j$ to decrypt incorrectly (since block ciphers have the **avalanche effect** — small input change → ~50% output bits flip). However, all other blocks decrypt correctly. This is the **localized error propagation** property of ECB. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When comparing ECB with CBC in error propagation, remember: ECB = localized error, CBC = localized error **plus** garbled $P_{j+1}$ due to XOR-chaining. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ECB Encryption Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Plaintext Block P1"] --> E1["E_K Encryption Function"] P2["Plaintext Block P2"] --> E2["E_K Encryption Function"] P3["Plaintext Block P3"] --> E3["E_K Encryption Function"] Pn["Plaintext Block Pn"] --> En["E_K Encryption Function"] E1 --> C1["Ciphertext Block C1"] E2 --> C2["Ciphertext Block C2"] E3 --> C3["Ciphertext Block C3"] En --> Cn["Ciphertext Block Cn"] C1 --> OUT["Concatenated Ciphertext C"] C2 --> OUT C3 --> OUT Cn --> OUT KEY["Secret Key K"] -.-> E1 KEY -.-> E2 KEY -.-> E3 KEY -.-> En ``` **Diagram Explanation:** This schematic shows the four defining characteristics of ECB: 1. **Same Key:** A single key $K$ feeds into every encryption function (dashed lines indicate parameter input). 2. **Independent Blocks:** Each $P_i$ enters its own $E_K$ box with no cross-block connections. 3. **No IV:** There is no Initialization Vector input — purely deterministic. 4. **Parallelizable:** The four encryption boxes can operate simultaneously on multi-core hardware. --- ## 4.2 ECB Decryption Architecture (Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR C1d["Ciphertext Block C1"] --> D1["D_K Decryption Function"] C2d["Ciphertext Block C2"] --> D2["D_K Decryption Function"] C3d["Ciphertext Block C3"] --> D3["D_K Decryption Function"] Cnd["Ciphertext Block Cn"] --> Dn["D_K Decryption Function"] D1 --> P1d["Plaintext Block P1"] D2 --> P2d["Plaintext Block P2"] D3 --> P3d["Plaintext Block P3"] Dn --> Pnd["Plaintext Block Pn"] P1d --> OUTd["Concatenated Plaintext P"] P2d --> OUTd P3d --> OUTd Pnd --> OUTd KEYd["Secret Key K"] -.-> D1 KEYd -.-> D2 KEYd -.-> D3 KEYd -.-> Dn ``` **Diagram Explanation:** Decryption mirrors encryption structure exactly. Each $C_i$ is fed to its own $D_K$ function with the same shared key $K$. The decrypted blocks are concatenated to form the original plaintext. --- ## 4.3 ECB Mode Functional Pipeline (End-to-End) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Original Plaintext P"] --> B["Block Segmentation
b bits per block"] B --> C{"Last Block
Complete?"} C -- No --> D["Apply Padding
PKCS#7 / ANSI X.923"] C -- Yes --> E["Proceed to Encryption"] D --> E E --> F["Apply E_K to Each Block
Independently and in Parallel"] F --> G["Concatenate Ciphertext Blocks"] G --> H["Transmit Over Insecure Channel"] H --> I["Receive Ciphertext C"] I --> J["Apply D_K to Each Block
Independently and in Parallel"] J --> K["Concatenate Plaintext Blocks"] K --> L["Remove Padding"] L --> M["Recovered Plaintext P"] ``` **Diagram Explanation:** This end-to-end pipeline illustrates the complete ECB workflow from plaintext input to plaintext recovery. Note the **parallelization** opportunities at the encryption and decryption stages — this is ECB's only performance advantage. --- ## 4.4 ECB Pattern Leakage Visualization (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P["Original Plaintext Structure"] P_A["Block A: HELLO"] P_B["Block B: HELLO"] P_C["Block C: WORLD"] P_D["Block D: HELLO"] end subgraph C["ECB Ciphertext Output"] C_X["Block X: 8F3A2C"] C_Y["Block Y: 8F3A2C"] C_Z["Block Z: B7E1D4"] C_W["Block W: 8F3A2C"] end P_A -->|"E_K"| C_X P_B -->|"E_K (same as above)"| C_Y P_C -->|"E_K"| C_Z P_D -->|"E_K (same as A)"| C_W ``` **Diagram Explanation:** This diagram makes the **pattern preservation flaw** explicit. The three identical plaintext blocks "HELLO" all map to the identical ciphertext block "8F3A2C". An attacker observing $C$ immediately knows which blocks are duplicates, leaking structural information about $P$. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Electronic Codebook (ECB) mode. Why is it considered the simplest mode of operation for block ciphers?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Electronic Codebook (ECB) mode is a method of encrypting a plaintext message using a block cipher where the message is divided into blocks of fixed size, and each block is encrypted **independently** using the **same secret key** $K$. The encryption equation is: $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ It is considered the simplest mode because: 1. **(1 mark)** It requires no Initialization Vector (IV), no chaining, and no feedback mechanism. 2. **(1 mark)** Each block is processed in isolation, making it a direct application of the underlying block cipher. 3. **(1 mark)** Both encryption and decryption can be performed in parallel, with no dependency between blocks. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "ECB is the most secure mode" — this is **wrong**. Always emphasize simplicity, not security. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for confusing simplicity with strength. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State any two disadvantages of ECB mode.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **(1.5 marks) Pattern Preservation:** Since identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks under the same key, ECB leaks the structure of the plaintext. An attacker can perform frequency analysis or pattern recognition without breaking the cipher. 2. **(1.5 marks) Vulnerability to Replay and Cut-Paste Attacks:** Because blocks are independent, an adversary can rearrange, duplicate, or remove ciphertext blocks without detection. For example, if a transaction message contains an amount field, swapping blocks can alter the transaction surreptitiously. --- ## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **a) Explain the working of ECB mode with a neat block diagram. Show the encryption and decryption equations.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Encryption Process (3.5 Marks):** In ECB mode, the plaintext $P$ is first divided into $n$ blocks of size $b$ bits each: $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$. If the last block is incomplete, padding is applied. Each plaintext block is then encrypted **independently** using the same secret key $K$: $$C_i = E_K(P_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ The ciphertext blocks are concatenated: $$C = C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \cdots \parallel C_n$$ **Decryption Process (2 Marks):** At the receiver, each ciphertext block is decrypted independently: $$P_i = D_K(C_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$ **Block Diagram (1.5 Marks):** ``` P1 ──→ [E_K] ──→ C1 ──┐ P2 ──→ [E_K] ──→ C2 ──┼──→ C = C1‖C2‖...‖Cn Pn ──→ [E_K] ──→ Cn ──┘ ↑ └── Same key K used for all blocks ``` **b) Encrypt the plaintext "NETWORK" using ECB mode with a toy block cipher $E_K(x) = (x + 3) \mod 26$ and key $K = 3$. Use block size of 2 characters. Show all steps.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 Mark): Map letters to numbers (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25).** - N = 13, E = 4, T = 19, W = 22, O = 14, R = 17, K = 10 **Step 2 (0.5 Mark): Segment plaintext into 2-character blocks.** Since "NETWORK" has 7 characters, we pad with 'A' to make it 8 characters (4 blocks of 2): - $P_1 = \text{NE} = (13, 4)$ - $P_2 = \text{TW} = (19, 22)$ - $P_3 = \text{OR} = (14, 17)$ - $P_4 = \text{KA} = (10, 0)$ (padded with 'A') **Step 3 (3.5 Marks): Apply encryption $C_i = (P_i + 3) \mod 26$ to each block.** **Block 1: $P_1 = (13, 4)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_1[\text{first}] &= (13 + 3) \mod 26 = 16 \rightarrow \text{Q} \\ C_1[\text{second}] &= (4 + 3) \mod 26 = 7 \rightarrow \text{H} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_1 = \text{QH}$. **Block 2: $P_2 = (19, 22)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_2[\text{first}] &= (19 + 3) \mod 26 = 22 \rightarrow \text{W} \\ C_2[\text{second}] &= (22 + 3) \mod 26 = 25 \rightarrow \text{Z} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_2 = \text{WZ}$. **Block 3: $P_3 = (14, 17)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_3[\text{first}] &= (14 + 3) \mod 26 = 17 \rightarrow \text{R} \\ C_3[\text{second}] &= (17 + 3) \mod 26 = 20 \rightarrow \text{U} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_3 = \text{RU}$. **Block 4: $P_4 = (10, 0)$** $$\begin{aligned} C_4[\text{first}] &= (10 + 3) \mod 26 = 13 \rightarrow \text{N} \\ C_4[\text{second}] &= (0 + 3) \mod 26 = 3 \rightarrow \text{D} \end{aligned}$$ So $C_4 = \text{ND}$. **Step 4 (1 Mark): Concatenate ciphertext blocks.** $$C = \text{QH} \parallel \text{WZ} \parallel \text{RU} \parallel \text{ND} = \text{"QHWZRUND"}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark): Decryption verification using $D_K(y) = (y - 3) \mod 26$.** - $D_K(\text{QH}) = (16-3, 7-3) = (13, 4) = \text{NE}$ ✓ - $D_K(\text{WZ}) = (22-3, 25-3) = (19, 22) = \text{TW}$ ✓ - $D_K(\text{RU}) = (17-3, 20-3) = (14, 17) = \text{OR}$ ✓ - $D_K(\text{ND}) = (13-3, 3-3) = (10, 0) = \text{KA}$ ✓ (padding 'A' removed) Decryption recovers the original "NETWORK". --- ### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]` **a) Discuss the security weaknesses of ECB mode. Explain with an example how an attacker can exploit pattern preservation.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Security Weaknesses of ECB Mode (4 Marks):** 1. **(1 Mark) Pattern Preservation:** ECB preserves the statistical structure of plaintext in ciphertext. Identical plaintext blocks always produce identical ciphertext blocks. This is the most critical weakness. 2. **(1 Mark) Susceptibility to Known-Plaintext Attacks (KPA):** If an attacker knows a plaintext-ciphertext pair $(P_i, C_i)$, they can build a partial codebook. With enough samples, they can decrypt subsequent messages without knowing $K$. 3. **(1 Mark) Reordering and Cut-Paste Attacks:** Since blocks are independent, an attacker can shuffle, remove, or duplicate ciphertext blocks to manipulate the message. For example, in a fund transfer "Transfer 1000 from A to B," an attacker might reorder blocks to change the destination. 4. **(1 Mark) No Semantic Security:** ECB is **not IND-CPA secure** (indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack). The same plaintext always encrypts to the same ciphertext, so the encryption scheme is not probabilistic. **Example of Pattern Preservation Exploitation (3 Marks):** Consider a bitmap image of a logo (e.g., a 1024-byte image with 8×8 pixel blocks). The logo has a large solid-color background and a smaller distinct foreground shape. **Plaintext blocks:** - 80% of blocks: solid white background → $P = \text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}\text{FF}$ - 20% of blocks: foreground pattern → $P = \text{various non-white bytes}$ **ECB Ciphertext blocks:** - 80% of blocks: identical ciphertext (say, $C_{\text{white}}$) - 20% of blocks: varied ciphertexts **Attacker's Observation:** Even without decrypting, the attacker sees that 80% of the ciphertext blocks are identical, inferring that 80% of the original image had identical content. Reconstructing a "silhouette" of the original image becomes trivial — the **ECB penguin image** demonstration is the classic example. **Countermeasure:** This is why real-world systems use CBC, CTR, or GCM modes where the IV/counter ensures different ciphertexts for identical plaintexts. **b) Compare ECB mode with CBC mode in terms of security, error propagation, and parallelization. Which is preferred for secure communication and why?** **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Property | ECB Mode | CBC Mode | |----------|----------|----------| | **IV Required** | No | Yes (random IV per encryption) | | **Encryption Equation** | $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ | $C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$, with $C_0 = \text{IV}$ | | **Decryption Equation** | $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ | $P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$ | | **Parallelizable Encryption** | Yes (full) | No (sequential — depends on $C_{i-1}$) | | **Parallelizable Decryption** | Yes (full) | Yes (after receiving all blocks) | | **Error Propagation** | 1 block affected | 2 blocks affected ($P_j$ garbled, $P_{j+1}$ has 1-bit flip) | | **Security vs. Pattern Leakage** | Poor (patterns visible) | Strong (randomized by IV and chaining) | | **Determinism** | Deterministic (same $P$ → same $C$) | Probabilistic (different IV → different $C$) | | **IND-CPA Secure?** | No | Yes (with random IV) | **Preferred Mode for Secure Communication: CBC (or CTR/GCM)** **(2 Marks)** CBC mode is preferred over ECB for secure communication because: 1. **(1 Mark) The random IV ensures semantic security:** Even if the same plaintext is encrypted twice, different IVs produce different ciphertexts. This makes frequency analysis and pattern detection impossible. 2. **(1 Mark) Chaining provides diffusion:** Each ciphertext block depends on all previous plaintext blocks (via the chaining mechanism), making the cipher resistant to block manipulation attacks. **When ECB is Acceptable (Bonus Insight):** ECB is acceptable only in narrow use cases: encrypting a single block (where CBC would be equivalent with zero IV), or when the plaintext is genuinely random (e.g., a one-time session key). For all practical secure communications, **never use ECB**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention IV:** When writing $C_i = E_K(P_i)$, students often omit stating that ECB requires **no IV** — this is a common 1-mark loss. > 2. **Confusing block size with key size:** Many students write "ECB uses 128-bit blocks with 128-bit key for AES" without realizing block size and key size are independent parameters. > 3. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU board examiners allocate 1.5–2 marks specifically for "neat block diagram." Always draw the parallel $E_K$ boxes with shared key input. > 4. **Padding oversight:** In numerical problems, students often forget to pad the last block when plaintext length is not a multiple of block size. This causes incorrect block counts and cascading errors. > 5. **Wrong error propagation claim:** Some students claim ECB has "no error propagation" — incorrect. It has **localized** error propagation (one block garbled), which is different from "no error." --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — ECB Mode** ### Core Definition - ECB = **Electronic Codebook** mode - Equation: $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ and $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ - Each block encrypted **independently** with the **same key** ### Essential Properties - ✅ **Deterministic:** Same $P$ + same $K$ → same $C$ (always) - ✅ **No IV required** - ✅ **No chaining, no feedback** - ✅ **Parallelizable** (both encrypt and decrypt) - ✅ **Localized error propagation** (1 block affected per bit error) - ❌ **Pattern leakage** (fatal flaw) - ❌ **Not IND-CPA secure** - ❌ **Vulnerable to replay/cut-paste attacks** ### Key Numbers to Memorize - DES block size = **64 bits**, DES key = **56 bits** - AES block size = **128 bits**, AES key = 128/192/256 bits - ECB codebook size (theoretical) = $2^{64}$ entries for DES ### Equation Bank - Encryption: $C_i = E_K(P_i)$ - Decryption: $P_i = D_K(C_i)$ - Number of blocks: $n = \lceil \vert P \vert / b \rceil$ ### Use-Case Decision Rule - ✅ Use ECB only for: single-block messages, random data, academic demonstration - ❌ Never use ECB for: file encryption, network communication, image encryption, real-world security ### Comparison Anchors (for CBC/other modes in future modules) - ECB vs CBC: CBC adds IV + chaining → security improvement, encryption parallelism loss - ECB vs CTR: CTR uses counter instead of plaintext → fully parallelizable + secure - ECB vs GCM: GCM adds authentication tag → confidentiality + integrity ### Common KTU Exam Keywords (Trigger Words) - "Demonstrate ECB with example" → numerical toy cipher problem - "Why is ECB insecure?" → pattern preservation + determinism - "Compare ECB with ___" → table format expected - "State properties of ECB" → list all 5–6 key properties

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modes of Operation: CTR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Counter (CTR) Mode** is a block cipher mode of operation that converts a block cipher into a **stream cipher** by encrypting a sequentially incrementing counter value using the block cipher's encryption function, and then XORing the resulting keystream with the plaintext blocks. CTR mode requires **only the forward encryption function** $E_K$ of the block cipher; the decryption process is structurally identical to encryption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** CTR mode is officially classified as a **stream-cipher-like** mode because it generates a **keystream** independent of the plaintext or ciphertext. This is the single most important conceptual distinction students must internalize for the exam.

## 1.2 Formal Encryption/Decryption Equations

For a plaintext $P$ split into blocks $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ and an initial counter $C_0$ (often called the **nonce** or **Initial Vector**):

**Encryption:**

$$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$$

**Decryption:**

$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Observation:** Encryption and decryption use the **same function** $E_K(\cdot)$. The block cipher's *inverse* (i.e., the decryption algorithm $D_K$) is **never invoked**. This is unique among the major modes of operation (ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Ticket Dispenser" Model

Imagine a **ticket dispenser machine** in a bank queue:

1. The machine has a **secret internal configuration** (the secret key $K$).
2. Each time you press the button, it outputs a **unique, unpredictable, but deterministic** number — 0001, 0002, 0003, ... (the encrypted counters $E_K(C_1), E_K(C_2), \dots$).
3. You **XOR-mix** each ticket number with your message to scramble it (ciphertext).
4. Anyone with the **same machine and same starting number** (shared key + shared nonce) can reproduce the *exact* same ticket sequence and "unscramble" your message.

The key insights are:
- The "tickets" are produced **independently of the message** (unlike CBC, where each block depends on the previous ciphertext).
- A counter that **never repeats** across all messages guarantees security.
- The recipient only needs the *forward* button, not a "reverse" button.

## 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants

- **Block size** of the underlying cipher: **128 bits** (for AES, the KTU-standard cipher).
- **Counter increment size**: typically **1**, but can be any fixed integer agreed upon by sender and receiver.
- **Nonce (initial counter) size**: typically **96–128 bits** to prevent birthday-paradox collisions.
- **Counter overflow tolerance**: the counter must be large enough to never wrap around during the lifetime of the key. For a **64-bit counter with 128-bit blocks**, NIST allows up to $2^{64}$ blocks per message.

## 1.5 Why CTR Mode Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> CTR mode is a **favourite KTU question topic** because it tests:
> 1. The student's ability to compute a full encryption/decryption sequence by hand.
> 2. Parallelism and random-access properties.
> 3. Comparison with CBC, OFB, and CFB modes (commonly asked as "Compare CTR with CBC").
> 4. Error propagation behaviour (CTR has **no error propagation** — a single bit error in $C_i$ affects *only* $P_i$).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step by Step

The CTR mode algorithm executes in the following sequence:

### Encryption Algorithm
1. **Key & Nonce Setup:** Sender and receiver agree on a secret key $K$ and a public initial counter value $C_0$ (the **nonce**).
2. **Counter Generation:** For block $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$), construct the counter input as:
   $$\text{Counter}_i = C_0 + (i-1) \pmod{2^m}$$
   where $m$ is the counter bit-length.
3. **Keystream Block Production:** Compute the encrypted counter:
   $$O_i = E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$$
   This output $O_i$ is the **keystream block**.
4. **XOR with Plaintext:** Compute the ciphertext block:
   $$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$
5. **Transmit:** Send the concatenation $C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel \dots \parallel C_n$ to the receiver. The nonce $C_0$ is transmitted in the clear (or implicitly known).

### Decryption Algorithm
1. Receive $C_0$ (nonce) and ciphertext blocks $C_1, \dots, C_n$.
2. For each $i$, regenerate the keystream block $O_i = E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ using the *same* shared key and nonce.
3. Recover the plaintext:
   $$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" works:** Because XOR is its own inverse ($X \oplus Y \oplus Y = X$), the receiver simply regenerates the same keystream and XORs it with the ciphertext. No inverse cipher function is required — this is the *defining feature* of CTR mode.

## 2.2 Security Properties of CTR Mode

| Property | Behaviour | KTU Exam Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Semantic Security (IND-CPA)** | ✅ Achieved (provided the nonce is unique per message) | High |
| **Parallelizability** | ✅ Fully parallelizable in both encryption and decryption | Very High |
| **Random Access** | ✅ Any block $P_i$ can be decrypted independently | High |
| **Error Propagation** | ❌ A 1-bit error in $C_i$ corrupts only $P_i$ (same bit position) | Very High |
| **Padding Required** | ❌ No padding needed (acts on arbitrary-length input) | Medium |
| **Authentication** | ❌ Provides **confidentiality only**, not integrity (vulnerable to bit-flipping) | High |
| **Throughput** | ✅ Highest among all standard modes (no chaining dependency) | Medium |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ | Encryption rule | Bits; $i = 1 \dots n$ |
| 2 | $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ | Decryption rule | Identical to encryption |
| 3 | $\text{Counter}_i = C_0 + (i-1)$ | Counter increment (most common form) | Integer addition |
| 4 | $\text{Counter}_i = (T_i \parallel \text{Nonce})$ | Concatenated counter format (NIST SP 800-38A) | $T_i$ = 64-bit, Nonce = 64-bit |
| 5 | Max blocks per key | $2^m - 1$ where $m$ = counter bits | $2^{32}$ for 32-bit counter |
| 6 | Bit-flip attack | Flipping bit $j$ of $C_i$ flips bit $j$ of $P_i$ | No integrity check |
| 7 | Birthday bound on nonce | $q^2 / 2^N \ll 1$ for safety, where $N$ = nonce bits | Statistical security |
| 8 | Throughput vs CBC | CTR throughput $\approx 1.5 \times$ CBC throughput | Hardware-dependent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown table cells.** For mathematical "divides" or "such that" notation in any future table, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. The current table uses `parallel` for concatenation and `\ll` for "much less than" to stay safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CTR mode is the **default mode in modern protocols**:
- **TLS 1.3** (replaced CBC for AES).
- **IPsec** (ESP payload encryption often uses AES-CTR).
- **Disk encryption** (e.g., Linux `dm-crypt` uses XTS, a CTR-variant).
- **Wi-Fi WPA3** uses AES-GCM, which internally uses CTR.
- **SSH** (modern implementations use ChaCha20, a stream cipher with CTR-like structure).

**Why industry prefers CTR:**
- **Parallel hardware implementations** (AES-NI instructions) achieve multi-Gbps throughput.
- **Seekable**: in database encryption, block $i$ can be decrypted on-demand without processing all preceding blocks.
- **No padding oracle** vulnerabilities (unlike CBC).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem Statement:** Encrypt the plaintext $P = \text{"CRYPTO"}$ using AES-128 in **CTR mode** with:
- Key: pre-agreed (we will treat the encrypted counters as given)
- Counter $C_0 = 0110\,0110$ (initial counter)
- Block size: 8 bits (for hand-computation)
- Increment size: 1

**Given Encrypted Counters (assume a simplified block cipher):**
- $E_K(C_1) = 1010\,1100$
- $E_K(C_2) = 0101\,0110$
- $E_K(C_3) = 1111\,0000$

**Plaintext in binary (ASCII, 8 bits per block):**
- $P_1$ = 'C' = $0100\,0011$
- $P_2$ = 'R' = $0101\,0010$
- $P_3$ = 'Y' = $0101\,1001$

(Note: We are working with 3 blocks for demonstration; the real plaintext "CRYPTO" would have 6 bytes = 6 blocks, but we show the core 3-block logic.)

### Step 1 — Compute Counter Values

The counter increments by 1 starting from $C_0$:

$$\text{Counter}_1 = C_0 + 0 = 0110\,0110$$

$$\text{Counter}_2 = C_0 + 1 = 0110\,0111$$

$$\text{Counter}_3 = C_0 + 2 = 0110\,1000$$

### Step 2 — Derive Keystream Blocks

Using the given $E_K$ outputs (these would normally be computed by the block cipher on the counter inputs):

$$O_1 = E_K(\text{Counter}_1) = 1010\,1100$$

$$O_2 = E_K(\text{Counter}_2) = 0101\,0110$$

$$O_3 = E_K(\text{Counter}_3) = 1111\,0000$$

### Step 3 — XOR Plaintext with Keystream to Produce Ciphertext

**Block 1 computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= P_1 \oplus O_1 \\
&= 0100\,0011 \oplus 1010\,1100 \\
&= \underbrace{(0\oplus 1)}_{1}\underbrace{(1\oplus 0)}_{1}\underbrace{(0\oplus 1)}_{1}\underbrace{(0\oplus 0)}_{0}\underbrace{(0\oplus 1)}_{1}\underbrace{(0\oplus 1)}_{1}\underbrace{(1\oplus 0)}_{1}\underbrace{(1\oplus 0)}_{1} \\
&= 1110\,1111
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block 2 computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= P_2 \oplus O_2 \\
&= 0101\,0010 \oplus 0101\,0110 \\
&= 0000\,0100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block 3 computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_3 &= P_3 \oplus O_3 \\
&= 0101\,1001 \oplus 1111\,0000 \\
&= 1010\,1001
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Ciphertext

$$C = C_1 \parallel C_2 \parallel C_3 = 1110\,1111 \;\Vert\; 0000\,0100 \;\Vert\; 1010\,1001$$

### Step 5 — Verification via Decryption

The receiver regenerates the *same* keystream $O_1, O_2, O_3$ (since the key and counter are shared) and XORs:

**Block 1 verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1' &= C_1 \oplus O_1 \\
&= 1110\,1111 \oplus 1010\,1100 \\
&= 0100\,0011 = \text{'C'} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block 2 verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2' &= C_2 \oplus O_2 \\
&= 0000\,0100 \oplus 0101\,0110 \\
&= 0101\,0010 = \text{'R'} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Block 3 verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_3' &= C_3 \oplus O_3 \\
&= 1010\,1001 \oplus 1111\,0000 \\
&= 0101\,1001 = \text{'Y'} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decryption succeeded without invoking the block cipher's inverse function** $D_K$. This confirms the defining property of CTR mode.

## 3.2 Error Propagation Analysis (Numerical Derivation)

Suppose a 1-bit transmission error flips bit 3 (from the right) of ciphertext block $C_2$, so the *received* $C_2' = 0000\,1100$ (instead of $0000\,0100$).

**Effect on $P_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2' &= C_2' \oplus O_2 \\
&= 0000\,1100 \oplus 0101\,0110 \\
&= 0101\,1010
\end{aligned}
$$

Original $P_2 = 0101\,0010$. Compare:
$$P_2 \oplus P_2' = 0000\,1000$$

Only **one bit flipped**. All other blocks $P_1, P_3$ are unaffected because each block is encrypted *independently* of every other block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is *the* classic 7-mark sub-question. The expected answer must state: "CTR mode has **no error propagation beyond the affected block**, because there is no ciphertext feedback into subsequent blocks."

## 3.3 Counter-Reuse Catastrophe (Analytical Derivation)

Suppose the *same* key $K$ and the *same* nonce $C_0$ are used to encrypt two different plaintexts $P$ and $P'$.

**Encryption of block 1 of $P$:**

$$C_1 = P_1 \oplus E_K(C_1) = P_1 \oplus O_1$$

**Encryption of block 1 of $P'$:**

$$C_1' = P_1' \oplus E_K(C_1) = P_1' \oplus O_1$$

**XOR of the two ciphertexts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 \oplus C_1' &= (P_1 \oplus O_1) \oplus (P_1' \oplus O_1) \\
&= P_1 \oplus P_1' \quad \text{(since } O_1 \oplus O_1 = 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The attacker recovers the **XOR of the two plaintexts** — a complete loss of confidentiality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter reuse is the #1 fatal error in CTR mode implementations.** KTU examiners often ask: "What happens if the same counter is reused with the same key?" The answer is: **complete compromise of IND-CPA security**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CTR vs. CBC

| Property | CTR Mode | CBC Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Stream-cipher-like | Block-cipher (chained) |
| Encryption uses $D_K$? | No (only $E_K$) | No (only $E_K$) |
| Decryption uses $D_K$? | **No** (only $E_K$) | **Yes** ($D_K$ required) |
| Parallel encryption? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (sequential) |
| Parallel decryption? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (after IV) |
| Random access? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (after IV) |
| Error propagation | 1 block | 2 blocks (current + next) |
| Padding needed? | No | Yes (unless ciphertext stealing) |
| IV/nonce reuse consequence | Key + nonce reuse = plaintext XOR leaked | IV reuse leaks first block XOR |
| Standardized by | NIST SP 800-38A | NIST SP 800-38A |
| Hardware throughput | Highest (no feedback) | Lower (feedback dependency) |

## 4.3 Counter Format Schematic (NIST SP 800-38A)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTR128["128-bit Counter Block for AES-CTR"]
        N["Nonce  64 bits public random"]:::nonce
        T["T Counter  64 bits starts at 0"]:::counter
    end
    N --- T
    classDef nonce fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef counter fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** In the NIST-standard 128-bit counter format, the **first 64 bits** are the **nonce** (must be unique per message) and the **last 64 bits** are the **counter** (starts at 0 and increments by 1 per block). This separation is purely a design choice — the counter is *not* required to be split this way, but it is the most common convention seen in KTU numerical problems.
