# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Why is CTR mode considered a stream-cipher-like mode, and what is its primary advantage over CBC mode?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
CTR mode generates a **keystream** $O_i = E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ that is **independent of the plaintext and ciphertext**, and the ciphertext is produced by $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$. This structure is identical to a synchronous stream cipher.
**Primary advantage over CBC:** CTR mode supports **full parallelization** in both encryption and decryption, whereas CBC encryption is inherently **sequential** because each plaintext block must be XORed with the previous ciphertext block before encryption. (1 mark for the stream-cipher identification, 1 mark for the keystream-independence property, 1 mark for the parallelization advantage.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"What happens if the same nonce (initial counter) and key are used to encrypt two different plaintexts in CTR mode? Justify your answer mathematically."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
If the same key $K$ and nonce $C_0$ are reused, the keystream blocks are identical for both messages: $O_i = O_i'$. The XOR of the two ciphertext blocks becomes:
$$C_i \oplus C_i' = (P_i \oplus O_i) \oplus (P_i' \oplus O_i) = P_i \oplus P_i'$$
The attacker recovers the **XOR of the two plaintexts**, completely breaking IND-CPA security. (1 mark for stating the consequence, 2 marks for the mathematical derivation.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **encryption and decryption algorithms of CTR mode** with suitable diagrams. Show that CTR mode is invertible using only the forward encryption function $E_K$.

**Model Answer:**

**Encryption Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Split plaintext $P$ into $n$ blocks of $b$ bits: $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$.
2. Initialise counter: $\text{Counter}_1 = C_0$ (nonce).
3. For $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
   - Compute keystream block: $O_i = E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$.
   - Compute ciphertext block: $C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$.
   - Increment counter: $\text{Counter}_{i+1} = \text{Counter}_i + 1$.

**Decryption Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. For $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
   - Regenerate keystream: $O_i = E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ (using shared key and nonce).
   - Recover plaintext: $P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$.

**Proof of invertibility using only $E_K$ (2 marks):**
The receiver regenerates the *same* $O_i$ because both sender and receiver share $K$ and $C_0$. Applying XOR twice with the same $O_i$ recovers the original:
$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i = (P_i \oplus O_i) \oplus O_i = P_i \oplus (O_i \oplus O_i) = P_i \oplus 0 = P_i$$
The block cipher's inverse $D_K$ is **never needed**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A message is encrypted using **AES-128 in CTR mode** with block size **128 bits**. The initial counter value (nonce) is $C_0 = \text{0x00000000000000000000000000000001}$ and the key $K$ is such that the encrypted counters for the first two blocks are:
- $E_K(C_1) = \text{0xA3F1 2B6C 4E89 1057 DA0B 7723 CC9E 4F16}$
- $E_K(C_2) = \text{0x5B7E 9C0D 1A2F 3E48 6F19 04BD 7C2A 8E33}$

If the first plaintext block (128 bits) is $\text{0x48656C6C6F20576F726C64212121}$ (ASCII "Hello World!!!"), compute the first two ciphertext blocks and verify the decryption.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify $C_1$ and $C_2$ (counter values):**
- $C_1 = C_0 + 0 = \text{0x00000000000000000000000000000001}$
- $C_2 = C_0 + 1 = \text{0x00000000000000000000000000000002}$

**Step 2 — Write down keystream blocks (given):**
- $O_1 = E_K(C_1) = \text{0xA3F1 2B6C 4E89 1057 DA0B 7723 CC9E 4F16}$
- $O_2 = E_K(C_2) = \text{0x5B7E 9C0D 1A2F 3E48 6F19 04BD 7C2A 8E33}$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_1 = P_1 \oplus O_1$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= \text{0x48656C6C6F20576F726C64212121} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{0xA3F12B6C4E891057DA0B7723CC9E4F16} \\
&= \text{0xEB944700218E4738AD671102EDBF6E37}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification by bitwise XOR of first word: $\text{0x48656C6C} \oplus \text{0xA3F12B6C} = \text{0xEB944700}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute $C_2 = P_2 \oplus O_2$ (2 marks):**

We need $P_2$ (the second 128-bit block). Assuming the plaintext continues with " (Example)" in ASCII = $\text{0x2028204578616D706C652920090909}$ (illustrative):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= \text{0x2028204578616D706C652920090909} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{0x5B7E9C0D1A2F3E486F1904BD7C2A8E33} \\
&= \text{0x7B56BC48624E5328037C2F9D752F6F3A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify decryption of $C_1$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= C_1 \oplus O_1 \\
&= \text{0xEB944700218E4738AD671102EDBF6E37} \\
&\quad \oplus \text{0xA3F12B6C4E891057DA0B7723CC9E4F16} \\
&= \text{0x48656C6C6F20576F726C64212121} \;\; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — State conclusion (1 mark):**
Decryption recovered the original plaintext "Hello World!!!" without using the AES inverse function, confirming CTR's structural property.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **increment the counter by 1** for each block, leading to $C_1 = C_2$. Marks deducted: 1–2.
> 2. Students sometimes **use $D_K$ (AES decryption) instead of $E_K$ (AES encryption)** in the decryption step. This is *wrong* for CTR mode — the forward function is used both times. Marks deducted: 2.
> 3. The **nonce must be transmitted** to the receiver (usually prepended to the ciphertext). Forgetting to mention this loses 1 mark.
> 4. In the final XOR step, students often **write $C_i$ instead of $O_i$** as the second operand of the verification XOR. Always re-verify with the *keystream*, not the ciphertext.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast **CTR mode with CBC mode** under the following heads: (i) underlying cipher requirement, (ii) parallelizability, (iii) error propagation, (iv) padding requirement, (v) random access capability, and (vi) IV/nonce reuse consequence.

**Model Answer (1 mark per head):**

| # | Aspect | CTR Mode | CBC Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Cipher requirement | Only $E_K$ needed | $E_K$ for encryption, $D_K$ for decryption |
| (ii) | Parallelizable? | ✅ Fully (enc + dec) | ❌ Enc sequential, ✅ Dec parallel |
| (iii) | Error propagation | 1 block affected | 2 blocks affected (current + next) |
| (iv) | Padding? | Not required | Required (block-aligned) |
| (v) | Random access? | Yes | Yes (after IV) |
| (vi) | IV/nonce reuse | Leaks XOR of all plaintext blocks | Leaks XOR of first blocks + weakens security |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system uses **AES-256-CTR** with a **64-bit counter** and **64-bit nonce**. A sender encrypts a file of size **2 GB** using a single key. Each AES block is **128 bits** (16 bytes). The counter starts at 0 and increments by 1 per block.

**(i)** Compute the **number of blocks** encrypted.
**(ii)** Determine whether the **counter will overflow** during encryption.
**(iii)** Recommend a fix if overflow is detected.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute number of blocks (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File size} &= 2\;\text{GB} = 2 \times 2^{30}\;\text{bytes} \\
&= 2{,}147{,}483{,}648\;\text{bytes} \\
\text{Block size} &= 16\;\text{bytes} \\
n &= \frac{2 \times 2^{30}}{16} = \frac{2^{31}}{2^4} = 2^{27} \\
&= 134{,}217{,}728\;\text{blocks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Counter capacity check (3 marks):**

A 64-bit counter can represent values $0$ to $2^{64} - 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max counter value reached} &= n - 1 = 2^{27} - 1 \\
&\approx 1.34 \times 10^8 \\
\text{Counter capacity} &= 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $2^{27} \ll 2^{64}$, the counter **does not overflow** in this case.

**Step 3 — Recommendation (2 marks):**

Even though no overflow occurs for this specific 2 GB file, a defensive engineering practice is to:
- Use a **96-bit nonce + 32-bit counter** (per RFC 5116) so that the counter limit $2^{32}$ blocks ($\approx$ 68 GB at 16 bytes/block) is the bottleneck, **not** the nonce.
- Implement a **runtime counter-overflow check** that aborts encryption if the counter would exceed $2^{32} - 1$ blocks per (key, nonce) pair, and triggers a key/nonce refresh.
- This is the standard used in **AES-GCM** (the most common AEAD mode in TLS 1.3).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistakes on This Problem:**
> 1. Converting **2 GB to bits** by multiplying by 8 *and then* dividing by 16, instead of dividing 2 GB directly by 16 bytes. Marks lost: 1.
> 2. Confusing the **nonce bits** with the **counter bits** when stating the overflow threshold. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **key-refresh** mitigation. Marks lost: 1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for CTR Mode**

- **Core Equation:** $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ and $P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$. Both encryption and decryption use the **same forward function** $E_K$.
- **Counter Construction:** $\text{Counter}_i = C_0 + (i-1)$, where $C_0$ is the **nonce** (public, unique per message).
- **Stream-Cipher Nature:** The keystream $E_K(\text{Counter}_i)$ is **independent of plaintext and ciphertext** — this is the defining property.
- **Parallelism:** Full parallelization in **both** directions. (CBC encryption is sequential.)
- **Error Propagation:** **1 block only.** A 1-bit error in $C_i$ flips exactly 1 bit of $P_i$ in the same position. No other blocks are affected.
- **No Padding:** CTR mode can encrypt plaintexts of any byte length, not just multiples of the block size.
- **Random Access:** Block $P_i$ can be decrypted directly by computing $E_K(C_0 + i - 1)$ — no need to process blocks $1, 2, \dots, i-1$.
- **Nonce Reuse Catastrophe:** Reusing $(K, C_0)$ leaks the **XOR of the two plaintexts** for every block — a complete break of confidentiality.
- **Bit-Flipping Attack:** Without an authentication tag (e.g., MAC or AEAD), an attacker can flip any bit of $C_i$ and cause a predictable flip in $P_i$. CTR provides **confidentiality only**, not integrity.
- **Standardization:** Defined in **NIST SP 800-38A** (2001) for AES and Triple DES.
- **Modern Use:** Default in **TLS 1.3** (as AES-GCM, which is CTR + GMAC) and **IPsec**.
- **Comparison with OFB:** OFB also uses a forward-only keystream, but OFB's keystream depends on the previous output: $O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$. CTR replaces this sequential dependency with a **deterministic counter**, gaining parallelism. CTR is therefore strictly superior to OFB in hardware throughput.
- **Recommended Counter Format (NIST):** 128-bit block = **64-bit nonce $\parallel$ 64-bit counter**.
- **Maximum Blocks per (Key, Nonce) Pair:** Bounded by the counter bit length, e.g., $2^{64}$ blocks for a 64-bit counter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymmetric Key Cryptography: Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Asymmetric Key Cryptography: Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Key Cryptography (PKC) / Asymmetric Key Cryptography** is a cryptographic paradigm that employs a **mathematically related pair of distinct keys** — a publicly distributable **Public Key** ($K_{pub}$) for encryption or signature verification, and a secretly held **Private Key** ($K_{priv}$) for decryption or signature generation — such that knowledge of one key does not feasibly permit derivation of the other, and computational operations performed with one key can only be feasibly inverted by the other.

In the formal notation adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with **Stallings' Cryptography and Network Security** principles):

$$C = E(K_{pub}, M) \quad \text{and} \quad M = D(K_{priv}, C)$$

where:
- $M$ = plaintext message
- $C$ = ciphertext
- $E$ = encryption function (trapdoor one-way function)
- $D$ = decryption function (trapdoor one-way inversion)
- $K_{pub} \neq K_{priv}$, yet $K_{priv} = f(K_{pub})$ under a secret trapdoor

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems include (i) the conceptual foundations of asymmetric encryption, (ii) the role of **one-way functions** and **trapdoor one-way functions**, (iii) the **confidentiality and authentication services** offered, and (iv) a structured **comparison with symmetric key cryptography** including key management overhead.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Public Mailbox" Model

Imagine a city with thousands of residents. Each resident installs a **transparent mailbox** on their front porch:

| Element in Analogy | Cryptographic Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| The **transparent mailbox slot** | **Public Key ($K_{pub}$)** | Anyone can drop a letter (encrypt a message) into the slot. |
| The **secret physical key** that opens the mailbox | **Private Key ($K_{priv}$)** | Only the owner can open and retrieve letters (decrypt messages). |
| A **letter sealed inside** | **Ciphertext ($C$)** | Unreadable without the secret key. |
| **Reverse use: sealing a letter with the owner's unique stamp only they possess** | **Digital Signature** | Anyone with the public stamp can verify the letter came from the owner. |

In this analogy:
- The mailbox slot is **public knowledge** — yet its design makes it practically impossible to retrieve a sealed letter without the secret key.
- The "trapdoor" is the physical key — without it, the slot is a **one-way function**: easy to insert, practically impossible to extract.
- This elegantly captures the essence of asymmetric cryptography: **public accessibility without sacrificing private confidentiality.**

---

## 1.3 The Two Foundational Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 1 — One-Way Function (OWF):** A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ such that for any $x \in X$, $y = f(x)$ is computationally **easy** to compute, but for virtually all $y \in \text{Range}(f)$, finding an $x$ such that $f(x) = y$ is computationally **infeasible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pillar 2 — Trapdoor One-Way Function (TOWF):** A specialized OWF $f_k: X \rightarrow Y$ parameterized by a secret $k$ (the trapdoor), such that computing $f_k(x)$ is easy, computing $f_k^{-1}(y)$ is infeasible without $k$, but **trivial** with $k$.

Examples from KTU syllabus:
- **Multiplication of two large primes** $p \times q = n$ — easy to compute, but factoring $n$ back into $p$ and $q$ is hard (basis of **RSA**).
- **Modular exponentiation** $a^x \mod p$ — basis of **Diffie-Hellman** and **ElGamal**.
- **Elliptic curve point multiplication** $k \cdot P$ — basis of **ECC**.

---

## 1.4 Services Provided by Public Key Cryptosystems

| Service | Mechanism | Key Used | Real-World Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality / Secrecy** | Encrypt with recipient's $K_{pub}$ | Recipient's Public Key | Secure email (PGP), HTTPS handshakes |
| **Authentication / Digital Signature** | Sign with sender's $K_{priv}$ | Sender's Private Key | Code signing, certificate authorities |
| **Both Confidentiality + Authentication** | Sign-then-encrypt (or vice versa) | Both parties' key pairs | S/MIME, secure electronic transactions |
| **Key Establishment** | Key exchange protocols | Both | Diffie-Hellman, RSA key transport |

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Requirements of a Public Key Cryptosystem (Stallings' Six Conditions)

For a cryptosystem to be considered a valid PKC, the following **six conditions** must hold simultaneously:

1. **Computationally easy** for party $B$ to generate a key pair $(K_{pub}^{B}, K_{priv}^{B})$.
2. **Computationally easy** for sender $A$ to encrypt: $C = E(K_{pub}^{B}, M)$.
3. **Computationally easy** for receiver $B$ to decrypt: $M = D(K_{priv}^{B}, C)$.
4. **Computationally infeasible** for an adversary to determine $K_{priv}^{B}$ from $K_{pub}^{B}$.
5. **Computationally infeasible** for an adversary to recover $M$ from $C$ and $K_{pub}^{B}$.
6. The encryption and decryption functions can be applied in **either order** (for signature schemes): $M = E(K_{priv}^{A}, D(K_{pub}^{A}, M))$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Blueprint — How a Public Key Cryptosystem Functions

A public key cryptosystem operates through a carefully orchestrated sequence of mathematical operations. The following structured breakdown captures the **why** and **how** of each phase:

### Phase 1: Key Generation
- Each user $U$ invokes a probabilistic algorithm $\mathcal{G}$ that outputs a key pair $(K_{pub}^{U}, K_{priv}^{U})$.
- The algorithm internally leverages the **trapdoor** knowledge (e.g., the factorization of $n$ in RSA, the discrete logarithm in DSA).
- $K_{pub}^{U}$ is published in a **digital certificate** signed by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.
- $K_{priv}^{U}$ is stored in a **Hardware Security Module (HSM)**, **Trusted Platform Module (TPM)**, or encrypted keystore.

### Phase 2: Key Distribution
- Public keys are distributed via **X.509 certificates** in a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)**.
- The integrity and authenticity of $K_{pub}^{U}$ is guaranteed by the CA's digital signature.
- Recipients need not — and should not — establish a prior secret channel to obtain $K_{pub}^{U}$.

### Phase 3: Encryption Operation
- Sender $A$ obtains the certified $K_{pub}^{B}$ of receiver $B$.
- $A$ computes $C = E(K_{pub}^{B}, M)$.
- $C$ is transmitted over an **insecure channel** (e.g., the Internet).

### Phase 4: Decryption Operation
- Receiver $B$ retrieves $C$ and computes $M = D(K_{priv}^{B}, C)$.
- Without $K_{priv}^{B}$, the adversary observing $C$ and $K_{pub}^{B}$ faces a computationally infeasible inversion problem.

### Phase 5: Authentication (Digital Signature Variant)
- Signer $A$ computes $S = D(K_{priv}^{A}, H(M))$, where $H$ is a cryptographic hash function.
- Verifier $B$ recovers $H(M)$ by computing $E(K_{pub}^{A}, S)$ and compares it with a freshly computed $H(M)$.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundations — The Engine Room

The "magic" of PKC rests on three pillars of number theory that every KTU Applied Cryptography student must internalize:

### 2.2.1 Modular Arithmetic
For integers $a, b, n$ with $n > 0$:

$$a \equiv b \pmod{n} \iff n \mid (a - b)$$

This equivalence relation partitions the integers into $n$ residue classes $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$, forming the ring $\mathbb{Z}_n$.

### 2.2.2 Euler's Totient Function
For a positive integer $n$:

$$\phi(n) = \vert \{k \in \mathbb{Z} \mid 1 \le k \le n,\ \gcd(k, n) = 1\} \vert$$

**Key values:**
- If $p$ is prime: $\phi(p) = p - 1$
- If $p, q$ are distinct primes: $\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$
- If $n = p_1^{e_1} p_2^{e_2} \cdots p_k^{e_k}$: $\phi(n) = n \prod_{i=1}^{k}\left(1 - \frac{1}{p_i}\right)$

### 2.2.3 Euler's Theorem
For $\gcd(a, n) = 1$:

$$a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

### 2.2.4 Fermat's Little Theorem (Special Case)
For prime $p$ and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$:

$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

These theorems are the **algebraic backbone** of RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and ElGamal — the trio of algorithms the KTU Module 2 syllabus prioritizes.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a student must memorize for KTU Module 2 examinations. Pay particular attention to units, modular conditions, and edge cases.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modular Congruence | $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$ | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+},\ n \mid (a-b)$ | All PKC schemes |
| 2 | Euler's Totient | $\phi(n) = n\prod_{i}\left(1 - \frac{1}{p_i}\right)$ | $n = \prod p_i^{e_i}$ | RSA key generation |
| 3 | $\phi$ for prime $p$ | $\phi(p) = p-1$ | $p$ prime | RSA |
| 4 | $\phi$ for $n = pq$ | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | $p, q$ distinct primes | RSA |
| 5 | Euler's Theorem | $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ | $\gcd(a, n) = 1$ | RSA correctness proof |
| 6 | Fermat's Little Theorem | $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ | $p$ prime, $\gcd(a,p)=1$ | Primality testing |
| 7 | RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $1 \le M < n$ | RSA |
| 8 | RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $n = pq,\ ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | RSA |
| 9 | RSA Key Pair | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | RSA |
| 10 | Discrete Log Problem | Given $y, g, p$, find $x: y \equiv g^{x} \pmod{p}$ | $p$ prime | DH, ElGamal, DSA |
| 11 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{a b} \pmod{p}$ | $a, b$ private, $p$ prime | DH Key Exchange |
| 12 | ElGamal Encryption (1) | $C_1 \equiv g^{k} \pmod{p}$ | Random $k$ | ElGamal |
| 13 | ElGamal Encryption (2) | $C_2 \equiv M \cdot y^{k} \pmod{p}$ | $y = g^{x}$ is recipient's public | ElGamal |
| 14 | ElGamal Decryption | $M \equiv C_2 \cdot (C_1^{x})^{-1} \pmod{p}$ | $x$ is recipient's private | ElGamal |
| 15 | Elliptic Curve Point Addition | $P + Q = R$ on curve $y^{2} \equiv x^{3} + ax + b \pmod{p}$ | $\Delta = 4a^{3} + 27b^{2} \not\equiv 0$ | ECC |
| 16 | ECC Point Multiplication | $Q = k \cdot P$ (repeated addition) | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | ECDSA, ECDH |
| 17 | Key Size Comparison | RSA 2048 bits $\approx$ ECC 224 bits | Equivalent security | ECC advantage |

---

## 2.4 Why Public Key Cryptography Matters in Real Engineering

The transition from symmetric to asymmetric key cryptography is one of the most consequential paradigm shifts in modern engineering:

- **Internet Commerce (TLS/SSL Handshake):** Browsers and servers use RSA or ECC to exchange a symmetric session key, then encrypt bulk data with AES.
- **Digital Signatures in Software Distribution:** Operating system updates are signed with the vendor's private key; the OS verifies integrity using the embedded public key.
- **Blockchain & Cryptocurrencies:** Bitcoin and Ethereum use **ECDSA (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)** over the secp256k1 curve.
- **Smart Cards & National ID Programs (e.g., Aadhaar, ePassports):** ICAO-compliant ePassports embed PKI-signed biometric data.
- **Secure Email (PGP, S/MIME):** Combines symmetric session encryption with asymmetric key wrapping.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** Lightweight ECC enables authentication in resource-constrained sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** PKC is **never** used to encrypt bulk data directly because it is **100–1000× slower** than symmetric ciphers. Instead, PKC is used as a **key encapsulation mechanism (KEM)** to securely deliver a symmetric session key — a hybrid approach essential to modern protocols like TLS 1.3.

---

## 2.5 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric — A Critical Comparison Table

| Parameter | Symmetric Key Crypto | Asymmetric Key Crypto |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | 1 shared key per pair | Key pair per user (2 keys) |
| Key Distribution Problem | **Severe** — requires secure channel | **Resolved** — public key freely distributed |
| Number of Keys for $n$ Users | $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $2n$ (one pair per user) |
| Speed | **Fast** (100 MB/s – GB/s) | **Slow** (1–100 KB/s for RSA) |
| Primary Use | Bulk data encryption | Key exchange, digital signatures |
| Key Length (Equivalent Security) | AES-128 = 128 bits | RSA-3072 or ECC-256 |
| Example Algorithms | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal |
| Confidentiality Service | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes (using recipient's public key) |
| Authentication Service | ✗ No (MAC provides integrity only) | ✓ Yes (digital signatures) |
| Non-repudiation | ✗ Not provided | ✓ Provided (via signatures) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, step-by-step mathematical derivations** of the foundational results on which asymmetric cryptography rests. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Theorem: $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$

### Statement
For any integer $a$ with $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, we have $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Construct the reduced residue system modulo $n$.**
Since $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, multiplication by $a$ modulo $n$ defines a permutation of the integers in $\{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ that are coprime to $n$. Let us denote this set as:

$$R = \{r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_{\phi(n)}\}$$

where every $r_i$ satisfies $1 \le r_i \le n$ and $\gcd(r_i, n) = 1$. The cardinality of $R$ is $\phi(n)$ by definition.

**Step 2: Multiply each element by $a$ modulo $n$.**
Consider the set:

$$R' = \{a \cdot r_1 \bmod n,\ a \cdot r_2 \bmod n,\ \ldots,\ a \cdot r_{\phi(n)} \bmod n\}$$

Since $\gcd(a, n) = 1$ and each $\gcd(r_i, n) = 1$, every product $a \cdot r_i$ is also coprime to $n$. Therefore $R' \subseteq R$.

**Step 3: Show $R'$ is also a permutation of $R$.**
Suppose for contradiction that two elements coincide: $a \cdot r_i \equiv a \cdot r_j \pmod{n}$. Then $n \mid a(r_i - r_j)$, which combined with $\gcd(a, n) = 1$ implies $n \mid (r_i - r_j)$. But since $1 \le r_i, r_j \le n$, the only possibility is $r_i = r_j$. Hence all elements of $R'$ are distinct, and because $\vert R' \vert = \phi(n) = \vert R \vert$, we conclude $R' = R$ (as sets, modulo $n$).

**Step 4: Take the product over all elements of both sets.**
Because $R'$ is a permutation of $R$:

$$\prod_{i=1}^{\phi(n)} (a \cdot r_i \bmod n) \equiv \prod_{i=1}^{\phi(n)} r_i \pmod{n}$$

The product of all elements of $R'$ can be rewritten as:

$$a^{\phi(n)} \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{\phi(n)} r_i \equiv \prod_{i=1}^{\phi(n)} r_i \pmod{n}$$

**Step 5: Cancel the common factor $\prod r_i$.**
Since $\prod r_i$ is coprime to $n$ (each $r_i$ is coprime to $n$, so their product is too), we may cancel this factor on both sides:

$$a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

This completes the proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Fermat's Little Theorem (Special Case of Euler's Theorem)

### Statement
If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Apply Euler's theorem with $n = p$.**
Since $p$ is prime, every integer from $1$ to $p-1$ is coprime to $p$. Hence:

$$\phi(p) = p - 1$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Euler's theorem.**
By Euler's theorem:

$$a^{\phi(p)} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

$$a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of $\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for Distinct Primes

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Use the multiplicative property of Euler's totient.**
For coprime positive integers $m, n$:

$$\phi(mn) = \phi(m) \cdot \phi(n)$$

**Step 2: Apply this with $m = p$ and $n = q$.**
Since $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes, $\gcd(p, q) = 1$. Therefore:

$$\phi(pq) = \phi(p) \cdot \phi(q)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate each factor.**
For a prime $p$, every integer from $1$ to $p-1$ is coprime to $p$, so $\phi(p) = p - 1$. Similarly, $\phi(q) = q - 1$.

**Step 4: Conclude.**

$$\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$$

$\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of RSA Decryption Correctness

### Claim
If $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ and $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, then $C^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1: Express the relationship between $e$ and $d$.**
Since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, there exists an integer $k$ such that:

$$e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$$

**Step 2: Raise $C$ to the power $d$ modulo $n$.**
By definition of ciphertext:

$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \pmod{n}$$

$$C^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$.**

$$C^{d} \equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 4: Split the exponent.**

$$C^{d} \equiv M^{1} \cdot M^{k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n}$$

$$C^{d} \equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \pmod{n}$$

**Step 5: Apply Euler's theorem.**
Assuming $\gcd(M, n) = 1$ (which is ensured by RSA padding schemes like OAEP), Euler's theorem gives:

$$M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$$

**Step 6: Substitute and simplify.**

$$C^{d} \equiv M \cdot (1)^{k} \pmod{n}$$

$$C^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

This confirms that decryption recovers the original plaintext. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — RSA End-to-End

> [!NOTE]
> This **canonical KTU-style numerical** walks through RSA with deliberately small primes so the arithmetic remains tractable in an examination hall.

### Setup
Let $p = 61$, $q = 53$ (both prime).

### Step 1: Compute the modulus $n$.

$$n = p \times q = 61 \times 53$$

$$61 \times 53 = 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 = 3050 + 183 = 3233$$

Therefore $n = 3233$.

### Step 2: Compute Euler's totient $\phi(n)$.
Using $\phi(pq) = (p-1)(q-1)$:

$$\phi(n) = (61 - 1)(53 - 1) = 60 \times 52$$

$$60 \times 52 = 60 \times 50 + 60 \times 2 = 3000 + 120 = 3120$$

Therefore $\phi(n) = 3120$.

### Step 3: Choose the public exponent $e$.
Select $e = 17$ (commonly used in practice). Verify $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$:

- $3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9$
- $17 = 1 \times 9 + 8$
- $9 = 1 \times 8 + 1$
- $8 = 8 \times 1$

The gcd is $1$, so $e = 17$ is valid.

### Step 4: Compute the private exponent $d$.
Solve $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

**Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

$$3120 = 183 \times 17 + 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 9 = 3120 - 183 \times 17$$

$$17 = 1 \times 9 + 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 8 = 17 - 1 \times 9$$

$$9 = 1 \times 8 + 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 = 9 - 1 \times 8$$

Back-substitute:

$$1 = 9 - 8 = 9 - (17 - 9) = 2 \times 9 - 17 = 2(3120 - 183 \times 17) - 17$$

$$1 = 2 \times 3120 - 366 \times 17 - 17 = 2 \times 3120 - 367 \times 17$$

Therefore $d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 \pmod{3120}$:

$$d = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

Verify: $17 \times 2753 = 46801$. And $46801 \div 3120 = 15$ remainder $1$. ✓

### Step 5: Encrypt the message $M = 65$.
Compute $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 65^{17} \mod 3233$.

We use **repeated squaring modulo $n$**:

$$65^{1} \equiv 65 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{2} = 4225 \equiv 4225 - 3233 = 992 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{4} \equiv 992^{2} = 984064 \pmod{3233}$$
$$984064 \div 3233 \approx 304.38 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 304 \times 3233 = 982832$$
$$984064 - 982832 = 1232 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 65^{4} \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{8} \equiv 1232^{2} = 1517824 \pmod{3233}$$
$$1517824 \div 3233 \approx 469.48 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 469 \times 3233 = 1516277$$
$$1517824 - 1516277 = 1547 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 65^{8} \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$$
$$65^{16} \equiv 1547^{2} = 2393209 \pmod{3233}$$
$$2393209 \div 3233 \approx 740.27 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 740 \times 3233 = 2392420$$
$$2393209 - 2392420 = 789 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$$

Now $17 = 16 + 1$, so:

$$65^{17} \equiv 65^{16} \times 65^{1} \equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233}$$

$$789 \times 65 = 789 \times 60 + 789 \times 5 = 47340 + 3945 = 51285$$

$$51285 \mod 3233: \quad 51285 \div 3233 \approx 15.86 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 15 \times 3233 = 48495$$

$$51285 - 48495 = 2790 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C \equiv 2790 \pmod{3233}$$

So the ciphertext is $C = 2790$.

### Step 6: Decrypt to verify.
Compute $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 2790^{2753} \mod 3233$.

By the **correctness theorem** derived above, this must recover $M = 65$. The repeated-squaring computation would involve many intermediate steps; the verification is conceptual:

$$2790^{2753} \equiv 65 \pmod{3233} \quad \checkmark$$

### Summary of the Key Pair

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| Public Key $K_{pub}$ | $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$ |
| Private Key $K_{priv}$ | $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$ |
| Plaintext | $M = 65$ |
| Ciphertext | $C = 2790$ |

---

|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Hard Problem** | Integer Factorization | Discrete Logarithm | Discrete Logarithm | Elliptic Curve Discrete Log |
| **Key Size (≈128-bit security)** | 3072 bits | 3072 bits | 3072 bits | 256 bits |
| **Primary Service** | Encryption + Signature | Key Exchange | Encryption + Signature | Signature + Key Exchange |
| **Randomness Required** | No (deterministic) | No | Yes (per encryption) | Yes (per signature) |
| **Ciphertext Expansion** | None (1:1) | N/A (shared secret) | 2× plaintext | N/A / Signature |
| **Patent Status** | Expired 2000 | Expired 1997 | — | — |
| **Standard Body** | PKCS#1, FIPS 186 | RFC 2631 | — | FIPS 186, SECG, NIST |
