topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elliptic Curve Cryptography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)** is a branch of modern **public-key cryptography** whose security is founded on the algebraic hardness of the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)** over a finite field. The mathematical object at its heart is a non-singular elliptic curve, most commonly expressed in **short Weierstrass form**:

$$E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b, \quad \text{over a finite field } \mathbb{F}_p \text{ or } \mathbb{F}_{2^m}$$

For the curve to be **non-singular** (i.e., have no cusps or self-intersections that would break the group law), its parameters must satisfy the **discriminant condition**:

$$\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$$

The set of all points $(x, y)$ on $E$ together with a special identity element called the **point at infinity** ($\mathcal{O}$) forms an **abelian group** under a chord-and-tangent addition operation. This group is denoted $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$, and its cardinality $N = \vert E(\mathbb{F}_p) \vert$ is called the **order of the curve**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The KTU Module-2 syllabus explicitly expects students to master (a) elliptic curve arithmetic over prime fields $\mathbb{F}_p$, (b) point addition and scalar multiplication, (c) the ECDLP and its computational hardness, and (d) the practical protocols **ECDH** and **ECDSA**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bouncing Trampoline"

Imagine an invisible trampoline stretched over a finite grid. A ball is dropped at a starting point $P$ on the trampoline, and it bounces along the trampoline in a deterministic, geometrically-defined manner until it lands at a new point. Suppose we do this $k$ times, arriving at a destination point $Q = kP$.

- **Easy direction (Forward):** Given $P$ and $k$, simulate the bounces — anyone can compute $Q = kP$ in roughly $\log_2 k$ steps using a **double-and-add** strategy. This is the public operation.
- **Hard direction (Reverse):** Given only $P$ and $Q$, find the secret $k$. This is the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem**. On a well-chosen curve of cryptographic grade, no known classical algorithm can solve it in sub-exponential time.

This asymmetry — easy multiplication, hard discrete logarithm — is the precise mirror image of the RSA trapdoor (easy exponentiation, hard integer factorisation). ECC exploits it with a tiny key size: **256-bit ECC** offers comparable security to **3072-bit RSA** (both rated at ~128-bit security by NIST).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Length Comparison (NIST SP 800-57 Equivalence):**
> **128-bit** security → RSA-3072, DH-3072, **ECC-256**
> **192-bit** security → RSA-7680, DH-7680, **ECC-384**
> **256-bit** security → RSA-15360, DH-15360, **ECC-521**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Group Law — Geometric Intuition

Given an elliptic curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$, the points on $E$ form an abelian group under the **chord-and-tangent rule**:

1. **Point Addition ($P + Q$, where $P \neq Q$):** Draw a straight line through $P$ and $Q$. It intersects the curve at a *third* point $R'$. Then $P + Q$ is defined as the **reflection of $R'$** across the x-axis. Formally, $P + Q + R' = \mathcal{O}$.
2. **Point Doubling ($2P$, where $P = Q$):** Draw the **tangent line** at $P$. It intersects the curve at a second point $R'$. Then $2P$ is the reflection of $R'$ across the x-axis.
3. **Identity Element ($\mathcal{O}$):** The "point at infinity" serves as the additive identity. For every point $P$, we have $P + \mathcal{O} = P$.
4. **Inverse:** The inverse of $P = (x, y)$ is $-P = (x, -y \bmod p)$. So $P + (-P) = \mathcal{O}$.
5. **Closure:** All arithmetic is performed modulo $p$, keeping every intermediate result inside $\mathbb{F}_p$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Group Axioms Verified**
> Closure, Associativity, Identity ($\mathcal{O}$), and Inverses are all provably satisfied — making $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ a **finite abelian group**, the foundation that allows ECC to work.

## 2.2 Algebraic Formulas for the Group Law

### (a) Point Addition: $R = P + Q$ where $P = (x_1, y_1)$, $Q = (x_2, y_2)$, $P \neq \pm Q$

$$\lambda = (y_2 - y_1) \cdot (x_2 - x_1)^{-1} \pmod p$$

$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p$$

$$y_3 = \lambda \cdot (x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$$

### (b) Point Doubling: $R = 2P$ where $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $y_1 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$

$$\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a) \cdot (2y_1)^{-1} \pmod p$$

$$x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1 \pmod p$$

$$y_3 = \lambda \cdot (x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$$

### (c) Scalar Multiplication: $kP = P + P + \cdots + P$ ($k$ times)

Implemented using the **Double-and-Add algorithm** in $O(\log_2 k)$ point operations — the same asymptotic efficiency as fast exponentiation in RSA.

### (d) Hardness Anchor — ECDLP

Given $P$ and $Q = kP$, find $k$. Best known classical attack (Pollard's rho) runs in $O(\sqrt{N})$ steps where $N$ is the order of the subgroup. For a 256-bit curve, this is $2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Short Weierstrass Curve | $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$ | Operates over $\mathbb{F}_p$ for prime $p > 3$ |
| 2 | Non-Singularity (Discriminant) | $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$ | If $\Delta = 0$, the curve is singular — discard it |
| 3 | Identity Element | $\mathcal{O}$ (point at infinity) | Plays the role of $0$ in the additive group |
| 4 | Point Inverse | $-(x, y) = (x, -y \bmod p)$ | Reflection across x-axis |
| 5 | Slope — Addition | $\lambda \equiv (y_2 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)^{-1} \pmod p$ | Valid when $x_1 \neq x_2$ |
| 6 | Slope — Doubling | $\lambda \equiv (3x_1^2 + a)(2y_1)^{-1} \pmod p$ | Valid when $y_1 \not\equiv 0$ |
| 7 | Resulting x-Coordinate | $x_3 \equiv \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p$ | For addition; subtract $2x_1$ for doubling |
| 8 | Resulting y-Coordinate | $y_3 \equiv \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p$ | Reflects the intersection across x-axis |
| 9 | Order of Curve | $N = \vert E(\mathbb{F}_p) \vert$ | Counted via Schoof's or baby-step-giant-step |
| 10 | Hasse's Theorem | $\vert N - (p + 1) \vert \leq 2\sqrt{p}$ | Bounds curve order close to field size |
| 11 | Cofactor | $h = N / n$, where $n$ is the order of base point $G$ | $h$ must be small (ideally $h = 1$) for security |
| 12 | Scalar Multiplication | $kP = P + P + \cdots + P$ ($k$ times) | Double-and-add runs in $O(\log_2 k)$ steps |
| 13 | ECDLP (Hard Problem) | Given $P, Q$, find $k$ such that $Q = kP$ | Best attack: Pollard rho, $O(\sqrt{n})$ |
| 14 | Security Equivalence | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA $\approx$ 128-bit AES | NIST SP 800-57 standard mapping |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

ECC powers the majority of modern secure internet traffic. Specific deployment venues:

- **TLS 1.3 handshakes:** X25519 and P-256 curves dominate.
- **Cryptocurrencies:** Bitcoin and Ethereum use **secp256k1** for digital signatures on every transaction.
- **Mobile & IoT:** ECC's small key footprint is critical for bandwidth- and power-constrained devices.
- **Smart cards & passports:** ePassports use ECDSA over P-256 for authentication.
- **Government / Defence:** NSA's Suite B (now CNSA) mandates ECC for classified communications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Point Addition Formula

**Setup.** Let $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$ be two distinct points on the elliptic curve

$$E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$$

with $x_1 \neq x_2$. We wish to compute $R = P + Q = (x_3, y_3)$ in $\mathbb{F}_p$.

### Step 1 — Geometric Construction

By the chord-and-tangent rule, we draw a straight line $\ell$ through $P$ and $Q$. This line intersects the curve at exactly one additional point $R' = (x_3', y_3')$. The sum $R = P + Q$ is the reflection of $R'$ across the x-axis, so

$$R = (x_3, y_3) = (x_3', -y_3' \bmod p)$$

### Step 2 — Equation of the Secant Line

A non-vertical line through $P$ and $Q$ has the form

$$y = \lambda x + \nu \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Line into the Curve

Plugging $y = \lambda x + \nu$ into $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$:

$$(\lambda x + \nu)^2 = x^3 + ax + b$$

Expanding the left side:

$$\lambda^2 x^2 + 2\lambda\nu x + \nu^2 = x^3 + ax + b$$

Rearranging into standard polynomial form:

$$x^3 - \lambda^2 x^2 + (a - 2\lambda\nu) x + (b - \nu^2) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Vieta's Formulas

A cubic $x^3 + Bx^2 + Cx + D = 0$ with roots $x_1, x_2, x_3'$ satisfies

$$x_1 + x_2 + x_3' = -B$$

In our polynomial, $B = -\lambda^2$, so:

$$x_1 + x_2 + x_3' = \lambda^2$$

Solving for the unknown third root:

$$x_3' = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2$$

Since $R$ is the reflection of $R'$, they share the same x-coordinate:

$$\boxed{x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p}$$

### Step 5 — Recover the y-Coordinate

Since $R'$ lies on the line $\ell$, its y-coordinate is $y_3' = \lambda x_3' + \nu$. We eliminate $\nu$ using the fact that $P$ lies on $\ell$ as well: $\nu = y_1 - \lambda x_1$. Therefore:

$$y_3' = \lambda x_3' + (y_1 - \lambda x_1) = \lambda(x_3' - x_1) + y_1$$

Reflecting across the x-axis:

$$y_3 = -y_3' = -\lambda(x_3' - x_1) - y_1 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1$$

$$\boxed{y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Sanity Check:** When $P = Q$ (the doubling case), the secant line degenerates into the tangent, and the slope $\lambda$ is obtained by implicit differentiation of $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b$, giving $2y \cdot dy/dx = 3x^2 + a$, hence $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a)(2y_1)^{-1}$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full ECDH Key Exchange on a Toy Curve

Let us walk through a complete ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman) key exchange using a small, hand-calculable curve.

### Given

- Curve: $E: y^2 = x^3 + x + 1 \pmod 5$
- Discriminant check: $4(1)^3 + 27(1)^2 = 31 \equiv 1 \pmod 5 \neq 0$ ✓ (non-singular)
- Base point $G = (0, 1)$ of order $n = 9$ (verified below by direct computation)

### Step A — Pre-compute the scalar multiples of $G$

We apply the doubling/addition formulas repeatedly. Note that modular inverses are needed: $2^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod 5$ (since $2 \cdot 3 = 6 \equiv 1$), and $4^{-1} \equiv 4 \pmod 5$ (since $4 \cdot 4 = 16 \equiv 1$).

**Computing $2G$ (Point Doubling at $(0,1)$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \cdot 0^2 + 1}{2 \cdot 1} = \frac{1}{2} \equiv 1 \cdot 3 = 3 \pmod 5$$

$$x_3 = 3^2 - 2 \cdot 0 = 9 \equiv 4 \pmod 5$$

$$y_3 = 3 \cdot (0 - 4) - 1 = -13 \equiv -13 + 15 = 2 \pmod 5$$

$$\boxed{2G = (4, 2)}$$

**Computing $3G = 2G + G$ (Addition of $(4,2)$ and $(0,1)$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1 - 2}{0 - 4} = \frac{-1}{-4} = \frac{1}{4} \equiv 1 \cdot 4 = 4 \pmod 5$$

$$x_3 = 4^2 - 4 - 0 = 12 \equiv 2 \pmod 5$$

$$y_3 = 4 \cdot (4 - 2) - 2 = 6 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$$

$$\boxed{3G = (2, 1)}$$

**Computing $4G = 3G + G$ (Addition of $(2,1)$ and $(0,1)$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1 - 1}{0 - 2} = 0$$

$$x_3 = 0 - 2 - 0 = -2 \equiv 3 \pmod 5$$

$$y_3 = 0 \cdot (2 - 3) - 1 = -1 \equiv 4 \pmod 5$$

$$\boxed{4G = (3, 4)}$$

**Computing $5G = 4G + G$ (Addition of $(3,4)$ and $(0,1)$):**

$$\lambda = \frac{1 - 4}{0 - 3} = \frac{-3}{-3} = 1$$

$$x_3 = 1 - 3 - 0 = -2 \equiv 3 \pmod 5$$

$$y_3 = 1 \cdot (3 - 3) - 4 = -4 \equiv 1 \pmod 5$$

$$\boxed{5G = (3, 1)}$$

**Computing $6G = 5G + G$, $7G = 6G + G$, $8G = 7G + G$** (analogous steps) yield:

$$6G = (2, 4), \quad 7G = (4, 3), \quad 8G = (0, 4) = -G$$

Finally, $9G = 8G + G = -G + G = \mathcal{O}$. So the order of $G$ is exactly $n = 9$.

### Step B — Alice and Bob Perform ECDH

- **Alice** picks secret scalar $n_A = 3$, computes her public key $P_A = 3G = (2, 1)$, and sends it.
- **Bob** picks secret scalar $n_B = 7$, computes his public key $P_B = 7G = (4, 3)$, and sends it.

**Alice's shared secret** — she multiplies Bob's public key by her secret:

$$S_A = n_A \cdot P_B = 3 \cdot (4, 3)$$

First, double $(4, 3)$:

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \cdot 16 + 1}{2 \cdot 3} = \frac{49}{6} \equiv \frac{4}{1} = 4 \pmod 5$$

$$x = 4^2 - 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \equiv 3 \pmod 5, \quad y = 4 \cdot (4 - 3) - 3 = 1$$

So $2 \cdot (4, 3) = (3, 1) = 5G$. Then add $(4, 3)$ once more:

$$\lambda = \frac{3 - 1}{4 - 3} = \frac{2}{1} = 2, \quad x = 4 - 3 - 4 = -3 \equiv 2, \quad y = 2(3 - 2) - 1 = 1$$

$$\boxed{S_A = (2, 1)}$$

**Bob's shared secret** — he multiplies Alice's public key by his secret:

$$S_B = n_B \cdot P_A = 7 \cdot (2, 1) = 7 \cdot 3G = 21G$$

Since $9G = \mathcal{O}$, we have $21G \equiv 21 \bmod 9 \cdot G = 3G$.

$$\boxed{S_B = (2, 1) = 3G}$$

**Both parties arrive at the identical point $(2, 1)$.** An eavesdropper who intercepts $P_A = (2, 1)$ and $P_B = (4, 3)$ would have to solve the ECDLP (find $n_A$ from $3G$ or $n_B$ from $7G$) — infeasible on a real cryptographic curve.

## 3.3 The Double-and-Add Algorithm (Scalar Multiplication)

To compute $kP$ efficiently, scan the bits of $k$ from MSB to LSB:

```
Initialise: Q = O (point at infinity)
For each bit of k (MSB first):
    Q = 2Q              (point doubling)
    If bit == 1:
        Q = Q + P       (point addition)
Return Q
```

Complexity: roughly $\log_2 k$ doublings and at most $\log_2 k$ additions → $O(\log_2 k)$ elliptic curve operations, mirroring square-and-multiply for RSA exponentiation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ECC System Architecture Flow

The block diagram below maps the end-to-end flow of an ECC-based cryptographic operation, from curve parameterisation to key exchange / signature verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[STEP 1 SELECT CURVE E OVER F_p]
    node2[STEP 2 CHOOSE CURVE PARAMETERS a AND b]
    node3[STEP 3 VERIFY DISCRIMINANT 4a cubed plus 27b squared NOT ZERO mod p]
    node4[STEP 4 COMPUTE ORDER N OF CURVE]
    node5[STEP 5 SELECT BASE POINT G OF ORDER n]
    node6[STEP 6 PUBLISH PUBLIC PARAMETERS E p G n]
    node7[STEP 7 EACH USER PICKS PRIVATE KEY d RANDOM IN 1 to n minus 1]
    node8[STEP 8 COMPUTE PUBLIC KEY Q EQUALS d TIMES G]
    node9[STEP 9 EXCHANGE OR DISTRIBUTE PUBLIC KEYS]
    node10[STEP 10 ECDH COMPUTE SHARED SECRET OR ECDSA SIGN AND VERIFY]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
```

## 4.2 Point Operation Flow (Addition vs. Doubling Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[INPUT TWO POINTS P AND Q ON E]
    nodeB{CHECK P EQUALS Q}
    nodeC{CHECK X1 EQUALS X2 AND Y1 EQUALS MINUS Y2}
    nodeD[RESULT IS POINT AT INFINITY O]
    nodeE[USE POINT ADDITION SLOPE y2 minus y1 OVER x2 minus x1]
    nodeF[USE POINT DOUBLING SLOPE 3x1 squared plus a OVER 2y1]
    nodeG[COMPUTE x3 AND y3 USING SHARED FORMULAS]
    nodeH[RETURN RESULT POINT R]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeC
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeD
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 ECDH Protocol Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    aliceStart[ALICE PRIVATE KEY nA]
    alicePub[ALICE PUBLIC KEY PA EQUALS nA TIMES G]
    bobStart[BOB PRIVATE KEY nB]
    bobPub[BOB PUBLIC KEY PB EQUALS nB TIMES G]
    exchangeA[ALICE SENDS PA TO BOB OVER PUBLIC CHANNEL]
    exchangeB[BOB SENDS PB TO ALICE OVER PUBLIC CHANNEL]
    aliceShared[ALICE COMPUTES SA EQUALS nA TIMES PB]
    bobShared[BOB COMPUTES SB EQUALS nB TIMES PA]
    sharedSecret[BOTH HOLD IDENTICAL SHARED SECRET S EQUALS nA nB TIMES G]

    aliceStart --> alicePub
    alicePub --> exchangeA
    exchangeA --> bobShared
    bobStart --> bobPub
    bobPub --> exchangeB
    exchangeB --> aliceShared
    aliceShared --> sharedSecret
    bobShared --> sharedSecret
```

## 4.4 ECC vs RSA — Security-Per-Bit Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeK[SYMMETRIC KEY BITS]
    nodeR[RSA DH KEY BITS]
    nodeE[ECC KEY BITS]

    nodeK80[80 bit AES]
    nodeR1024[RSA 1024]
    nodeE160[ECC 160]

    nodeK128[128 bit AES]
    nodeR3072[RSA 3072]
    nodeE256[ECC 256]

    nodeK192[192 bit AES]
    nodeR7680[RSA 7680]
    nodeE384[ECC 384]

    nodeK256[256 bit AES]
    nodeR15360[RSA 15360]
    nodeE521[ECC 521]

    nodeK --> nodeK80
    nodeK --> nodeK128
    nodeK --> nodeK192
    nodeK --> nodeK256
    nodeK80 --> nodeR1024
    nodeK80 --> nodeE160
    nodeK128 --> nodeR3072
    nodeK128 --> nodeE256
    nodeK192 --> nodeR7680
    nodeK192 --> nodeE384
    nodeK256 --> nodeR15360
    nodeK256 --> nodeE521
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] Define an elliptic curve over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$. State and justify the discriminant condition that must be satisfied.

**Model Answer:**

An **elliptic curve** $E$ over a finite prime field $\mathbb{F}_p$ (with $p > 3$) is the set of all points $(x, y) \in \mathbb{F}_p \times \mathbb{F}_p$ satisfying

$$E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$$

together with a special element $\mathcal{O}$ called the **point at infinity**, where $a, b \in \mathbb{F}_p$ are constants.

The curve is required to be **non-singular** (geometrically smooth — no cusps or self-intersections), which is guaranteed when the **discriminant**

$$\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$$

This condition ensures that the curve has a well-defined tangent at every point, which is essential for the **chord-and-tangent group law** to function correctly. If $\Delta = 0 \pmod p$, the curve is *singular* and its rational points do not form a group, making it unusable for cryptography.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)? Why is it considered computationally intractable?

**Model Answer:**

The **ECDLP** is defined as follows:

> Given an elliptic curve $E$ over a finite field, a base point $G$ of order $n$, and a point $Q \in E$, find an integer $k \in [1, n-1]$ such that $Q = kG$.

**Why ECDLP is hard:** The forward direction — computing $Q = kG$ given $k$ and $G$ — is computationally efficient using the **double-and-add** algorithm in $O(\log_2 k)$ group operations. The reverse direction — recovering $k$ from $Q$ and $G$ — has no known sub-exponential classical algorithm on a general elliptic curve. The best generic attack is **Pollard's rho** method, which runs in $O(\sqrt{n})$ steps, where $n$ is the order of the base point.

For a 256-bit curve ($n \approx 2^{256}$), this requires approximately $2^{128}$ operations — a workload so vast that even the world's fastest supercomputers cannot complete it in any feasible time. Unlike the integer factorisation problem underlying RSA (which is sub-exponentially attacked by the Number Field Sieve), ECDLP on a carefully chosen curve is believed to be **fully exponential**, giving ECC its remarkable strength-per-bit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the algebraic formulas for computing the **sum of two distinct points** on an elliptic curve $E: y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$, where $P = (x_1, y_1)$ and $Q = (x_2, y_2)$ with $x_1 \neq x_2$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Consider the curve $E: y^2 = x^3 + x + 1 \pmod 5$ with base point $G = (0, 1)$.
- (i) Verify that $G$ is on the curve.
- (ii) Compute $2G$, $3G$, and $4G$.
- (iii) Demonstrate an **ECDH key exchange** between Alice (secret $n_A = 3$) and Bob (secret $n_B = 7$), showing that both arrive at the same shared secret.

**Model Solution to Q.A (a):**

The complete step-by-step derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key results are:

$$\boxed{\lambda = (y_2 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)^{-1} \pmod p}$$

$$\boxed{x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2 \pmod p}$$

$$\boxed{y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 \pmod p}$$

The derivation proceeds by drawing a secant line through $P$ and $Q$, substituting $y = \lambda x + \nu$ into the curve equation, expanding, and applying **Vieta's formula** to the resulting cubic so that the sum of the three intersection x-coordinates equals $\lambda^2$, isolating the third root, and finally reflecting across the x-axis to obtain $y_3$. *(Full algebraic detail appears in Section 3.1.)*

**Model Solution to Q.A (b):**

**(i) Verification of $G = (0, 1)$:**

$$y^2 = 1^2 = 1, \quad x^3 + x + 1 = 0 + 0 + 1 = 1, \quad 1 \equiv 1 \pmod 5 \quad \checkmark$$

**(ii) Computing $2G$, $3G$, $4G$:** (see Section 3.2, Step A)

$$2G = (4, 2), \quad 3G = (2, 1), \quad 4G = (3, 4)$$

**(iii) ECDH demonstration:** (see Section 3.2, Step B)

- Alice: $P_A = 3G = (2, 1)$, Bob: $P_B = 7G = (4, 3)$.
- Alice computes $S_A = 3 \cdot (4, 3) = (2, 1)$.
- Bob computes $S_B = 7 \cdot (2, 1) = 21G = 3G = (2, 1)$.
- **Shared secret = $(2, 1)$.** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024 — Alternate]

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)**. List all the parameters involved, the signature generation algorithm, and the verification algorithm in detail.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Compare ECC with RSA in terms of **key size, security level, computational efficiency, and real-world applications**. Use a comparative table and discuss why ECC is preferred for resource-constrained environments.

**Model Solution to Q.B (a):**

**ECDSA Parameters:** Curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$, base point $G$ of order $n$, hash function $H$ (typically SHA-256), and per-user key pair: **private key** $d$ (random integer in $[1, n-1]$) and **public key** $Q = dG$.

**Signature Generation (signer holds private key $d$, signs message $m$):**

1. Select a random integer $k \in [1, n-1]$.
2. Compute the curve point $(x_1, y_1) = kG$.
3. Compute $r = x_1 \bmod n$. If $r = 0$, restart with a new $k$.
4. Compute $s = k^{-1}(H(m) + d \cdot r) \bmod n$. If $s = 0$, restart with a new $k$.
5. Output the signature as the pair $(r, s)$.

**Signature Verification (verifier holds public key $Q$, signature $(r, s)$, message $m$):**

1. Verify that $r, s \in [1, n-1]$. If not, reject.
2. Compute $w = s^{-1} \bmod n$.
3. Compute $u_1 = H(m) \cdot w \bmod n$ and $u_2 = r \cdot w \bmod n$.
4. Compute the curve point $(x_1, y_1) = u_1 G + u_2 Q$.
5. Compute $v = x_1 \bmod n$.
6. Accept the signature if and only if $v = r$.

**Why it works:** For a valid signature, $u_1 G + u_2 Q = u_1 G + u_2 (dG) = (u_1 + u_2 d)G = kG$, because algebraic manipulation of the verification equation recovers $kG$, matching $r$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (ECDSA):** Students commonly lose marks by **reusing $k$ across signatures** — this leaks the private key $d$ (as famously happened in the 2010 Sony PlayStation 3 hack). Each signature MUST use a fresh random $k$. KTU examiners specifically test this on the 7-mark question by asking "What happens if $k$ is reused?"

**Model Solution to Q.B (b):**

| Criterion | ECC | RSA |
|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Hard Problem** | Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm (ECDLP) | Integer Factorisation (IFP) |
| **Key Size for 128-bit Security** | **256 bits** | **3072 bits** |
| **Best Known Attack** | Pollard rho: $O(\sqrt{n})$ — fully exponential | General Number Field Sieve: sub-exponential $e^{O((\ln n)^{1/3}(\ln \ln n)^{2/3})}$ |
| **Key Generation Speed** | Fast (random scalar multiplication) | Slow (must find two large primes via primality testing) |
| **Signature Size** | ~512 bits (two 256-bit integers $r, s$) | ~2048 bits (entire modulus) |
| **Computational Cost per Operation** | Lower for equivalent security | Higher |
| **Memory / Bandwidth Footprint** | Very small — ideal for mobile, IoT, smart cards | Large — problematic for constrained devices |
| **Standardised Curves** | NIST P-256, Curve25519, secp256k1 | PKCS #1, FIPS 186 |
| **Patent Status** | Initially patent-encumbered (expired) | Expired long ago |
| **Real-World Adoption** | TLS 1.3, Bitcoin, Ethereum, Apple iMessage, ePassports | TLS legacy, SSL certificates, PGP, S/MIME |
| **Vulnerability to Quantum Attack** | Broken by Shor's algorithm (both, in polynomial time) | Broken by Shor's algorithm (both, in polynomial time) |

**Why ECC is preferred for resource-constrained environments:** A 256-bit ECC private key is just 32 bytes, while an equivalent-security 3072-bit RSA modulus is 384 bytes — a **12× reduction** in storage and transmission cost. Operations on a small-curve point require only a handful of modular multiplications on 256-bit integers, whereas RSA-3072 needs modular exponentiation on 3072-bit integers — orders of magnitude more arithmetic per signature. This makes ECC the de facto choice for mobile devices, embedded systems, RFID tags, and IoT sensors.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in ECC Questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the discriminant check:** Many students omit verifying $4a^3 + 27b^2 \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$. Examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Not reducing modulo $p$ at every step:** Each intermediate result ($\lambda$, $x_3$, $y_3$) MUST be taken mod $p$. A common error is to compute $\lambda$ as a fraction without reducing the inverse.
> 3. **Confusing addition and doubling formulas:** When $P = Q$, you must use the **doubling slope** $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a)(2y_1)^{-1}$, not the addition slope. Examiners test this trap explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step in ECDH:** Always explicitly compute **both** sides of the shared secret and state they match. Showing only one side loses at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing ECC and RSA parameter sizes:** State the **NIST equivalence** explicitly (e.g., "256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA for 128-bit security"). Vague phrases like "ECC is faster" without numbers score poorly.
> 6. **Mixing additive and multiplicative notation:** ECDH is described **additively** ($S = n_A \cdot P_B$), not multiplicatively like classical Diffie–Hellman. Writing $S = P_B^{n_A}$ will be marked wrong.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Curve equation (short Weierstrass):** $y^2 = x^3 + ax + b \pmod p$, with the **discriminant condition** $\Delta = -16(4a^3 + 27b^2) \not\equiv 0 \pmod p$.
- **Group identity:** $\mathcal{O}$ (point at infinity); **inverse:** $-(x, y) = (x, -y \bmod p)$.
- **Point addition** (distinct $P \neq Q$): slope $\lambda = (y_2 - y_1)(x_2 - x_1)^{-1} \bmod p$, then $x_3 = \lambda^2 - x_1 - x_2$, $y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1$.
- **Point doubling** ($P = Q$): slope $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a)(2y_1)^{-1} \bmod p$, then $x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1$, $y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1$.
- **Scalar multiplication** $kP$: implemented via **double-and-add** in $O(\log_2 k)$ steps.
- **ECDLP:** Given $P, Q$ find $k$ such that $Q = kP$; best attack is Pollard-rho in $O(\sqrt{n})$ — exponential in key bits.
- **Hasse's Theorem:** Curve order $N$ satisfies $\vert N - (p+1) \vert \leq 2\sqrt{p}$.
- **Cofactor $h = N/n$ must be small** (ideally 1) for cryptographic curves; otherwise small-subgroup attacks apply.
- **ECDH protocol:** Each party multiplies the other's public key by their own private scalar; both arrive at the same shared secret $n_A n_B G$.
- **ECDSA:** Signature = $(r, s)$; $r$ comes from $kG$, $s$ involves hash, private key, and $r$; verification reconstructs $kG$ from $Q$ and checks $x_1 \equiv r \pmod n$.
- **NIST equivalence (must memorise):** 80-bit → ECC-160 / RSA-1024; 128-bit → **ECC-256 / RSA-3072**; 192-bit → ECC-384 / RSA-7680; 256-bit → ECC-521 / RSA-15360.
- **Quantum vulnerability:** Both ECC and RSA are broken in polynomial time by Shor's algorithm — post-quantum cryptography (lattice-based, code-based, hash-based schemes) is the future direction being standardised by NIST.
- **Standardised curves in practice:** NIST P-256, P-384, P-521; Curve25519 (X25519 for ECDH); Ed25519 for signatures; **secp256k1** for Bitcoin/Ethereum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hash Function Requirements and Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hash Function Requirements and Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Hash Function Requirements and Properties

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptographic Hash Function (KTU 2024 Definition):** A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a deterministic mathematical transformation that accepts an input (message) $M$ of arbitrary, finite length and produces a fixed-length output $h = H(M)$, where $h$ is called the **message digest**, **hash value**, or **fingerprint**, with the output length being independent of the input length.

Formally expressed as:
$$H : \{0,1\}^* \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$

where $\{0,1\}^*$ denotes the set of all binary strings of any length, and $\{0,1\}^n$ denotes the set of all binary strings of exactly $n$ bits. For a standard secure algorithm such as **SHA-256**, the output width is **n = 256 bits**, yielding $2^{256}$ possible digests.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Digital Fingerprint

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a human fingerprint. Every person on Earth has a **unique** fingerprint (collision-free), it is **always the same size** regardless of the person (fixed-length), and you **cannot reconstruct the person from their fingerprint** (one-way). A cryptographic hash function is essentially a "digital fingerprint" for any piece of data — a file, a password, a transaction, or a message. Two completely different documents will essentially never produce the same fingerprint, and even flipping a single bit in the document will completely scramble the fingerprint.

## 1.3 Why Hash Functions Are Required in Cryptography

| Application Area | Role of Hash Function |
|------------------|------------------------|
| **Digital Signatures** | Sign the hash instead of the full message for efficiency |
| **Password Storage** | Store $H(\text{password})$ instead of plaintext |
| **Data Integrity** | Detect any tampering via hash comparison |
| **Message Authentication (HMAC)** | Combine hash with a secret key for authentication |
| **Blockchain / Cryptocurrency** | Link blocks using hashes (Merkle trees) |
| **Key Derivation** | Derive cryptographic keys from master secrets |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Examiners frequently test whether a student can clearly differentiate between a **regular hash function** (e.g., used in hash tables) and a **cryptographic hash function**. The former only requires uniform distribution; the latter requires the three security properties discussed in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — The Three Security Properties

A function $H$ qualifies as a **cryptographic** hash function only if it satisfies three mandatory security requirements, often called the **"CIA Triad of Hashing"** — *Preimage Resistance*, *Second Preimage Resistance*, and *Collision Resistance*. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as foundational and tests them regularly.

## 2.1 Property 1 — Preimage Resistance (One-Way Property)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preimage Resistance:** For any given hash value $h$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find any message $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.

In simpler words, the hash function must be **one-way**: easy to compute forward ($M \rightarrow h$), but practically impossible to invert ($h \rightarrow M$).

- **Attacker Goal:** Given $h$, find *any* $M'$ such that $H(M') = h$.
- **Work Factor for Brute-Force Attack:** Approximately $2^n$ hash evaluations, where $n$ is the bit-length of the output.
- **Example Use Case:** Password verification. The server stores $H(\text{pwd})$; an attacker who steals the database cannot directly recover the password.

## 2.2 Property 2 — Second Preimage Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Second Preimage Resistance:** Given a specific message $M_1$, it must be **computationally infeasible** to find a *different* message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

- **Attacker Goal:** Given a chosen $M_1$, find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
- **Work Factor for Brute-Force Attack:** Approximately $2^n$ hash evaluations.
- **Example Use Case:** Digital signature forgery. If an attacker can find $M_2$ with the same hash as a signed $M_1$, they can claim the signature applies to $M_2$.

## 2.3 Property 3 — Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collision Resistance:** It must be **computationally infeasible** to find *any two distinct messages* $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

- **Attacker Goal:** Find *any* pair $(M_1, M_2)$ with $M_1 \neq M_2$ and $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
- **Work Factor for Brute-Force Attack (Birthday Bound):** Approximately $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ hash evaluations (via the **Birthday Paradox**).
- **Example Use Case:** Preventing two different contracts/documents from having the same digital fingerprint.

## 2.4 Secondary / Auxiliary Properties

Beyond the three core security properties, KTU 2024 also lists several **desirable properties**:

- **Determinism:** The same input $M$ must always produce the same hash $h$.
- **Fixed Output Length:** The digest size $n$ is constant, irrespective of input length.
- **Avalanche Effect:** A single bit change in $M$ should change roughly **50%** of the bits in $h$.
- **Pseudorandomness:** The hash output should be indistinguishable from a truly random bit string.
- **Efficiency:** The function must be fast to compute for any practical input size.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Use this table as a one-shot revision before the exam. The columns **n**, **Work Factor**, and **Attack Type** are the most frequently tested.

| Property | Formal Goal | Attacker Input | Brute-Force Work Factor | Example Failure Case |
|----------|-------------|----------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Preimage Resistance | Find $M : H(M) = h$ | Hash value $h$ only | $\approx 2^n$ | Inverting $H$ to recover password |
| Second Preimage Resistance | Find $M_2 \neq M_1 : H(M_2) = H(M_1)$ | Specific $M_1$ | $\approx 2^n$ | Forging alternate signed message |
| Collision Resistance | Find $M_1 \neq M_2 : H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ | Nothing (free choice) | $\approx 2^{n/2}$ (Birthday) | Two contracts with same hash |
| Avalanche Criterion | 1-bit flip $\Rightarrow \approx n/2$ bits change | Any $M$ | Statistical test | Linear / weak design |

> **Security levels for common hash algorithms (Kerala Board Reference):**
>
> $$\text{MD5 (n = 128 bits)} \Rightarrow \text{Collision resistance: } 2^{64} \text{ (BROKEN)}$$
> $$\text{SHA-1 (n = 160 bits)} \Rightarrow \text{Collision resistance: } 2^{80} \text{ (DEPRECATED)}$$
> $$\text{SHA-256 (n = 256 bits)} \Rightarrow \text{Collision resistance: } 2^{128} \text{ (SECURE)}$$
> $$\text{SHA-512 (n = 512 bits)} \Rightarrow \text{Collision resistance: } 2^{256} \text{ (HIGH SECURITY)}$$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking & Finance:** HMAC-SHA256 secures API requests and JWT tokens in financial software.
- **Cybersecurity:** Integrity verification of downloaded software (e.g., Linux ISO checksums).
- **Forensics:** Hashing disk images to prove evidence was not tampered with.
- **Blockchain:** Each block contains the SHA-256 hash of the previous block, ensuring immutability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

This section develops the *why* behind the $2^{n/2}$ birthday bound, the iterative Merkle–Damgård padding scheme, and the avalanche-effect quantitative test. Each step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

## 3.1 The Birthday Paradox — Why Collision Resistance is $2^{n/2}$

The Birthday Paradox states that in a group of only $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ randomly chosen people, the probability of at least two sharing a birthday exceeds **50%**. Applied to hashes, if we draw $k$ random $n$-bit hashes from a space of $N = 2^n$ possible values, the probability of **no collision** is:

$$P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=1}^{k-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

**Step 1.** Apply the standard approximation $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$ for small $x$:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{i}{N}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the arithmetic sum $\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} i = \frac{k(k-1)}{2}$:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{k(k-1)}{2N}\right)$$

**Step 3.** For large $k$, approximate $k(k-1) \approx k^2$:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Set the probability of a collision to 0.5 (i.e., $P(\text{no collision}) = 0.5$):

$$0.5 = \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Take the natural logarithm on both sides ($\ln 0.5 = -0.693$):

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{k^2}{2N} \;\Rightarrow\; -0.693 \approx -\frac{k^2}{2N}$$

**Step 6.** Solve for $k$:

$$k^2 \approx 1.386 \cdot N \;\Rightarrow\; k \approx \sqrt{1.386 \cdot N} \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 7.** Substitute $N = 2^n$ and take $\log_2$:

$$k \approx \sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why NIST mandates **at least 224-bit hashes** for digital signatures (to ensure $2^{112}$ collision resistance, well above the $2^{128}$ security floor required today) and why MD5 (128 bits, $2^{64}$ collision resistance) is utterly broken in 2024.

## 3.2 The Merkle–Damgård Iterative Construction

A cryptographic hash is rarely built as a single monolithic function. Instead, the message is **padded** and split into fixed-size **blocks** processed iteratively by a **compression function** $f$. The structure is:

**Step 1 — Padding:** Append a single **'1' bit** to the message, followed by $k$ **'0' bits**, where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that:

$$L + 1 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{b}$$

where $L$ is the original message length in bits and $b$ is the block size in bits (e.g., $b = 512$ for SHA-256).

**Step 2 — Append Length Block:** Append the 64-bit (or 128-bit for SHA-512) representation of the original message length $L$. Final padded length is a multiple of $b$.

**Step 3 — Iterative Processing:** The padded message is split into $t$ blocks $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_t$. With initial value $H_0 = IV$ (Initial Vector), compute for $i = 1$ to $t$:

$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$$

**Step 4 — Final Hash Output:** The final hash value is:

$$H(M) = H_t$$

The **avalanche property** of $f$ guarantees that any change in $M_i$ propagates forward, scrambling all subsequent $H_j$ values.

## 3.3 The Avalanche Effect — Quantitative Test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):** A hash function exhibits the **Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC)** if, for every input bit $i$, flipping that single bit changes *each* output bit with probability exactly **0.5**.

**Verification Procedure:**

1. Choose a random message $M$ of length $L$ bits.
2. Compute $h = H(M)$.
3. For each $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, L\}$:
   - Flip bit $i$ to obtain $M^{(i)}$.
   - Compute $h^{(i)} = H(M^{(i)})$.
   - Count $d_i = $ Hamming distance between $h$ and $h^{(i)}$.
4. Compute the average:

$$\bar{d} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{i=1}^{L} d_i$$

**Expected Result for an Ideal Hash:** $\bar{d} \approx n/2$, where $n$ is the output bit-length. For SHA-256, $\bar{d} \approx 128$ bits, with each individual bit flipped approximately 50% of the time.

> **Worked KTU-style Example:** If you flip **one bit** of an input to SHA-256, the resulting 256-bit digest differs from the original in, on average, **128 bits** — a complete visual scramble.

## 3.4 Strength Hierarchy of the Three Properties

Mathematically, the three properties are not equal in strength:

$$\text{Collision Resistance} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Second Preimage Resistance} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Preimage Resistance}$$

The arrow $\Rightarrow$ means "*implies*". If a hash function is collision-resistant, it is automatically second-preimage-resistant; if it is second-preimage-resistant, it is automatically preimage-resistant. The **reverse** is not true — preimage resistance alone does **not** guarantee collision resistance. This hierarchy is a frequent 7-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Hash Function Block Diagram

The diagram below illustrates the end-to-end transformation of an arbitrary-length message into a fixed-length digest, mirroring the Merkle–Damgård iterative construction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Arbitrary Length Input Message M] --> nodeB[Step 1 Padding Append 1 bit then k zeros]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2 Append 64 bit Length Block]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Padded Message Split into t Blocks of b bits]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Block M1]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Block M2]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Block Mt]
    nodeH[Initial Vector IV of n bits] --> nodeI[Compression Function f Stage 1]
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Intermediate Hash H1 of n bits]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compression Function f Stage 2]
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Intermediate Hash H2 of n bits]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Final Compression Function f Stage t]
    nodeG --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Final Hash Digest H of n bits]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Output Fixed Length n bit Hash]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Property Verification Flow

The following schematic maps how a security auditor or examiner would verify the three core properties for a given hash algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Hash Function Under Test H] --> chk1{Property 1 Preimage Resistance}
    chk1 -->|Try Inversion| op1[Random hash h chosen]
    op1 -->|Attempt 2 to n operations| out1[Computationally Infeasible to find M]
    startA --> chk2{Property 2 Second Preimage Resistance}
    chk2 -->|Fix M1| op2[Chosen message M1 given]
    op2 -->|Attempt 2 to n operations| out2[Cannot find M2 with same hash]
    startA --> chk3{Property 3 Collision Resistance}
    chk3 -->|Free Pair Search| op3[Birthday Attack 2 to n/2]
    op3 -->|Attempt Birthday Bound| out3[No pair M1 M2 with same hash found]
    out1 --> verdict[Algorithm is Cryptographically Secure]
    out2 --> verdict
    out3 --> verdict
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Regular vs. Cryptographic Hash Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG[Regular Hash Function e.g. CRC32, Hash Table]
        r1[Fast Computation] --> r2[Uniform Distribution]
        r2 --> r3[No Security Properties]
        r3 --> r4[Collisions are Acceptable and Expected]
    end
    subgraph CRYP[Cryptographic Hash Function e.g. SHA 256]
        c1[Deterministic] --> c2[Fixed Output Length]
        c2 --> c3[Preimage Resistance]
        c3 --> c4[Second Preimage Resistance]
        c4 --> c5[Collision Resistance]
        c5 --> c6[Avalanche Effect]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cue for Exams:** When a KTU question asks "*Why is CRC32 not used in cryptography?*" — point to the absence of the three security properties and the deliberate presence of easy-to-find collisions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define a cryptographic hash function. List its three fundamental security properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A cryptographic hash function $H$ is a deterministic function that maps an input $M$ of arbitrary finite length to a fixed-length output $h = H(M)$ of $n$ bits, where $n$ is the algorithm parameter (e.g., 256 for SHA-256). It must satisfy three fundamental security properties:

**(i) Preimage Resistance:** Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $M$ such that $H(M) = h$.

**(ii) Second Preimage Resistance:** Given $M_1$, it is computationally infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

**(iii) Collision Resistance:** It is computationally infeasible to find any two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between preimage resistance and collision resistance. Why is collision resistance considered the strongest property?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preimage Resistance | Collision Resistance |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Attacker Input** | A given hash value $h$ | Nothing — free choice |
| **Goal** | Find $M$ such that $H(M) = h$ | Find $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ |
| **Brute-Force Work** | $2^n$ | $2^{n/2}$ (Birthday attack) |
| **Easier to Break?** | Harder | Easier (fewer trials) |

Collision resistance is considered the **strongest property in the sense that it is the hardest to achieve and easiest to attack**, because the Birthday Paradox reduces the attack cost from $2^n$ to $2^{n/2}$. However, in the formal implication hierarchy, collision resistance *implies* second preimage resistance, which in turn *implies* preimage resistance.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three security requirements (properties) of a cryptographic hash function, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the iterative Merkle–Damgård construction of a hash function. How does padding ensure message uniqueness? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

The three security requirements of a cryptographic hash function are:

**1. Preimage Resistance (One-Way Property) — 2 marks:**
For a hash function $H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$, given any hash value $h$, it should be computationally infeasible to find a preimage $M$ such that $H(M) = h$. The brute-force effort required is approximately $2^n$ operations. **Example:** Password storage — the server stores $H(\text{password})$ so that even if the database is leaked, recovering the original password is infeasible.

**2. Second Preimage Resistance (Weak Collision Resistance) — 2 marks:**
Given a specific message $M_1$, it must be computationally infeasible to find a different message $M_2 \neq M_1$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. Brute-force effort is approximately $2^n$ operations. **Example:** Digital signature — if an attacker could find $M_2$ with the same hash as the signed $M_1$, the signature would be transferable to a different document.

**3. Collision Resistance (Strong Collision Resistance) — 2 marks:**
It must be computationally infeasible to find *any* two distinct messages $M_1$ and $M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$. By the Birthday Paradox, brute-force effort is approximately $2^{n/2}$ operations. **Example:** Two legal contracts crafted to have identical digests, defeating digital signature integrity.

**Conclusion — 1 mark:** All three properties are required to consider a function cryptographically secure. A violation of any one breaks the suitability of the algorithm for security applications.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Merkle–Damgård Construction Steps — 5 marks:**

The Merkle–Damgård construction processes the input message in fixed-size blocks using a compression function. The steps are:

**Step 1 — Padding:** Append a single **'1' bit** to the message, followed by the minimum number of **'0' bits** $k$ such that the total length $L + 1 + k \equiv 0 \pmod{b}$, where $b$ is the block size in bits (e.g., 512 for SHA-256).

**Step 2 — Append Length:** Append a 64-bit (or 128-bit) block representing the original message length $L$. This ensures that messages of different lengths but identical content cannot yield the same padded form (uniqueness).

**Step 3 — Block Division:** Split the padded message into $t$ blocks of $b$ bits each: $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_t$.

**Step 4 — Iterative Compression:** With $H_0 = IV$ (a fixed initial value), compute iteratively:

$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, t$$

where $f$ is the compression function that takes an $n$-bit chaining value and a $b$-bit message block and outputs a new $n$-bit chaining value.

**Step 5 — Final Output:** The final hash digest is $H(M) = H_t$, a fixed $n$-bit value.

**Padding and Uniqueness — 2 marks:** The padding scheme guarantees that no two different messages of any length can produce the same padded block sequence. The **'1' bit followed by zeros** unambiguously marks the end of the original message, and the **length block** disambiguates messages that differ only by trailing zeros. Without the length block, messages "abc" and "abc0...0" of different lengths would map to the same internal state.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is the Birthday Paradox? How does it affect the security of an $n$-bit hash function? Calculate the number of trials required to find a collision in a 128-bit hash with 50% probability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Avalanche Effect and Strict Avalanche Criterion. Why is it critical for cryptographic hash functions? Show with an example how a single-bit change impacts the SHA-256 output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Birthday Paradox Definition — 1 mark:**
The Birthday Paradox is a counterintuitive probability result stating that in a set of randomly chosen items drawn from a universe of $N$ equally likely possibilities, a collision (two items being identical) becomes likely after only about $\sqrt{N}$ trials, rather than the $N$ trials one might naively expect.

**Effect on Hash Security — 2 marks:**
For an $n$-bit hash function, the output space is $N = 2^n$. A naive expectation suggests an attacker would need $2^n$ trials to find a collision. However, the Birthday Paradox shows that only $2^{n/2}$ trials are needed for a 50% probability of collision. This effectively **halves the bit-strength** of the hash for collision resistance.

**Calculation for 128-bit hash — 4 marks:**

We have $n = 128$, so $N = 2^{128}$.

The probability of **no collision** in $k$ trials is:

$$P(\text{no collision}) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2N}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2 \cdot 2^{128}}\right)$$

Setting $P(\text{no collision}) = 0.5$:

$$0.5 = \exp\left(-\frac{k^2}{2^{129}}\right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{k^2}{2^{129}}$$

$$-0.693 = -\frac{k^2}{2^{129}}$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k^2 = 0.693 \cdot 2^{129} = 2^{129} \cdot 2^{-0.529} \approx 2^{128.471}$$

$$k = 2^{64.235} \approx 2^{64}$$

**Result:** Approximately $2^{64}$ trials are required to find a collision in a 128-bit hash with 50% probability. This is precisely why **MD5 (128-bit) is broken** and why modern designs use at least 256-bit output to obtain $2^{128}$ collision security.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Avalanche Effect — 2 marks:**
The Avalanche Effect is a desirable property of cryptographic hash functions and block ciphers in which a small change in the input (e.g., flipping a single bit) causes a significant and unpredictable change in the output — typically affecting **about half** of the output bits.

**Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC) — 2 marks:**
The Strict Avalanche Criterion, formalized by Webster and Tavares in 1995, requires that for every input bit $i$, flipping bit $i$ changes *each* output bit with probability **exactly 0.5**. This is a stronger formalization of the avalanche property and ensures uniform diffusion throughout the algorithm.

**Why It Is Critical — 2 marks:**
The avalanche effect is critical because it ensures that no statistical correlation exists between the input and output. Without it, an attacker could exploit partial knowledge of the input to predict the output, or craft inputs that produce only localized changes in the digest. Strong avalanche is what makes hash outputs appear pseudorandom and prevents differential cryptanalysis.

**Example — 1 mark:**
For **SHA-256("Hello")** and **SHA-256("hello")** (only the case of the first letter differs):

$$\text{SHA-256("Hello")} = \text{185f8db32271fe25f561a6fc938b2e264306ec304eda518007d1764826381969}$$
$$\text{SHA-256("hello")} = \text{2cf24dba5fb0a30e26e83b2ac5b9e29e1b161e5c1fa7425e73043362938b9824}$$

The two digests are **completely different**, with no discernible relationship — illustrating the avalanche effect in action.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing that "preimage resistance is the strongest property." This is **wrong** — collision resistance is harder to provide but easier to attack, and the correct hierarchy is *Collision $\Rightarrow$ Second Preimage $\Rightarrow$ Preimage*.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **second preimage resistance** with **collision resistance**. The former fixes $M_1$; the latter allows the attacker free choice of both messages.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **length block** in Merkle–Damgård padding — this is a 1–2 mark loss. The 64-bit (or 128-bit) length is **mandatory** for uniqueness.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In Birthday Paradox calculations, forgetting to **square root** the search space. Write the formula derivation, do not just state $2^{n/2}$.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Listing CRC, MD5, or SHA-1 as *currently secure* — they are all deprecated for cryptographic use as of 2024.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision List — Use Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- A **cryptographic hash function** maps any-length input to a **fixed-length** output, deterministically.
- The **three security properties** are: (1) Preimage Resistance, (2) Second Preimage Resistance, (3) Collision Resistance.
- The **strength hierarchy** is: Collision Resistance $\Rightarrow$ Second Preimage $\Rightarrow$ Preimage.
- Brute-force work factors: Preimage = $2^n$, Second Preimage = $2^n$, Collision = $2^{n/2}$ (Birthday).
- The **Birthday Paradox** reduces collision attack cost from $2^n$ to $2^{n/2}$ — this is why hash output must be at least 256 bits today.
- The **Merkle–Damgård construction** processes messages in fixed-size blocks via a compression function $f$, using **padding + length append** to ensure uniqueness.
- The **Avalanche Effect** requires that flipping one input bit changes approximately $n/2$ output bits.
- The **Strict Avalanche Criterion (SAC)** requires each output bit to flip with probability 0.5 for any single input bit flip.
- **MD5 (128 bits) is broken**; **SHA-1 (160 bits) is deprecated**; use **SHA-256 or SHA-3** for modern systems.
- Common applications: **digital signatures, password hashing (with salt), HMAC, blockchain, integrity verification**.
- **Determinism, fixed output length, efficiency, pseudorandomness** are auxiliary but expected properties.
- Always remember the four-stage pipeline: **Padding $\rightarrow$ Length Append $\rightarrow$ Block Splitting $\rightarrow$ Iterative Compression**.
- KTU-specific: questions on hash properties are usually worth 7 marks; Birthday Paradox derivations are common 7-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SHA-256
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SHA-256

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit)** is a cryptographic hash function standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** as part of the **FIPS PUB 180-4** specification. It belongs to the **SHA-2 family** of hash functions and produces a fixed-length output of **256 bits (32 bytes)**, regardless of the size of the input message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> SHA-256 is a **deterministic**, **one-way** hash function that satisfies the classical three security properties of cryptographic hash functions: **Pre-image resistance**, **Second pre-image resistance**, and **Collision resistance**.

Formally, SHA-256 can be defined as the mapping:

$$H : \{0,1\}^* \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$$

where $\{0,1\}^*$ denotes the set of all binary strings of arbitrary length, and $\{0,1\}^{256}$ denotes the set of all 256-bit binary strings.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Digital Fingerprint" Machine

Imagine SHA-256 as a **high-precision fingerprinting machine**:
- You feed it **any document** (a single page, an entire encyclopedia, or even a single character).
- The machine processes it through a complex series of chemical reactions (the compression rounds).
- It outputs a **unique 256-bit fingerprint** — a fixed-length identifier that uniquely represents the input.
- The process is **one-way**: you cannot reconstruct the original document from its fingerprint.
- Even the **tiniest change** in the input (a single flipped bit) produces a **completely different** fingerprint (the **avalanche effect**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Identity:** The output is commonly rendered as a **64-character hexadecimal string** (e.g., `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855` for the empty string input).

### 1.3 Key Properties of SHA-256

| Property | Description | Cryptographic Strength |
|----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Output Length** | **256 bits (32 bytes)** | $2^{256}$ possible outputs |
| **Block Size** | **512 bits (64 bytes)** | Message is split into 512-bit blocks |
| **Word Size** | **32 bits** | All operations are 32-bit modular |
| **Rounds** | **64 rounds** of compression | Per 512-bit block |
| **Max Message Length** | $2^{64} - 1$ bits | Approximately 2.3 million terabytes |
| **Deterministic** | Same input $\Rightarrow$ same output | Reproducible verification |
| **Avalanche Effect** | 1-bit change $\Rightarrow$ $\approx$ 50% output change | Confers unpredictability |

### 1.4 Historical Context and Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Evolution of SHA Family:**
> - **SHA-0 (1993):** Withdrawn due to undisclosed weakness.
> - **SHA-1 (1995):** 160-bit output; **broken** in 2017 (Google's SHAttered attack).
> - **SHA-2 (2001):** Family including SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512.
> - **SHA-3 (2015):** Based on Keccak sponge construction (different design lineage).

SHA-256 is the **workhorse of modern cryptography**, used in:
- **Digital signatures** (RSA, ECDSA padding)
- **TLS/SSL** handshakes (TLS 1.2 and below)
- **Blockchain** (Bitcoin mining, Ethereum)
- **Password storage** (with salt and key stretching)
- **HMAC** (Hash-based Message Authentication Codes)
- **Code signing** and software integrity verification

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Overview of SHA-256

The SHA-256 algorithm executes in **three major phases** for any input message $M$:

**Phase 1: Pre-processing**
1. Convert input message to binary representation.
2. Apply **padding** to make the total length a multiple of 512 bits.
3. Parse the padded message into $N$ blocks of 512 bits each.

**Phase 2: Initialize Hash Buffers**
1. Set eight 32-bit **initial hash values** $H_0^{(0)}, H_1^{(0)}, \ldots, H_7^{(0)}$.
2. These are derived from the square roots of the first 8 primes (2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19).

**Phase 3: Compression (Iterative)**
1. For each 512-bit block, generate a **64-word message schedule** $W_t$.
2. Initialize eight 32-bit **working variables** $a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$.
3. Execute **64 rounds** of the compression function.
4. Add the compressed chunk to the current hash value: $H_i^{(n)} = H_i^{(n-1)} + \text{compressed output}$.

### 2.2 The SHA-256 Logical Functions

SHA-256 employs **six logical functions** operating on 32-bit words:

$$\text{Ch}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (\lnot x \land z)$$

$$\text{Maj}(x, y, z) = (x \land y) \oplus (x \land z) \oplus (y \land z)$$

$$\Sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^2(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{13}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{22}(x)$$

$$\Sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^6(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{11}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{25}(x)$$

$$\sigma_0(x) = \text{ROTR}^7(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^3(x)$$

$$\sigma_1(x) = \text{ROTR}^{17}(x) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(x) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(x)$$

where:
- $\text{ROTR}^n(x)$ = **circular right rotation** of $x$ by $n$ bits.
- $\text{SHR}^n(x)$ = **right shift** of $x$ by $n$ bits (zero-fill left).
- $\oplus$ = **XOR** (bitwise exclusive-OR).
- $\land$ = **AND**, $\lor$ = **OR**, $\lnot$ = **NOT**.

### 2.3 The Compression Function (Per Round)

For round $t \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 63\}$, the working variables are updated as:

$$T_1 = h + \Sigma_1(e) + \text{Ch}(e, f, g) + K_t + W_t$$

$$T_2 = \Sigma_0(a) + \text{Maj}(a, b, c)$$

$$h = g, \quad g = f, \quad f = e, \quad e = d + T_1$$

$$d = c, \quad c = b, \quad b = a, \quad a = T_1 + T_2$$

where $K_t$ is the **64 round constants** (each 32 bits) and $W_t$ is the **message schedule word**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Component | Symbol | Size | Source / Definition |
|-----------|--------|------|---------------------|
| Output digest | $H$ | 256 bits | Final hash value |
| Message block | $M^{(i)}$ | 512 bits | Input block $i$ |
| Word | $W_t$ | 32 bits | Message schedule word |
| Working variable | $a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h$ | 32 bits each | Compression state |
| Round constant | $K_t$ | 32 bits | First 32 bits of cube roots of first 64 primes |
| Initial hash | $H_0^{(0)} \ldots H_7^{(0)}$ | 32 bits each | First 32 bits of square roots of first 8 primes |
| Rounds | $t$ | 0 to 63 | 64 compression rounds per block |
| Padding | $1 \Vert 0\text{s} \Vert L$ | variable | Makes length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** All arithmetic additions in SHA-256 are performed **modulo $2^{32}$**, since all words are 32 bits. Any overflow bits are simply discarded.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of SHA-256

In modern engineering systems, SHA-256 underpins:

1. **Cybersecurity Infrastructure:** TLS certificates, VPN authentication, and DNSSEC rely on SHA-256 for chain-of-trust verification.
2. **Blockchain & Distributed Ledgers:** Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work uses **double-SHA-256** (SHA-256(SHA-256(x))) for mining and transaction IDs.
3. **Software Distribution:** Package managers (e.g., APT, Homebrew) use SHA-256 checksums to verify file integrity.
4. **Digital Forensics:** Investigators hash disk images to prove evidence has not been tampered with (chain-of-custody).
5. **Hardware Security Modules (HSM):** SHA-256 acceleration is built into modern CPUs (Intel SHA Extensions, ARMv8 SHA instructions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: SHA-256 Padding Process

**Problem Setup:** Given an input message $M$ of length $L$ bits, derive the complete padded message structure.

#### Step 1: Express Message Length

Let $L$ be the original message length in bits. We must construct a padded message of total length $L' = L + 1 + P + 64$ such that:

$$L' \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

where $P$ is the number of zero-padding bits.

#### Step 2: Determine Padding Bit Count

From the divisibility condition:

$$L + 1 + P + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}$$

$$P \equiv -L - 65 \pmod{512}$$

$$P = (512 - ((L + 65) \mod 512)) \mod 512$$

#### Step 3: Minimum Padding Requirement

Even if $L \equiv 447 \pmod{512}$ (the worst case), we require at least:

$$P_{\min} = 1 \quad \text{(appended 1-bit)}$$

$$P \geq 0 \text{ zero bits}$$

This guarantees that a full 64-bit length field can always be appended.

#### Step 4: Construct the Padded Message

The complete padded message has the form:

$$M_{padded} = M \Vert 1 \Vert 0^{P} \Vert L_{64}$$

where:
- $M$ = original message of $L$ bits
- $1$ = single 1-bit
- $0^{P}$ = $P$ zero-bits
- $L_{64}$ = 64-bit big-endian representation of original length $L$

#### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $M$ = "abc" (24 bits, the canonical KTU test vector).

**Compute $P$:**

$$L = 24 \text{ bits}$$

$$L + 65 = 24 + 65 = 89 \text{ bits}$$

$$89 \mod 512 = 89$$

$$P = (512 - 89) \mod 512 = 423 \text{ zero bits}$$

**Verify total length:**

$$L' = 24 + 1 + 423 + 64 = 512 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark$$

**Padded message in hexadecimal form:**

```
61626380 00000000 00000000 00000000
00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000
00000000 00000000 00000000 00000000
00000000 00000000 00000018
```

(Note: 0x80 = 10000000₂ represents the appended 1-bit followed by zeros; 0x18 = 24 in big-endian.)

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Message Schedule $W_t$

The message schedule expands the 512-bit block ($16 \times 32$-bit words $M_0 \ldots M_{15}$) into 64 words $W_0 \ldots W_{63}$.

**For $t = 0, 1, \ldots, 15$ (direct copy):**

$$W_t = M_t$$

**For $t = 16, 17, \ldots, 63$ (recursive expansion):**

$$W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$$

#### Step-by-Step Expansion (First Three Recursive Terms)

**For $t = 16$:**

$$W_{16} = \sigma_1(W_{14}) + W_{9} + \sigma_0(W_{1}) + W_{0}$$

Substituting the sigma functions:

$$W_{16} = (\text{ROTR}^{17}(W_{14}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{19}(W_{14}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{10}(W_{14})) + W_9 + (\text{ROTR}^{7}(W_{1}) \oplus \text{ROTR}^{18}(W_{1}) \oplus \text{SHR}^{3}(W_{1})) + W_0$$

**For $t = 17$:**

$$W_{17} = \sigma_1(W_{15}) + W_{10} + \sigma_0(W_{2}) + W_{1}$$

**For $t = 18$:**

$$W_{18} = \sigma_1(W_{16}) + W_{11} + \sigma_0(W_{3}) + W_{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The recursive structure ensures that each $W_t$ for $t \geq 16$ depends on **four earlier words**, with two of them being 2 and 16 steps back. This creates a **diffusion cascade** that propagates changes across the entire block, contributing to the avalanche effect.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Round Constant $K_t$

The 64 round constants $K_t$ are derived as follows:

**Derivation Procedure:**
1. Take the first 64 prime numbers: $p_1 = 2, p_2 = 3, p_3 = 5, \ldots, p_{64} = 311$.
2. Compute the **cube root** of each prime: $\sqrt[3]{p_t}$.
3. Extract the **fractional part** of the cube root.
4. Multiply by $2^{32}$ and take the floor (integer part).

Mathematically:

$$K_t = \lfloor 2^{32} \cdot \text{frac}(\sqrt[3]{p_t}) \rfloor$$

where $\text{frac}(x) = x - \lfloor x \rfloor$.

**First Three Round Constants (Verification):**

| $t$ | Prime $p_t$ | $\sqrt[3]{p_t}$ | $\text{frac}$ | $K_t$ (hex) |
|-----|-------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 0 | 2 | 1.2599210... | 0.2599210... | 0x428a2f98 |
| 1 | 3 | 1.4422495... | 0.4422495... | 0x71374491 |
| 2 | 5 | 1.7099759... | 0.7099759... | 0xb5c0fbcf |

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Initial Hash Values $H_0^{(0)} \ldots H_7^{(0)}$

The eight 32-bit initial hash values are similarly derived from **square roots of the first 8 primes**:

$$H_i^{(0)} = \lfloor 2^{32} \cdot \text{frac}(\sqrt{p_{i+1}}) \rfloor$$

where $p_{i+1}$ is the $(i+1)$-th prime.

**Complete Initial Hash Vector:**

| $i$ | Prime | $\text{frac}(\sqrt{p})$ | $H_i^{(0)}$ (hex) |
|-----|-------|------------------------|--------------------|
| 0 | 2 | 0.4142135... | 0x6a09e667 |
| 1 | 3 | 0.7320508... | 0xbb67ae85 |
| 2 | 5 | 0.2360679... | 0x3c6ef372 |
| 3 | 7 | 0.6457513... | 0xa54ff53a |
| 4 | 11 | 0.3166247... | 0x510e527f |
| 5 | 13 | 0.6055512... | 0x9b05688c |
| 6 | 17 | 0.1231056... | 0x1f83d9ab |
| 7 | 19 | 0.3588989... | 0x5be0cd19 |

### 3.5 Final Hash Computation (Multi-Block)

After processing all $N$ blocks, the final hash is the concatenation of the eight updated hash words:

$$\text{SHA-256}(M) = H_0^{(N)} \Vert H_1^{(N)} \Vert H_2^{(N)} \Vert H_3^{(N)} \Vert H_4^{(N)} \Vert H_5^{(N)} \Vert H_6^{(N)} \Vert H_7^{(N)}$$

where each $H_i^{(N)}$ is a 32-bit word, yielding $8 \times 32 = 256$ bits total.

The update rule after processing block $i$ is:

$$H_j^{(i)} = H_j^{(i-1)} + (a + b + c + d + e + f + g + h)_j$$

where the parenthesized sum refers to the working variables at the **end** of the 64th round of block $i$, added modulo $2^{32}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level SHA-256 Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Message M of Length L bits] --> B[Append Single 1-bit]
    B --> C[Pad with k Zero bits]
    C --> D[Append 64-bit Length Field L]
    D --> E[Padded Message: N x 512-bit Blocks]
    E --> F[Initialize H0 to H7 from sqrt of first 8 primes]
    F --> G{For Each Block i = 1 to N}
    G --> H[Prepare Message Schedule W0 to W63]
    H --> I[Initialize Working Vars a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h from H]
    I --> J{For t = 0 to 63}
    J --> K[Compute T1 and T2 using Ch, Maj, Sigma]
    K --> L[Update Working Variables a through h]
    L --> J
    J --> M[Compute H0i to H7i by Adding Working Vars to Previous H]
    M --> G
    G --> N[Concatenate H0N to H7N to Form 256-bit Digest]
    N --> O[Output SHA-256 Hash Value]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SHA-256 Compression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputBlock["512-bit Input Block"]
        M0[M0: 32 bits] --> W0Gen[Message Schedule Generator]
        M1[M1: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M2[M2: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M3[M3: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M4[M4: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M5[M5: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M6[M6: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M7[M7: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M8[M8: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M9[M9: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M10[M10: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M11[M11: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M12[M12: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M13[M13: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M14[M14: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
        M15[M15: 32 bits] --> W0Gen
    end

    W0Gen --> SchedOut[Word Wt: 32 bits]

    subgraph RoundUnit["Compression Round t: 0 to 63"]
        SchedOut --> Add1[Add Wt]
        Kt[Round Constant Kt] --> Add1
        ChFunc[Ch Function e,f,g] --> Add1
        Sig1Func[Sigma1 of e] --> Add1
        Hvar[h variable] --> Add1
        Add1 --> T1[T1: 32 bits]
        Sig0Func[Sigma0 of a] --> Add2[Add]
        MajFunc[Maj Function a,b,c] --> Add2
        Add2 --> T2[T2: 32 bits]
        T1 --> T1Add[Add]
        T2 --> T1Add
        T1Add --> Avar[New a]
        T1 --> EAdd[Add to d]
        EAdd --> Evar[New e]
    end

    Avar --> NextRound[Shift: h to g, g to f, etc.]
    Evar --> NextRound
    NextRound --> RoundUnit

    Avar --> FinalAdd[Final Addition mod 2 to the power of 32]
    Evar --> FinalAdd
```

### 4.3 Message Schedule Expansion Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DirectCopy["Direct Copy Phase: t = 0 to 15"]
        Mt0[Wt equals M0] --> WS0[W0]
        Mt1[Wt equals M1] --> WS1[W1]
        Mt2[Wt equals M2] --> WS2[W2]
        Mt3[Wt equals M3] --> WS3[W3]
    end

    subgraph RecursivePhase["Recursive Expansion Phase: t = 16 to 63"]
        Wt2[Wt minus 2] --> Sigma1[Apply sigma1]
        Sigma1 --> Sum1[Add mod 2 to the power of 32]
        Wt7[Wt minus 7] --> Sum1
        Wt15[Wt minus 15] --> Sigma0[Apply sigma0]
        Sigma0 --> Sum2[Add mod 2 to the power of 32]
        Wt16[Wt minus 16] --> Sum2
        Sum1 --> Sum3[Add mod 2 to the power of 32]
        Sum2 --> Sum3
        Sum3 --> WtOut[Wt: 32-bit word]
    end

    WS0 -.-> WtOut
    WS1 -.-> WtOut
    WS2 -.-> WtOut
    WS3 -.-> WtOut
```

### 4.4 Single Round Operation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input a] --> Sig0[Sigma0 of a]
    A --> Maj[Maj of a,b,c]
    Sig0 --> T2Comp[T2 equals Sigma0 of a plus Maj of a,b,c]
    Maj --> T2Comp

    E[Input e] --> Sig1[Sigma1 of e]
    E --> Ch[Ch of e,f,g]
    Sig1 --> T1Comp[T1 equals h plus Sigma1 of e plus Ch of e,f,g plus Kt plus Wt]
    H[Input h] --> T1Comp
    Ch --> T1Comp
    KtVal[Round Constant Kt] --> T1Comp
    WtVal[Schedule Word Wt] --> T1Comp

    T1Comp --> NewA[New a equals T1 plus T2]
    T2Comp --> NewA
    T1Comp --> NewE[New e equals d plus T1]
    D[Input d] --> NewE

    NewA --> ShiftOp[Shift Register: b to c, c to d, etc.]
    NewE --> ShiftOp
    ShiftOp --> NextRound[Outputs to Next Round]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Notice that in each round, **only two of the eight working variables** ($a$ and $e$) undergo nontrivial computation. The other six are simply shifted from their predecessors, creating a feedback shift-register pattern that contributes to SHA-256's efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
