For $n = 64$ bits (e.g., truncated HMAC), this is only $2^{32} \approx 4 \times 10^9$ operations — **trivially breakable on a modern GPU**. This is why KTU 2024 problems specifically mark truncated-MAC schemes as insecure below $n = 128$.

### 3.3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 2024 Numerical)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A 64-bit MAC is used to authenticate transactions. An attacker can mount a birthday attack. How many messages must they collect to expect one collision with probability ≥ 0.5?

> **Solution:**

We need:

$$
\Pr[\text{collision}] \ge 0.5 \;\Longrightarrow\; q(q-1)/2 \cdot 2^{-64} \approx 0.5
$$

$$
q^2 \approx 2^{64} \;\Longrightarrow\; q \approx 2^{32}
$$

$$
q \approx 4.29 \times 10^9
$$

The attacker must collect approximately **4.3 billion valid MAC samples** — a few hours of work on a single GPU cluster.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MAC vs. Digital Signature (KTU 2024 Classic Comparison Question)

| Property | MAC | Digital Signature |
|---|---|---|
| **Key type** | Shared symmetric key | Asymmetric key pair (private/public) |
| **Non-repudiation** | ❌ No (both parties can produce it) | ✅ Yes (only signer has private key) |
| **Speed** | 🚀 Very fast (μs per MB) | 🐢 Slow (ms per MB, RSA) |
| **Verifier** | Must hold the secret | Anyone with public key |
| **PKI required?** | No | Yes (certificates) |
| **Algorithms** | HMAC, CMAC, GMAC | RSA-PSS, ECDSA, Ed25519 |
| **Confidentiality** | No | No |
| **Integrity + Auth** | Yes | Yes |
| **Use case** | Internal/closed systems, TLS, IPsec | Public documents, code signing, certificates |
| **Attack model** | Chosen-message forgery | Existential unforgeability under CMA |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: MAC Functional Architecture (Top-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#202124
    classDef crypto fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#202124
    classDef output fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#137333,color:#202124

    K[Secret Key K]:::input
    M[Message M of arbitrary length]:::input
    Sender["Sender Side:\nMac Algorithm"]:::crypto
    T[Tag T of fixed length n]:::output
    Channel["Insecure Channel"]:::input
    Receiver["Receiver Side:\nVerify Algorithm"]:::crypto
    Result[Accept or Reject bit]:::output

    K --> Sender
    M --> Sender
    Sender --> T
    T --> Channel
    M --> Channel
    K --> Receiver
    Channel --> Receiver
    Receiver --> Result
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: HMAC Internal Flow (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef key fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#202124
    classDef pad fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#202124
    classDef hash fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#202124
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#202124

    K0["K0 (key padded to b bits)"]:::key
    ipad["ipad = 0x36 x b/8 bytes"]:::pad
    opad["opad = 0x5C x b/8 bytes"]:::pad

    Kinner["K0 XOR ipad"]:::key
    Kouter["K0 XOR opad"]:::key

    M["Message M"]:::out
    Inner["H(Kinner || M)"]:::hash
    Outer["H(Kouter || Inner)"]:::hash
    Tag["HMAC Output Tag T"]:::out

    K0 --> Kinner
    K0 --> Kouter
    ipad --> Kinner
    opad --> Kouter
    Kinner --> Inner
    M --> Inner
    Kouter --> Outer
    Inner --> Outer
    Outer --> Tag
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: MAC vs. Digital Signature — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef start fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#202124
    classDef decision fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,color:#202124
    classDef rec fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#202124
    classDef disc fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#202124

    Q1{Need Non-Repudiation?}:::decision
    Q2{Are parties pre-shared key?}:::decision
    Q3{Throughput critical?}:::decision

    UseMAC[Use MAC: HMAC or CMAC]:::rec
    UseSig[Use Digital Signature: RSA-PSS or ECDSA]:::rec
    UseAEAD[Use Authenticated Encryption: AES-GCM]:::rec

    Start([Choose Authenticator]):::start
    NeedConf{Also need Confidentiality?}:::decision

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> UseSig
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3
    Q2 -- No --> UseSig
    Q3 -- Yes --> UseMAC
    Q3 -- No --> UseSig
    UseMAC --> NeedConf
    NeedConf -- Yes --> UseAEAD
    NeedConf -- No --> UseMAC
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Authenticated Encryption (AEAD) — GCM/CCM Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef data fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#202124
    classDef enc fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#202124
    classDef out fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#202124

    P[Plaintext P]:::data
    K[AES Key K]:::data
    N[Nonce N, 96 bits]:::data
    A[Associated Data A optional]:::data

    ENC[GCTR Encryption Counter Mode]:::enc
    GHASH["GHASH over (A || P)"]:::enc
    Tag[Authentication Tag T 128 bits]:::out
    CT[Ciphertext C]:::out
    Pkg[Package C + T transmitted]:::out

    P --> ENC
    K --> ENC
    N --> ENC
    ENC --> CT
    A --> GHASH
    P --> GHASH
    K --> GHASH
    N --> GHASH
    GHASH --> Tag
    CT --> Pkg
    Tag --> Pkg
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The block diagrams above represent *functional flow*, not physical layouts. GCM combines a CTR-mode encryption (confidentiality) with a polynomial-evaluation MAC (authentication) over both the ciphertext and any unencrypted associated data — this is the modern **Encrypt-then-MAC** paradigm recommended in NIST SP 800-38D.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Message Authentication Code (MAC). List the two security services it provides and the one service it does NOT provide. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a cryptographic function that uses a shared secret key to produce a fixed-length authentication tag on a variable-length message, enabling the receiver to verify both the **integrity** and **origin authenticity** of the message.

**Two security services provided:**
1. **Data Integrity** — detection of any unauthorized modification to the message.
2. **Source Authentication** — confirmation that the message was produced by a party holding the shared secret key.

**One service NOT provided:** **Non-repudiation** — because both sender and receiver share the same key, either party could have produced the tag, so the receiver cannot prove to a third party who created the message. *(Confidentiality is also not provided.)*

---

### Q2. Distinguish between a MAC and a hash function. Why is a plain hash function not sufficient for message authentication? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hash Function | MAC |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | Message only | Secret key + Message |
| **Output** | Fixed-length digest | Fixed-length tag |
| **Keyed?** | No (public) | Yes (shared secret) |
| **Authentication** | No | Yes |

A plain hash function is **not sufficient** for authentication because it is a **public, deterministic, unkeyed** operation. An attacker can recompute the hash on a forged message and substitute the new digest, fooling the receiver. A MAC binds the digest to a **secret key** that the attacker does not possess, making forgery computationally infeasible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split across two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must attempt **either** the entire Question A **or** the entire Question B (Module Internal Choice).

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q.A(a) Construct the HMAC of a message using SHA-256. Explain the role of ipad and opad. Why is HMAC resistant to length-extension attacks? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block size identification:**
For SHA-256, the block size $b = 512$ bits and output size $n = 256$ bits.

**Step 2 — Pad the key:**
If the secret key $K$ is shorter than 512 bits, append zero bits to make $K_0$ exactly 512 bits long. If $K$ is longer than 512 bits, first compute $H(K)$ and zero-pad the 256-bit digest to 512 bits.

**Step 3 — Define padding constants:**
- $\text{ipad}$ = byte $0\text{x}36$ repeated 64 times (the inner pad)
- $\text{opad}$ = byte $0\text{x}5\text{C}$ repeated 64 times (the outer pad)

**Step 4 — Compute the inner hash:**

$$
H_{\text{inner}} = \text{SHA-256}\!\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \,\Vert\, M \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the outer hash and final tag:**

$$
\text{HMAC-SHA-256}(K, M) = \text{SHA-256}\!\left( (K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \,\Vert\, H_{\text{inner}} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Role of ipad and opad:**
- **ipad** marks the start of the inner hash. The XOR with $K_0$ produces a value that is statistically uncorrelated with $K_0$ itself, preventing simple key-recovery attacks.
- **opad** similarly marks the outer hash, ensuring the outer and inner hash computations are **independent and distinguishable** by the receiver.

**Step 7 — Resistance to length-extension attacks:**
Merkle–Damgård hashes (SHA-256 included) suffer from a length-extension vulnerability: given $H(M)$ and $\vert M \vert$, an attacker can compute $H(M \parallel \text{pad} \parallel M')$ for any suffix $M'$ without knowing $M$. For a plain keyed construction $H(K \parallel M)$, this is fatal — an attacker can extend the message and recompute the hash using the captured state.

HMAC defeats this by:
- Pre-hashing the message in the **inner** hash, producing a fixed-size intermediate $H_{\text{inner}}$.
- Re-hashing that intermediate in the **outer** hash.
- The outer hash does not have the same iterative Merkle–Damgård state as the inner, so length-extension of $H_{\text{inner}}$ does not yield a valid outer hash for an extended message.

Thus, even though the inner computation is technically vulnerable in isolation, the outer hash re-keying closes the attack vector.

#### Q.A(b) What is a birthday attack on a MAC? Compute the number of MAC samples required to find a collision with probability $\ge 0.5$ for a 128-bit MAC. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Pattern]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
A **birthday attack** exploits the birthday paradox to find two distinct messages $M_i \ne M_j$ such that their MAC tags $T_i = T_j$ collide. Such a collision may enable a forgery: if an attacker can find $M_i, M_j$ with the same tag, they can substitute $M_i$ for $M_j$ in any context where the tag is trusted.

**Step 2 — Mathematical formulation:**
For $q$ samples drawn uniformly from an $n$-bit tag space, the expected number of pairs is $q(q-1)/2$, and the probability of at least one collision is approximately:

$$
P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2 \cdot 2^n)} \approx \dfrac{q^2}{2 \cdot 2^n} \quad \text{(for small } q \text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Setting probability $\ge 0.5$:**

$$
\dfrac{q^2}{2 \cdot 2^n} \ge 0.5
$$

$$
q^2 \ge 2^n
$$

$$
q \ge 2^{n/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in $n = 128$:**

$$
q \ge 2^{128/2} = 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The attacker must collect approximately $1.84 \times 10^{19}$ valid MAC samples. This is computationally infeasible on classical hardware (estimated time on a $10^{18}$ ops/sec supercomputer ≈ 18+ seconds for the simple squaring, but actually closer to many years due to the requirement of **obtaining** $2^{64}$ valid samples from a real system, which is operationally impossible at the required rate). Hence a 128-bit MAC is considered **secure against birthday attacks** in 2024-era classical computing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common 2-mark deduction):** Students often answer "$q = 2^{128}$" — confusing the brute-force forgery bound ($2^n$) with the birthday bound ($2^{n/2}$). Memorize the factor of 2: **birthday attacks are *quadratically* faster than brute force.**

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks)

#### Q.B(a) Describe the structure of CMAC. How does CMAC overcome the length-extension weakness of plain CBC-MAC? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Context:**
Plain **CBC-MAC** is secure only for fixed-length messages. When messages can be of variable length, CBC-MAC is existentially forgeable: an attacker who learns $\text{MAC}(M_1)$ and $\text{MAC}(M_1')$ for single-block messages $M_1, M_1'$ can construct a two-block message $M_1 \parallel (M_1' \oplus \text{MAC}(M_1))$ whose MAC equals $\text{MAC}(M_1')$, without ever querying the oracle on that two-block message.

**Step 2 — CMAC Structure (NIST SP 800-38B):**
CMAC derives **two subkeys** $K_1$ and $K_2$ from the master key $K$ using the block cipher $E_K$ and a doubling operation in $\text{GF}(2^b)$. The steps are:

1. Compute $L = E_K(0^b)$.
2. If the most significant bit of $L$ is 0, set $K_1 = L \ll 1$; otherwise $K_1 = (L \ll 1) \oplus \text{const}_b$, where $\text{const}_b$ is a constant depending on the block size.
3. Similarly derive $K_2$ from $K_1$.

**Step 3 — Tag generation:**
Given a message $M$ of $L$ blocks, with final block $M_L$:
- If $M_L$ is a **complete** block (length $b$): output $T = E_K(M_1 \oplus \ldots \oplus M_L \oplus K_1)$.
- If $M_L$ is **incomplete** (padded): output $T = E_K(M_1 \oplus \ldots \oplus \text{pad}(M_L) \oplus K_2)$.

**Step 4 — How CMAC fixes the weakness:**
The two distinct subkeys $K_1$ and $K_2$ are tied to whether the last block is complete or padded. An attacker cannot substitute blocks of a different length class without first obtaining a tag of a message in the *other* class, which requires either a successful key recovery or a MAC forgery — both infeasible for a secure block cipher. This makes CMAC existentially unforgeable for messages of any length.

**Step 5 — CMAC Applications:**
- Used in **EMV chip cards** for offline transaction authentication.
- Used in **Kerberos v5** for ticket integrity.
- Used in **S/MIME** for message authentication.
- Standardized in **NIST SP 800-38B** and **ISO 9797-1 (MAC algorithm 5)**.

#### Q.B(b) What is Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)? Explain GCM with a block diagram and list two applications. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of AEAD:**
An **AEAD** scheme provides confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity in a single primitive, returning both a ciphertext and an authentication tag. It accepts four inputs: a key, a **nonce**, **plaintext**, and **associated data** (which is authenticated but not encrypted, e.g., IP headers or packet metadata).

Formally, an AEAD is a pair $(\text{Enc}, \text{Dec})$:

$$
\text{Enc}(K, N, P, A) = (C, T)
$$

$$
\text{Dec}(K, N, C, T, A) = P \quad \text{or} \quad \bot \text{ (rejection)}
$$

**Step 2 — GCM Construction:**
**Galois/Counter Mode (GCM)** combines:
- A **CTR-mode** encryption for confidentiality.
- A **GHASH** universal-hash function over $\text{GF}(2^{128})$ for authentication.

**Step 3 — Operation:**
1. Encryption: $C = P \oplus E_K(\text{counter}_0), E_K(\text{counter}_1), \ldots$ — a stream-cipher keystream.
2. GHASH input: $A \parallel \text{pad} \parallel C \parallel \text{pad} \parallel \text{length}(A) \parallel \text{length}(C)$.
3. Tag: $T = \text{GHASH}_{H} \oplus E_K(N \parallel 0^{31}1)$, where $H = E_K(0^{128})$ is the hash subkey.

**Step 4 — Block Diagram (refer Section 4.4 above):**
The diagram shows plaintext and key feeding CTR encryption to produce ciphertext, while both plaintext and ciphertext are fed into GHASH (along with associated data) to produce the tag.

**Step 5 — Two Applications:**
1. **TLS 1.3** — every record layer uses AES-128-GCM or AES-256-GCM as the default AEAD.
2. **IPsec ESP (RFC 4106)** — AES-GCM-16 is the most widely deployed ESP cipher suite for VPNs.
3. *(Bonus)* 5G mobile networks use ZUC-256-GCM for subscriber data confidentiality and integrity.

**Step 6 — Security Properties:**
GCM is proven to be IND-CCA2 secure (indistinguishable under adaptive chosen-ciphertext attack) provided the underlying AES is a secure PRP and the nonce is **never reused**. Reuse of a nonce under the same key catastrophically breaks both confidentiality and authenticity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Common 3-mark deduction):** Students frequently:
> - **(a)** Forget the role of associated data and say GCM *encrypts* everything.
> - **(b)** Confuse **CTR-mode encryption** (for confidentiality) with **GHASH authentication** (for integrity). These are two separate operations.
> - **(c)** Fail to mention the **nonce-reuse catastrophe** — KTU 2024 specifically tests this.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Top 5 Places KTU Students Lose Marks in MAC Questions (2024 Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Confusing MAC with Digital Signature:** Writing "MAC provides non-repudiation" is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up birthday and brute-force bounds:** Birthday = $2^{n/2}$, brute-force forgery = $2^n$, key search = $2^k$. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the ipad/opad explanation:** KTU expects both the *definition* and the *role* of these constants. Simply stating the formula without explaining why yields only 4/7.
> 4. **Forgetting the security assumption of HMAC:** HMAC is secure assuming the underlying hash is a PRF (Pseudo-Random Function). Without stating this assumption, the security bound derivation is incomplete.
> 5. **Forgetting nonce uniqueness in GCM/CCM:** Reusing a nonce with the same key in GCM reveals the XOR of plaintexts and enables forgery. This is the most common KTU 2024 trick question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **MAC = Keyed hash** providing **integrity + authenticity** (NOT confidentiality, NOT non-repudiation).
- ✅ **EUF-CMA** is the gold-standard security definition.
- ✅ **HMAC** = $H((K_0 \oplus \text{opad}) \Vert H((K_0 \oplus \text{ipad}) \Vert M))$ — the workhorse of TLS, IPsec, JWT.
- ✅ **CMAC** = CBC-MAC with two subkeys for length-independent security — used in EMV, Kerberos, NIST SP 800-38B.
- ✅ **GMAC** = GHASH-only mode, suitable for environments with no plaintext (e.g., header-only authentication).
- ✅ **GCM** = CTR-mode encryption + GHASH authentication = AEAD — used in TLS 1.3 and IPsec.
- ✅ **CCM** = CTR-mode + CBC-MAC = AEAD — used in 802.11i (Wi-Fi WPA2/WPA3).
- ✅ **Brute-force forgery** requires $2^n$ trials on $n$-bit tag.
- ✅ **Birthday forgery** requires only $2^{n/2}$ trials — why $n \ge 128$ is mandatory.
- ✅ **Truncated MACs** below 96 bits are insecure in 2024 threat models.
- ✅ **MAC vs Digital Signature** — MAC = symmetric, fast, no non-repudiation; Signature = asymmetric, slow, full non-repudiation.
- ✅ **Length-extension attack** is defeated by HMAC's nested-hash construction.
- ✅ **Nonce reuse in GCM** is catastrophic — never repeat (key, nonce) pairs.
- ✅ **Authenticated encryption** combines confidentiality and authentication in a single primitive — always prefer over encrypt-then-MAC DIY constructions.
- ✅ **RFC 2104** = HMAC standard, **FIPS 198-1** = HMAC validation, **NIST SP 800-38B** = CMAC, **NIST SP 800-38D** = GCM/CCM.

> **End of Module 3 Topic: Message Authentication Codes — Structure and Usage**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** CMAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CMAC (Cipher-based Message Authentication Code)** is a block-cipher-based MAC algorithm standardized by **NIST SP 800-38B (May 2005)**. It is also known as **OMAC1 (One-key MAC)**. CMAC provides data origin authentication and integrity verification by producing a fixed-length tag $T$ of length $t$ bits from an arbitrary-length input message $M$ using a symmetric block cipher $E$ with a secret key $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMAC is the standardized successor to CBC-MAC** and is provably secure for messages of *any* length, including variable-length messages — a critical limitation of the original CBC-MAC construction.

Mathematically, CMAC is defined as the tuple:

$$\text{CMAC} : \{0,1\}^* \longrightarrow \{0,1\}^t, \quad M \longmapsto T = \text{CMAC}_K(M)$$

### 1.2 The Three Algorithmic Variants of CMAC

| Variant | Block Cipher | Block Size $b$ | Key Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-CMAC** | AES-128 | **128 bits** | 128 bits |
| **3DES-CMAC** | Triple DES | **64 bits** | 112 / 168 bits |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sealed Wax Letter" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine sending a series of sealed wax envelopes through a courier. Each envelope is stamped with a wax seal that **depends on the contents of the previous envelope AND a master die** known only to the sender and receiver. The final seal is your "MAC tag." Even if a thief tampers with one envelope, the wax seal of the *last* envelope (the tag) won't match, because every prior seal was chained to it. CMAC works exactly like this — but with the elegant refinement of using **two master dies ($K_1$ and $K_2$)** — one for "completed" envelope chains and one for "the last envelope had to be padded."

### 1.4 Why CMAC Was Needed — The CBC-MAC Problem

> [!WARNING]
> **CBC-MAC is ONLY secure for fixed-length messages.** For variable-length messages, an attacker can forge a valid tag by combining two previously tagged messages. CMAC fixes this by deriving two subkeys $K_1, K_2$ and XOR-ing them at the final block, ensuring domain separation between "complete final block" and "padded final block" cases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Architecture of CMAC

CMAC operates in **two distinct stages**:

#### **Stage 1 — Subkey Generation (One-time per key $K$)**
- Compute $L = E_K(0^b)$ where $0^b$ is the all-zero block.
- Generate $K_1$ and $K_2$ by left-shifting $L$ and applying a constant (depending on block size).

#### **Stage 2 — MAC Tag Generation (Per message $M$)**
- Partition $M$ into $n$ blocks $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$.
- Process blocks using CBC-MAC chaining: $C_i = E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})$ with $C_0 = 0^b$.
- **Differentiate** the last block:
  - If message length is a multiple of $b$: XOR $M_n$ with $K_1$ before final encryption.
  - If padding is needed: pad $M_n$ with `10...0`, XOR with $K_2$.
- Output: $T = \text{MSB}_t(C_n)$.

### 2.2 Subkey Derivation — The Core "Why"

The constants $R_b$ and the multiplication-by-$x$ operation in $\text{GF}(2^b)$ handle the **carry propagation** from the left-shift operation:

| Block Cipher | $b$ | $R_b$ (constant polynomial in hex) |
|---|---|---|
| AES | 128 | $\texttt{0}^{120} \Vert \texttt{10000111}$ |
| 3DES | 64 | $\texttt{0}^{59} \Vert \texttt{11011}$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | KTU-Critical Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Block-cipher encryption under key $K$ | AES-CMAC uses AES-128 |
| $b$ | Block size (bits) | **128** for AES, **64** for 3DES |
| $L$ | $E_K(0^b)$ | Intermediate value used to derive subkeys |
| $K_1$ | Subkey for complete last block | Derived via $L \cdot x$ in $\text{GF}(2^b)$ |
| $K_2$ | Subkey for padded last block | Derived via $K_1 \cdot x$ in $\text{GF}(2^b)$ |
| $R_b$ | Reduction constant | Depends on block size |
| $n$ | Number of blocks in $M$ | $\lfloor m/b \rfloor$ |
| $m$ | Message length (bits) | May be non-multiple of $b$ |
| $C_i$ | $i$-th CBC chaining variable | $C_0 = 0^b$ |
| $t$ | Tag length (bits) | Recommended: $64 \le t \le 128$ |
| $T$ | Output MAC tag | $\text{MSB}_t(C_n)$ |
| $\text{MSB}_t(\cdot)$ | Most significant $t$ bits | Truncation operator |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of CMAC

> [!NOTE]
> CMAC is deployed in **TLS cipher suites (RFC 4493)**, **IPsec**, **IEEE 802.1X EAPOL** authentication, **smart card applications (EMV)**, and **cryptographic key-wrapping protocols** (e.g., AES Key Wrap RFC 3394 uses CMAC principles). It is the IEEE 802.1X standard for LAN authentication tags.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Subkey Generation

The subkey derivation is the **mathematical heart of CMAC security**. Let $b$ denote the block size. The constant $R_b$ is defined such that $\text{MSB}_1(R_b) = 1$ and all other bits serve as the irreducible polynomial coefficients.

**Step 1 — Encrypt the zero block to obtain $L$:**

$$L = E_K(0^b)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_1$ via multiplication by $x$ in $\text{GF}(2^b)$:**

$$K_1 = \begin{cases} L \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(L) = 0 \\ (L \ll 1) \oplus R_b & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(L) = 1 \end{cases}$$

where $L \ll 1$ denotes a left-shift-by-1 with right-padding zero.

**Step 3 — Compute $K_2$ via multiplication by $x^2$ in $\text{GF}(2^b)$:**

$$K_2 = \begin{cases} K_1 \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(K_1) = 0 \\ (K_1 \ll 1) \oplus R_b & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(K_1) = 1 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Logic Explanation (Why this works):**

The polynomial representation: $L$ is interpreted as a polynomial $l(x)$ in $\text{GF}(2)[x]$. Then:

$$K_1 \leftrightarrow l(x) \cdot x \bmod p_b(x)$$

where $p_b(x)$ is the irreducible polynomial defining $\text{GF}(2^b)$. For AES: $p_b(x) = x^{128} + x^7 + x^2 + x + 1$. The XOR with $R_b$ corresponds to reducing modulo $p_b(x)$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — MAC Tag Generation

For a message $M = M_1 \Vert M_2 \Vert \cdots \Vert M_{n-1} \Vert M_n^*$:

**Case A: $m$ is a positive multiple of $b$ (no padding required)**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^b \\
C_1 &= E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) \\
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
C_{n-1} &= E_K(M_{n-1} \oplus C_{n-2}) \\
C_n &= E_K(M_n \oplus C_{n-1} \oplus K_1) \\
T &= \text{MSB}_t(C_n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Case B: $m$ is NOT a multiple of $b$ (padding required)**

Append the bit string `10...0` to $M$ to form the final complete block $M_n$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^b \\
C_i &= E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } 1 \le i \le n-1 \\
C_n &= E_K(M_n \oplus C_{n-1} \oplus K_2) \\
T &= \text{MSB}_t(C_n)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Illustrative Mini-Demo)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **simplified pedagogical example with small symbolic blocks** to demonstrate the chaining logic. Real CMAC uses AES/3DES; here we use compact symbols for student legibility.

Suppose $b = 4$ bits, message $M = \texttt{1010\;1100\;11}$ (length $m = 10$ bits, $n = 3$ blocks).

**Block partition:** $M_1 = \texttt{1010}$, $M_2 = \texttt{1100}$, $M_3^* = \texttt{11}$ (incomplete).

**Pad $M_3^*$** with `100` to get $M_3 = \texttt{1110}$.

Assume $L = E_K(0000) = \texttt{1100}$. Since $\text{MSB}_1(L) = 1$:
$$K_1 = (L \ll 1) \oplus R_b = \texttt{1000} \oplus \texttt{1101} = \texttt{0101}$$

Since $\text{MSB}_1(K_1) = 0$:
$$K_2 = K_1 \ll 1 = \texttt{1010}$$

**CBC chain with $C_0 = 0000$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = E_K(\texttt{1010} \oplus \texttt{0000}) = E_K(\texttt{1010}) = \texttt{0110} \\
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1) = E_K(\texttt{1100} \oplus \texttt{0110}) = E_K(\texttt{1010}) = \texttt{0110} \\
C_3 &= E_K(M_3 \oplus C_2 \oplus K_2) = E_K(\texttt{1110} \oplus \texttt{0110} \oplus \texttt{1010}) \\
    &= E_K(\texttt{0010}) = \texttt{1001}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final MAC tag:** $T = \text{MSB}_t(C_3)$. If $t = 4$: $T = \texttt{1001}$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Related MAC Schemes

| Property | CBC-MAC | EMAC | CMAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Handles variable-length messages | ❌ (insecure) | ✅ (uses 2 keys) | ✅ (uses 1 key + subkeys) |
| Standardized by NIST | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (SP 800-38B) |
| Number of secret keys required | 1 | 2 | **1** |
| Provable security | Limited | Strong | **Strong (proven by Iwata & Kurosawa, 2003)** |
| Output truncation support | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Used in TLS / IPsec | Limited | Limited | **✅** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CMAC

<!-- Figure: High-level CMAC architecture showing message input, block partitioning, CBC chaining with K1/K2 differentiation, and MAC tag output. -->

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end data flow** of the CMAC algorithm, from message input to MAC tag generation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT Message M (variable length)"] --> B["Partition M into n blocks of b bits"]
    B --> C{"Is M length a multiple of b?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["XOR last block Mn with K1"]
    C -- No --> E["Pad M last block with 100...0"]
    E --> F["XOR padded last block with K2"]
    D --> G["CBC Chaining: C_i = E_K M_i XOR C_i_minus_1"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Apply final block cipher encryption"]
    H --> I["Truncate to t bits: T = MSB_t C_n"]
    I --> J["OUTPUT MAC Tag T"]

    K["Subkey Generation One-time"] --> L["L = E_K of zero block"]
    L --> M["Compute K1 from L using GF2^b arithmetic"]
    M --> N["Compute K2 from K1 using GF2^b arithmetic"]
    N --> D
    N --> F

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#37474f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 CMAC Verification Flow

The receiver performs the same CMAC computation on the received message and compares the freshly computed tag with the received tag.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Received Message M"] --> B["Compute T_prime = CMAC_K M"]
    C["Received Tag T"] --> D{"T_prime equals T?"}
    B --> D
    D -- Yes --> E["ACCEPT Message Authentic"]
    D -- No --> F["REJECT Message Tampered or Forged"]

    style E fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Subkey Generation Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Secret Key K"] --> B["Compute L = E_K of zero block"]
    B --> C{"MSB of L equals 1?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["K1 = L shifted left XOR R_b"]
    C -- No --> E["K1 = L shifted left with zero pad"]
    D --> F{"MSB of K1 equals 1?"}
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G["K2 = K1 shifted left XOR R_b"]
    F -- No --> H["K2 = K1 shifted left with zero pad"]
    G --> I["OUTPUT K1 and K2 ready for tag generation"]
    H --> I

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`

**Define CMAC. List any two advantages of CMAC over plain CBC-MAC.**

**Model Answer:**
CMAC (Cipher-based Message Authentication Code) is a block-cipher-based MAC construction standardized by NIST SP 800-38B that produces a fixed-length authentication tag from an arbitrary-length message using a single secret key and a block cipher.

**Advantages over plain CBC-MAC:**
1. CMAC securely handles variable-length messages, whereas plain CBC-MAC is provably insecure when message length varies.
2. CMAC requires only one secret key $K$ (with internally derived subkeys $K_1, K_2$), whereas alternative schemes like EMAC require two independent keys.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

**What is the role of the constant $R_b$ in the CMAC subkey generation process?**

**Model Answer:**
The constant $R_b$ is the **reduction polynomial** for the irreducible field $\text{GF}(2^b)$ used during the multiplication-by-$x$ operation. When the MSB of $L$ (or $K_1$) is 1, the left-shift operation would push a 1 out of the $b$-bit boundary. The XOR with $R_b$ performs the polynomial reduction modulo the irreducible polynomial that defines the field — for AES, $R_b = \texttt{0}^{120} \Vert \texttt{10000111}$ corresponds to $x^{128} + x^7 + x^2 + x + 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks in three areas:
> 1. **Forgetting $C_0 = 0^b$** — the initial chaining value must be the all-zero block.
> 2. **Confusing $K_1$ vs $K_2$** — $K_1$ is used when the message is block-aligned; $K_2$ is used when padding is required.
> 3. **Omitting the bit `'1' followed by zeros'** in padding — the padding is mandatory `10...0`, not just zeros.
> Always clearly state the **Case A (block-aligned)** vs **Case B (padding required)** distinction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete CMAC algorithm with a clear block diagram. Show the subkey generation procedure and the MAC tag generation procedure for both block-aligned and non-block-aligned messages.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Preliminaries:**
Let $E_K(\cdot)$ be a block cipher with $b$-bit blocks and key $K$. Let $M$ be a message of length $m$ bits, $n = \lceil m/b \rceil$.

**Step 2 — Subkey Generation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L &= E_K(0^b) \\
K_1 &= \begin{cases} L \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(L) = 0 \\ (L \ll 1) \oplus R_b & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(L) = 1 \end{cases} \\
K_2 &= \begin{cases} K_1 \ll 1 & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(K_1) = 0 \\ (K_1 \ll 1) \oplus R_b & \text{if } \text{MSB}_1(K_1) = 1 \end{cases}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Tag Generation (Case A — Block-aligned):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_0 &= 0^b \\
C_i &= E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n-1 \\
C_n &= E_K((M_n \oplus K_1) \oplus C_{n-1}) \\
T &= \text{MSB}_t(C_n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Tag Generation (Case B — Padding required):**
Form $M_n$ by appending `10...0` to incomplete $M$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_n &= E_K((M_n \oplus K_2) \oplus C_{n-1}) \\
T &= \text{MSB}_t(C_n)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:** The verifier recomputes $T' = \text{CMAC}_K(M)$ and accepts iff $T' = T$.

The block diagram has already been presented in **Section 4.1** above.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A message $M$ of length **256 bits** is to be authenticated using AES-CMAC with tag length $t = 128$ bits. Given that the block size $b = 128$ bits and that $L = E_K(0^{128}) = \texttt{0x80...00}$ (the most significant bit is 1), determine the values of $K_1$ and $K_2$ in hexadecimal.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the block size:**
For AES-CMAC, $b = 128$ bits.

**Step 2 — Identify $R_b$ for $b = 128$:**
$$R_b = \texttt{0}^{120} \Vert \texttt{10000111} = \texttt{0x00...0087}$$

The least significant byte is $\texttt{0x87}$ and the rest are zeros.

**Step 3 — Compute $K_1$:**
Since $\text{MSB}_1(L) = 1$, we use the second branch:

$$K_1 = (L \ll 1) \oplus R_b$$

The left shift of $L = \texttt{0x80...00}$ gives $\texttt{0x00...00}$ (the 1 shifts out):

$$L \ll 1 = \texttt{0x00...00}$$

Therefore:
$$K_1 = \texttt{0x00...00} \oplus \texttt{0x00...0087} = \texttt{0x00...0087}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $K_2$:**
Now check $\text{MSB}_1(K_1)$: since $K_1 = \texttt{0x00...0087}$, the MSB is **0**. So we use the first branch:

$$K_2 = K_1 \ll 1$$

Left-shifting $\texttt{0x00...0087}$ by 1 bit:
$$K_2 = \texttt{0x00...010E}$$

**Final Answers:**
$$\boxed{K_1 = \texttt{0x00000000000000000000000000000087}, \quad K_2 = \texttt{0x0000000000000000000000000000010E}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare CMAC with HMAC and EMAC. Highlight the security advantages of CMAC over the other two.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | HMAC | EMAC | CMAC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underlying Primitive** | Hash function | Block cipher (CBC-MAC) | Block cipher |
| **Standardization** | RFC 2104, FIPS 198-1 | None (academic) | NIST SP 800-38B |
| **Output Length** | Equal to hash output (e.g., 256 bits) | Equal to block size | Configurable ($t$ bits) |
| **Key Requirement** | 1 key | **2 keys** ($K_1, K_2$) | **1 key** ($K$ with derived subkeys) |
| **Provable Security** | Strong (Bellare et al., 1996) | Strong but needs 2 keys | **Strong (Iwata-Kurosawa, 2003)** with 1 key |
| **Domain Separation** | Inner/outer padding (`ipad`, `opad`) | None — separate keys needed | **Subkeys $K_1, K_2$ provide separation** |
| **Speed** | Fast (hash optimized) | Slower (block cipher) | Slower but hardware-friendly |

**Security Advantages of CMAC:**

1. **Single-key efficiency:** CMAC achieves the same security as EMAC with only **one** secret key, reducing key-management overhead — critical in constrained environments like smart cards and IoT.
2. **Provable security:** Iwata and Kurosawa (2003) proved that CMAC is a secure PRF (pseudorandom function) assuming the underlying block cipher is a secure PRP. This formal proof is stronger than empirical analysis.
3. **Length-independent security:** CMAC remains secure for messages of *any* length, including length-extension attacks that historically plagued hash-based MACs.
4. **Tag length flexibility:** Unlike HMAC (fixed by hash output), CMAC supports arbitrary $t \in \{32, 64, 96, 128\}$ bits, allowing trade-offs between bandwidth and security.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sender uses AES-CMAC to authenticate a 200-bit message $M$. Given that AES-CMAC uses a 128-bit block cipher and that the message is **NOT** block-aligned, walk through the complete CMAC computation showing every block. Use symbolic labels $M_1, M_2$ for blocks and clearly indicate which subkey ($K_1$ or $K_2$) is applied at the final block.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- Block size $b = 128$ bits.
- Message length $m = 200$ bits.
- Since $200 \neq 0 \pmod{128}$, padding is required (Case B).

**Step 2 — Block partition:**
$$n = \lceil 200/128 \rceil = 2$$

So $M$ is split into $M_1$ (128 bits, complete) and $M_2^*$ (72 bits, incomplete).

**Step 3 — Apply padding:**
Append a single `'1'` bit followed by $(128 - 72 - 1) = 55$ `'0'` bits to $M_2^*$:

$$M_2 = M_2^* \Vert \texttt{1} \Vert \texttt{0}^{55}$$

This makes $M_2$ exactly **128 bits** long.

**Step 4 — Initialize CBC chain:**
$$C_0 = 0^{128}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $C_1$:**
$$C_1 = E_K(M_1 \oplus C_0) = E_K(M_1 \oplus 0^{128}) = E_K(M_1)$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_2$ (with subkey $K_2$, since padding was applied):**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_2 &= E_K(M_2 \oplus C_1 \oplus K_2) \\
    &= E_K(M_2 \oplus E_K(M_1) \oplus K_2)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU point:** Since the message required padding, we use **$K_2$ at the final block**, **NOT $K_1$**. This domain separation is what prevents an attacker from confusing a padded message with a non-padded one.

**Step 7 — Output MAC tag:**
$$T = \text{MSB}_t(C_2)$$

For $t = 128$: $T = C_2$. For $t = 64$: $T$ is the first 64 bits of $C_2$.

**Verification:** The receiver performs the same chain and confirms that the recomputed tag equals the received tag.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for CMAC:**

- **Full form:** Cipher-based Message Authentication Code (NIST SP 800-38B), also known as OMAC1.
- **Purpose:** Provides **integrity** and **authentication** (NOT confidentiality).
- **Primitive:** Symmetric block cipher; standardized variants are **AES-CMAC** ($b = 128$) and **3DES-CMAC** ($b = 64$).
- **Number of secret keys required:** **One** (a major advantage over EMAC).
- **Subkeys:** $K_1$ and $K_2$ are derived from $L = E_K(0^b)$ using polynomial multiplication in $\text{GF}(2^b)$.
- **Reduction constant $R_b$:** $\texttt{0x87}$ for AES ($b = 128$); $\texttt{0x1B}$ for 3DES ($b = 64$).
- **Subkey rule:**
  - Use $K_1$ when message length is a **multiple of $b$** (no padding).
  - Use $K_2$ when padding is required.
- **Padding:** A single `'1'` bit followed by zeros — format `10...0`.
- **Chaining:** $C_0 = 0^b$; $C_i = E_K(M_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.
- **Final block:** $C_n = E_K((M_n \oplus \text{subkey}) \oplus C_{n-1})$ where subkey $\in \{K_1, K_2\}$.
- **Output tag:** $T = \text{MSB}_t(C_n)$, where $t$ is the desired tag length.
- **Security advantages:** Provable PRF security (Iwata-Kurosawa 2003); single-key efficiency; length-independent security.
- **Applications:** TLS, IPsec, IEEE 802.1X EAPOL, EMV smart cards, AES Key Wrap.
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing CBC-MAC with CMAC — CMAC's subkey mechanism is the *entire reason* it overcomes the variable-length forgery vulnerability of CBC-MAC.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GMAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GMAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Galois Message Authentication Code (GMAC)** is a cryptographic construct specifically designed to provide **data integrity and authentication** without the accompanying confidentiality (encryption) service. It is essentially the authentication-only variant of **Galois/Counter Mode (GCM)**.

Formally, GMAC is a *deterministic authenticated-encryption scheme* built upon universal hashing in the finite field $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$, combined with a block cipher (standardized with **AES-128**, **AES-192**, or **AES-256**). It produces a fixed-length **tag** of size **t bits**, where $t \in \{128, 120, 112, 104, 96\}$ as per NIST standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** GMAC is the authentication-only mode derived from GCM. When studying for KTU exams, always remember: **GCM = Encryption + Authentication**, whereas **GMAC = Authentication ONLY** (no ciphertext is produced).

The mathematical core of GMAC is the function **GHASH** — a universal hash function that maps an arbitrary-length message to a single 128-bit output via polynomial evaluation in the Galois Field $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are sending a sealed letter to a friend, but instead of putting it inside a locked safe (encryption), you simply attach a **tamper-proof wax seal** that bears your unique royal insignia. The seal itself reveals nothing about the letter's contents, but anyone who receives the letter can verify:

1. **Authenticity** — The seal bears *your* insignia, so it must have come from you.
2. **Integrity** — If even a single drop of wax is chipped, the seal breaks, revealing tampering.

GMAC works identically. The letter is the **message (plaintext)**, the seal is the **authentication tag**, and your insignia is a secret cryptographic key. The seal reveals no content (no confidentiality), but guarantees that the message was not altered in transit and originated from the legitimate sender.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While the letter (plaintext) travels openly, the *seal* (MAC tag) is mathematically bound to the letter's content. Changing even one character of the letter invalidates the seal.

## 1.3 Standardized Parameters

- **Block Cipher:** AES (FIPS-197 compliant)
- **Field:** $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ constructed with primitive polynomial $p(x) = x^{128} + x^{7} + x^{2} + x + 1$
- **Tag Lengths (t):** 128, 120, 112, 104, or **96** bits (recommended)
- **IV Length:** 96 bits (standard) or arbitrary length (with $J_0$ derivation)
- **Standard Reference:** NIST SP 800-38D

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** GMAC is widely deployed in high-speed networking protocols such as **IPsec (ESP)**, **TLS 1.2/1.3**, and **IEEE 802.1AE (MACsec)** because its parallelizable architecture allows for hardware-accelerated authentication at multi-gigabit speeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Components of GMAC

The GMAC construction relies on three fundamental building blocks:

### 2.1.1 The Hash Subkey ($H$)
$$H = E_K(0^{128})$$

- $E_K$ denotes the **AES block cipher** keyed with secret key $K$.
- $0^{128}$ is a 128-bit block of all zeros.
- $H$ serves as the "multiplicative" secret used inside the Galois Field arithmetic.

### 2.1.2 The Pre-counter Block ($J_0$)
$$J_0 = \begin{cases} IV & \text{if } \vert IV \vert = 96 \\ GHASH_H(IV \Vert 0^s \Vert [\vert IV \vert]_{64}) & \text{if } \vert IV \vert \neq 96 \end{cases}$$

- For 96-bit IVs, $J_0$ is simply the IV itself.
- For non-standard IV lengths, $J_0$ is derived via a hashing operation.

### 2.1.3 The GHASH Function
This is the algebraic heart of GMAC. For a bitstring $X = X_1 \Vert X_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert X_m$ partitioned into 128-bit blocks:

$$Y_0 = 0^{128}$$
$$Y_i = (Y_{i-1} \oplus X_i) \cdot H \quad \text{in } \mathbb{F}_{2^{128}} \text{ for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$
$$GHASH_H(X) = Y_m$$

## 2.2 The Complete GMAC Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

1. **Input Validation:** Verify that the Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) and the plaintext $P$ are well-formed.
2. **Key Setup:** Compute $H = E_K(0^{128})$.
3. **IV Processing:** Compute $J_0$ using the IV as described above.
4. **AAD Authentication:** The AAD is padded to a multiple of 128 bits and authenticated via GHASH.
5. **Plaintext Length Encoding:** Construct the block $S = [A]_{64} \Vert [C]_{64}$ where $[A]_{64}$ and $[C]_{64}$ are 64-bit big-endian representations of the bit-lengths of AAD and ciphertext. *Note: For GMAC, since there is no ciphertext, $[C]_{64} = 0$.*
6. **Tag Computation:**
$$T = GHASH_H(A \Vert 0^v \Vert C \Vert 0^u \Vert S) \oplus E_K(J_0)$$
   - Where $v$ and $u$ are zero-padding lengths to align blocks.
   - $E_K(J_0)$ is the "counter mode encryption" of the pre-counter block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why XOR with $E_K(J_0)$?** This final step binds the algebraic tag to the secret key, preventing forgeries even if an attacker learns $H$ through side channels. It is the cryptographic "insignia burnishing" of the seal analogy.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Component** | **Formula / Definition** | **Domain / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Hash Subkey | $H = E_K(0^{128})$ | 128 bits |
| Primitive Polynomial | $p(x) = x^{128} + x^{7} + x^{2} + x + 1$ | $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ |
| Standard IV Length | $\vert IV \vert = 96$ bits | 96 bits |
| Non-standard $J_0$ | $GHASH_H(IV \Vert 0^s \Vert [\vert IV \vert]_{64})$ | 128 bits |
| GHASH Recursion | $Y_i = (Y_{i-1} \oplus X_i) \cdot H$ | $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ |
| Tag Equation | $T = GHASH_H(A \Vert 0^v \Vert C \Vert 0^u \Vert S) \oplus E_K(J_0)$ | $t$ bits (default 128) |
| Length Block $S$ | $S = [A]_{64} \Vert [C]_{64}$ | 128 bits |
| Tag Lengths $t$ | $128, 120, 112, 104, 96$ | bits |
| Block Cipher | AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 | FIPS-197 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Vertical Pipes:** In the table above, $\vert IV \vert$ denotes the bit-length of the IV (the absolute value notation). All formulas are mathematically rigorous and reproducible.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

GMAC is the workhorse of high-performance authentication:

- **MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE):** Used to authenticate Ethernet frames in switched networks at line rate.
- **IPsec:** Provides the ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) authentication in tunnel-mode VPNs.
- **TLS 1.3:** Modern HTTPS connections authenticate records using GCM/GMAC suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`).
- **5G Networks:** Integrity protection of control-plane signaling in 5G AKA protocol.
- **Storage Security:** Authentication in NVMe-over-Fabrics and encrypted disk arrays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $J_0$ for Non-Standard IVs is Necessary

**Problem:** When the IV is not exactly 96 bits, GMAC must convert it into a deterministic 128-bit pre-counter block that is collision-resistant and unique per session.

**Derivation:**

Given an IV of length $\ell$ bits where $\ell \neq 96$, we proceed as follows:

**Step 1 — Padding the IV:**
The IV is concatenated with the minimum number of zero bits ($0^s$) such that its total length becomes a positive multiple of 128 bits. Let $s$ be the smallest non-negative integer such that $(\ell + s) \mod 128 = 0$.

**Step 2 — Appending the Length:**
We append a 64-bit big-endian integer representing $\ell$ (the original IV length in bits). The total construct is:

$$IV_{padded} = IV \Vert 0^s \Vert [\ell]_{64}$$

The total length of $IV_{padded}$ is $(\ell + s + 64)$, which is always a multiple of 128 bits.

**Step 3 — Hashing:**
$$J_0 = GHASH_H(IV_{padded})$$

**Example Numerical Trace:**

Let $\ell = 256$ bits, $s = 0$ (already a multiple of 128), and $\ell$ encoded in 64 bits as $0x0000000000000100$.

$$IV_{padded} = IV \Vert [256]_{64} = IV_1 \Vert IV_2 \Vert [256]_{64}$$

This is a 3-block sequence, so GHASH processes it as:

$$Y_0 = 0^{128}$$
$$Y_1 = (0^{128} \oplus IV_1) \cdot H = IV_1 \cdot H$$
$$Y_2 = (Y_1 \oplus IV_2) \cdot H = (IV_1 \cdot H \oplus IV_2) \cdot H$$
$$J_0 = (Y_2 \oplus [256]_{64}) \cdot H$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This padding scheme ensures that an attacker cannot craft two different-length IVs that hash to the same $J_0$, which would otherwise enable IV-confusion attacks.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Algebraic Authentication of AAD

**Problem:** How does GHASH ensure that every bit of the Additional Authenticated Data (AAD) is cryptographically tied to the tag?

**Derivation:**

Let $A$ be the AAD, partitioned into 128-bit blocks $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{a-1}, A_a^*$ where $A_a^*$ may be shorter than 128 bits and is right-padded with zeros to form $A_a$. Define $v = 128(a \cdot 128 - \vert A \vert)$ as the number of zero-padding bits.

The authenticated AAD is:
$$A_{auth} = A_1 \Vert A_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert A_a$$

Step-by-step recursion:
$$Y_0^{(A)} = 0^{128}$$

$$Y_1^{(A)} = (0^{128} \oplus A_1) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H$$

$$Y_2^{(A)} = (A_1 \cdot H \oplus A_2) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H^2 \oplus A_2 \cdot H$$

$$Y_3^{(A)} = (A_1 \cdot H^2 \oplus A_2 \cdot H \oplus A_3) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H^3 \oplus A_2 \cdot H^2 \oplus A_3 \cdot H$$

Generalizing:
$$Y_a^{(A)} = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{a} A_i \cdot H^{a-i+1}$$

**Final Tag Equation (Full Derivation):**

Once $Y_a^{(A)}$ is computed, the plaintext (or empty string in GMAC) and length block are processed:

$$T = \left[ \bigoplus_{i=1}^{a} A_i \cdot H^{a-i+1} \oplus \left(\bigoplus_{i=1}^{c} C_i \cdot H^{c-i+1}\right) \oplus S \cdot H \right] \oplus E_K(J_0)$$

Since GMAC has no ciphertext, $c = 0$ and the ciphertext term vanishes:

$$\boxed{T = \left[ \bigoplus_{i=1}^{a} A_i \cdot H^{a-i+1} \oplus S \cdot H \right] \oplus E_K(J_0)}$$

Where $S = [A]_{64} \Vert [C]_{64} = [A]_{64} \Vert 0^{64}$ for pure GMAC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polynomial Interpretation:** The expression $A_1 \cdot H^{a} \oplus A_2 \cdot H^{a-1} \oplus \ldots \oplus A_a \cdot H$ is precisely the evaluation of the polynomial $A(x) = A_1 x^{a-1} + A_2 x^{a-2} + \ldots + A_a$ at the secret point $x = H$ in $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$. This is why GMAC inherits the security properties of a **universal hash function family**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GMAC Top-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Secret Key K] --> A1[AES Encrypt 0 to the 128]
    A1 --> H[Hash Subkey H]
    IV[Initialization Vector IV] --> A2[Compute J0 via GHASH or Passthrough]
    A2 --> J0[Pre-counter Block J0]
    J0 --> A3[AES Encrypt J0]
    A3 --> MASK[Mask Value M = E sub K of J0]
    AAD[Additional Authenticated Data AAD] --> P1[Pad to 128-bit Blocks]
    P1 --> GH[GHASH Computation]
    H --> GH
    AADlen[Length of AAD in Bits] --> LB[Length Block S]
    Czerolen[Zero Bit Count for C] --> LB
    GH --> ACC[Accumulator XOR]
    LB --> ACC
    ACC --> XOR1[XOR Operation]
    MASK --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> TAG[Authentication Tag T of 128 bits]
    TAG --> OUT[Send to Receiver]
```

## 4.2 GHASH Recursion — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Block X1 of 128 bits] --> XORa[XOR with Y0]
    Y0[Register Y0 starts at zero to the 128] --> XORa
    XORa --> MUL[Multiply in F 2 to the 128]
    H[Hash Subkey H] --> MUL
    MUL --> Y1[New Register Y1]
    Y1 --> XORb[XOR with Y1]
    A2[Input Block X2 of 128 bits] --> XORb
    XORb --> MUL2[Multiply in F 2 to the 128]
    H --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> Y2[New Register Y2]
    Y2 --> DOTS[Continue for all blocks]
    DOTS --> YM[Final Ym equals GHASH Output]
```

## 4.3 GMAC Tag Generation — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start GMAC Tag Generation] --> S1[Step 1: Compute H = AES sub K of 0 to the 128]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Derive J0 from IV]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Construct Length Block S]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute GHASH over AAD and S]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Compute Mask = AES sub K of J0]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Tag T = GHASH XOR Mask]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Truncate T to t bits]
    S7 --> END[Output Authentication Tag]
```

## 4.4 GMAC Verification — Receiver Side Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RSTART[Receiver Receives Tag T and AAD] --> R1[Step 1: Independently Compute H]
    R1 --> R2[Step 2: Independently Derive J0]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3: Recompute Expected GHASH]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4: Recompute Expected Mask]
    R4 --> R5[Step 5: Compute Expected T star = GHASH XOR Mask]
    R5 --> R6{Step 6: Compare T star with Received T}
    R6 -- Match --> ACCEPT[Message Authenticated and Accepted]
    R6 -- Mismatch --> REJECT[Message Rejected as Forged or Tampered]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `MUL`, `R6`) and avoid reserved keywords. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text, free of markdown bold/italic tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define GMAC. How does it differ from GCM?**

**Model Answer:**

GMAC (Galois Message Authentication Code) is a cryptographic mode of operation that provides data integrity and authentication services **without** confidentiality. It is based on a universal hash function (GHASH) defined over the Galois Field $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$, combined with the AES block cipher.

**Differences from GCM:**

| **Aspect** | **GCM** | **GMAC** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Purpose | Encryption + Authentication | Authentication ONLY |
| Output | Ciphertext + Tag | Tag ONLY |
| Plaintext Processing | Counter-mode encryption | None |
| Use Case | Bulk data protection | Header / control message authentication |
| NIST Standard | SP 800-38D | SP 800-38D (as variant) |

In essence, GMAC is the "GMAC-only authentication" sub-mode of GCM, suitable when confidentiality is already provided by a separate mechanism (e.g., MACsec integrity-only mode).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is the role of the hash subkey $H$ in GMAC?**

**Model Answer:**

The hash subkey $H$ is defined as:
$$H = E_K(0^{128})$$

It is a **128-bit secret value** generated by encrypting the all-zero block with the secret key $K$ using AES. Its roles are:

1. **Multiplicative Mask:** $H$ acts as the secret multiplier in the GHASH function, evaluated in $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$.
2. **Authentication Strength:** The secrecy of $H$ ensures that an attacker cannot forge valid tags without knowing $K$.
3. **Polynomial Evaluation Point:** $H$ serves as the evaluation point at which the polynomial representing the authenticated data is computed.

If $H$ is leaked, the universal hashing property collapses, and forgery becomes possible.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the complete GMAC algorithm. Explain each step with the relevant mathematical equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a GMAC instance with AES-128, a 96-bit IV, and AAD of 256 bits, compute the structure of the final tag $T$ symbolically. Show how the length block $S$ is constructed.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Key Setup:**
$$H = E_K(0^{128})$$
Here, $E_K$ is AES-128 encryption using secret key $K$. $H$ is a fixed 128-bit hash subkey for this session.

**Step 2 — IV Processing:**
Since the IV is 96 bits, $J_0 = IV$ (no GHASH computation needed).

**Step 3 — Mask Computation:**
$$M = E_K(J_0)$$
This is the "counter-mode encryption" of $J_0$, providing a secret mask.

**Step 4 — AAD Padding:**
The AAD is right-padded with the minimum number of zero bits to make its length a multiple of 128. If $\vert A \vert$ is already a multiple of 128, no padding is added.

**Step 5 — AAD Authentication via GHASH:**
Partition AAD into blocks $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_a$:
$$Y_i = (Y_{i-1} \oplus A_i) \cdot H \quad \text{in } \mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$$

**Step 6 — Length Block Construction:**
$$S = [A]_{64} \Vert [C]_{64}$$
For pure GMAC, $C = \emptyset$, so $[C]_{64} = 0^{64}$.

**Step 7 — Final Tag Computation:**
$$T = GHASH_H(A \Vert 0^v \Vert S) \oplus M$$

**Step 8 — Truncation:**
If a shorter tag is required (e.g., 96 bits), $T$ is truncated to the leftmost $t$ bits.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- AES-128 (block size = 128 bits)
- $\vert IV \vert = 96$ bits
- $\vert A \vert = 256$ bits
- $\vert C \vert = 0$ (no ciphertext)

**Step 1 — Pad AAD:**
256 bits is already a multiple of 128, so $v = 0$ and no padding is required.

**Step 2 — Partition AAD:**
$$A = A_1 \Vert A_2, \quad a = 2$$

**Step 3 — Compute GHASH:**
$$Y_0 = 0^{128}$$
$$Y_1 = (0^{128} \oplus A_1) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H$$
$$Y_2 = (A_1 \cdot H \oplus A_2) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H^2 \oplus A_2 \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Length Block $S$:**
$[A]_{64}$ in big-endian for 256 bits is $0x0000000000000100$ (decimal 256).
$[C]_{64} = 0x0000000000000000$ (zero block).

$$S = 0x0000000000000100 \Vert 0x0000000000000000$$

**Step 5 — Full GHASH with $S$:**
$$Y_3 = (Y_2 \oplus S) \cdot H = A_1 \cdot H^3 \oplus A_2 \cdot H^2 \oplus S \cdot H$$

**Step 6 — Compute Mask:**
$$M = E_K(J_0) = E_K(IV)$$

**Step 7 — Final Tag:**
$$\boxed{T = A_1 \cdot H^3 \oplus A_2 \cdot H^2 \oplus S \cdot H \oplus E_K(IV)}$$

This tag is truncated to the desired length $t$ (default 128 bits).

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the GHASH function used in GMAC. Why is the field $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ chosen, and what is the role of the primitive polynomial?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the security properties of GMAC. What are the implications of nonce/IV reuse, and which real-world attacks have exploited this?

#### Model Solution for (a):

**The GHASH Function:**

GHASH is a universal hash function family. Given secret $H \in \mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ and input $X = X_1 \Vert X_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert X_m$:

$$GHASH_H(X) = \left(\left(\left(X_1 \cdot H \oplus X_2\right) \cdot H \oplus X_3\right) \cdot H \oplus \ldots \oplus X_m\right) \cdot H$$

In polynomial form:
$$GHASH_H(X) = X_1 H^m \oplus X_2 H^{m-1} \oplus \ldots \oplus X_m H$$

**Why $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$?**

1. **Hardware Efficiency:** All arithmetic is carry-less (XOR-based), enabling ultra-fast bit-parallel implementations.
2. **Block Cipher Alignment:** Matches the 128-bit block size of AES exactly, so one AES block = one $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ element.
3. **Cryptographic Strength:** 128-bit field provides $2^{128}$ possible secret points $H$, making brute-force infeasible.
4. **Provable Security:** Universal hash functions in finite fields have well-studied collision probabilities $\leq m \cdot 2^{-128}$.

**The Primitive Polynomial:**
$$\boxed{p(x) = x^{128} + x^7 + x^2 + x + 1}$$

This polynomial is **primitive**, meaning its root $\alpha$ generates the entire multiplicative group $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}^*$. This guarantees:

- Every non-zero field element has a multiplicative inverse.
- The discrete logarithm problem is well-defined and believed hard in this field.
- The polynomial's coefficients (`x^7 + x^2 + x + 1`) are encoded as `0x100000000000000000000000000000087` for efficient reduction.

**Reduction Operation:** When a multiplication produces a 256-bit result, the upper 128 bits are reduced using $p(x)$:
$$x^{128} \equiv x^7 + x^2 + x + 1 \pmod{p(x)}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Security Properties of GMAC:**

1. **Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attack (UF-CMA):** GMAC is provably secure assuming AES is a secure PRP and the IV is unique.
2. **Tag Length Bound:** Forgery probability is $\leq q \cdot 2^{-(t-1)} + \text{PRP-advantage}(E_K)$, where $q$ is the number of queries and $t$ is the tag length.
3. **Universal Hashing Property:** The probability of an internal hash collision is at most $m \cdot 2^{-128}$ for an $m$-block message.

**Implications of Nonce/IV Reuse:**

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL SECURITY WARNING:** If the same IV is used with the same key $K$ to authenticate two different messages, an attacker can recover $H$ and forge arbitrary tags.

**The Attack (Forbidden Attack on GCM/GMAC):**

If two message authentications use the same $(K, IV)$ pair, producing tags $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$T_1 = GHASH_H(M_1) \oplus E_K(J_0)$$
$$T_2 = GHASH_H(M_2) \oplus E_K(J_0)$$

The $E_K(J_0)$ terms cancel:
$$T_1 \oplus T_2 = GHASH_H(M_1) \oplus GHASH_H(M_2)$$

For single-block messages $M_1$ and $M_2$:
$$T_1 \oplus T_2 = (M_1 \oplus M_2) \cdot H$$

Therefore:
$$H = (M_1 \oplus M_2)^{-1} \cdot (T_1 \oplus T_2)$$

Once $H$ is recovered, the attacker can forge tags for arbitrary messages.

**Real-World Exploits:**

- **CRIME / BEAST-style attacks:** Although primarily targeting encryption, related IV-reuse vulnerabilities have been documented.
- **IEEE 802.11ad (WiGig):** Demonstrated IV-reuse attacks due to 24-bit nonce space exhaustion under high traffic.
- **IPsec implementations:** Historical CVEs (e.g., CVE-2016-0270 in OpenHSM) involved weak IV derivation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **DO NOT** confuse GMAC with CMAC. CMAC uses a different construction (CBC-MAC variant) and does NOT use Galois Field multiplication. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 2. **NEVER** omit the $+ E_K(J_0)$ term in the tag equation. Many students forget this, losing 2-3 marks.
> 3. **ALWAYS** state the primitive polynomial explicitly when explaining $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ — generic phrases like "Galois field" without specifying the polynomial lose credit.
> 4. For IV-reuse attack questions, **show the algebraic cancellation step** explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for setup even if the final $H$ recovery is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for GMAC:**

- ☐ **GMAC = Authentication ONLY** (no encryption); GCM = Encryption + Authentication.
- ☐ **Primitive Polynomial:** $p(x) = x^{128} + x^{7} + x^{2} + x + 1$ — memorize verbatim.
- ☐ **Hash Subkey:** $H = E_K(0^{128})$ — the "secret multiplier."
- ☐ **For 96-bit IVs:** $J_0 = IV$ directly (no GHASH).
- ☐ **For non-96-bit IVs:** $J_0 = GHASH_H(IV \Vert 0^s \Vert [\vert IV \vert]_{64})$.
- ☐ **Tag Equation:** $T = GHASH_H(A \Vert 0^v \Vert C \Vert 0^u \Vert S) \oplus E_K(J_0)$.
- ☐ **Length Block:** $S = [A]_{64} \Vert [C]_{64}$ — both are 64-bit big-endian.
- ☐ **Recommended Tag Length:** 96 bits (optimal security-efficiency tradeoff).
- ☐ **IV Reuse is FATAL:** Recovers $H$ and breaks the scheme completely.
- ☐ **GHASH = Universal Hash Function** in $\mathbb{F}_{2^{128}}$ — collision probability $\leq m \cdot 2^{-128}$.
- ☐ **Standard:** NIST SP 800-38D (defines both GCM and GMAC).
- ☐ **Field Arithmetic:** Carry-less (XOR-based); no carries — everything is modulo 2.
- ☐ **Security Bound:** Forgery probability $\leq q / 2^{t-1}$ for $q$ queries and $t$-bit tags.
- ☐ **Hardware Friendly:** Parallelizable across message blocks → multi-Gbps throughput.
- ☐ **Application Domains:** IPsec, TLS 1.2/1.3, MACsec (802.1AE), 5G integrity protection.
- ☐ **Distinguish from CMAC:** CMAC is a CBC-MAC variant, NOT Galois-field based.
- ☐ **Key Property:** $H$ must remain secret; if $H$ is exposed, forgeries become trivial.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Signatures: RSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Digital Signatures: RSA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital Signature** is a cryptographic primitive that provides **authenticity, integrity, and non-repudiation** for a digital message. It is the electronic analogue of a handwritten signature, but with mathematically verifiable proof. The **RSA Digital Signature Scheme**, proposed by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman (1978), is the first and most widely deployed signature scheme. It inverts the role of public and private keys compared to RSA encryption: the signer signs using the **private key** (kept secret), and any verifier checks authenticity using the publicly distributed **public key**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** RSA digital signatures are the *inverse* of RSA encryption. Encryption uses recipient's public key; **signing uses sender's private key**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine Alice mails a sealed letter. A handwritten signature can be forged. Now imagine Alice instead:
1. Writes the message $m$.
2. Computes a unique mathematical "fingerprint" $s$ using a *secret formula* known only to her.
3. Attaches $s$ to the message publicly.

Anyone can take $(m, s)$ and run a *public formula* to verify that $s$ could only have come from Alice. If the public formula outputs $m$, the signature is valid. Because the secret formula requires knowledge of Alice's private key, **only Alice could have produced $s$**. This is exactly how RSA signatures work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Inversion Property of RSA**
> - RSA **Encryption**: $c = m^e \bmod n$ (public key $e, n$); decryption $m = c^d \bmod n$ (private key $d$).
> - RSA **Signature**: $s = m^d \bmod n$ (private key $d$); verification $m = s^e \bmod n$ (public key $e, n$).
> The same key pair works in opposite directions.

### Why RSA is Reversible

Because RSA's encryption and decryption are **commutative modular exponentiations** governed by Euler's theorem, swapping the exponent $e$ with its modular inverse $d$ swaps the roles of "encrypt" and "decrypt" — the mathematical basis of RSA signatures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The RSA Digital Signature Scheme — Three Algorithms

The scheme is a triplet of algorithms: **Key Generation, Signing, Verification**.

#### Step 1 — Key Generation (by the signer, say Alice)

- **Step 1.1:** Generate two large distinct primes $p, q$ (each $\geq 1024$ bits in practice).
- **Step 1.2:** Compute the RSA modulus  
  $n = p \cdot q$
- **Step 1.3:** Compute Euler's totient  
  $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$
- **Step 1.4:** Choose public exponent $e$ such that  
  $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, with $1 < e < \phi(n)$
- **Step 1.5:** Compute private exponent $d$ as the modular inverse of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$  
  $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$
- **Public key:** $\text{PK} = (e, n)$  
  **Private key:** $\text{SK} = (d, n)$

#### Step 2 — Signing (Alice signs message $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n$)

$$s \equiv m^{d} \pmod{n}$$

Alice publishes the signed pair $(m, s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, $m$ is **never signed directly**. Alice first hashes $m$ with a secure hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256) and signs the hash: $s = H(m)^d \bmod n$. This provides *integrity* and avoids existential forgery on arbitrary messages.

#### Step 3 — Verification (Bob verifies)

Bob receives $(m, s)$, retrieves Alice's public key $(e, n)$, and computes:

$$m' \equiv s^{e} \pmod{n}$$

- If $m' = m$ (or $m' = H(m)$ in the hashed variant), the signature is **valid**.
- Otherwise, it is **rejected**.

#### The 'Why' Behind Correctness

Verification works because:

$$s^{e} \equiv (m^{d})^{e} \equiv m^{de} \equiv m^{1 + k\phi(n)} \equiv m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv m \cdot 1^{k} \equiv m \pmod{n}$$

This invokes **Euler's Theorem** ($m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ for $\gcd(m, n) = 1$), which is the algebraic engine of the entire scheme.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Constraint |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ | RSA modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| $\phi(n)$ | Euler's totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ |
| $e$ | Public exponent | $1 < e < \phi(n)$, $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| $d$ | Private exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| $m$ | Message (integer) | $0 \le m < n$ |
| $s$ | Signature | $s \equiv m^{d} \pmod{n}$ |
| Verify | Check identity | $s^{e} \equiv m \pmod{n}$ |
| Euler's Thm. | Core identity | $m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ when $\gcd(m,n) = 1$ |
| Security | Hard problem | Integer Factorization of $n$ into $p, q$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

RSA signatures underpin **PKI (Public Key Infrastructure)**: TLS/SSL certificate signing, code signing for software distribution, S/MIME email, JWT tokens, blockchain transactions, and government e-signature platforms (e.g., India's eMudhra, Aadhaar eSign). They are standardized in **PKCS#1 v1.5** and **RSA-PSS** (Probabilistic Signature Scheme, more secure).

### Security & Pitfall Notes

> [!WARNING]
> **Forgery Risks — Common Exam & Real-World Pitfalls**
> - **Plain RSA (no hash)**: Existential forgery trivial — choose any $s$, compute $m = s^e \bmod n$; pair $(m, s)$ is a "valid signature". Always hash first.
> - **Bleichenbacher's attack (PKCS#1 v1.5)**: Improper padding during signing allows signature forgery on RSA with low public exponents ($e = 3$). Use **RSA-PSS** for new systems.
> - **Small message space**: Signing $m$ directly when $m$ is small (e.g., a 3-digit number) is vulnerable. Always hash.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Correctness of RSA Signature Verification

We must show that if $s = m^d \bmod n$, then $s^e \equiv m \pmod{n}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^{e} &\equiv (m^{d})^{e} \pmod{n} && \text{(Substitute the signing equation)} \\
      &\equiv m^{de} \pmod{n} && \text{(Combine exponents: } (m^d)^e = m^{de}\text{)} \\
de &\equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)} && \text{(By definition of } d \text{ as the modular inverse of } e \text{)} \\
\Rightarrow de &= 1 + k \cdot \phi(n) \text{ for some integer } k \ge 0 && \text{(Definition of congruence)} \\
\therefore s^{e} &\equiv m^{1 + k \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \\
                &\equiv m^{1} \cdot m^{k \phi(n)} \pmod{n} && \text{(Exponent rule: } a^{b+c} = a^b \cdot a^c\text{)} \\
                &\equiv m \cdot (m^{\phi(n)})^{k} \pmod{n} && \text{(Exponent rule: } a^{bc} = (a^b)^c\text{)} \\
                &\equiv m \cdot 1^{k} \pmod{n} && \text{(Euler's Theorem: } m^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}\text{, if } \gcd(m,n)=1\text{)} \\
                &\equiv m \pmod{n} && \text{(Since } 1^k = 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the verifier recovers $m$, confirming authenticity. $\blacksquare$

### Derivation 2: Exhaustive Worked Example (Classic KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** In an RSA signature scheme, $p = 7$ and $q = 11$. Alice's public exponent is $e = 13$. Alice wishes to sign the message $m = 5$. Compute the signature and verify it.

#### Phase A — Key Generation

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= p \cdot q = 7 \cdot 11 = 77 \\
\phi(n) &= (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \cdot 10 = 60 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

We need $d$ such that $13d \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$. Apply the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
60 &= 4 \cdot 13 + 8 \\
13 &= 1 \cdot 8 + 5 \\
8  &= 1 \cdot 5 + 3 \\
5  &= 1 \cdot 3 + 2 \\
3  &= 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \\
2  &= 2 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Back-substitute to express 1 as a combination of 13 and 60:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= 3 - 1 \cdot 2 \\
  &= 3 - 1 \cdot (5 - 1 \cdot 3) = 2 \cdot 3 - 5 \\
  &= 2(8 - 1 \cdot 5) - 5 = 2 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 5 \\
  &= 2 \cdot 8 - 3(13 - 1 \cdot 8) = 5 \cdot 8 - 3 \cdot 13 \\
  &= 5(60 - 4 \cdot 13) - 3 \cdot 13 = 5 \cdot 60 - 23 \cdot 13
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $-23 \cdot 13 \equiv 1 \pmod{60}$, giving $d \equiv -23 \equiv 37 \pmod{60}$.

**Public key** $\text{PK} = (13, 77)$, **Private key** $\text{SK} = (37, 77)$.

#### Phase B — Signing $m = 5$

Compute $s = 5^{37} \bmod 77$ using **successive squaring**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{1} &\equiv 5 \pmod{77} \\
5^{2} &\equiv 25 \pmod{77} \\
5^{4} &\equiv 25^{2} = 625 \equiv 625 - 8 \cdot 77 = 625 - 616 = 9 \pmod{77} \\
5^{8} &\equiv 9^{2} = 81 \equiv 81 - 77 = 4 \pmod{77} \\
5^{16} &\equiv 4^{2} = 16 \pmod{77} \\
5^{32} &\equiv 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 3 \cdot 77 = 256 - 231 = 25 \pmod{77} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Note $37 = 32 + 4 + 1$ (binary decomposition):

$$
\begin{aligned}
5^{37} &\equiv 5^{32} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{1} \pmod{77} \\
       &\equiv 25 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 \pmod{77} \\
       &\equiv (25 \cdot 9) \cdot 5 = 225 \cdot 5 = 1125 \pmod{77} \\
       &\equiv 1125 - 14 \cdot 77 = 1125 - 1078 = 47 \pmod{77} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Signature:** $s = 47$.

#### Phase C — Verification by Bob

Bob computes $s^e = 47^{13} \bmod 77$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
47^{1} &\equiv 47 \pmod{77} \\
47^{2} &\equiv 2209 \equiv 2209 - 28 \cdot 77 = 2209 - 2156 = 53 \pmod{77} \\
47^{4} &\equiv 53^{2} = 2809 \equiv 2809 - 36 \cdot 77 = 2809 - 2772 = 37 \pmod{77} \\
47^{8} &\equiv 37^{2} = 1369 \equiv 1369 - 17 \cdot 77 = 1369 - 1309 = 60 \pmod{77} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Note $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
47^{13} &\equiv 47^{8} \cdot 47^{4} \cdot 47^{1} \pmod{77} \\
        &\equiv 60 \cdot 37 \cdot 47 \pmod{77} \\
        &\equiv (60 \cdot 37) \cdot 47 = 2220 \cdot 47 \pmod{77} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Step-by-step reduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
60 \cdot 37 &= 2220 \equiv 2220 - 28 \cdot 77 = 2220 - 2156 = 64 \pmod{77} \\
64 \cdot 47 &= 3008 \equiv 3008 - 39 \cdot 77 = 3008 - 3003 = 5 \pmod{77} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Final verification: $s^e \equiv 5 \equiv m \pmod{77}$. **Signature is VALID.** $\checkmark$

### Analytical Matrix — Multiple-Choice Style (KTU Conceptual Drill)

| Scenario | Correct Property Invoked | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Signer reveals $d$ | Total forgery possible | Anyone can now sign as the signer |
| Attacker factors $n$ | Computes $d$, forges any signature | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ recoverable from $p, q$ |
| Verifier uses wrong $e$ | Verification fails | Public key is bound to one specific $(e, n)$ |
| Hashing omitted, $m$ small | Existential forgery | Attacker picks $s$, computes $m = s^e \bmod n$ |
| Same $m$ signed twice | Identical $s$ produced | Determinism — solved by RSA-PSS padding |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A — RSA Digital Signature Workflow (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alice: Message m to sign] --> B[Step 1: Hash m with SHA-256]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute s = hash_d mod n using private key d]
    C --> D[Step 3: Publish pair m and s over insecure channel]
    D --> E[Bob: Receives pair m and s]
    E --> F[Step 4: Hash received m with SHA-256 to get mHash]
    F --> G[Step 5: Compute mCheck = s_e mod n using Alice public key e n]
    G --> H{Step 6: mCheck equals mHash?}
    H -- YES --> I[Signature Accepted: Message Authentic and Untampered]
    H -- NO --> J[Signature Rejected: Tampered or Forged]
```

### Diagram B — RSA Key Generation State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Generate large prime p] --> M[Compute n = p times q]
    P2[Generate large prime q] --> M
    M --> T[Compute phi n = p-1 times q-1]
    T --> E[Choose public exponent e with gcd e phi n = 1]
    E --> D[Compute private exponent d such that e times d = 1 mod phi n]
    D --> PK[Public Key PK = e n]
    D --> SK[Private Key SK = d n]
```

### Diagram C — RSA Signature vs RSA Encryption: Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ENC[RSA Encryption - Confidentiality]
        E1[Plaintext m] --> E2[c = m_e mod n]
        E2 --> E3[Ciphertext c travels to Bob]
        E3 --> E4[m = c_d mod n using private key d]
        E4 --> E5[Recovered Plaintext m]
    end

    subgraph SIG[RSA Signature - Authentication]
        S1[Message m] --> S2[s = m_d mod n using private key d]
        S2 --> S3[Pair m and s travels to verifier]
        S3 --> S4[m = s_e mod n using public key e]
        S4 --> S5{Equals original m?}
        S5 -- YES --> S6[Valid Signature]
        S5 -- NO --> S7[Invalid Signature]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Note the **symmetry inversion** in Subgraph ENC vs Subgraph SIG — the exponent $e$ and $d$ swap roles between the two cryptographic goals (confidentiality vs authentication).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*List any three properties that a digital signature scheme must satisfy and briefly explain each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Authenticity** — The signature must verifiably originate from the claimed sender. In RSA, only the holder of private key $d$ can compute $s = m^d \bmod n$, so a valid $s^e \equiv m$ check proves sender identity.
2. **Integrity** — Any modification of $m$ must invalidate the signature. In practice this is achieved by signing $H(m)$ (a cryptographic hash), so even a 1-bit change in $m$ produces a different hash, causing verification to fail.
3. **Non-repudiation** — The signer cannot later deny signing. Since only the signer's private key could have produced the signature, the signer is cryptographically bound to it.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Differentiate between RSA digital signature and RSA encryption schemes. Highlight the role of the keys.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | RSA Encryption | RSA Digital Signature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Goal | Confidentiality | Authentication, Integrity, Non-repudiation |
| Operation | $c = m^e \bmod n$ | $s = m^d \bmod n$ |
| Key used by sender | Recipient's **public** key $(e, n)$ | Sender's own **private** key $(d, n)$ |
| Operation by receiver | Decrypt with own **private** key $d$ | Verify with sender's **public** key $e$ |
| Math identity | $m = c^d \bmod n$ | $m = s^e \bmod n$ |

**Key Insight (1 mark):** Both schemes share the same RSA key pair but use it in *opposite directions*. The commutativity of modular exponentiation makes this dual use possible.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice Format)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the RSA digital signature scheme with its key generation, signing, and verification algorithms. State the mathematical correctness condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In an RSA signature scheme, $p = 61$, $q = 53$, and $e = 17$. Alice signs the message $m = 42$. Compute the signature $s$ and verify it. (Hint: use successive squaring.)

##### Model Solution

**(a) Algorithm Description [7 marks]**

The RSA Digital Signature Scheme consists of three algorithms:

**1. KeyGen (run once by signer):**
- Choose two large distinct primes $p, q$.
- Compute $n = p \cdot q$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ with $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.
