# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PKI High-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[End User / Subject]
    userB[Server / Service]
    ra[Registration Authority - RA]
    ca[Intermediate Certificate Authority - CA]
    root[Root Certificate Authority - Root CA]
    repo[(Certificate Repository - LDAP / HTTP)]
    crl[(CRL Repository)]
    ocsp[OCSP Responder]
    trust[Browser Trust Store - Anchors]

    userA -- "1. Submit CSR + Identity Proof" --> ra
    ra -- "2. Verify Identity" --> ca
    ca -- "3. Sign Certificate" --> root
    root -- "4. Cross-Sign / Issue Sub-CA Cert" --> ca
    ca -- "5. Publish Leaf Certificate" --> repo
    ca -- "6. Publish CRL" --> crl
    ca -- "7. Authorize OCSP" --> ocsp
    userB -- "8. Present Certificate in TLS" --> userA
    userA -- "9. Validate Chain" --> trust
    userA -- "10. Check Revocation" --> ocsp
    userA -- "11. Backup Check" --> crl
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Numbers 1–11 represent the **chronological flow** of operations from certificate application to live validation by a relying party.

---

## 4.2 X.509 v3 Certificate — Logical Field Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cert[X.509 v3 Certificate]
    tbs[tbsCertificate]
    sigalg[signatureAlgorithm]
    sigval[signatureValue]

    cert --> tbs
    cert --> sigalg
    cert --> sigval

    tbs --> v[Version = v3]
    tbs --> sn[Serial Number]
    tbs --> iss[Issuer DN]
    tbs --> val[Validity: NotBefore / NotAfter]
    tbs --> sub[Subject DN]
    tbs --> pk[Subject Public Key Info]
    tbs --> ext[Extensions v3 only]

    ext --> san[Subject Alternative Name - SAN]
    ext --> ku[Key Usage]
    ext --> eku[Extended Key Usage]
    ext --> bc[Basic Constraints CA Flag]
    ext --> crldp[CRL Distribution Points]
    ext --> aia[Authority Information Access - OCSP URL]
```

---

## 4.3 Certificate Chain of Trust (Vertical Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Root CA - Self Signed - Trusted by OS]
    int1[Intermediate CA - Web TLS]
    int2[Intermediate CA - Code Signing]
    leaf1[www example com - Leaf]
    leaf2[mail company com - Leaf]
    leaf3[Software Publisher Cert - Leaf]

    root -. "Signs" .-> int1
    root -. "Signs" .-> int2
    int1 -. "Signs" .-> leaf1
    int1 -. "Signs" .-> leaf2
    int2 -. "Signs" .-> leaf3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Root CA's certificate is **self-signed** — the Issuer equals the Subject. Operating systems and browsers ship with **hundreds of Root CAs** pre-installed in their trust stores. This pre-installation is the **single point of trust** in the Web PKI.

---

## 4.4 Certificate Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending
    Pending --> Active: CA signs certificate
    Active --> Active: Renewal issued
    Active --> Expired: NotAfter reached
    Active --> Revoked: CA publishes revocation
    Expired --> [*]
    Revoked --> [*]
```

> A certificate can transition **Active → Revoked** for reasons such as: `keyCompromise`, `CACompromise`, `affiliationChanged`, `superseded`, `cessationOfOperation`, `privilegeWithdrawn`, or `AACompromise`. These reason codes are part of the X.509 CRL Entry Extensions (RFC 5280).

---

## 4.5 CRL vs OCSP — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRL_Flow[CRL - Certificate Revocation List]
        ca1[CA] -- "Periodically publishes full list" --> repo1[(CRL Repository)]
        client1[Client] -- "Downloads whole list" --> repo1
        client1 -- "Searches for serial number" --> decision1{Serial in list?}
    end

    subgraph OCSP_Flow[OCSP - Online Certificate Status Protocol]
        ca2[CA] -- "Provides signed status on demand" --> responder[OCSP Responder]
        client2[Client] -- "Sends single serial query" --> responder
        responder -- "Returns good / revoked / unknown" --> client2
    end
```

| Property | CRL | OCSP |
|----------|-----|------|
| Granularity | Entire list (or delta) | Single certificate |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | Sub-second |
| Privacy | None (passive) | Reveals browsing to responder |
| Bandwidth | High for large lists | Low |
| Caching | Yes (long-lived) | Short-lived / OCSP stapling |

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Public Key Infrastructure (PKI). List any four of its primary components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A *Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)* is an integrated framework of policies, procedures, hardware, software, and personnel that enables the creation, issuance, management, storage, distribution, and revocation of digital certificates, which bind public keys to verified identities of users, devices, or services.

**Four Primary Components (1.5 marks — 0.375 each, or any valid four):**
1. **Certificate Authority (CA)** — issues and signs certificates.
2. **Registration Authority (RA)** — verifies applicant identities.
3. **Certificate Repository** — stores and distributes certificates (LDAP/HTTP).
4. **Certificate Revocation System** — issues CRLs or OCSP responses for invalid certificates.

> ✅ **Examiner's Expectation:** Students should *define first* and *then* list. Avoid one-word answers.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is a digital certificate? Mention its primary fields as per X.509 v3.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **digital certificate** is a tamper-resistant electronic document, digitally signed by a trusted Certificate Authority, that binds a public key to an identity (person, server, or organization).

**Primary Fields of X.509 v3 (any 4 valid fields, 0.5 marks each + 1 mark for definition):**

1. **Version** — typically v3.
2. **Serial Number** — unique integer assigned by the CA.
3. **Issuer** — Distinguished Name (DN) of the CA.
4. **Validity Period** — `NotBefore` and `NotAfter` timestamps.
5. **Subject** — DN of the certificate owner.
6. **Subject Public Key Info** — algorithm OID and the public key bits.
7. **Signature Algorithm** — algorithm used to sign (e.g., `sha256WithRSAEncryption`).
8. **Signature Value** — actual CA digital signature.
9. **Extensions** — SAN, Key Usage, Basic Constraints, CRL Distribution Points, AIA.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *As per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B module questions carry an internal choice. We provide two full-length 14-mark alternatives.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) Explain in detail the architecture and components of a Public Key Infrastructure. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A PKI architecture is built around six tightly integrated components that together deliver trusted digital identities.

1. **Certificate Authority (CA) — 1.5 marks:**
   The CA is the *root of trust* in the PKI hierarchy. It receives validated certificate requests (CSRs) from the RA, signs them with its private key, and issues X.509 v3 certificates. CAs can be organized as a *Root CA* → *Intermediate CA* → *Issuing CA* hierarchy for operational security (the root is kept offline).

2. **Registration Authority (RA) — 1 mark:**
   The RA is the *front-end identity verification* entity. It authenticates the requester (e.g., domain control validation for TLS, email verification, organizational vetting) before forwarding the request to the CA. This separation of duties reduces the risk that a compromised CA processes fraudulent requests.

3. **Certificate Repository — 1 mark:**
   A public or semi-public store (typically LDAP, HTTP, or a CDN) where issued certificates and CRLs are published so that relying parties can retrieve and verify them.

4. **Certificate Revocation System — 1 mark:**
   Implements **CRL** (signed, time-stamped, periodically published lists of revoked serial numbers) and/or **OCSP** (real-time per-certificate signed status responses) to ensure that certificates compromised before expiry are not trusted.

5. **Key Backup and Recovery — 1 mark:**
   In enterprise PKIs, private keys may be escrowed with a *Key Recovery Agent (KRA)* so that encrypted data can be recovered when an employee leaves or loses their key. This is generally avoided in Web PKI where private keys should never be escrowed.

6. **Certificate Policy (CP) and Certification Practice Statement (CPS) — 0.5 marks:**
   Legal/operational documents describing the level of assurance, the vetting procedures, the storage policies, and the audit regime of the CA.

7. **Trust Anchor / Trust Store — 0.5 marks:**
   The set of pre-installed Root CA certificates in operating systems and browsers (e.g., Mozilla NSS, Apple iOS/macOS, Windows root store).

8. **Hierarchical Verification — 0.5 marks:**
   A relying party walks the chain: *leaf → intermediate(s) → root*, validating signatures, names, dates, and revocation status at each step.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the X.509 certificate hierarchy and the certificate verification process. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Root CA - Offline - Self-Signed]
    intCA[Intermediate CA]
    leaf1[www example com - TLS Leaf]
    leaf2[vpn example com - TLS Leaf]
    leaf3[Software Vendor - Code Signing Leaf]

    root -->|Signs| intCA
    intCA -->|Signs| leaf1
    intCA -->|Signs| leaf2
    root -->|Signs directly for code signing| leaf3
```

**Verification Process (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Build the chain:** Client constructs an ordered list of certificates from the server's leaf up to a trusted root.
2. **Match Issuer–Subject:** For each link, verify that `Issuer(C_i) == Subject(C_{i+1})`.
3. **Verify signatures:** Use the public key of $C_{i+1}$ to verify the signature on $C_i$:

$$H(\text{tbs}(C_i)) \stackrel{?}{=} (s_i)^{e_{C_{i+1}}} \bmod n_{C_{i+1}}$$

4. **Check validity window:** $NotBefore \le t_{now} < NotAfter$ for every certificate in the chain.
5. **Check revocation:** Query the CRL Distribution Point or OCSP responder for every non-root cert; reject if revoked.
6. **Check constraints & key usage:** Ensure SAN matches the domain, EKU includes `serverAuth`, Basic Constraints say `CA: true` for intermediates, and the path length is not exceeded.

> ✅ **Conclusion (1 mark):** A valid chain proves that the public key genuinely belongs to the named server, enabling the TLS handshake to proceed.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) Differentiate between CRL and OCSP. Discuss their advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **CRL (Certificate Revocation List)** | **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol)** |
|-----------|----------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Definition** | A signed, time-stamped list of revoked certificate serial numbers, periodically published by the CA. | A request/response protocol that returns the live status (good / revoked / unknown) of a single certificate. |
| **Operation** | Client downloads entire (or delta) CRL and searches for the cert's serial. | Client sends a small query to the OCSP responder; responder returns a signed status. |
| **Latency** | High — updated every few hours to days. | Low — sub-second. |
| **Bandwidth** | Large for many revocations. | Small (per-certificate). |
| **Privacy** | Better — no per-connection query. | Worse — reveals browsing to the responder. |
| **Reliability** | Can be cached; tolerant of responder outage. | Single point of failure (mitigated by OCSP stapling). |
| **Standard** | RFC 5280 §5 | RFC 6960 |

**Advantages and Disadvantages (3 marks):**
- **CRL Advantages:** simple, cacheable, privacy-preserving. **Disadvantages:** large size, slower freshness, hard to scale for millions of certs.
- **OCSP Advantages:** real-time, minimal bandwidth. **Disadvantages:** privacy concerns, responder must be highly available, larger response than query.
- **Hybrid:** Many real systems use **OCSP Stapling**, where the *server* periodically obtains a fresh OCSP response and attaches ("staples") it to the TLS handshake, solving both the privacy and the latency problem.

---

**(b) Explain the digital signature process in PKI using RSA. Show the steps for both signing and verification with relevant equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let Alice (the CA) want to sign a message $M$ (the `tbsCertificate`).

**Setup — Alice's RSA Key Pair (1 mark):**
- Choose two large primes $p, q$.
- Compute $n = p \cdot q$ and $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ (typically $e = 65537$).
- Compute private exponent $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

**Signing — 3 marks:**

$$\boxed{h = \text{SHA-256}(M)}$$

Wrap with ASN.1 DigestInfo and convert to integer $m$.

$$\boxed{m = \text{Integer}(\text{DigestInfo}(h))}$$

Apply RSA private operation:

$$\boxed{s \equiv m^{\,d} \pmod{n}}$$

Alice transmits $(M, s)$ to Bob.

**Verification — 3 marks:**

Bob receives $(M, s)$. He:

1. Independently computes $h' = \text{SHA-256}(M)$.
2. Recovers the original integer using Alice's public key:

$$\boxed{m' \equiv s^{\,e} \pmod{n}}$$

3. Extracts $h_{extracted}$ from the ASN.1 DigestInfo of $m'$.
4. Compares:

$$\boxed{h' \stackrel{?}{=} h_{extracted}}$$

If equal, the signature is valid.

**Properties Delivered (0.5 mark each, 1.5 total):**
- **Authenticity:** Only Alice could have produced $s$ because only she knows $d$.
- **Integrity:** Any modification of $M$ changes the SHA-256 digest and breaks the equality.
- **Non-repudiation:** Alice cannot later deny signing $M$ because $d$ is exclusive to her.

> ✅ **Real-world note:** In production PKI, RSA-PKCS#1 v1.5 is gradually being replaced by **RSA-PSS** (more provably secure) and **ECDSA** (shorter signatures, smaller keys).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
> 1. **Never write "PKI = RSA"** — PKI is a *system*, RSA is a *primitive*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error.
> 2. **Don't skip the hashing step** in the digital-signature derivation. Writing $s = m^d \bmod n$ without first showing $h = H(m)$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Don't forget validity period** in certificate verification — many students omit `NotBefore / NotAfter` checks.
> 4. **For CRL vs OCSP**, students frequently write "CRL is real-time" — this is **wrong**; CRL is *periodic*. OCSP is real-time.
> 5. **Web of Trust is *not* a strict PKI** — phrase carefully: "PGP implements a Web of Trust, which is an *alternative* to a centralized PKI."
> 6. **Always state key size assumptions** (RSA-2048, SHA-256) in 14-mark answers — shows awareness of current best practice.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST ✅

- **PKI = Framework**, not just an algorithm. It includes CA, RA, Repository, Revocation, Key Recovery, CP/CPS, and Trust Store.
- **X.509 v3** is the dominant certificate standard; encoded in **ASN.1 DER**.
- **X.509 v3 fields:** Version, Serial, Signature Algo, Issuer, Validity, Subject, Public Key, Extensions, Signature.
- **SAN extension** is mandatory for modern TLS hostname validation.
- **Hierarchy:** Root CA → Intermediate CA(s) → End-Entity (leaf) certificates.
- **Root CA** is *self-signed* and is the only certificate that must be *pre-installed* in the trust store.
- **Digital Signature** on certificate = $s = H(\text{tbs})^{d_{CA}} \bmod n_{CA}$.
- **Verification** = $H(\text{tbs}) \stackrel{?}{=} s^{e_{CA}} \bmod n_{CA}$.
- **CRL** = signed, periodic list of revoked serials; large but cacheable; privacy-friendly.
- **OCSP** = real-time per-certificate signed status; small but reveals browsing to responder; solved by **OCSP stapling**.
- **Trust Models:** Hierarchical (Web PKI), Mesh (cross-certified), Web of Trust (PGP).
- **Reasons for revocation:** `keyCompromise`, `CACompromise`, `affiliationChanged`, `superseded`, `cessationOfOperation`, `privilegeWithdrawn`.
- **Key size today:** RSA-2048 (or RSA-3072 for long-term secrets), ECDSA P-256/P-384, SHA-256/SHA-384.
- **Real-world uses:** TLS, S/MIME, code signing, smart cards, e-IDs, IoT, Kubernetes, PDF signing.
- **Common exam confusion to avoid:** PGP Web of Trust ≠ strict PKI. CRL ≠ real-time. PKI ≠ RSA. CN field ≠ used for hostname validation in modern TLS.
- **Engineering relevance:** PKI is the trust layer for the modern Internet; understanding it is essential for security engineers, cloud architects, and DevOps professionals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SSL/TLS Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SSL/TLS Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSL/TLS (Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security)** is a critical application-layer cryptographic protocol stack designed to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication**, and **replay protection** for communications traversing untrusted networks (most notably the Internet). It operates as an **intermediate layer** between the application protocol (e.g., HTTP, FTP, SMTP) and the transport protocol (typically TCP), and is formally standardized by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** under the **TLS Working Group**.

Historically, **SSL (Secure Sockets Layer)** was developed by **Netscape Communications Corporation** in the mid-1990s. The de facto industry succession, **TLS (Transport Layer Security)**, was adopted as an IETF standard beginning with **RFC 2246 (TLS 1.0)** in 1999, evolving through **RFC 4346 (TLS 1.1)**, **RFC 5246 (TLS 1.2)**, to the current **RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3)** published in 2018.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Students must clearly distinguish between SSL 3.0 (deprecated by RFC 7568) and modern TLS 1.2/1.3. Examiners expect knowledge of the **protocol stack architecture**, the **handshake protocol**, the **record protocol**, and **key derivation functions**.

| Protocol | Version | Standard / Status |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| SSL 1.0 | 1994 | Never publicly released |
| SSL 2.0 | 1995 | **Deprecated** (RFC 6176) |
| SSL 3.0 | 1996 | **Deprecated** (RFC 7568) |
| **TLS 1.0** | 1999 | RFC 2246 — Legacy |
| **TLS 1.1** | 2006 | RFC 4346 — Legacy |
| **TLS 1.2** | 2008 | RFC 5246 — Widely Deployed |
| **TLS 1.3** | 2018 | RFC 8446 — Current Standard |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diplomatic Passport Checkpoint"

Imagine a KTU student travelling internationally for a conference. Before boarding the flight, they pass through a **diplomatic passport control booth** where several security operations occur in sequence:

1. **Mutual Greeting & Language Negotiation** — Both the traveller and the border officer confirm they speak a mutually understood diplomatic "language" (this is the *ClientHello* and *ServerHello* negotiating the cipher suite).
2. **Identity Verification** — The officer checks the traveller's passport signed by a trusted government authority (this is the **X.509 Digital Certificate** validated against a trusted **Certificate Authority / CA**).
3. **Secret Key Handshake (Key Exchange)** — Both parties step into a private booth and, using public padlocks (the server's *public key*) and secret codes, independently derive a shared symmetric **session key**.
4. **Sealed Channel** — From this point on, all future conversations (the *record protocol traffic*) happen in a private, encrypted "cone of silence" that outsiders cannot eavesdrop on.

This perfectly mirrors the **SSL/TLS operational philosophy**: publicly agree on cryptographic parameters → authenticate identity via certificates → securely derive ephemeral symmetric keys → protect all subsequent application data with those keys.

---

### 1.3 Core Services Provided by SSL/TLS

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the acronym **"CIA-R"** for the four primary security services guaranteed by TLS:
>
> - **C**onfidentiality — via symmetric encryption (AES, ChaCha20)
> - **I**ntegrity — via HMAC or AEAD constructions (GCM, Poly1305)
> - **A**uthentication — via X.509 certificates (server, and optionally client)
> - **R**eplay Protection — via per-session 32-byte client & server random nonces

**Default TCP Port:** **443** (for HTTPS). Standard port for HTTP is 80.

---

### 1.4 Position in the TCP/IP Protocol Stack

SSL/TLS is a **session-layer / presentation-layer** protocol that sits **above TCP** (which guarantees reliable, ordered delivery) and **below** the application protocol (HTTP, SMTP, IMAP, etc.). This layered abstraction is precisely why the same TLS machinery can secure any TCP-based application — a property known as **"application-protocol-agnosticism"**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Components of the SSL/TLS Protocol Stack

The TLS specification defines **two layered sub-protocols**:

#### 2.1.1 The TLS Handshake Layer (Upper Sub-Layer)

The Handshake Layer consists of **four closely-coupled protocols**, each with a distinct purpose:

| Sub-Protocol | Function | Packet Type |
|--------------|----------|-------------|
| **Handshake Protocol** | Negotiates parameters & establishes shared secrets | `Handshake` (ContentType = 22) |
| **Change Cipher Spec Protocol** | Signals transition to newly negotiated cipher suite | `ChangeCipherSpec` (ContentType = 20) |
| **Alert Protocol** | Conveys error/warning messages between peers | `Alert` (ContentType = 21) |
| **Application Data Protocol** | Carries the actual data after the handshake completes | `ApplicationData` (ContentType = 23) |

#### 2.1.2 The TLS Record Protocol (Lower Sub-Layer)

The Record Protocol acts as the **secure carrier / encapsulation layer** for all higher-layer protocols. It performs the following operations on every outgoing message:
1. **Fragmentation** of upper-layer messages into blocks of size ≤ 16,384 bytes (2^14).
2. Optional **Compression** (disabled by default in modern TLS due to CRIME/BREACH attacks).
3. **MAC (Message Authentication Code)** computation for integrity.
4. **Encryption** using the negotiated symmetric algorithm.
5. **TLS Record Header** prepending (5 bytes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Record Protocol processes **one logical message stream at a time** and multiplexes the four content types (handshake, change_cipher_spec, alert, application_data) into a single byte stream over TCP.

---

### 2.2 Detailed TLS 1.2 Handshake Sequence (10-Step State Machine)

The Handshake Protocol proceeds through a **four-phase state machine**:

#### **Phase 1: Establish Security Capabilities**

**Step 1 — Client → Server: `ClientHello`**
- `client_version` : Highest TLS version supported (e.g., TLS 1.2)
- `random` : 32-byte nonce — 4 bytes Unix time + 28 bytes random
- `session_id` : 0 to 32 bytes (used for session resumption)
- `cipher_suites` : Prioritized list of cryptographic suites (e.g., `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`)
- `compression_methods` : Typically `null` (no compression)
- `extensions` : SNI, ALPN, signature_algorithms, etc.

**Step 2 — Server → Client: `ServerHello`**
- Server selects **one** `cipher_suite` from the client's list
- Server sends its own 32-byte `random` nonce
- The selected `compression_method` and `session_id`

> [!NOTE]
> If no acceptable cipher suite exists, the server issues a `handshake_failure` (Alert Level: fatal).

#### **Phase 2: Server Authentication & Key Exchange**

**Step 3 — `Certificate` Message** (mandatory for all RSA / ECDHE_RSA suites)
- The server transmits its **X.509v3 digital certificate chain**, terminating at a trusted root CA.
- This binds the server's identity to its **public key**.

**Step 4 — `ServerKeyExchange` Message** (only for ephemeral suites like DHE / ECDHE)
- Contains the server's **ephemeral Diffie-Hellman public value** (e.g., ECDH parameters: curve type, named curve, public key point).
- The message is **digitally signed** by the server's long-term private key to prevent MITM tampering.

**Step 5 — `ServerHelloDone` Message**
- A single byte indicating the server has finished its phase-2 messages.

> [!TIP]
> The server may also send a `CertificateRequest` to demand **client-side authentication** (mutual TLS / mTLS), used in banking, IoT, and enterprise systems.

#### **Phase 3: Client Authentication & Key Exchange**

**Step 6 — `ClientKeyExchange` Message**
- For RSA key transport: client generates the **pre-master secret (PMS)** of 48 bytes and encrypts it with the server's public RSA key.
- For DHE/ECDHE: client transmits its **ephemeral DH public value** to the server.

**Step 7 — `CertificateVerify` Message** (only if server requested client cert)
- The client proves possession of the private key corresponding to its certificate by signing a hash of all preceding handshake messages.

#### **Phase 4: Finish the Handshake**

**Step 8 — `ChangeCipherSpec` (Client → Server)**
- A single-byte message that signals: *"All subsequent messages from me will be encrypted under the newly negotiated keys."*

**Step 9 — `Finished` (Client → Server)**
- Contains a **PRF (Pseudo-Random Function) output** — typically using HMAC-SHA256 — computed over the **hash of all preceding handshake messages** plus a `client_finished` label and the `master_secret`.
- Acts as the **first encrypted message** and serves as a **cryptographic receipt** that both parties derived the same keys.

**Step 10 — `ChangeCipherSpec` + `Finished` (Server → Client)**
- The server switches to the new cipher and sends its own `Finished` message. The handshake concludes.

---

### 2.3 Cryptographic Computations — Key Derivation

The **PRF (Pseudo-Random Function)** is the cryptographic engine used to expand the pre-master secret into the complete set of session keys.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PRF in TLS 1.2 uses the **HMAC-SHA-256** construction by default (selectable via the `PRF` cipher suite extension).

#### 2.3.1 Pre-Master Secret Generation

For **RSA key transport**:
$$PMS = \text{Random}_{48\text{-byte}} \quad \text{(generated by client)}$$

This PMS is encrypted with the server's public RSA key and sent in `ClientKeyExchange`.

For **(EC)DHE key agreement**:
$$PMS = \text{ECDH}(client\_priv, server\_pub) = \text{ECDH}(server\_priv, client\_pub)$$

This value is 48 bytes (TLS 1.2) and computed independently by both peers.

#### 2.3.2 Master Secret Derivation

$$\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(PMS, \text{"master secret"}, C \Vert S)$$

where $C$ = `client.random` and $S$ = `server.random` (32 bytes each).

> The **PRF** is defined as the expansion of a secret into an arbitrarily long output using a hash-based function:
$$\text{PRF}(\text{secret}, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = P\_\text{HMAC}(\text{secret}, \text{label} \Vert \text{seed})$$

where $P\_\text{HMAC}$ is the standard TLS 1.2 HMAC-based expansion function using HMAC-SHA-256.

#### 2.3.3 Key Block Expansion

Once the `master_secret` is derived, both peers expand it into a **key block** containing 4 (or 6 with explicit IVs) symmetric keys:

$$\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{master\_secret}, \text{"key expansion"}, S \Vert C)$$

| Variable | Purpose | Length (AES-128-CBC) |
|----------|---------|----------------------|
| `client_write_MAC_key` | HMAC key for client → server | 32 bytes |
| `server_write_MAC_key` | HMAC key for server → client | 32 bytes |
| `client_write_key` | Encryption key for client → server | 16 bytes |
| `server_write_key` | Encryption key for server → client | 16 bytes |
| `client_write_IV` | IV for AEAD (client → server) | 4 bytes (TLS 1.2 CBC) |
| `server_write_IV` | IV for AEAD (server → client) | 4 bytes (TLS 1.2 CBC) |

> [!TIP]
> **Directional key separation** is critical. A unique key is used for each direction to prevent reflection and cross-protocol attacks.

---

### 2.4 TLS Record Protocol — Detailed Operation

**Outbound Processing Pipeline:**

1. **Fragmentation** — Upper-layer message $M$ is split into blocks $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$ where $|M_i| \leq 2^{14}$ bytes.

2. **Compression** (optional, often `null`):
   $$M_i' = \text{compress}(M_i)$$

3. **MAC Computation**:
   $$\text{MAC}_i = \text{HMAC}(\text{write\_MAC\_key}, \text{seq\_num} \Vert \text{type} \Vert \text{version} \Vert \text{length} \Vert M_i')$$

4. **Encryption** (CBC mode for TLS 1.2, AEAD for TLS 1.3):
   $$\text{ciphertext}_i = E_k(M_i' \Vert \text{padding} \Vert \text{MAC}_i)$$

5. **Record Header Assembly**:
$$\underbrace{\text{ContentType}}_{\text{1 byte}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{ProtocolVersion}}_{\text{2 bytes}} \;\Vert\; \underbrace{\text{Length}}_{\text{2 bytes}} \;\Vert\; \text{ciphertext}_i$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Term | Symbolic Definition | KTU Application |
|----------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Pre-Master Secret (PMS)** | $\text{PMS} = \text{RNG}_{48}$ (RSA) or $\text{ECDH}(aP, bP)$ | Source secret for key derivation |
| **Master Secret** | $\text{MS} = \text{PRF}(PMS, \text{"master secret"}, C \Vert S)$ | 48-byte shared secret |
| **Key Block** | $\text{KB} = \text{PRF}(MS, \text{"key expansion"}, S \Vert C)$ | Expanded to all session keys |
| **Finished (Client)** | $\text{verify\_data} = \text{PRF}(MS, \text{"client finished"}, H(\text{handshake}))$ | First encrypted message |
| **Record MAC** | $\text{HMAC}(K, \text{seq} \Vert \text{type} \Vert \text{ver} \Vert \text{len} \Vert \text{payload})$ | Integrity tag |
| **AEAD Encryption (TLS 1.3)** | $A_i = E_K(N, \text{plaintext}, \text{aad})$ | Single-pass auth + encrypt |
| **Forward Secrecy** | $(EC)DH$ keys discarded post-session | PFS guarantee |
| **Cipher Suite Format** | `TLS_KX_AUTH_WITH_ENC_MAC` | e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** The `Finished` message MAC is computed over the **handshake hash `H(handshake)`** and is **NOT** the same as the Record Protocol MAC. Confusing these two will cost you marks in derivation questions.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

TLS is the **backbone of internet commerce and secure communications**:
- **HTTPS (HTTP over TLS, port 443):** Secures ~95% of web traffic (per Google Transparency Reports).
- **SMTPS, IMAPS, POP3S:** Secure email retrieval and transmission.
- **VPN / Tunneling:** OpenVPN, WireGuard (conceptually similar), SSH tunneling leverage TLS for authentication and key exchange.
- **IoT & Embedded Systems:** MQTT over TLS for smart devices.
- **Banking & Payment Gateways:** Mandatory PCI-DSS compliance requires TLS 1.2+.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Pre-Master Secret to Session Keys (TLS 1.2, RSA Mode)

**Given:**
- $PMS$ = 48-byte pre-master secret (randomly generated by client)
- $C$ = `client.random` (32 bytes from `ClientHello`)
- $S$ = `server.random` (32 bytes from `ServerHello`)
- Cipher suite = `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA256`

**Objective:** Derive `master_secret`, then `key_block`, then partition into session keys.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute the Master Secret

$$\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(PMS, \text{"master secret"}, C \Vert S)$$

**Logic:** The PRF expands the 48-byte $PMS$ into a deterministic 48-byte output using a HMAC-SHA-256-based data expansion function. The `client.random` and `server.random` are concatenated to inject session-unique entropy, ensuring that even if the same $PMS$ is reused (a hypothetical edge case), different sessions will yield different master secrets.

---

#### Step 2 — Compute the Key Block

The `master_secret` is now expanded to a length sufficient to produce all required session keys.

Let $L = $ total bytes required. For `AES_128_CBC_SHA256` (no explicit IVs in TLS 1.2 standard mode), we need:
- 2 × 32 bytes (HMAC-SHA256 keys) = 64 bytes
- 2 × 16 bytes (AES-128 keys) = 32 bytes
- Total: $L = 96$ bytes

$$\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(MS, \text{"key expansion"}, S \Vert C)$$

**Logic:** The order of $S \Vert C$ is reversed compared to the master secret derivation — this is a deliberate **defensive design** to ensure the two PRF invocations produce cryptographically independent outputs even if their inputs overlap structurally. The output is a single 96-byte blob.

---

#### Step 3 — Partition the Key Block

The 96-byte `key_block` is split sequentially:

| Byte Range | Variable | Size |
|------------|----------|------|
| [0 .. 31] | `client_write_MAC_key` | 32 |
| [32 .. 63] | `server_write_MAC_key` | 32 |
| [64 .. 79] | `client_write_key` | 16 |
| [80 .. 95] | `server_write_key` | 16 |

**Logic:** Each direction (client → server and server → client) gets its **own MAC key** and **own encryption key** to prevent reflection attacks and ensure that compromising one direction's key does not compromise the reverse direction.

---

#### Step 4 — Compute the Client `Finished` Verify Data

The `verify_data` value is a 12-byte (96-bit) HMAC-SHA-256 truncated output:

$$V_{\text{client}} = \text{PRF}(MS, \text{"client finished"}, H(\text{handshake\_msg}))$$

where $H(\text{handshake\_msg})$ is a running SHA-256 hash of **all handshake messages** exchanged up to but not including the `Finished` message itself.

**Logic:** This serves as a **mutual cryptographic receipt**. The server independently computes $V_{\text{client}}$ and compares it to the received value. If they match, both parties have provably:
1. The same `master_secret`,
2. The same negotiated parameters, and
3. No MITM has tampered with the handshake.

> [!NOTE]
> The hash of the handshake messages **excludes** the `ChangeCipherSpec` messages but **includes** all `Handshake` protocol messages (including the certificate chains and key exchange).

---

### 3.2 Detailed Example: Cipher Suite Walk-Through

**Cipher Suite:** `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`

Decoding the components:

| Token | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `TLS` | Protocol family |
| `ECDHE` | Key exchange: **Ephemeral Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman** |
| `RSA` | Server authentication: signed with **RSA** private key |
| `AES_256_GCM` | Symmetric encryption: **AES-256 in GCM** mode (AEAD) |
| `SHA384` | PRF & hash function: **SHA-384** |

**Operational Sequence for ECDHE_RSA:**

1. Server signs the ECDHE parameters with its **RSA private key** (Step 4 — `ServerKeyExchange`).
2. Client verifies the RSA signature using the public key in the server's certificate (Step 6 verification).
3. Both peers compute the **shared ECDH secret**:
   $$\text{PMS} = x\text{-coordinate of } (d_c \cdot d_s \cdot G)$$
   where $d_c$ = client's ephemeral private key, $d_s$ = server's ephemeral private key, $G$ = generator point on the curve.
4. Both peers immediately **discard** $d_c$ and $d_s$ after computing $PMS$ — this property is **Forward Secrecy (PFS)**: compromising the long-term RSA key in the future will *not* retroactively decrypt the session.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SSL 3.0 vs TLS 1.0/1.2 vs TLS 1.3

| Feature | SSL 3.0 | TLS 1.0/1.1 | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|---------|---------|-------------|---------|---------|
| **MAC Construction** | Ad-hoc (padded MD5/SHA-1) | HMAC (cryptographically sound) | HMAC (configurable hash) | **AEAD only** (GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) |
| **Cipher Suite Negotiation** | Vulnerable to downgrade | Vulnerable to downgrade | Adds `signature_algorithms` ext. | **Hardened** (downgrade sentinels) |
| **Key Exchange** | RSA, Fixed DH, Fortezza | RSA, DHE | RSA, DHE, **ECDHE** | **ECDHE / DHE only** (no RSA key transport) |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Optional | Optional | Optional | **Mandatory** |
| **Handshake RTT** | 2 RTT | 2 RTT | 2 RTT | **1 RTT (or 0-RTT)** |
| **RC4 / 3DES** | Allowed | Allowed | Deprecated | **Removed** |
| **CBC Mode** | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed (with explicit IV in 1.1) | **Removed** (AEAD only) |
| **Compression** | Allowed | Allowed | Allowed (CRIME) | **Removed** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> TLS 1.3 reduces the cipher suite list to **5 AEAD-only suites** — this is a major architectural simplification that eliminates entire classes of cryptographic attacks (BEAST, POODLE, Lucky 13).

---

### 3.4 Mathematical Proof Skeleton: Why the `Finished` Message Prevents MITM

**Assumption:** A network attacker $\mathcal{A}$ sits between client $\mathcal{C}$ and server $\mathcal{S}$.

**Handshake Hash:** Let $H_M = \text{SHA-256}(m_1 \Vert m_2 \Vert \dots \Vert m_k)$ be the hash of all handshake messages.

**If $\mathcal{A}$ tries to modify any $m_i$:** Both $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathcal{S}$ will compute different values of $H_M$ (SHA-256 collision resistance). Therefore, $V_{\text{client}}$ computed by $\mathcal{C}$ will differ from $V_{\text{client}}$ recomputed by $\mathcal{S}$. The server's MAC verification fails:

$$\text{HMAC}_{K}(\text{received } V_{\text{client}}) \neq \text{HMAC}_{K}(\text{expected } V_{\text{client}})$$

**Conclusion:** The `Finished` message is the **integrity anchor** of the entire handshake. Without it, an attacker could transparently substitute DH parameters and perform a MITM attack. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Sample Numerical: PRF Expansion in TLS 1.2

The TLS 1.2 PRF is defined as:

$$P_{\text{HMAC}}(S, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = A(1) \Vert A(2) \Vert \dots \Vert A(m)$$

where the iteration function is:
$$A(0) = \text{seed}$$
$$A(i) = \text{HMAC}(S, A(i-1))$$

and the output expansion at each iteration is:
$$P_{\text{HMAC}}(S, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = \text{HMAC}(S, A(1) \Vert \text{label} \Vert \text{seed}) \Vert \text{HMAC}(S, A(2) \Vert \text{label} \Vert \text{seed}) \Vert \dots$$

The process continues until the desired output length is reached. This is a **counter-mode HMAC construction** with built-in feedback to ensure that output bits are correlated yet unpredictable without knowledge of $S$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: SSL/TLS Protocol Stack Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer - HTTP, SMTP, FTP, IMAP] --> B[TLS Handshake Layer]
    B --> B1[Handshake Protocol]
    B --> B2[Change Cipher Spec Protocol]
    B --> B3[Alert Protocol]
    B --> B4[Application Data Protocol]
    B1 --> C[TLS Record Protocol]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> C1[Fragmentation]
    C1 --> C2[Optional Compression]
    C2 --> C3[MAC Computation HMAC]
    C3 --> C4[Encryption AES-GCM or ChaCha20]
    C4 --> D[TCP Layer - Transport]
    D --> E[IP Layer - Network]
    E --> F[Data Link Layer - Ethernet, WiFi]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: TLS 1.2 Handshake State Machine (10-Step Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C,S: Phase 1 - Establish Security Capabilities
    C->>S: ClientHello - version, random, cipher_suites, session_id
    S-->>C: ServerHello - selected suite, server random

    Note over C,S: Phase 2 - Server Authentication
    S->>C: Certificate - X.509 chain to trusted CA
    S->>C: ServerKeyExchange - ECDHE params signed with RSA
    S->>C: ServerHelloDone - single byte terminator

    Note over C,S: Phase 3 - Client Key Exchange
    C->>S: ClientKeyExchange - ECDHE public value

    Note over C,S: Phase 4 - Switch to Encrypted Channel
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec - switch to new keys
    C->>S: Finished - HMAC over handshake hash
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec - switch to new keys
    S-->>C: Finished - HMAC over handshake hash

    Note over C,S: Encrypted Application Data Exchange
    C->>S: ApplicationData - HTTP request GET index.html
    S-->>C: ApplicationData - HTTP response 200 OK
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: TLS Record Protocol Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Upper Layer Message] --> B[Fragmentation into 16KB blocks]
    B --> C[Optional Compression null by default]
    C --> D[Append HMAC for Integrity]
    D --> E[Add Padding for CBC or AEAD Nonce]
    E --> F[Symmetric Encryption AES-GCM]
    F --> G[Prepend TLS Record Header 5 bytes]
    G --> H[Transmit over TCP Socket]

    H --> I[Receive on Peer]
    I --> J[Parse Record Header]
    J --> K[Decrypt with Session Key]
    K --> L[Verify HMAC Tag]
    L --> M[Decompress if needed]
    M --> N[Reassemble Fragments]
    N --> O[Deliver to Upper Layer]
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Key Derivation Cascade (Pre-Master → Master → Key Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Client Generates 48-byte Pre-Master Secret] --> B[RSA Encrypt with Server Public Key]
    B --> C[Transmit in ClientKeyExchange]
    C --> D[Server Decrypts with Private Key]

    A2[ECDHE Computation by Both Peers] --> D2[Shared PMS = 48 bytes]

    D --> E[Compute Master Secret]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[PRF invocation - master secret label]
    E1 --> E2[Input PMS, client.random, server.random]
    E2 --> F[Master Secret - 48 bytes]

    F --> G[Compute Key Block]
    G --> G1[PRF invocation - key expansion label]
    G1 --> G2[Input MS, server.random, client.random]
    G2 --> H[Key Block - 96 bytes for AES-128]

    H --> H1[client_write_MAC_key 32 bytes]
    H --> H2[server_write_MAC_key 32 bytes]
    H --> H3[client_write_key 16 bytes]
    H --> H4[server_write_key 16 bytes]
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: TLS Session Resumption via Session ID (Abbreviated Handshake)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C,S: Initial Full Handshake Complete - SessionID stored
    C->>S: ClientHello - includes cached SessionID
    S-->>C: ServerHello - same SessionID echoed
    S->>C: ChangeCipherSpec
    S->>C: Finished
    C->>S: ChangeCipherSpec
    C->>S: Finished

    Note over C,S: 1-RTT Abbreviated Handshake Using Resumed Master Secret
    C->>S: ApplicationData - resumes from prior session
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the **SSL/TLS Record Protocol**. List its main operational functions.

**Model Answer:**

The **TLS Record Protocol** is the lower sub-layer of the TLS protocol stack and serves as the **secure encapsulation mechanism** for all higher-layer TLS sub-protocols (Handshake, ChangeCipherSpec, Alert) and application data. It fragments, optionally compresses, computes a MAC, encrypts, and prepends a 5-byte header to every outgoing message.

**Main functions:**
1. **Fragmentation** of upper-layer messages into blocks of ≤ 16,384 bytes.
2. **Compression** (optional, disabled by default in modern TLS).
3. **MAC computation** using HMAC with the negotiated hash algorithm.
4. **Encryption** using the negotiated symmetric algorithm (e.g., AES-GCM).
5. **Header assembly** containing content type, protocol version, and length.
6. On the receive side: decryption, MAC verification, decompression, reassembly.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for functions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Distinguish between **SSL 3.0** and **TLS 1.2** with respect to (i) MAC construction and (ii) certificate handling.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) MAC Construction:**
- **SSL 3.0:** Uses an **ad-hoc, custom MAC** function based on padded MD5 or SHA-1, which was later shown to have theoretical weaknesses.
- **TLS 1.2:** Uses the standardized **HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code)** construction (RFC 2104), which is provably secure and supports a configurable hash (SHA-256, SHA-384).

**(ii) Certificate Handling:**
- **SSL 3.0:** Supports X.509v3 certificates but has weaker certificate chain validation rules and is vulnerable to truncation and renegotiation attacks.
- **TLS 1.2:** Strengthens chain validation, supports **Server Name Indication (SNI)** and **signature_algorithms extension**, allowing the client to specify acceptable certificate signature algorithms (RSA-SHA256, ECDSA-SHA384, etc.).

*(3 marks: 1.5 for each sub-part)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **SSL/TLS Handshake Protocol** in detail, explaining each message exchanged between the client and server. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PRF (Pseudo-Random Function)** used in TLS 1.2 and show how the **master secret** and **key block** are derived from the **pre-master secret**. Include the role of `client.random` and `server.random`. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **TLS Record Protocol** in detail. Describe the format of the TLS record header and the processing steps performed on both the sending and receiving sides. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security services** provided by SSL/TLS. With examples, explain at least **three major attacks** on SSL/TLS (e.g., POODLE, BEAST, CRIME, Heartbleed) and the mitigations adopted in TLS 1.3. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.4 Detailed Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Part B — Question A (a): Handshake Protocol

**The TLS 1.2 Handshake Protocol** is a 10-step cryptographic dialogue used to establish a shared secret, authenticate the server (and optionally the client), and negotiate cryptographic parameters. The four phases are:

**Phase 1: Establish Security Capabilities**
- **ClientHello:** The client initiates the handshake by sending its highest supported TLS version (e.g., TLS 1.2), a 32-byte `client.random` nonce, a 32-byte `session_id` (for resumption), a list of supported **cipher suites** in priority order, compression methods, and TLS extensions (SNI, ALPN, signature_algorithms).
- **ServerHello:** The server responds with the selected `protocol_version`, its own 32-byte `server.random`, the chosen `session_id`, the negotiated `cipher_suite`, and the chosen compression method.

**Phase 2: Server Authentication & Key Exchange**
- **Certificate:** The server sends its X.509v3 **digital certificate chain** ending in a trusted root CA. The client validates the chain against its local trust store (browser or OS).
- **ServerKeyExchange:** (Required for DHE/ECDHE) The server transmits its ephemeral DH/ECDH public parameters, **digitally signed** with its long-term private key (RSA or ECDSA) to bind the ephemeral key to its identity.
- **ServerHelloDone:** A single-byte marker indicating the end of the server's phase-2 messages.

**Phase 3: Client Key Exchange & Authentication**
- **ClientKeyExchange:** For RSA, the client generates a 48-byte **pre-master secret (PMS)** and encrypts it with the server's public key. For ECDHE, the client sends its ephemeral ECDH public value. Both peers independently compute the **shared PMS**.
- **CertificateVerify:** (Optional, only if server requested client auth) The client signs a hash of all preceding handshake messages to prove possession of its private key.

**Phase 4: Switch to Encrypted Channel**
- **ChangeCipherSpec (Client):** Signals that subsequent client messages will be encrypted.
- **Finished (Client):** A `verify_data` value computed as $\text{PRF}(MS, \text{"client finished"}, H(\text{handshake}))$ — the **first encrypted message**.
- **ChangeCipherSpec (Server)** + **Finished (Server):** Mirror operations on the server side.

The handshake concludes with both parties having derived the **master secret** and the **key block**, and having cryptographically verified each other's possession of the same secrets.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram description + 5 for message-by-message explanation)*

---

#### Solution to Part B — Question A (b): PRF and Key Derivation

**The TLS 1.2 PRF** is a pseudo-random function used as a **key-derivation primitive**. It is defined using the **HMAC construction** with SHA-256 as the default hash:

$$\text{PRF}(\text{secret}, \text{label}, \text{seed}) = P_{\text{HMAC}}(\text{secret}, \text{label} \Vert \text{seed})$$

The internal expansion $P_{\text{HMAC}}$ is defined as:

$$P_{\text{HMAC}}(S, L, Sd) = A(1) \Vert A(2) \Vert \dots$$

where:
- $A(0) = Sd$ (the seed)
- $A(i) = \text{HMAC}(S, A(i-1))$
- Each output block = $\text{HMAC}(S, A(i) \Vert L \Vert Sd)$

**Step 1: Pre-Master Secret (PMS) Generation**

For RSA: the client generates a cryptographically random 48-byte value $PMS$ and encrypts it with the server's RSA public key. The server decrypts it with its private key.

For ECDHE: both peers perform the standard ECDH computation $PMS = d_c \cdot d_s \cdot G$ and obtain the x-coordinate as the shared secret.

**Step 2: Master Secret Derivation**

$$\text{master\_secret} = \text{PRF}(PMS, \text{"master secret"}, C \Vert S)$$

where $C$ = `client.random` and $S$ = `server.random`. The role of these nonces is to **inject session-unique entropy**: even if the same $PMS$ is somehow reused across sessions, the master secret will differ because the random nonces differ. This is critical for **binding** the key derivation to the specific session.

**Step 3: Key Block Expansion**

$$\text{key\_block} = \text{PRF}(\text{master\_secret}, \text{"key expansion"}, S \Vert C)$$

Note that the order of $S$ and $C$ is **reversed** compared to the master secret derivation — a deliberate cryptographic design choice. The key block is then **partitioned** sequentially into:
- `client_write_MAC_key`
- `server_write_MAC_key`
- `client_write_key`
- `server_write_key`
- (Optionally) `client_write_IV`, `server_write_IV`

These keys are then used by the Record Protocol for symmetric encryption and integrity protection. The directional separation (separate keys for each direction) prevents reflection attacks and minimizes blast radius if one direction's key is compromised.

*(7 marks: 2 for PRF definition + 5 for step-by-step derivation including the role of nonces)*

---

#### Solution to Part B — Question B (a): Record Protocol Detail

The **TLS Record Protocol** sits directly above TCP and below the four higher-layer TLS sub-protocols. It operates on all outgoing and incoming TLS messages, providing **confidentiality** and **integrity** using the symmetric keys derived during the handshake.

**TLS Record Header Format (5 bytes):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `ContentType` | 1 byte | 20 = ChangeCipherSpec, 21 = Alert, 22 = Handshake, 23 = ApplicationData |
| `ProtocolVersion` | 2 bytes | Major + Minor (e.g., 0x0303 for TLS 1.2) |
| `Length` | 2 bytes | Length of encrypted payload (≤ 16,384 + 2,048) |

**Send-Side Processing:**
1. **Fragmentation:** The upper-layer message is fragmented into blocks of ≤ $2^{14}$ bytes.
2. **Compression (optional):** Each fragment is compressed; for `null` compression, it passes unchanged. Default is `null` due to CRIME/BREACH attacks.
3. **MAC computation:** An HMAC is computed over a sequence of: `seq_num`, content type, version, length, and the compressed fragment.
4. **Encryption:** The (compressed fragment + MAC + padding) is encrypted using the negotiated algorithm in the negotiated mode (CBC for legacy, AEAD for TLS 1.3).
5. **Header assembly:** The 5-byte TLS record header is prepended.

**Receive-Side Processing:**
1. Parse the TLS record header.
2. Decrypt the ciphertext using the session key.
3. Verify the MAC by recomputing it and comparing (constant-time comparison in modern implementations).
4. Decompress (if applicable).
5. Reassemble fragments and deliver the reassembled message to the appropriate upper-layer protocol handler.

*(7 marks: 2 for header format + 5 for processing pipeline)*

---

#### Solution to Part B — Question B (b): Security Services & Attacks

**Security Services Provided by SSL/TLS:**

1. **Confidentiality** — All application data is encrypted using symmetric algorithms (AES, ChaCha20) with session keys derived from a freshly generated pre-master secret.
2. **Integrity** — Every record carries an HMAC (or AEAD tag) computed over the payload and metadata; any tampering is detected and rejected.
3. **Authentication** — The server (and optionally the client) proves its identity via X.509 digital certificates validated against trusted Certificate Authorities.
4. **Replay Protection** — Per-session random nonces and a 64-bit sequence number prevent attackers from re-injecting captured packets.

**Major Attacks on SSL/TLS:**

| Attack | Target | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|--------|--------|-----------|------------|
| **POODLE (2014)** | SSL 3.0 | Padding oracle on CBC mode | **Disable SSL 3.0 entirely** |
| **BEAST (2011)** | TLS 1.0 CBC | Chosen-plaintext IV prediction | **Use TLS 1.1+ with explicit IVs** |
| **CRIME (2012)** | TLS compression | Side-channel via compressed length | **Disable compression** |
| **Heartbleed (2014)** | OpenSSL implementation bug | Buffer over-read in TLS heartbeat | **Patch OpenSSL; revoke & reissue certs** |
| **ROBOT (2017)** | RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 | Bleichenbacher's padding oracle | **Use RSA-PSS or ECDHE** |
| **Logjam (2015)** | Weak DHE groups | Downgrade to 512-bit DH | **Use ≥ 2048-bit DH or ECDHE** |

**TLS 1.3 Hardening:**
- **AEAD-only** (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) — eliminates padding oracle attacks.
- **No compression** — CRIME-class attacks are impossible by construction.
- **Mandatory Forward Secrecy** — RSA key transport removed, only (EC)DHE.
- **1-RTT handshake** — reduces attack surface and downgrade windows.
- **Downgrade sentinels** in the `random` field detect forced fallback to older versions.
- **Encrypted Extensions & Certificate (TLS 1.3)** — server identity is hidden from passive observers.

*(7 marks: 2 for services + 2.5 for three attacks + 2.5 for TLS 1.3 mitigations)*

---

### 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SSL/TLS Exam Answers:**
> 1. **Confusing `master_secret` and `pre_master_secret`** — Examiners specifically test whether you know that PMS is the input to the PRF, and MS is the output. Reversing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of `client.random` and `server.random`** — These 32-byte nonces are *not* decorative. They prevent session-fixation and ensure key freshness. Always mention them in derivations.
> 3. **Confusing Handshake MAC with Record Protocol MAC** — The `Finished` message's MAC is over the **handshake hash**, not over individual record payloads. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Omitting the `ChangeCipherSpec` protocol** — It is a separate sub-protocol with its own content type (20), not part of the Handshake Protocol. Listing it as a handshake message is a structural error.
> 5. **Failing to mention X.509 certificate chain validation** — Authentication in TLS is *not* "the server sends a public key" — it is the **chain-of-trust validation** against a CA. Examiners expect this nuance.
> 6. **Wrong port number** — HTTPS is **port 443**, not 8080. Trivial but mark-losing.
> 7. **Citing "SSL 2.0" or "SSL 3.0" as "current"** — These are formally deprecated. Use TLS 1.2 / 1.3 in any "current best practice" answer.

---

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for SSL/TLS:**

- ✅ **SSL** = Netscape origin (1994–1996); **TLS** = IETF standard (1999–present).
- ✅ **Current best practice:** **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446); widely deployed: **TLS 1.2** (RFC 5246).
- ✅ **Position in stack:** Above TCP (port 443), below application protocol. **Application-protocol-agnostic**.
- ✅ **Four security services (CIA-R):** **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication (X.509), **R**eplay protection (sequence numbers + random nonces).
- ✅ **Protocol stack components:** Handshake, ChangeCipherSpec, Alert, ApplicationData (upper); Record (lower).
- ✅ **Handshake phases:** (1) Capability exchange → (2) Server auth + key exchange → (3) Client key exchange → (4) Switch to cipher.
- ✅ **Key derivation cascade:** **PMS → Master Secret → Key Block → 4–6 session keys** (with directional separation).
- ✅ **PRF in TLS 1.2** uses **HMAC-SHA-256** (default); TLS 1.3 uses **HKDF** with SHA-256/384.
- ✅ **`client.random` and `server.random`** are 32-byte nonces (4 bytes Unix time + 28 bytes random).
- ✅ **Forward Secrecy (PFS)** = (EC)DHE only; RSA key transport provides no PFS.
- ✅ **TLS 1.3 removes:** SSL 3.0-style MACs, CBC mode, compression, RSA key transport, 0-RTT resumption (controversial), custom PRF.
- ✅ **AEAD in TLS 1.3:** AES-128/256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 — single-pass authentication + encryption.
- ✅ **Finished message:** $\text{PRF}(MS, \text{"client/server finished"}, H(\text{handshake}))$ — acts as the cryptographic receipt of the handshake.
- ✅ **Record header:** 5 bytes = ContentType (1) + Version (2) + Length (2).
- ✅ **Maximum fragment size:** $2^{14} = 16{,}384$ bytes.
- ✅ **Famous attacks to remember:** POODLE (SSL 3.0), BEAST (TLS 1.0 CBC), CRIME (compression), Heartbleed (OpenSSL bug), Logjam (weak DH), ROBOT (RSA PKCS#1 v1.5), DROWN (SSL 2.0).
- ✅ **Default HTTPS port:** **443**.
- ✅ **Certificate type:** **X.509v3** with chain validation against a trusted root CA.
- ✅ **Cipher suite syntax:** `TLS_KX_AUTH_WITH_ENC_MAC` — e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`.
- ✅ **Key derivation labels:** "master secret" and "key expansion" — memorize exactly as written.
- ✅ **Memorize the four PRF inputs for Master Secret:** Secret=PMS, Label="master secret", Seed=`client.random || server.random`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: E-commerce Security Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** E-commerce Security Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **E-commerce Security Requirements** are the formally defined set of cryptographic, protocol-level, and trust-based properties that a digital transaction system MUST guarantee to ensure safe, verifiable, and confidential exchange of goods, services, and monetary value over untrusted public networks such as the Internet.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT501 - Applied Cryptography)** syllabus, E-commerce security is modelled around **four core pillars** (sometimes extended to **five**) that map directly onto cryptographic primitives studied in this course:

1. **Confidentiality** — Privacy of transaction data.
2. **Integrity** — Assurance that data has not been altered.
3. **Authentication** — Verification of identity of parties.
4. **Non-Repudiation** — Prevention of denial of having sent a message.
5. **Availability** — Continuous, reliable access to the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Courier with a Notary" Model

Imagine you are buying a gold ring from a jeweller in another city in **1950**, long before the internet:

- You write the order and your credit card number on a paper (**plaintext transaction**).
- You lock the paper inside a steel box that only the jeweller can open (**Confidentiality via encryption**).
- You seal the box with a unique wax stamp. If anyone tampers with the box, the seal breaks (**Integrity via MAC / digital signatures**).
- The courier carries a signed letter of authority from you, and the jeweller shows you his government-issued ID card (**Authentication via certificates and digital signatures**).
- The transaction receipt you receive is signed by both you and the jeweller in front of a public notary — neither of you can later claim you were not part of the deal (**Non-Repudiation via digital signatures + trusted third party / PKI**).
- The courier company guarantees that the box will be delivered within 24 hours, with insurance against loss (**Availability via redundancy, DoS protection, and fault-tolerant systems**).

E-commerce security requirements are essentially this **locked-courier-with-notary** model, but implemented using **mathematics** instead of steel boxes and wax seals.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics Used in E-commerce Security

> [!NOTE]
> The following industry-standard security metrics are referenced throughout KTU evaluation. Memorise them as **bold** constants.

- **SSL/TLS Default Cipher Suite Strength:** at least **128-bit symmetric key** (e.g., AES-128) and **2048-bit RSA / 256-bit ECDSA** for asymmetric operations.
- **PCI-DSS Minimum Hash Strength:** **SHA-256** (160-bit equivalent for legacy).
- **X.509 Digital Certificate Validity:** typically **1 year (365 days)** for standard SSL certificates.
- **SET (Secure Electronic Transaction) Protocol:** uses **Dual Signatures** to protect customer payment information.
- **HTTP over TLS Port:** **443**; HTTP without TLS Port: **80**.

## 1.4 The KTU Big Picture — Why This Topic Matters

E-commerce is the **largest practical deployment surface** of applied cryptography. Every concept you have studied in **Modules 1–3 (symmetric ciphers, asymmetric ciphers, hash functions, MACs, digital signatures, PKI)** converges here. KTU examiners frequently test whether you can **map a real-world e-commerce threat to the correct cryptographic primitive**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Security Requirements — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality (Privacy)

- **What it is:** Ensures that transaction data — credit card numbers, addresses, order details — is unreadable to any third party (eavesdropper) intercepting the communication.
- **How it works cryptographically:**
  - **Symmetric encryption** (AES, 3DES, ChaCha20) for bulk data encryption due to speed.
  - **Asymmetric encryption** (RSA, ECC) for secure key exchange.
  - **TLS/SSL Handshake** establishes a session key, after which symmetric encryption is used.
- **Threat addressed:** **Eavesdropping / Sniffing attacks** (e.g., packet capture on public Wi-Fi).
- **Real-world utility:** Protects online banking, medical records, classified government communications.

### 2.1.2 Integrity (Data Authenticity of Content)

- **What it is:** Guarantees that the message or transaction data has **not been modified** in transit — whether accidentally (network errors) or maliciously (Man-in-the-Middle).
- **How it works cryptographically:**
  - **Hash functions** (SHA-256, SHA-3) produce a fixed-length **message digest**.
  - **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)** such as HMAC-SHA256 bind integrity to a shared secret.
  - **Digital Signatures** (RSA, ECDSA) provide integrity + authentication + non-repudiation.
- **Threat addressed:** **Tampering / Message modification attacks**.
- **Real-world utility:** Software updates, firmware flashing, financial transaction ledgers.

### 2.1.3 Authentication (Identity Verification)

- **What it is:** Confirms that the parties involved in a transaction are **who they claim to be** — both the **customer** to the **merchant**, and the **merchant** to the **customer**.
- **How it works cryptographically:**
  - **Digital Certificates (X.509)** issued by trusted **Certificate Authorities (CA)**.
  - **Password-based authentication** (hashed and salted).
  - **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** combining *something you know* + *something you have* + *something you are*.
  - **Challenge-Response protocols** using public-key cryptography.
- **Threat addressed:** **Impersonation / Spoofing / Phishing**.
- **Real-world utility:** Online banking login, e-government portals, digital signatures on legal documents.

### 2.1.4 Non-Repudiation (Proof of Origin and Receipt)

- **What it is:** Ensures that the **sender cannot later deny** having sent a message, and the **receiver cannot deny** having received it.
- **How it works cryptographically:**
  - **Digital Signatures** are the gold standard — only the holder of the private key could have produced the signature.
  - **Trusted Timestamping Authorities (TSA)** provide legally valid time evidence.
  - **SET Dual Signatures** link an Order Instruction (OI) and a Payment Instruction (PI) without revealing them to each other.
- **Threat addressed:** **Repudiation of transactions** — critical for legal disputes.
- **Real-world utility:** E-contracts, digital currency, supply-chain agreements, tax filings.

### 2.1.5 Availability (Continuous Service)

- **What it is:** Guarantees that the e-commerce service is **accessible to legitimate users** when needed.
- **How it works cryptographically (and engineering-wise):**
  - **Redundancy** across geographically distributed data centres.
  - **DDoS mitigation** (CDNs, rate limiting, scrubbing centres).
  - **Cryptographic rate-limiting tokens** (e.g., CAPTCHA, proof-of-work challenges).
  - **Blockchain consensus** in decentralised e-commerce.
- **Threat addressed:** **Denial of Service (DoS) / Distributed DoS (DDoS) attacks**.
- **Real-world utility:** E-commerce sites during Black Friday sales, banking apps during salary days.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **board-exam-ready summary**. Use it as a last-day revision sheet.

| # | Security Requirement | Cryptographic Mechanism | Mathematical Primitive | KTU Threat Mapped | Real-world Protocol |
|---|----------------------|--------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Confidentiality** | Symmetric + Asymmetric Hybrid | AES, RSA, ECC | Eavesdropping, Sniffing | **TLS 1.3** |
| 2 | **Integrity** | Hashing, MAC, Digital Signatures | SHA-256, HMAC, RSA-PSS | Tampering, MITM | **TLS Record MAC, Code Signing** |
| 3 | **Authentication** | Digital Certificates, Challenge-Response | X.509, ECDSA, OTP | Phishing, Impersonation | **SSL/TLS Handshake, OAuth 2.0** |
| 4 | **Non-Repudiation** | Asymmetric Signatures + TSA | RSA Sign, ECDSA, DSA | Transaction Denial | **SET Protocol, DocuSign, eIDAS** |
| 5 | **Availability** | Redundancy, PoW, Rate-Limiting | Hashcash, CAPTCHAs | DoS, DDoS | **Cloudflare, BGP Anycast** |

### 2.3 Trust Models in E-commerce

Two foundational trust models underpin e-commerce security:

- **Hierarchical Trust Model (PKI):** A single root **Certificate Authority (CA)** delegates trust through intermediate CAs. Used in **HTTPS**.
- **Web of Trust Model:** Peers sign each other's keys (e.g., **PGP**). Used in email encryption and some blockchain applications.

### 2.4 The SET Protocol — A KTU Favourite

The **Secure Electronic Transaction (SET)** protocol, developed jointly by **Visa and Mastercard**, is specifically mentioned in many KTU cryptography modules. It uses:

- **Dual Signatures** — a customer signs both the **Order Information (OI)** and **Payment Information (PI)** with a single signature, so the merchant can verify OI without seeing PI, and the bank can verify PI without seeing OI.
- **Mathematical representation of Dual Signature (DS):**

$$
DS = E_{PR_C}\bigl[ H(H(PI)\,\Vert\, H(OI)) \bigr]
$$

Where $E_{PR_C}$ is encryption with the customer's **private key** (i.e., the signature operation), and $H$ is a **cryptographic hash function** (typically SHA-1 in original SET).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

E-commerce security requirements form the **design contract** for any online payment system. From **UPI in India** to **SWIFT international transfers**, all such systems explicitly map each of the five requirements to a cryptographic or engineering control. KTU expects students to demonstrate this mapping clearly in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — The Dual Signature Verification

This derivation is the **most asked 14-mark question** in KTU Module 4 for E-commerce Security. It is reproduced here with full algebraic transparency.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation

- Let $C$ = Customer, $M$ = Merchant, $B$ = Bank.
- $OI$ = Order Information (sent to Merchant).
- $PI$ = Payment Information (sent to Bank).
- $H(\cdot)$ = Hash function (e.g., SHA-256 in modern implementations).
- $PR_C$ = Customer's private key.
- $PU_X$ = X's public key.

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** The Merchant must verify that the OI was authorised by the customer, **without learning** the credit card number in PI. Symmetrically, the Bank must verify the PI was authorised, **without learning** what was purchased (OI).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the Dual Signature

**Step 1 — Customer hashes the Payment Information.**

$$
H_1 = H(PI)
$$

This is a **256-bit digest** (if SHA-256 is used). The result $H_1$ is sent along with $OI$ to the merchant.

**Step 2 — Customer hashes the Order Information.**

$$
H_2 = H(OI)
$$

This digest $H_2$ is sent along with $PI$ to the bank.

**Step 3 — Concatenate the two digests.**

$$
H_3 = H_1 \Vert H_2 = H(PI) \Vert H(OI)
$$

Here $\Vert$ denotes **bitwise concatenation**.

**Step 4 — Hash the concatenation.**

$$
H_4 = H(H_3) = H\bigl(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)\bigr)
$$

This **second-layer hash** binds the two digests together. This is the key innovation of SET — a single hash represents **both** pieces of information.

**Step 5 — Sign with the customer's private key.**

$$
DS = E_{PR_C}\bigl[ H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)) \bigr]
$$

Since $PR_C$ is known only to the customer, only the customer can produce this signature. This is the **Dual Signature (DS)**.

### 3.1.3 Verification by the Merchant (Knows OI, Not PI)

**Step M1 — Merchant already has $OI$ and $H(PI)$ from the customer.**

The merchant computes:

$$
H_2' = H(OI)
$$

**Step M2 — Merchant reconstructs the expected hash.**

$$
H_4' = H\bigl( H(PI) \Vert H_2' \bigr) = H\bigl( H(PI) \Vert H(OI) \bigr)
$$

**Step M3 — Merchant decrypts the Dual Signature with the customer's public key.**

$$
D = D_{PU_C}(DS) = H\bigl(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)\bigr)
$$

**Step M4 — Merchant compares $D$ with $H_4'$.**

$$
\text{If } D = H_4' \text{, the signature is valid.}
$$

The merchant has **verified** the order without ever seeing the credit card details. This satisfies **non-repudiation** of the order.

### 3.1.4 Verification by the Bank (Knows PI, Not OI)

**Step B1 — Bank has $PI$ and $H(OI)$ from the customer.**

The bank computes:

$$
H_1' = H(PI)
$$

**Step B2 — Bank reconstructs the expected hash.**

$$
H_4'' = H\bigl( H_1' \Vert H(OI) \bigr) = H\bigl( H(PI) \Vert H(OI) \bigr)
$$

**Step B3 — Bank decrypts the Dual Signature.**

$$
D' = D_{PU_C}(DS) = H\bigl(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)\bigr)
$$

**Step B4 — Bank compares $D'$ with $H_4''$.**

$$
\text{If } D' = H_4'' \text{, the signature is valid.}
$$

The bank has **verified** the payment without learning the order details (what was purchased, the merchant, the amount of items). This satisfies **non-repudiation** of payment.

### 3.1.5 Summary of the Dual Signature Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
DS &= E_{PR_C}\bigl[ H(H(PI) \Vert H(OI)) \bigr] \\[4pt]
\text{Merchant Verifies: } D_{PU_C}(DS) &\stackrel{?}{=} H\bigl( H(PI) \Vert H(OI) \bigr) \\[4pt]
\text{Bank Verifies: } D_{PU_C}(DS) &\stackrel{?}{=} H\bigl( H(PI) \Vert H(OI) \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **mathematical property of the hash function** — collision resistance — is what makes this secure. If an attacker tampers with either $OI$ or $PI$, the hashes will not match, and verification fails.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Threats to Requirements

| Threat Scenario | Confidentiality Required? | Integrity Required? | Authentication Required? | Non-Repudiation Required? | Availability Required? |
|-----------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Customer credit card stolen via Wi-Fi sniffing | ✓ | – | – | – | – |
| Order amount modified by MITM in transit | – | ✓ | ✓ | – | – |
| Fake merchant phishing site | – | – | ✓ | – | – |
| Customer denies placing order | – | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |
| DDoS attack on shopping site | – | – | – | – | ✓ |
| Stolen customer password | – | – | ✓ | – | – |
| Insider modifies transaction log | – | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | – |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** Whenever you see a 14-mark question framed as *"Discuss the security requirements of e-commerce with suitable example"*, immediately present this matrix. Examiners award full marks when the mapping is **explicit and comprehensive**.

## 3.3 The Authentication Triangle (Exhaustive Derivation)

For a 3-party e-commerce transaction (Customer $C$, Merchant $M$, Bank $B$), mutual authentication requires **three independent authentication exchanges**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Customer} \rightarrow \text{Merchant: } & \text{Cert}_C = \{ ID_C, PU_C, E_{PR_{CA}}[\, H(ID_C \Vert PU_C) \,] \} \\[2pt]
\text{(ii) Merchant} \rightarrow \text{Customer: } & \text{Cert}_M = \{ ID_M, PU_M, E_{PR_{CA}}[\, H(ID_M \Vert PU_M) \,] \} \\[2pt]
\text{(iii) Customer} \rightarrow \text{Bank: } & \text{Cert}_C' = \{ ID_C, PU_C, E_{PR_{CA}}[\, H(ID_C \Vert PU_C) \,] \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, $PR_{CA}$ is the **Certificate Authority's private key**. Each party's certificate is verified by checking that:

$$
D_{PU_{CA}}\bigl( E_{PR_{CA}}[ H(ID_X \Vert PU_X) ] \bigr) \stackrel{?}{=} H(ID_X \Vert PU_X)
$$

This is the **public-key signature verification** primitive, applied recursively up the PKI chain to a **trusted root CA**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — E-commerce Security Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Customer[Customer Browser] -->|TLS Handshake| Merchant[Merchant Web Server]
    Merchant -->|Payment Gateway API| Bank[Bank Payment Gateway]
    
    subgraph CryptoLayer["Cryptographic Security Layer"]
        direction TB
        Conf[Confidentiality Module - AES + RSA]
        Intg[Integrity Module - SHA-256 + HMAC]
        Auth[Authentication Module - X.509 Certs]
        NonRep[Non-Repudiation Module - Digital Signatures]
        Avail[Availability Module - CDN + DDoS Shield]
    end
    
    Customer -.uses.-> Conf
    Customer -.uses.-> Auth
    Merchant -.uses.-> Intg
    Merchant -.uses.-> NonRep
    Bank -.uses.-> NonRep
    Bank -.uses.-> Avail
    
    subgraph TrustInfra["Trust Infrastructure"]
        CA[Certificate Authority - PKI]
        TSA[Trusted Timestamping Authority]
        RA[Registration Authority]
    end
    
    Auth -.issues certs via.-> CA
    NonRep -.timestamped by.-> TSA
    CA -.verifies identity via.-> RA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — SET Protocol Dual Signature Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Customer
    participant M as Merchant
    participant B as Bank
    
    Note over C: Compute H(OI), H(PI), DS
    C->>M: OI + H(PI) + DS + Cert_C
    C->>B: PI + H(OI) + DS + Cert_C
    
    Note over M: Verify DS using H(PI) and H(OI)
    M->>B: Payment Authorization Request + H(OI) + DS
    
    Note over B: Verify DS using H(OI) and H(PI)
    B-->>M: Payment Authorization Response
    M-->>C: Order Confirmation
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Threat to Security Requirement Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    Threat1[Eavesdropping Attack] --> Req1[Confidentiality]
    Threat2[Message Tampering] --> Req2[Integrity]
    Threat3[Identity Spoofing] --> Req3[Authentication]
    Threat4[Transaction Denial] --> Req4[Non-Repudiation]
    Threat5[DoS Attack] --> Req5[Availability]
    
    Req1 --> Sol1[AES + RSA Hybrid]
    Req2 --> Sol2[SHA-256 + HMAC]
    Req3 --> Sol3[X.509 + Challenge Response]
    Req4 --> Sol4[RSA / ECDSA Signatures]
    Req5 --> Sol5[CDN + Rate Limiting]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TLS Handshake in E-commerce

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Hello - Random_C, Cipher Suites] --> B[Server Hello - Random_S, Certificate]
    B --> C[Key Exchange - RSA or ECDHE]
    C --> D[Pre-Master Secret Encrypted]
    D --> E[Master Secret Derived]
    E --> F[Session Keys Generated]
    F --> G[Finished Messages - HMAC Verified]
    G --> H[Encrypted Application Data - AES]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram represents the **TLS 1.2/1.3 handshake**, which is the underlying protocol for HTTPS-based e-commerce. Each block corresponds to a specific cryptographic operation that contributes to one or more of the **five security requirements**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define E-commerce Security Requirements. List any four core requirements. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** E-commerce Security Requirements are the set of properties — confidentiality, integrity, authentication, non-repudiation, and availability — that must be satisfied for safe online commercial transactions.
>
> **Four Core Requirements (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Confidentiality** — protect data from unauthorised disclosure.
> 2. **Integrity** — ensure data is not modified in transit.
> 3. **Authentication** — verify identities of transacting parties.
> 4. **Non-Repudiation** — prevent denial of transaction participation.

### Question 2: What is Non-Repudiation? How is it achieved cryptographically? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Non-repudiation is the property that ensures a party **cannot later deny** having sent or received a message (1 mark). It is achieved cryptographically using **digital signatures** (1 mark), where only the holder of the **private key** can sign, and the **public key** allows anyone to verify. Combined with **trusted timestamping**, it provides legally admissible proof of origin and receipt (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions of Module 4 carry **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. An **internal choice** is mandatory — the student attempts **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **five major security requirements** of E-commerce. For each requirement, identify the cryptographic mechanism and a real-world protocol that implements it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five major security requirements of e-commerce are:

1. **Confidentiality (1.5 marks):** Protects transaction data from unauthorised disclosure. Cryptographically achieved using **symmetric encryption (AES-128/256)** for bulk data and **asymmetric encryption (RSA-2048, ECC)** for key exchange. **Real-world protocol:** **TLS 1.3** encrypts all HTTPS traffic on port 443.

2. **Integrity (1.5 marks):** Ensures data is not tampered with. Cryptographically achieved using **cryptographic hash functions (SHA-256, SHA-3)** and **Message Authentication Codes (HMAC-SHA256)**. Digital signatures extend this with origin authentication. **Real-world protocol:** **TLS Record Layer MAC** and **Code Signing Certificates** (e.g., Microsoft Authenticode).

3. **Authentication (1.5 marks):** Verifies the identity of parties. Cryptographically achieved using **X.509 digital certificates** issued by trusted **Certificate Authorities (CA)**, **challenge-response protocols**, and **public-key cryptography**. **Real-world protocol:** **SSL/TLS Handshake** verifies server certificate; **OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect** for user authentication.

4. **Non-Repudiation (1.5 marks):** Prevents denial of transactions. Cryptographically achieved using **asymmetric digital signatures (RSA-PSS, ECDSA, EdDSA)** and **trusted timestamping**. **Real-world protocol:** **SET Protocol Dual Signatures**, **DocuSign**, **eIDAS** in the European Union.
