# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — read this carefully before you write the exam.**
> 1. **Do not confuse the Dual Signature with a normal digital signature.** Examiners will fail any answer that writes "the dual signature is two signatures" — it is a **single** RSA signature over the *concatenation of two digests*.
> 2. **Do not write "SET is used today for online payments."** This is a top-3 reason students lose marks. SET was abandoned circa 2002; 3-D Secure is the modern replacement.
> 3. **For 3DS questions, do not omit the three domains by name.** At minimum write: Acquirer Domain, Interoperability Domain, Issuer Domain. A bare "Visa server" is treated as incomplete.
> 4. **For 3DS, do not claim the merchant never sees the PAN.** Unlike SET, in 3DS the merchant **does** see the PAN; the privacy is shifted to the *authentication result*, not the card number.
> 5. **Hash order matters:** the dual-signature spec mandates $H(\text{OI}) \Vert H(\text{PI})$ — reversed concatenation will give a *correct-looking* but mathematically wrong verification.

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Secure Electronic Transaction (SET). List any two cryptographic services it provides.

**Model Answer:**

**Secure Electronic Transaction (SET)** is an open, multi-party cryptographic protocol specification designed by **Visa and Mastercard in 1996** to safeguard credit- and debit-card transactions conducted over open networks such as the Internet.

Two of the cryptographic services SET provides (any two for 3 marks — all four for full bonus credit):

1. **Confidentiality** of payment information (the PAN and card data are encrypted with DES under a session key transported via RSA).
2. **Integrity** of all transmitted messages (mandatory SHA-1 digest).
3. **Authentication** of cardholder, merchant, payment gateway, and acquirer via X.509v3 digital certificates.
4. **Non-repudiation** through the cardholder's RSA digital signature on the order.

> *Award 1 mark for the formal definition, 1 mark per correctly stated service up to 2 marks.*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is 3-D Secure? Name its three trust domains.

**Model Answer:**

**3-D Secure (3DS)** is a card-network messaging protocol that performs **cardholder authentication at the issuing bank** during an online card-not-present transaction, thereby enabling a *liability shift* from the merchant to the issuer.

The three trust domains are:

1. **Acquirer Domain** — the merchant and the merchant's acquiring bank.
2. **Interoperability Domain** — the card network (Visa, Mastercard, RuPay, JCB, AmEx, UnionPay) operating the **Directory Server (DS)**.
3. **Issuer Domain** — the issuing bank operating the **Access Control Server (ACS)**.

> *Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming two domains, the third domain for the final 1 mark.*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Secure Electronic Transaction (SET) protocol**. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **entities** involved in SET and their roles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams and step-by-step derivation, explain the **Dual Signature** mechanism in SET. Show how the merchant verifies the **Order Information (OI)** using the dual signature even though the merchant does not receive the **Payment Information (PI)**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark).** SET is an open cryptographic protocol specification for securing credit-card transactions over the Internet, developed by Visa and Mastercard in 1996, integrating DES, RSA, SHA-1 and X.509v3 certificates.

**Entities (1 mark each — list & describe 6 to score full 6).**

| # | Entity | Role |
|---|--------|------|
| 1 | **Cardholder** | Authorised holder of the payment card; runs SET-compliant e-wallet. |
| 2 | **Merchant** | Sells goods/services; runs SET-aware server. |
| 3 | **Issuer** | Cardholder's bank; issues the card and the cardholder X.509 certificate. |
| 4 | **Acquirer** | Merchant's bank; holds the merchant's account. |
| 5 | **Payment Gateway** | Bridge between the Internet and the private bankcard network; operated by the acquirer. |
| 6 | **Certificate Authority** | Hierarchical PKI root; issues X.509v3 certificates to all other entities. |

> *The examiner gives 1 mark per entity × 6 = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for an accurate, labelled block diagram (see Section 4.1). Total = 7.*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Inputs (1 mark).** The cardholder holds:

- Order Information OI (list of items, prices, merchant ID).
- Payment Information PI (PAN, expiry, amount, issuer ID).
- RSA key pair $(e_C, d_C, n_C)$ with $e_C$ published in their X.509 certificate.

**Step 2 — Compute the digests (1 mark).**

$$
H(\text{OI}) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{OI}), \quad H(\text{PI}) = \text{SHA-1}(\text{PI})
$$

**Step 3 — Form the message-to-be-signed (1 mark).**

$$
H_{\text{PO}} = H\!\left(\,H(\text{OI})\ \Vert\ H(\text{PI})\,\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Sign (1 mark).**

$$
\text{DS} = (H_{\text{PO}})^{d_C} \bmod n_C
$$

**Step 5 — Transmit (1 mark).**

- To **Merchant** : $\{\ \text{OI},\ H(\text{PI}),\ \text{DS},\ C_M\ \}$
- To **Bank** : $\{\ \text{PI},\ H(\text{OI}),\ \text{DS},\ C_M\ \}$

**Step 6 — Merchant verification (2 marks).**

The merchant computes:

$$
H_M = H\!\left(\,H(\text{OI})\ \Vert\ H(\text{PI})\,\right)
$$

It decrypts the dual signature using the cardholder's public key from $C_M$:

$$
\text{DS}^{e_C} \bmod n_C
$$

If $H_M = \text{DS}^{e_C} \bmod n_C$, the merchant accepts. **The merchant never needed to see PI**; it only needed $H(\text{PI})$, which the bank eventually forwards through the protocol during the authorisation exchange.

> *Block diagram of dual-signature construction from Section 4.2 to be reproduced; 1 mark.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, modified]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **3-D Secure**? With a clear sequence diagram, describe the **authentication flow** between the cardholder, the merchant's 3DS Server, the Directory Server, and the Access Control Server for a frictionless and a challenge transaction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the following with respect to 3-D Secure:
  (i) **Frictionless vs Challenge flow** — when is each triggered?
  (ii) **Liability shift** concept and the conditions under which it occurs.
  (iii) **PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication (SCA)** and how 3DS 2.x supports it.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (1 mark).** 3-D Secure is an XML/JSON-based card-network authentication protocol that adds an **issuer-side authentication step** to online card-not-present transactions.

**Flow description — Frictionless (3 marks):**

1. Cardholder submits checkout with PAN.
2. Merchant's 3DSS sends `AReq` to the Directory Server.
3. DS looks up the BIN and forwards the `AReq` to the issuer's ACS.
4. ACS evaluates the risk using ~120 data elements.
5. ACS returns `ARes` with status **`Y`**.
6. Merchant submits the authorisation with the returned `authenticationValue`.
7. Customer sees a seamless approval; no prompt is shown.

**Flow description — Challenge (3 marks):**

1. Steps 1–4 are identical.
2. ACS returns `ARes` with status **`C`**.
3. Merchant renders the issuer's challenge UI in an iframe or mobile SDK.
4. Cardholder submits OTP / biometric / password.
5. ACS validates the factor and returns a final `ARes` with `Y` or `N`.
6. Merchant proceeds accordingly.

> *Marks: 1 for the protocol naming, 1 for the entity naming, 3 for the frictionless path, 3 for the challenge path = 7. Examiner will give 1 bonus mark for a correctly drawn sequence diagram (Section 4.3 reference).*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Frictionless vs Challenge (2 marks).** Frictionless is triggered when the issuer's risk engine returns a score above its *frictionless threshold* (e.g., ≥ 0.85 in the worked example in Section 3.3) — typically a trusted device, low value, or repeat customer. Challenge is triggered when the score is below the threshold OR when the merchant sets `challengeMandated = Y` in the `AReq`.

**(ii) Liability shift (2 marks).** When 3DS authentication completes with status `Y` and the merchant includes a valid `authenticationValue` (CAVV) in the clearing message, the **liability for fraud-related chargebacks shifts from the acquiring merchant to the issuing bank**. Conditions for full liability shift:
  - The transaction is fully authenticated (`Y`).
  - The `authenticationValue` is present in the authorisation and matches the issuer's expectation.
  - The merchant has transmitted `ThreeDSVersion` in the request.
  
Partial-authentication statuses (`A`, `U`, `R`) may *not* trigger a complete shift, depending on the card network's programme rules.

**(iii) PSD2 SCA support (3 marks).** The **EU Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2)** mandates **Strong Customer Authentication (SCA)** for all in-scope online card payments unless an exemption applies. SCA requires at least two of three independent factors:
  1. **Knowledge** — password, PIN, secret question.
  2. **Possession** — mobile device, smart card, hardware token.
  3. **Inherence** — fingerprint, face, iris, voice.

3-D Secure 2.x supports PSD2 compliance by:
  - Allowing the issuer to demand any combination of the three factors during the challenge.
  - Supporting exemptions (`lowValue` ≤ €30, `TRA` ≤ €500, `trustedBeneficiary`, `recurringTransaction`) via the `ARes` exemption flags.
  - Carrying the **Exemption Transaction Status** (`authenticationExemption`) in the authorisation flow.

> *Total: 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 marks.*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before you enter the exam hall.**

- **SET (1996, Visa + Mastercard)** — *protocol*, not a payment system; combines DES + RSA + SHA-1 + X.509v3.
- **Six SET entities** — Cardholder, Merchant, Issuer, Acquirer, Payment Gateway, CA. Remember the count and the roles.
- **Dual Signature** — *one* RSA signature over $H\!\left(\,H(\text{OI}) \Vert H(\text{PI})\,\right)^{d_C} \bmod n_C$. Order of concatenation is **OI then PI** — *not* the other way.
- **OMV** = Order Message Verification (merchant side); **OPV** = Order Payment Verification (bank side).
- **SET is DEAD** commercially. Use this phrasing in the exam if asked "is SET used today?".
- **3-D Secure** = three domains: **Acquirer, Interoperability, Issuer**. Memorise the spelling.
- **3DS messages** — `AReq`, `ARes`, `CReq`, `CRes`, `RReq`, `RRes`, `PReq`, `PRes`.
- **3DS status codes** — `Y` (yes), `C` (challenge), `N` (no), `U` (unauthenticated), `R` (rejected), `A` (attempted). Frictionless = `Y` without challenge.
- **Liability shift** triggers only on full `Y` with a valid `authenticationValue`.
- **3DS 2.x transport** — TLS 1.2+; message signing uses **JWS** (RS256 / ES256).
- **Authentication value** is **28 bytes** in 3DS v2.3 (was 20 bytes in 1.0).
- **PSD2 SCA** = 2 of 3 factors: Knowledge, Possession, Inherence. 3DS 2.x is the EU's chosen enforcement vehicle.
- **3DS 1.0 → 2.x** — mobile SDK, frictionless flow, ~120 data elements, EMV 3DS v2.3 is the current production version.
- **SET vs 3DS** — SET hides the PAN from the merchant; 3DS does NOT. SET is heavier (~3,900 bytes); 3DS is lighter. SET is dead; 3DS is mandatory.
- **Examiner red flags** — never claim "SET is used today"; never write "3DS hides the card number"; never mix the dual-signature formula with a *two-signature* interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptographic Tokens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cryptographic Tokens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptographic Token (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *cryptographic token* is a portable, tamper-resistant physical or virtual device that securely stores one or more cryptographic keys (secret, private, or session keys) and is capable of performing cryptographic operations — such as symmetric encryption/decryption, digital signature generation, key exchange, or one-time password (OTP) generation — typically without ever exposing the secret key material to the host system or network.

In the context of KTU Module 4, cryptographic tokens are studied as **authentication and key-storage primitives** that bridge the gap between *something you know* (password) and *something you have* (the physical token), thereby enabling **multi-factor authentication (MFA)** in real-world security architectures.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Cryptographic Tokens

Cryptographic tokens are broadly classified by their **intelligence level**, **interface**, and **cryptographic capability**:

| Classification Axis | Category 1 | Category 2 | Category 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intelligence | Memory Token | **Smart Token (Microprocessor)** | — |
| Interface | Contact (ISO 7816) | Contactless (ISO 14443 / NFC) | USB / Network-Attached |
| Function | One-Time Password | Digital Signature (PKI) | Secure Key Storage |
| Form Factor | Display Card | Smart Card (Chip Card) | USB Dongle (e.g., YubiKey) |
| Cryptographic Engine | None (static) | **Hardware RSA / ECC / AES** | Hardware + Secure Element |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Memory Token** = stores a static secret; cannot perform computation. Example: magnetic stripe card, older OTP display cards.
> - **Smart Token / Microprocessor Token** = contains an embedded CPU + secure element; can execute on-board cryptographic algorithms. Example: EMV chip credit card, smart card with JavaCard OS.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Safe Key"

Imagine you check into a **premium hotel**. The receptionist hands you a **physical key card** (the token). This key card is special:

1. It **does not store your identity** in plain text — it stores an encrypted reference number tied to the front-desk vault.
2. When you insert it into your room door, the **door's reader challenges the card**, the card performs a tiny cryptographic computation internally, and the door either opens or stays locked.
3. A **thief who steals the card** cannot clone it because the card's internal secret is **never transmitted to the door** — only the *result* of a cryptographic handshake is.
4. If you lose the card, the front desk simply **revokes that reference** in their central system, and the card becomes useless even if physically recovered.

> **This is exactly how a cryptographic token works in digital systems.** The token = hotel key card, the door reader = authentication server, and the cryptographic handshake = challenge-response protocol.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Mnemonic — "T.O.K.E.N"**
> - **T** — Tamper-resistant hardware
> - **O** — On-board cryptographic computation
> - **K** — Key never leaves the device
> - **E** — Enforces two-factor authentication
> - **N** — Non-repudiation via digital signatures

---

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **FIPS 140-2 / FIPS 140-3** — the U.S. federal standard validating cryptographic module security levels (**Level 1 to Level 4**).
- **PKCS#11** — the dominant **C**ryptographic **T**oken **I**nterface **S**tandard (**Cryptoki**) used to communicate with hardware tokens.
- **ISO/IEC 7816** — international standard governing **contact smart card** electrical and protocol specifications.
- **ISO/IEC 14443** — governs **contactless (NFC) smart cards** used in payments and transit.
- **Common Criteria EAL5+** — minimum evaluation assurance level required for **eIDAS-qualified electronic signature tokens** in the European Union.
- **OTP Time Step (X)** = **30 seconds** (default in RFC 6238 TOTP).
- **HOTP Counter Window** = typically **3 to 10** lookahead values for clock-skew tolerance.
- **RSA Key Size for Smart Cards** = **2048 bits minimum** (NIST SP 800-131A, current as of 2024).

---

## 1.4 Why Cryptographic Tokens Matter in Modern Security

Cryptographic tokens are the **physical root of trust** for:

1. **Banking** — EMV chip cards, hardware OTP tokens for corporate banking.
2. **Government e-IDs** — Aadhaar, ePassports, national ID smart cards.
3. **Enterprise PKI** — employee smart cards for Windows logon (PIV / CAC).
4. **Cryptocurrency** — hardware wallets (Ledger, Trezor) storing private keys offline.
5. **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** — modern passwordless authentication using platform or roaming authenticators.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:**
> Module 4 transitions from pure *algorithmic cryptography* (Modules 1–3) to *applied authentication systems*. Cryptographic tokens are the **first applied topic** in this module, leading into biometric authentication, digital signatures, and certificate-based PKI in subsequent sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Workflow — "How a Smart Token Authenticates a User"

The complete authentication flow between a **User (U)**, **Token (T)**, and **Authentication Server (AS)** proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Initialization
- The token is **personalized** at issuance: a unique secret key $K_i$ is injected into the secure memory of the token's microprocessor.
- The same $K_i$ is securely stored in the AS database, bound to the user's identity $\text{ID}_U$.

### Step 2: User Presents Token
- User inserts the token into a **contact reader** OR taps it on a **contactless (NFC) reader**.
- A logical channel is established per **ISO/IEC 7816-4** APDU (Application Protocol Data Unit) framing.

### Step 3: Server Issues Challenge
- AS generates a fresh, high-entropy random nonce $N$ (typically **128 bits**).
- AS transmits $N$ to the token over the secure channel.

### Step 4: Token Computes Response
- The token computes a response $R$ using the stored secret $K_i$ and the challenge $N$:

$$R = f(K_i, N)$$

- Where $f$ is typically a **challenge-response function** such as HMAC-SHA-256, RSA signature, or ECDSA signature.

### Step 5: Server Verifies Response
- AS independently computes $R' = f(K_i, N)$ using the stored copy of $K_i$.
- If $R \stackrel{?}{=} R'$, the user is **authenticated**.
- Otherwise, the session is **terminated** and a security event is logged.

### Step 6: Replay Protection
- Because $N$ is unique per session, a captured $R$ from a previous session **cannot be replayed**.
- This is a critical advantage over static password authentication.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the secret never leaves the token:**
> The function $f$ is computed *inside* the tamper-resistant secure element. The host computer sees only the **input $N$** and the **output $R$**. This design is the cornerstone of token security — even a fully compromised host cannot extract $K_i$.

---

## 2.2 Types of Cryptographic Tokens — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Memory Tokens (Passive)
- **Storage only** — no CPU, no on-board computation.
- Hold a static secret (PIN, magnetic stripe data, simple OTP value).
- **Vulnerability:** Trivially cloneable using a card reader/writer.
- **Use case:** Legacy access cards, simple prepaid cards.

### 2.2.2 Microprocessor / Smart Tokens (Active)
- Contain an **8-bit to 32-bit secure microcontroller** (e.g., Infineon SLE 78, NXP P60).
- Run a **Card Operating System (COS)** — e.g., JavaCard, MULTOS, native.
- Capable of:
  - RSA up to **4096 bits**
  - ECC (NIST P-256, P-384, brainpool curves)
  - AES-128/256, SHA-256/384/512
  - True Random Number Generator (TRNG) — **AIS-31 Class PTG.2** or **NIST SP 800-90A**
- **Tamper resistance:** Active mesh, voltage glitch detectors, light sensors, temperature sensors.

### 2.2.3 USB Hardware Tokens
- Plug directly into a USB-A / USB-C / Lightning port.
- Examples: **YubiKey 5 Series**, **Feitian K9**, **OnlyKey**.
- Support **FIDO2 / WebAuthn**, **PIV**, **OpenPGP**, **OATH-TOTP / HOTP**.
- **Form factor advantage:** No reader hardware required.

### 2.2.4 Standalone OTP Tokens
- Battery-powered, generate OTPs every **30 or 60 seconds** (TOTP) or per event (HOTP).
- Display the OTP on a small LCD; user types it into the login form.
- **Algorithm:** HMAC-SHA-1 (legacy) or HMAC-SHA-256 (modern).

### 2.2.5 Embedded Secure Elements (SE)
- A **chip soldered onto a device's motherboard** (smartphones, IoT).
- Examples: Apple Secure Enclave, Samsung Knox eSE, Android StrongBox Keymaster.
- Functionally equivalent to smart cards but permanently integrated.

### 2.2.6 Hardware Security Modules (HSM)
- **High-end, network-attached appliances** for data centers.
- Generate, store, and use cryptographic keys at very high throughput (**>10,000 RSA-2048 sign/s**).
- **FIPS 140-2 Level 4** certified models exist.
- Used by **Certificate Authorities (CAs)**, payment processors, and large enterprises.

---

## 2.3 Token Authentication Protocols

### 2.3.1 Static Password Authentication
- Token stores a fixed secret $S$.
- User presents $S$ for comparison.
- **Weakness:** Vulnerable to replay, shoulder-surfing, keylogging.

### 2.3.2 Challenge–Response Authentication
- Server sends nonce $N$.
- Token computes $R = f(K_i, N)$.
- **Strength:** Replay-resistant; secret never transmitted.

### 2.3.3 One-Time Password (OTP) Authentication
- Token generates a single-use code that expires.
- Subtypes:
  - **HOTP** (HMAC-based) — counter-based, RFC 4226.
  - **TOTP** (Time-based) — time-based, RFC 6238.
  - **OCRA** (Challenge–Response) — RFC 6287.

### 2.3.4 PKI-Based Token Authentication (Digital Signature)
- Token holds an **asymmetric private key** $SK$.
- To authenticate, token signs a server-provided challenge: $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK}(N)$.
- Server verifies: $\text{Verify}_{PK}(N, \sigma) = \text{True}$.
- **Strongest method** — provides **non-repudiation** in addition to authentication.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Description | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = f(K_i, N)$ | Generic challenge–response | $N$ is **128-bit nonce** |
| 2 | $HOTP(K, C) = \text{truncate}(HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}1(K, C)) \bmod 10^D$ | Counter-based OTP | $D = 6 \text{ or } 8$ digits; $C$ = 8-byte counter |
| 3 | $T = \left\lfloor \dfrac{t - t_0}{X} \right\rfloor$ | TOTP time step index | $t_0 = 0$ (Unix epoch); $X = 30 \text{ s}$ (default) |
| 4 | $TOTP(K, T) = HOTP(K, T)$ | Time-based OTP, RFC 6238 | $T$ = 8-byte Unix time / 30 |
| 5 | $\sigma = SK^{-1}(H(M))$ | Token-based digital signature (RSA) | $H = \text{SHA-256}$; key = 2048-bit |
| 6 | $V = PK(H(M), \sigma)$ | Signature verification (RSA) | Returns True / False |
| 7 | $\text{FAR}$ | False Acceptance Rate (biometric/token mimicry) | $\le 10^{-6}$ for banking |
| 8 | $\text{FRR}$ | False Rejection Rate (genuine user rejected) | $\le 10^{-2}$ for usability |
| 9 | $E_\text{otp} \in [0, 2^D - 1]$ | OTP dynamic range | $D = 6 \Rightarrow 10^6 = 1{,}000{,}000$ codes |
| 10 | $\text{Adv}_\text{CR} \le \dfrac{q_H}{2^{n}}$ | Adversary's forgery bound (challenge-response) | $n$ = hash output bits; $q_H$ = hash queries |
| 11 | $\text{rate}_\text{HOTP} = \dfrac{1}{10^D}$ | Probability of guessing a 6-digit HOTP | $= 10^{-6}$ |
| 12 | $C_\text{collision} \approx 10^{D/2}$ | Birthday bound for OTP collision | $\approx 1000$ for $D = 6$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Cell Delimiters:**
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all modulus expressions, absolute values, and conditional equality operators are written using $\bmod$, $\vert$, and $\stackrel{?}{=}$ outside the table cell markdown pipe structure to preserve rendering integrity.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Token Form Factor | Cryptographic Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Banking (EMV)** | Contact + contactless smart card | Offline RSA/ECC payment authorization |
| **E-Government (eID)** | Smart card with PKI applet | Digital signature for legal documents (eIDAS) |
| **Enterprise SSO** | USB token (YubiKey) | FIDO2 passwordless login |
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | Smart card | Mutual TLS + clinician signature on records |
| **Defense (CAC/PIV)** | DoD Common Access Card | PKI + biometric + PIN (3-factor) |
| **Cryptocurrency** | Hardware wallet | Offline storage of secp256k1 private keys |
| **IoT / Automotive** | Embedded SE (TPM 2.0) | Secure boot, attestation, V2X signing |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HOTP Computation Pipeline (RFC 4226)

The **HOTP** algorithm transforms a secret key $K$ and a counter $C$ into a human-readable numeric code. The derivation proceeds in four stages.

### Step 1: HMAC Computation
The first step computes the **HMAC-SHA-1** of the counter using the secret key.

$$
\text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA-1}(K, C)
$$

Here, $K$ is a secret key of at least **128 bits** (recommended **160 bits**), and $C$ is the 8-byte counter.

### Step 2: Truncation (Dynamic Truncation)
The HMAC output is 20 bytes. We extract a 31-bit dynamic binary code $S$ using the "dynamic offset" $O$ (last nibble of the last byte):

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= \text{HMAC}[19] \mathbin{\&} 0x0F \\
\text{offset} &= O \times 8 \\
S &= (\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}] \mathbin{\&} 0x7F) \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+1] \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+2] \,\|\, (\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+3] \mathbin{\&} 0xFF)
\end{aligned}
$$

The mask `0x7F` clears the most significant bit to avoid sign issues, yielding a non-negative 31-bit integer.

### Step 3: Modular Reduction
Reduce $S$ modulo $10^D$, where $D$ is the number of OTP digits (typically 6):

$$
\text{HOTP} = S \bmod 10^D
$$

### Step 4: Zero-Padding
The result is left-padded with leading zeros to exactly $D$ digits:

$$
\text{HOTP}_\text{displayed} = \text{zfill}_D(\text{HOTP})
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (HOTP)
Let $K = \text{0x4D3F5C8A2E1B7D90}$ (16 bytes) and $C = 5$ (8-byte big-endian: `0x0000000000000005`).

**Step 1 (HMAC-SHA-1):**

$$
\text{HMAC} = \text{0x75A48A0C2B6E91F3D4C0A2E8B9F71D04C3E5B7A2}
$$

**Step 2 (Truncation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= \text{0x2} \mathbin{\&} 0x0F = 2 \\
\text{offset} &= 2 \times 8 = 16 \\
S &= (\text{HMAC}[16] \mathbin{\&} 0x7F) \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[17] \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[18] \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[19] \\
&= (0xB9 \mathbin{\&} 0x7F) \,\|\, 0xF7 \,\|\, 0x1D \,\|\, 0x04 \\
&= 0x39F71D04 = 972{,}691{,}716 \text{ (decimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (Modular Reduction):**

$$
\text{HOTP} = 972{,}691{,}716 \bmod 10^6 = 691{,}716
$$

**Step 4 (Zero-Pad):**

$$
\text{HOTP}_\text{displayed} = 691716
$$

> This 6-digit value is what the user sees on the token display.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TOTP Time-Step Index (RFC 6238)

TOTP reuses the HOTP construction, but the counter $C$ is replaced with the **time-step index** $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_\text{now} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor \\
T_\text{OTP} &= \text{HOTP}(K, T)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_\text{now}$ = current Unix timestamp (seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC).
- $T_0$ = initial counter time, conventionally **0**.
- $X$ = time-step size in seconds (**30 s** default, **60 s** common in older tokens).

### Worked Numerical Example (TOTP)
Given $K$ as above, $T_\text{now} = 1{,}700{,}000{,}000$ s, $T_0 = 0$, $X = 30$ s:

$$
T = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1{,}700{,}000{,}000 - 0}{30} \right\rfloor = 56{,}666{,}666
$$

$$
T_\text{OTP} = \text{HOTP}(K, 56666666) = \text{(6-digit code)}
$$

> The server tolerates clock drift by accepting codes from the **current time step** plus/minus a small **look-ahead window** (typically $\pm 1$ step, i.e., $\pm 30$ s).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Security Strength of Token Types

Let $A$ denote the **adversary's probability of successful impersonation** per attempt, $q$ the number of attempts, and $n$ the entropy of the underlying secret in bits.

### 3.3.1 Static Password Token

$$
A = \left(\dfrac{q}{2^n}\right) \le 1
$$

For $n = 32$ bits (weak), $q = 1000$: $A \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-4}$.

### 3.3.2 HOTP Token (6 digits)

$$
A = \left(\dfrac{q}{10^D}\right) \le 1
$$

For $D = 6$, $q = 10$ (rate-limited): $A \approx 10^{-5}$.

### 3.3.3 TOTP Token (6 digits, rate-limited)

$$
A = \left(\dfrac{q}{10^D}\right) \le 1, \quad q \le \dfrac{60}{30} = 2 \text{ (per minute)}
$$

For $D = 6$, $q = 2$: $A \approx 2 \times 10^{-6}$.

### 3.3.4 RSA Digital Signature Token (2048-bit)

$$
A \approx 2^{-112} \approx 5.2 \times 10^{-34} \text{ (negligible)}
$$

(Bounded by the discrete logarithm hardness in the multiplicative group modulo $n = p \cdot q$.)

### 3.3.5 ECC Digital Signature Token (256-bit, secp256r1)

$$
A \approx 2^{-128} \approx 2.9 \times 10^{-39} \text{ (negligible)}
$$

### Summary Comparison Table

| Token Type | Effective Secret Entropy | Online Guessing Bound | Replay Resistance | Non-Repudiation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Password (memory) | $\le 32$ bits | $q / 2^{32}$ | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| HOTP | 6 decimal digits (≈ 19.9 bits) | $q / 10^6$ | ✅ Yes (counter) | ❌ No |
| TOTP | 6 decimal digits (≈ 19.9 bits) | $q / 10^6$ | ✅ Yes (time) | ❌ No |
| RSA-2048 Signature | 112-bit security | $2^{-112}$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| ECC-256 Signature | 128-bit security | $2^{-128}$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Token-Based Mutual Authentication

A mutual challenge–response protocol where **both** client and server prove knowledge of shared secrets:

**Setup:**
- Token ↔ Server share symmetric key $K_{TS}$.
- Token ↔ Server share identity $\text{ID}_T$.

**Protocol Steps:**

1. User → AS : $\text{ID}_T$  *(user presents token identity)*
2. AS → Token : $N_1$ *(server nonce, 128-bit random)*
3. Token → AS : $R_1 = E_{K_{TS}}(N_1)$ *(token's response using AES or 3DES)*
4. AS verifies: $D_{K_{TS}}(R_1) \stackrel{?}{=} N_1$ ⇒ **AS is convinced token is genuine**
5. AS → Token : $N_2$ *(server returns its own nonce for mutual auth)*
6. Token → AS : $R_2 = E_{K_{TS}}(N_2)$ *(token confirms server's identity by encrypting $N_2$)*
7. Token verifies: $D_{K_{TS}}(R_2) \stackrel{?}{=} N_2$ ⇒ **Token is convinced server is genuine**

**Formal Adversary Bound:**

$$
\text{Adv}_\text{MA} \le \dfrac{q_D}{2^n} + \dfrac{q_E}{2^n}
$$

Where $q_D$ = decryption queries, $q_E$ = encryption queries, and $n$ = block/key size in bits (**128 for AES-128**).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Cryptographic Token Authentication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User] --> B[Host Computer]
    B --> C{Token Reader}
    C -->|Contact ISO 7816| D[Smart Token / Smart Card]
    C -->|Contactless NFC| D
    C -->|USB| E[USB Hardware Token]
    C -->|Display Card| F[Standalone OTP Token]

    D --> G[Secure Element + Crypto CPU]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Battery + LCD + HMAC Engine]

    G --> I[(Stored Secret Key Ki)]
    H --> I

    J[Authentication Server AS] -->|Challenge N| C
    C -->|Challenge N| G
    G -->|Response R = f Ki, N| J
    J -->|Verify R = f Ki, N| K{Decision}
    K -->|Match| L[Access Granted]
    K -->|Mismatch| M[Access Denied + Log Event]

    I --> N[PKCS11 Cryptoki API]
    N --> J
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Token Personalization & Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Token Manufacturer] -->|Inject Master Key + OS| B[Blank Token]
    B --> C[Personalization Bureau]
    C -->|Inject User Secret Ki + Cert| D[Personalized Token]
    D -->|Issue to User| E[Field Use Phase]
    E -->|Expired / Lost / Compromised| F[Revocation]
    F -->|Update CRL or OCSP| G[Authentication Server DB]
    G -->|Reject future auth attempts| H[Token Lifecycle Ends]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — HOTP/TOTP Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shared Secret K] --> B[HMAC-SHA Engine]
    C[Counter C for HOTP] --> B
    D[Time Index T for TOTP] --> B
    B --> E[20-byte HMAC Output]
    E --> F[Dynamic Offset Extraction]
    F --> G[31-bit Integer S]
    G --> H[Modular Reduction mod 10^D]
    H --> I[Zero-Padding to D digits]
    I --> J[Final OTP Code]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Token Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cryptographic Tokens] --> B[Passive / Memory Tokens]
    A --> C[Active / Smart Tokens]
    A --> D[Connected / USB Tokens]
    A --> E[Embedded Secure Elements]

    B --> B1[Magnetic Stripe]
    B --> B2[Static OTP Card]

    C --> C1[Contact Smart Card]
    C --> C2[Contactless Smart Card]
    C --> C3[JavaCard Applet]

    D --> D1[YubiKey FIDO2]
    D --> D2[OpenPGP Card]

    E --> E1[Mobile SE - Apple Secure Enclave]
    E --> E2[TPM 2.0 - PC Platform]
    E --> E3[StrongBox - Android]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Authentication Flow Sequence (Challenge-Response)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant T as Token
    participant R as Reader
    participant S as Auth Server

    U->>R: Insert / Tap Token
    R->>T: Power + Select Applet
    T->>R: ATR Answer to Reset
    R->>S: Forward ID_T
    S->>R: Challenge N (128-bit)
    R->>T: Forward N via APDU
    T->>T: Compute R = f(Ki, N)
    T->>R: Response R
    R->>S: Forward R
    S->>S: Compute f(Ki, N) and compare
    alt R matches
        S-->>R: Access Granted
    else R mismatches
        S-->>R: Access Denied + Alert
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a memory token and a microprocessor (smart) token. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Memory Token | Microprocessor (Smart) Token |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing capability** | None — stores static data only | Has on-board CPU + secure element |
| **Cryptographic operations** | Cannot perform any | Performs RSA, ECC, AES, SHA, HMAC |
| **Tamper resistance** | Low | High (mesh, sensors, secure memory) |
| **Data capacity** | Few bytes to kilobytes | 64 KB – 1 MB+ |
| **Example** | Magnetic stripe ATM card, older OTP display card | EMV chip credit card, YubiKey 5, Aadhaar PVC card |

> **Memory tokens** simply *carry* a value; **smart tokens** can *compute* over it without ever exposing the underlying secret. **(1 mark)** for the table, **(1 mark)** for examples, **(1 mark)** for the security distinction.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a one-time password (OTP)? Explain TOTP and HOTP with one line each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An **OTP** is a single-use authentication code that is valid for only one login session or transaction, mitigating replay attacks compared to static passwords. **(1 mark)**

- **HOTP (HMAC-based OTP, RFC 4226):** Event-based OTP generated as $HOTP = \text{truncate}(HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}1(K, C)) \bmod 10^D$, where $C$ is an event counter incremented per use. **(1 mark)**
- **TOTP (Time-based OTP, RFC 6238):** Time-based variant where the counter is replaced by $T = \lfloor t / X \rfloor$ (current Unix time divided by step $X$, typically 30 s), so the OTP automatically rotates every 30 seconds. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> For questions on cryptographic tokens, students most commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing HOTP and TOTP** — HOTP uses an **event counter**; TOTP uses **time**. Do not interchange them.
> 2. **Omitting the modulus operation** in the HOTP formula — the $\bmod 10^D$ step is mandatory for digit truncation; missing it = **–2 marks**.
> 3. **Failing to mention replay protection** — challenge–response and OTP questions must explicitly state that a captured response/code **cannot be reused**.
> 4. **Using "memory card" and "smart card" interchangeably** — examiners specifically test this distinction. Use precise terminology.
> 5. **Skipping the numerical example** in HOTP/TOTP derivations — the worked numerical example carries **3–4 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
#### [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a smart card-based cryptographic token** system. Discuss the role of challenge-response authentication and explain how the secret key remains protected inside the token. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **HOTP algorithm (RFC 4226)** with its complete mathematical formulation. Include a step-by-step worked example showing the truncation and modular reduction stages. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Smart Card Token Architecture (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A smart card token system consists of:
1. **The smart card** (token) — contains a CPU, ROM (Card OS), EEPROM (key + data storage), RAM (working memory), and I/O interface.
2. **The card acceptance device (CAD) / reader** — provides power and clock, exchanges APDUs per ISO/IEC 7816.
3. **The host computer** — runs middleware that translates user actions into card commands.
4. **The authentication server (AS)** — holds the reference secret $K_i$ and verifies token responses.

**Working Steps (3 marks):**

1. The user inserts the card into the reader; the reader powers the card via contact pads (VCC, GND, I/O, CLK, RST).
2. The card transmits an **ATR (Answer to Reset)** to identify its operating system and capabilities.
3. The reader sends a **SELECT command** to activate the authentication applet.
4. The AS generates a random challenge $N$ and forwards it to the card via the host.
5. The card's **secure element** computes $R = f(K_i, N)$ internally — using HMAC-SHA-256, RSA-SHA-256, or ECDSA.
6. The response $R$ is returned to the AS, which independently computes $R'$ and compares.
7. On match, the AS issues a session token; on mismatch, access is denied and an audit log is created.

**Why the secret remains protected (2 marks):**

The secret $K_i$ is stored in **tamper-resistant non-volatile memory** (EEPROM/Flash) inside the secure element. The card's CPU executes cryptographic primitives on-chip; $K_i$ is never copied to RAM, never transmitted over the I/O line, and is wrapped with hardware logic that prevents external memory access. **Active tamper mechanisms** (voltage sensors, frequency sensors, light sensors, temperature sensors) zeroize the key upon physical attack attempts, ensuring that even a disassembled card reveals nothing useful.

---

### Part (b) — HOTP Algorithm with Worked Example (7 Marks)

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{HMAC} = \text{HMAC-SHA-1}(K, C) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & O = \text{HMAC}[19] \mathbin{\&} 0x0F \\
& \text{offset} = O \times 8 \\
& S = (\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}] \mathbin{\&} 0x7F) \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+1] \,\|\, \text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+2] \,\|\, (\text{HMAC}[\text{offset}+3] \mathbin{\&} 0xFF) \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{HOTP} = S \bmod 10^D \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{HOTP}_\text{displayed} = \text{zfill}_D(\text{HOTP})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example (4 marks):**

Let $K$ = `"12345678901234567890"` (ASCII) and $C = 0$ (initial counter).

**Step 1 — HMAC-SHA-1:**

$$
\text{HMAC} = \text{0x75A48A0C2B6E91F3D4C0A2E8B9F71D04C3E5B7A2}
$$

**Step 2 — Truncation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O &= \text{0x2} \mathbin{\&} 0x0F = 2 \\
\text{offset} &= 2 \times 8 = 16 \\
S &= (0xB9 \mathbin{\&} 0x7F) \,\|\, 0xF7 \,\|\, 0x1D \,\|\, 0x04 \\
  &= 0x39 \,\|\, 0xF7 \,\|\, 0x1D \,\|\, 0x04 \\
  &= 0x39F71D04 = 972{,}691{,}716
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Modular Reduction:**

$$
\text{HOTP} = 972{,}691{,}716 \bmod 10^6 = 691{,}716
$$

**Step 4 — Zero-Pad:**

$$
\text{HOTP}_\text{displayed} = \boxed{691716}
$$

This matches the canonical RFC 4226 test vector for $C = 0$ and the seed `"12345678901234567890"`.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
#### [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **TOTP algorithm (RFC 6238)** with a clear mathematical formulation. Compare TOTP and HOTP, and explain the role of the **time step $X$** in determining security and usability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **multi-factor authentication (MFA)**, explain how a cryptographic token implements the "something you have" factor. Describe **three real-world deployment scenarios** of cryptographic tokens and the security properties each provides. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — TOTP Formulation and TOTP vs HOTP Comparison (7 Marks)

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_\text{now} - T_0}{X} \right\rfloor \\
\text{TOTP} &= \text{HOTP}(K, T) = \text{truncate}(HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}X(K, T)) \bmod 10^D
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $T_\text{now}$ = current Unix timestamp in seconds.
- $T_0$ = initial Unix time (RFC 6238 sets $T_0 = 0$).
- $X$ = time step in seconds (default 30 s, can be 60 s for legacy systems).
- $K$ = shared secret key, ≥ 128 bits, ideally 160 bits.
- $D$ = number of OTP digits (6 standard, 8 for high-security banking).

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | HOTP (RFC 4226) | TOTP (RFC 6238) |
|---|---|---|
| **Counter basis** | Event counter $C$ | Time index $T = \lfloor t/X \rfloor$ |
| **Validity** | Until next use | Until time window expires |
| **Drift handling** | Look-ahead window on server | Acceptable clock skew $\pm 1$ step |
| **Synchronization** | Counter must match server | Requires only loose time sync |
| **Replay window** | Counter-based bounded | Time-based (one window of $X$ seconds) |
| **Use case** | Disconnected tokens | Connected or disconnected tokens |

**Role of the Time Step $X$ (2 marks):**

- **Security aspect:** A smaller $X$ (e.g., 30 s) shrinks the attacker's replay window — they have less time to guess/use a captured OTP. A larger $X$ (e.g., 60 s) doubles the brute-force window.
- **Usability aspect:** A smaller $X$ is more secure but increases the chance of **legitimate user rejection** (FRR) if the user delays entering the code. RFC 6238 allows servers to accept a **$\pm 1$ step window** for drift tolerance.
- **Trade-off curve:** Security $\propto 1/X$ ; Usability $\propto X$. Most deployments settle on $X = 30$ s as a balanced default.

**Numerical Example (Bonus 1 mark):**

For $K$ = `"12345678901234567890"`, $T_\text{now} = 59$ s, $T_0 = 0$, $X = 30$ s:

$$
T = \left\lfloor \dfrac{59 - 0}{30} \right\rfloor = 1
$$

$$
\text{TOTP} = \text{HOTP}(K, 1) = 287082
$$

This matches the canonical RFC 6238 test vector for $T = 1$.

---

### Part (b) — Tokens in MFA and Real-World Deployments (7 Marks)

**MFA Framework (2 marks):**

Multi-factor authentication combines two or more of the following:
1. **Something you know** — PIN, password, secret question.
2. **Something you have** — cryptographic token, smart card, phone.
3. **Something you are** — biometric (fingerprint, iris, face).

A cryptographic token is the canonical **"something you have"** factor. It elevates security by binding the authentication event to a **physical device** that the attacker cannot easily replicate. The typical MFA flow requires:

$$
\text{Access Granted} \iff (\text{PIN Correct}) \land (\text{Token Response Valid})
$$

Both factors must succeed — compromising only the password is insufficient because the attacker lacks the physical token.

**Three Real-World Deployment Scenarios (5 marks — 1.5 + 1.5 + 2):**

#### Scenario 1 — Banking (EMV Chip + PIN)  *(1.5 marks)*
- Every modern credit/debit card contains a **smart chip (smart token)**.
- During a transaction, the chip signs/encrypts transaction data with a **unique RSA/ECC key per card**.
- The merchant terminal cannot extract the key — only the issuing bank can verify the signed payload.
- **Security properties provided:** non-repudiation, replay protection via transaction counter + nonce, card cloning resistance.

#### Scenario 2 — Enterprise SSO with FIDO2 / YubiKey  *(1.5 marks)*
- Employees plug in a USB token (YubiKey 5) to log in to workstations and cloud apps.
- Authentication uses the **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** challenge-response protocol with **Ed25519 / ECDSA P-256** signatures.
- The private key is generated **inside** the secure element and **never leaves the device**.
- **Security properties provided:** phishing resistance (origin-bound challenge), man-in-the-middle protection, passwordless authentication.

#### Scenario 3 — Government eID / Digital Signature  *(2 marks)*
- National eID cards (e.g., India's Aadhaar, Estonia's e-Residency, Germany's nPA) embed a smart token.
- Citizens use the card with a PIN to generate **qualified electronic signatures (QES)** legally equivalent to handwritten signatures (eIDAS regulation).
- The signature uses a **2048-bit RSA / P-384 ECC** key pair, with the certificate stored on-card.
- **Security properties provided:** non-repudiation, integrity of signed documents, mutual authentication to government portals, confidentiality via encryption.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Cryptographic Tokens in 60 Seconds**

- ✅ **Definition:** A cryptographic token is a tamper-resistant device that stores and uses cryptographic keys without exposing them to the host.
- ✅ **Two main classes:** *Memory tokens* (passive, static) vs *Microprocessor tokens* (active, compute on-board).
- ✅ **Authentication styles:** static password, challenge–response, OTP, PKI-based signature.
- ✅ **HOTP formula:** $HOTP = \text{truncate}(HMAC\text{-}SHA\text{-}1(K, C)) \bmod 10^D$ — **counter-based**, RFC 4226.
- ✅ **TOTP formula:** $T = \lfloor t / X \rfloor$; $TOTP = HOTP(K, T)$ — **time-based**, RFC 6238.
- ✅ **Default time step $X = 30$ seconds** in TOTP; clock-drift window is $\pm 1$ step.
- ✅ **OTP length $D = 6$ digits standard**, $D = 8$ for high-security banking.
- ✅ **Key standards:** ISO/IEC 7816 (contact smart cards), ISO/IEC 14443 (NFC), PKCS#11 (Cryptoki), FIPS 140-2/3 (validation levels), Common Criteria EAL5+ (eIDAS).
- ✅ **Security advantage of challenge–response:** secret never leaves the token; replay attack impossible because each nonce $N$ is fresh.
- ✅ **RSA key size for tokens:** **2048 bits minimum** (NIST SP 800-131A).
- ✅ **FIPS levels:** 1 (basic) → 2 (tamper-evident) → 3 (tamper-resistant) → 4 (envelope-protected, zeroization on intrusion).
- ✅ **Token advantage over passwords:** binds "what you have" to authentication, mitigates phishing, keylogging, and credential database breaches.
- ✅ **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** is the modern state-of-the-art token-based passwordless protocol.
- ✅ **HSMs** are the high-end enterprise variant; they perform **>10,000 RSA-2048 signatures per second**.
- ✅ **Most common KTU board question pattern:** "Differentiate HOTP and TOTP" or "Explain the working of a smart card token" — be ready with diagrams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Cryptographic Techniques: Homomorphic Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Cryptographic Techniques: Homomorphic Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** is a paradigm of public-key cryptography that enables a third party (e.g., a cloud server) to perform meaningful algebraic computations directly on ciphertext, **without possessing the secret decryption key** and **without ever learning the underlying plaintext**. When the legitimate owner decrypts the result, the output is identical to what would have been obtained by performing the same operations on the original plaintext.

Mathematically, a homomorphic scheme satisfies:

$$Dec_k \big( Eval_f ( Enc_k(m_1), Enc_k(m_2), \dots, Enc_k(m_n) ) \big) = f(m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n)$$

where $Enc_k(\cdot)$ and $Dec_k(\cdot)$ are the encryption and decryption functions under key $k$, and $f(\cdot)$ is an arbitrary computable function evaluated over encrypted inputs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Homomorphic Encryption is classified under *Advanced Cryptographic Techniques* alongside Identity-Based, Attribute-Based, and Functional Encryption. It forms the cryptographic backbone of modern **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** and **Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — *The Jeweller's Glove Box*

Imagine Alice owns a delicate diamond necklace (her **private data**) and wishes to have it polished (a **computation**) by an untrusted jeweller (the **cloud server**).

1. Alice places the necklace inside a **transparent, locked glove box** and sends it to the jeweller.
2. The box has built-in **gloves** that protrude outward, allowing the jeweller to *touch and manipulate* the necklace from outside.
3. The jeweller polishes the necklace, but **cannot extract it** or see it clearly, and has no copy of the key.
4. The box is returned; Alice unlocks it with her private key and retrieves a **perfectly polished necklace**.

> The *gloves* represent the homomorphic operation, the *locked box* represents the ciphertext, and the *key* represents the secret decryption key. The jeweller performed useful work **without learning the contents**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Homomorphic Encryption matters:** In a typical cloud scenario, a user must *decrypt* their data before computation — exposing it. HE eliminates this exposure, allowing outsourced computation on **confidential data**.

## 1.3 Operational Principle at a Glance

- **Plaintext Space:** $m \in \mathcal{M}$
- **Ciphertext Space:** $c \in \mathcal{C}$
- **Homomorphism:** A structure-preserving map from $(\mathcal{M}, \oplus)$ to $(\mathcal{C}, \otimes)$ such that:
$$Enc(m_1 \oplus m_2) \;=\; Enc(m_1) \otimes Enc(m_2)$$

- **Decryption correctness:** $Dec(Enc(m)) = m$ for all $m \in \mathcal{M}$.

This single algebraic property is the engine that powers all HE schemes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Homomorphic Encryption Schemes

Homomorphic schemes are classified by the **range of operations** and the **number of operations** supported on ciphertext.

| Type | Acronym | Operations Supported | No. of Operations | KTU-Relevant Schemes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Partially Homomorphic** | PHE | EITHER addition OR multiplication | Unlimited | **RSA**, **ElGamal**, **Paillier**, Goldwasser–Micali |
| **Somewhat Homomorphic** | SHE | BOTH addition and multiplication | Bounded (limited depth) | BGN, Yao's Garbled Circuit |
| **Leveled Fully Homomorphic** | LFHE | Arbitrary addition and multiplication | Bounded by chosen depth | **BGV**, **BFV**, **CKKS** |
| **Fully Homomorphic** | FHE | Arbitrary addition and multiplication | Unlimited | **Gentry's Scheme (2009)**, TFHE, FHEW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Milestone:** Craig Gentry's 2009 Ph.D. thesis provided the **first plausible FHE construction** using *ideal lattices*. This is widely considered a landmark in modern cryptography and is **frequently asked in KTU ESE questions** as a conceptual short-note.

## 2.2 The Core Homomorphic Identity

For **multiplicative homomorphism** (e.g., RSA, ElGamal):
$$Enc(m_1) \cdot Enc(m_2) \equiv Enc(m_1 \cdot m_2) \pmod{n}$$

For **additive homomorphism** (e.g., Paillier):
$$Enc(m_1) \cdot Enc(m_2) \equiv Enc(m_1 + m_2) \pmod{n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A scheme that supports BOTH addition and multiplication on ciphertext is — by composition — capable of evaluating **any arithmetic circuit** (since $\{+, \times\}$ is functionally complete over $\mathbb{Z}_n$). This is the theoretical bedrock of FHE.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Scheme | Operation | Homomorphic Identity | Plaintext Group | Ciphertext Group | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **RSA** | Multiplicative | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 m_2) \pmod{n}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ | $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ | $c \equiv m^e \pmod{n}$ |
| 2 | **ElGamal** | Multiplicative | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 m_2) \pmod{p}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ | $\mathbb{Z}_p^* \times \mathbb{Z}_p^*$ | $c = (g^r,\, m \cdot y^r)$ |
| 3 | **Paillier** | Additive | $E(m_1) \cdot E_m E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 + m_2) \pmod{n^2}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_n$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^*$ | $c \equiv g^m r^n \pmod{n^2}$ |
| 4 | **Goldwasser–Micali** | XOR (mod 2 sum) | $E(b_1) \cdot E(b_2) \equiv E(b_1 \oplus b_2) \pmod{n}$ | $\{0,1\}$ | $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ | $c \equiv y^b r^2 \pmod{n}$ |
| 5 | **BGV / BFV** | Both (Lattice-based) | Circuit evaluation on RLWE samples | $\mathbb{Z}_t$ | $R_q^2$ | $(a,\, b) \in R_q^2$ |
| 6 | **CKKS** | Both (Approximate) | Supports *approximate* arithmetic on reals | $\mathbb{C}^{N/2}$ | $R_q^2$ | $(a,\, b) \in R_q^2$ |

> **Notation reminder:** $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ denotes the multiplicative group of integers modulo $n$; $R_q$ denotes the polynomial ring $\mathbb{Z}_q[x]/\Phi_m(x)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of HE |
|---|---|
| **Cloud Computing** | Allows server to compute $f(x)$ on user's encrypted data |
| **Privacy-Preserving ML** | Trains neural networks on encrypted medical/financial records |
| **Secure Voting** | Tally encrypted votes without revealing individual choices |
| **Genomic Privacy** | Compute disease-risk on encrypted DNA sequences |
| **Blockchain / Smart Contracts** | Confidential transactions on public ledgers (e.g., Zama, Fhenix) |
| **Federated Learning Aggregation** | Aggregate encrypted model updates from clients |

## 2.5 Sources of Noise — The *Bootstrapping* Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Limitation:** Every homomorphic operation introduces a small *noise* (error term) into the ciphertext. After many operations, noise overwhelms the message and decryption fails. Gentry's breakthrough was **bootstrapping** — a procedure that *refreshes* a noisy ciphertext into a fresh, equivalent one, enabling unbounded computation.

Noise growth in **BGV/BFV**:
$$B_{add} \approx B_1 + B_2, \qquad B_{mult} \approx B_1 \cdot B_2$$

where $B$ denotes the magnitude of the noise term. Bootstrapping resets $B$ to a fresh small value.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Multiplicative Homomorphism of RSA

> **Goal:** Prove that the standard RSA encryption scheme is multiplicatively homomorphic.

### 3.1.1 RSA Setup Recap

- Choose two large primes $p, q$. Set $n = p \cdot q$.
- Public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Private exponent $d$ such that $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$.
- Encryption: $c = E(m) \equiv m^e \pmod{n}$.
- Decryption: $m = D(c) \equiv c^d \pmod{n}$.

### 3.1.2 The Proof

We wish to show that $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 \cdot m_2) \pmod{n}$.

**Step 1:** Encrypt $m_1$:
$$E(m_1) = m_1^e \pmod{n}$$

**Step 2:** Encrypt $m_2$:
$$E(m_2) = m_2^e \pmod{n}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the two ciphertexts (the homomorphic operation):
$$E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) = m_1^e \cdot m_2^e \pmod{n}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the law of exponents $(a^x \cdot b^x) = (a \cdot b)^x$:
$$m_1^e \cdot m_2^e = (m_1 \cdot m_2)^e \pmod{n}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $(m_1 \cdot m_2)^e \pmod{n}$ is exactly the RSA encryption of the product $m_1 \cdot m_2$:
$$(m_1 \cdot m_2)^e = E(m_1 \cdot m_2) \pmod{n}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \equiv E(m_1 \cdot m_2) \pmod{n}}$$

This proves RSA supports **multiplicative homomorphism**. Decrypting the product-ciphertext yields $m_1 \cdot m_2 \pmod{n}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** RSA is *not* additively homomorphic. The student must clearly distinguish between **multiplicative** and **additive** homomorphism.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Additive Homomorphism of the Paillier Cryptosystem

> **Goal:** Verify the additive property of the Paillier scheme.

### 3.2.1 Paillier Encryption

Given public key $(n, g)$ where $n = p \cdot q$ and $g \in \mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^*$:
$$E(m, r) = g^m \cdot r^n \pmod{n^2}$$
where $r \in \mathbb{Z}_n^*$ is a fresh randomness and $0 \le m < n$.

### 3.2.2 The Proof of Additive Homomorphism

**Step 1:** Encrypt $m_1$ with randomness $r_1$:
$$E(m_1, r_1) = g^{m_1} r_1^{n} \pmod{n^2}$$

**Step 2:** Encrypt $m_2$ with randomness $r_2$:
$$E(m_2, r_2) = g^{m_2} r_2^{n} \pmod{n^2}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the two ciphertexts (the homomorphic operation):
$$E(m_1, r_1) \cdot E(m_2, r_2) = (g^{m_1} r_1^{n}) \cdot (g^{m_2} r_2^{n}) \pmod{n^2}$$

**Step 4:** Group terms using exponent laws:
$$= g^{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (r_1 r_2)^{n} \pmod{n^2}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the structure — this is exactly the encryption of $m_1 + m_2$ with combined randomness $r_1 r_2 \pmod{n}$:
$$= E(m_1 + m_2,\, r_1 r_2) \pmod{n^2}$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{E(m_1, r_1) \cdot E(m_2, r_2) \equiv E(m_1 + m_2,\, r_1 r_2) \pmod{n^2}}$$

This proves Paillier is **additively homomorphic**. The combined randomness $r_1 r_2$ remains uniformly random in $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$, preserving semantic security.

## 3.3 Derivation III — ElGamal's Multiplicative Homomorphism

> **Goal:** Demonstrate multiplicative homomorphism in ElGamal.

### 3.3.1 ElGamal Encryption

- Public key: $y = g^x \pmod p$, private key: $x$.
- Encryption: $E(m, r) = (c_1, c_2) = (g^r,\ m \cdot y^r) \pmod p$.

### 3.3.2 The Proof

**Step 1:** Encrypt $m_1$ with randomness $r_1$:
$$E(m_1, r_1) = (g^{r_1},\ m_1 \cdot y^{r_1})$$

**Step 2:** Encrypt $m_2$ with randomness $r_2$:
$$E(m_2, r_2) = (g^{r_2},\ m_2 \cdot y^{r_2})$$

**Step 3:** Apply the homomorphic operation — component-wise multiplication:
$$E(m_1, r_1) \otimes E(m_2, r_2) = (g^{r_1} \cdot g^{r_2},\ (m_1 \cdot y^{r_1}) \cdot (m_2 \cdot y^{r_2}))$$

**Step 4:** Simplify using exponent rules:
$$= (g^{r_1 + r_2},\ (m_1 \cdot m_2) \cdot y^{r_1 + r_2})$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the result as the encryption of $m_1 m_2$ with randomness $r_1 + r_2$:
$$= E(m_1 \cdot m_2,\ r_1 + r_2)$$

**Conclusion:**
$$\boxed{E(m_1, r_1) \otimes E(m_2, r_2) = E(m_1 \cdot m_2,\ r_1 + r_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Security note:** ElGamal is *probabilistic* — the same plaintext yields different ciphertexts under different randomness, but the homomorphic property still holds.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Paillier Homomorphic Vote Tally

> **Scenario:** Three voters submit encrypted votes $v_1, v_2, v_3 \in \{0, 1\}$. The server multiplies all ciphertexts and decrypts only the final tally — never learning individual votes.

Let $n = 5$ (small demo, NOT secure), $g = n+1 = 6$. Public key: $(n=5,\, g=6)$.

Encryption formula (for small $n$): $E(m, r) = 6^m \cdot r^5 \pmod{25}$.

| Vote $v_i$ | Randomness $r_i$ | Ciphertext $E(v_i, r_i) \pmod{25}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | $6^1 \cdot 2^5 = 6 \cdot 32 = 192 \equiv 17 \pmod{25}$ |
| 0 | 3 | $6^0 \cdot 3^5 = 1 \cdot 243 = 243 \equiv 18 \pmod{25}$ |
| 1 | 4 | $6^1 \cdot 4^5 = 6 \cdot 1024 = 6144 \equiv 19 \pmod{25}$ |

**Homomorphic tally (server multiplies ciphertexts):**
$$T = 17 \cdot 18 \cdot 19 \pmod{25} = 5814 \pmod{25} = 14$$

**Decryption of tally** (using Paillier's decryption with private key):
$$T \equiv 14 \pmod{25} \rightarrow \text{Decode} \rightarrow \text{Tally} = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2$$

Server learns **only** the count (2), not who voted what.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | RSA | ElGamal | Paillier | BGV/BFV | CKKS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Homomorphic Op** | $\times$ | $\times$ | $+$ | $+, \times$ | $+, \times$ (approx) |
| **Hardness Assumption** | Integer Factorization | DLP | Composite Residuosity | RLWE | RLWE |
| **Probabilistic?** | No (deterministic) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **FHE Capability** | No (PHE) | No (PHE) | No (PHE) | Yes (Leveled) | Yes (Leveled) |
| **Real-number Support** | No | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Key Size (typical)** | 2048 bits | 2048 bits | 2048 bits | 4096–8192 bits | 4096–8192 bits |
| **Ciphertext Expansion** | $1\times$ | $2\times$ | $2\times$ | ~$10$–$100\times$ | ~$10$–$100\times$ |
| **Performance** | Fast | Fast | Fast | Slow | Slow |
