## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Quantum Cryptography** is rooted in physics, not mathematics — security is *information-theoretic*, not computational.
- ✅ The **qubit** is a superposition $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ with $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.
- ✅ **Three Pillars:** Superposition, Measurement Collapse, and the **No-Cloning Theorem**.
- ✅ **Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle:** $\Delta A \cdot \Delta B \geq \frac{\hbar}{2}\vert \langle [\hat{A}, \hat{B}] \rangle \vert$ — the foundation of BB84's eavesdropper detection.
- ✅ **BB84 Protocol:** Uses **two non-orthogonal bases** (+ and ×). Sifting discards ~50% of bits. QBER threshold = **11%**.
- ✅ **B92 Protocol:** Simplified variant of BB84 — uses only **two non-orthogonal states** (e.g., 0° and 45°) instead of four.
- ✅ **E91 Protocol (Ekert):** Uses **entangled photon pairs** — security proved by violations of **Bell's inequality** $\vert S \vert \leq 2$ (classical) vs. $\vert S \vert \leq 2\sqrt{2}$ (quantum).
- ✅ **Shor's Algorithm (1994):** Polynomial-time integer factorization — breaks RSA, ECC, Diffie-Hellman. Complexity: $\mathcal{O}((\log N)^3)$.
- ✅ **Grover's Algorithm (1996):** Quadratic speedup for unstructured search — $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{N})$. Mitigated by **doubling key sizes** (AES-256 instead of AES-128).
- ✅ **Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) — NIST 2024 Standards:** CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium, SPHINCS+, Classic McEliece.
- ✅ **Quantum Channel + Classical Authenticated Channel** = the dual requirement for all QKD protocols.
- ✅ **Planck's constant** $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s — appears in the uncertainty principle; examiners love this constant.
- ✅ **Polarization angles** for BB84: Horizontal $= 0^\circ$, Vertical $= 90^\circ$, Diagonal $= 45^\circ$, Anti-diagonal $= 135^\circ$.
- ✅ **Sifted Key Rate** is theoretically **50%** of the raw transmission, before error correction.
- ✅ **Information Reconciliation** corrects bit errors; **Privacy Amplification** (universal hashing) eliminates Eve's partial information.
- ✅ **Engineering Reality (2024):** China's **Micius satellite** (2017) demonstrated intercontinental QKD over 1,200 km; commercial QKD systems are deployed by **ID Quantique**, **Toshiba**, and **QuantumCTek**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryptography in Cloud Computing: Security Challenges and Cryptographic Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cryptography in Cloud Computing: Security Challenges and Cryptographic Solutions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Cloud Computing Cryptography** refers to the disciplined application of mathematical cryptographic primitives, key management protocols, and secure architectural patterns used to preserve the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **authenticity**, and **non-repudiation** of data, applications, and services hosted on distributed, multi-tenant cloud infrastructures. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, it is positioned as the intersection of three core pillars: *Public-Key Infrastructure (PKI)*, *Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption Schemes*, and *Trusted Computing Models* deployed across the cloud service layers (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Cloud Cryptography is the use of cryptographic algorithms, protocols, and key management techniques to secure data-at-rest, data-in-transit, and data-in-use across public, private, hybrid, and community cloud deployment models, while addressing multi-tenancy, virtualization, and jurisdictional risks." ### Key Terminology (KTU-Standardized) - **Data-at-Rest (DAR)** – Static data stored on cloud media (disks, object stores, databases). - **Data-in-Transit (DIT)** – Data moving across networks between client and cloud, or between cloud nodes. - **Data-in-Use (DIU)** – Data being processed in CPU registers, RAM, or enclave memory. - **Multi-Tenancy** – Multiple customers sharing the same physical/virtual resources. - **Cryptographic Agility** – Ability to swap algorithms/keys without re-architecting the system. - **Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)** – Hardware-isolated memory region (e.g., Intel SGX, ARM TrustZone) where data can be processed in plaintext. - **Homomorphic Encryption (HE)** – Encryption scheme allowing computation directly on ciphertext. - **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key)** – Customer-supplied encryption keys for cloud-resident data. > [!NOTE] > **NIST SP 800-144** classifies cloud security into three canonical states: *Data-at-Rest*, *Data-in-Transit*, and *Data-in-Use*. Every cryptographic solution in the cloud must map to at least one of these states. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Bank Locker" Analogy:** > Imagine you keep your gold in a bank's safety locker. The bank is the *cloud provider* — they own the building, the cameras, and the vault. You own the *gold* (your data). Three things must be guaranteed: > > 1. **Confidentiality** — Only *you* hold the locker key; no bank employee can open it (≈ *encryption with a key only you control, i.e., BYOK*). > 2. **Integrity** — The gold you put in is the gold you take out; nothing is swapped or shaved (≈ *digital signatures and hash-based MACs*). > 3. **Availability** — The bank vault is open 24/7 and survives earthquakes (≈ *replication, sharding, and fault-tolerant key escrow*). > > The bank also faces *insider threats* (≈ malicious cloud admins), *break-ins* (≈ external attackers), and *regulatory audits* (≈ compliance with GDPR, DPDPA, HIPAA). Cryptography in the cloud is essentially the *lock, the alarm system, the key management protocol, and the audit trail* that make the bank safe enough for you to trust. ### Why This Matters in KTU Context - **Industry-Standard Algorithms** mandated: **AES-256** (symmetric), **RSA-2048 / RSA-3072**, **ECC (P-256, P-384)**, **SHA-256 / SHA-3**, **TLS 1.3**, **AES-GCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305**. - **Deployment Lifetimes**: Symmetric keys (≤ **2 years**), RSA keys (≤ **5 years**), Root CA keys (≥ **10 years**). - **Latency Targets**: TLS handshake < **100 ms**; HSM signing < **10 ms**; Key rotation < **1 second** for cloud-scale KMS. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Cloud Cryptography The KTU 2024 scheme organizes cloud cryptographic solutions into five engineering pillars. Each pillar is anchored to specific Course Outcomes (COs) and Bloom's Levels. ### Pillar 1: Encryption for Data-at-Rest - **Goal**: Protect stored objects, volumes, snapshots, and backups. - **Mechanisms**: - **Server-Side Encryption (SSE)** — Cloud provider encrypts using provider-managed keys (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS). - **Client-Side Encryption (CSE)** — Data encrypted *before* upload, key never leaves client. - **Envelope Encryption** — A *Data Encryption Key (DEK)* encrypts data; a *Key Encryption Key (KEK)* from a KMS encrypts the DEK. - **Standard Algorithms**: **AES-256-GCM**, **AES-256-CBC + HMAC-SHA-256**, **XTS-AES** (for full-disk encryption). ### Pillar 2: Encryption for Data-in-Transit - **Goal**: Protect data moving over untrusted networks. - **Protocols**: **TLS 1.3** (mandated in modern cloud APIs), **IPsec**, **WireGuard**, **MACsec** (data-center internal). - **Cipher Suites** (KTU-mandated strong suites): - `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` - `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256` - `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256` ### Pillar 3: Encryption for Data-in-Use (The Hardest Frontier) - **Goal**: Compute on data without ever decrypting it on the host. - **Mechanisms**: - **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)** — Supports arbitrary arithmetic on ciphertexts. - **Partially Homomorphic Encryption (PHE)** — Paillier (additive), ElGamal/RSA (multiplicative). - **Secure Multi-Party Computation (SMPC)** — Multiple parties jointly compute a function while keeping inputs private. - **Trusted Execution Environments (TEE)** — Intel SGX, AMD SEV-SNP, ARM Confidential Compute Architecture (CCA). - **Differential Privacy** — Add calibrated noise to query results. ### Pillar 4: Identity, Authentication & Key Management - **PKI in the Cloud** — X.509 certificates issued by cloud-hosted CAs (e.g., AWS Certificate Manager, Azure AD CS). - **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** — FIPS 140-2 Level 3+ certified devices for key custody (e.g., AWS CloudHSM, Azure Dedicated HSM, Google Cloud HSM). - **Key Management Service (KMS)** — Centralized key generation, rotation, revocation, audit. - **Secret Management** — HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager. - **Identity Federation** — SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC) over cloud IdPs. ### Pillar 5: Auditing, Attestation & Compliance - **Remote Attestation** — TEE generates a signed *quote* proving its code/state to a remote verifier. - **Transparent Data Encryption (TDE)** — Database-level encryption (SQL Server, Oracle, PostgreSQL TDE). - **Audit Logging** — Cryptographically chained logs (hash-linked, Merkle-tree structured) for tamper-evidence. - **Compliance Frameworks** — **GDPR**, **HIPAA**, **PCI-DSS 4.0**, **SOC 2 Type II**, **ISO 27001**, **DPDPA (India, 2023)**. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for Module 4. Memorize the symbol definitions, the modulus arithmetic, and the security levels — these appear every year. | # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning / Unit | |---|---------|-------------------|----------------------------| | 1 | Symmetric Encryption (Block) | $C = E_K(P)$, $\;P = D_K(C)$ | Confidentiality; key length = **128 / 192 / 256 bits** | | 2 | AES Round Operation | $S_{i+1} = \sigma\big(\text{MixCols}(\text{ShiftRows}(S_i \oplus K_i))\big)$ | 10/12/14 rounds for 128/192/256-bit keys | | 3 | RSA Encryption | $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ | Public key $(e, n)$; **e = 65537** is F4 standard | | 4 | RSA Decryption | $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ | Private key $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | | 5 | RSA Key Generation | $n = p \cdot q,\;\phi(n)=(p-1)(q-1)$ | **n ≥ 2048 bits** for security through **2030+** | | 6 | RSA Signature | $S = M^{d} \bmod n,\;\text{Verify: }M \stackrel{?}{=} S^{e} \bmod n$ | Authentication + Non-Repudiation | | 7 | ECDSA Signature | $(r, s)$ where $r = (kG)_x \bmod n$ | **256-bit ECC** ≈ **3072-bit RSA** security | | 8 | Diffie-Hellman (Cloud) | $A = g^{a} \bmod p,\;B = g^{b} \bmod p,\;K = B^{a} = A^{b} \bmod p$ | TLS 1.3 ephemeral key exchange | | 9 | Hash Function (DAR integrity) | $h = H(M),\;h:\{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$ | SHA-256 → 256 bits; collision resistance **2¹²⁸ ops** | | 10 | HMAC | $\text{HMAC}(K, M) = H\big((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M)\big)$ | Message Authentication Code; **RFC 2104** | | 11 | AES-GCM Authentication Tag | $T = \text{GHASH}(H, A, C) \oplus E_K(J_0)$ | 128-bit tag; provides AEAD | | 12 | Envelope Encryption | $C_{DEK} = E_{KEK}(DEK),\;C_{data} = E_{DEK}(data)$ | Two-tier key hierarchy in cloud KMS | | 13 | Paillier Additive HE | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \bmod n^{2} = E(m_1 + m_2 \bmod n)$ | Sum-preserving homomorphic encryption | | 14 | RSA Multiplicative HE | $E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) = m_1^{e}m_2^{e} = (m_1m_2)^{e} = E(m_1 m_2)$ | Product-preserving (mod n) | | 15 | FHE Noise Budget (BGV/CKKS) | $\text{depth}_{mult} \leq \frac{\log q}{\log B} - 1$ | Limits circuit depth before **bootstrapping** | | 16 | TLS 1.3 Handshake Latency | $T_{handshake} = 1\text{ RTT (full)} \text{ or } 0\text{ RTT (resumption)}$ | Standard cloud edge latency < **100 ms** | | 17 | Shannon Entropy (Key Strength) | $H(K) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits | AES-256 key → **256 bits** of entropy | | 18 | Work Factor (Brute Force) | $W = 2^{k}$ where $k$ = key bits | AES-128 → **2¹²⁸** operations | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** The examiner *will* test `e = 65537` and the modulus condition $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ in RSA problems. For HE questions, *always* state the homomorphic property explicitly (additive vs. multiplicative) before applying the formula. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Cryptographic Cloud Use Case | Algorithm Deployed | |----------|------------------------------|---------------------| | Banking / FinTech | PCI-DSS compliant tokenization | AES-256 + HSM (FIPS 140-3 L3) | | Healthcare (EHR) | HIPAA-encrypted patient records | AES-256-GCM + BYOK + audit logs | | Government / Defence | Sovereign cloud with classified data | FHE + air-gapped HSM clusters | | ML / AI-as-a-Service | Privacy-Preserving ML inference | CKKS FHE / SMPC / Federated Learning | | IoT / Edge | Lightweight device-to-cloud security | ChaCha20-Poly1305 + Ed25519 | | Multi-Cloud DR | Cross-cloud key replication | External KEK with quorum-based unwrap | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Envelope Encryption in a Cloud KMS > [!NOTE] > This derivation appears almost every KTU exam cycle under the tag *"cloud key management"* or *"hybrid encryption"*. Master the two-tier key wrap and you earn full marks. ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup A cloud user wants to upload a **4 GB** medical imaging file $D$ to a cloud object store. The user *does not trust* the cloud provider with the plaintext key. Design an envelope encryption scheme using a Customer-Managed Key (CMK) $K_{KEK}$ stored in a cloud HSM. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Generate a fresh symmetric Data Encryption Key (DEK).** The client uses a CSPRNG to generate a **256-bit** DEK: $$ K_{DEK} \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{256} $$ The notation $K_{DEK} \xleftarrow{\$}$ denotes *uniform random sampling* from the key space. This is essential for Shannon entropy $H(K_{DEK}) = 256$ bits. **Step 2 — Encrypt the data with the DEK using AES-256-GCM.** $$ C_{data} = \text{AES-GCM-Encrypt}(K_{DEK}, IV, AAD, D) $$ where $IV$ is a 96-bit random nonce, $AAD$ is authenticated additional data (e.g., object key, tenant ID), and $D$ is the plaintext data. The output $C_{data}$ includes both the ciphertext and the **128-bit authentication tag** $T$. **Step 3 — Wrap the DEK with the KEK from the cloud HSM.** $$ C_{DEK} = E_{KEK}(K_{DEK}) = \text{AES-Wrap-256}(K_{KEK}, K_{DEK}) $$ The KEK $K_{KEK}$ is a **256-bit** key that *never* leaves the HSM boundary. The standard key-wrap algorithm is **AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394)**, which itself is authenticated. **Step 4 — Store the wrapped key alongside the ciphertext.** $$ \text{ObjectMetadata} = \{\,C_{DEK},\;IV,\;AAD,\;T,\;\text{AlgorithmID},\;\text{KeyVersion}\,\} $$ The cloud provider stores $C_{data}$ in the object store and persists $\text{ObjectMetadata}$ as object metadata or in a separate key-map table. **Step 5 — Decryption (Reverse Operation).** $$ K_{DEK} = D_{KEK}(C_{DEK}) = \text{AES-Unwrap-256}(K_{KEK}, C_{DEK}) $$ $$ D = \text{AES-GCM-Decrypt}(K_{DEK}, IV, AAD, C_{data}, T) $$ The HSM performs Step 5a internally; the plaintext $D$ is never exposed to the cloud host. ### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Envelope Encryption? | Property | Direct KEK Encryption | Envelope Encryption | |----------|----------------------|----------------------| | HSM calls per upload | **O(data size)** | **O(1)** | | Throughput | **~10 MB/s** (HSM-bound) | **~500 MB/s** (AES-NI) | | Key rotation cost | Re-encrypt entire dataset | Re-wrap DEK only | | Blast radius of key leak | Whole dataset | Single object | | AWS KMS / Azure Key Vault support | Limited | **Native** | > [!TIP] > **Mark-Saver:** When asked *"why envelope encryption?"* always list these 5 properties. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for the throughput comparison and **2 marks** for key rotation efficiency. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Paillier Additive Homomorphic Encryption for Cloud Aggregation ### 3.2.1 Problem Setup A cloud aggregator collects encrypted sensor readings from $N$ IoT devices. Each device encrypts its reading $m_i \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ and uploads $E(m_i)$. The cloud must compute $\sum m_i$ **without ever decrypting** any individual reading. Solve this using Paillier PHE. ### 3.2.2 Key Generation Choose two large primes $p, q$ of equal length (≥ **1024 bits** each). Compute: $$ n = p \cdot q, \qquad \lambda = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1) $$ Pick $g \in \mathbb{Z}_{n^2}^{*}$ such that $\gcd(L(g^{\lambda} \bmod n^2), n) = 1$, where $L(x) = \frac{x-1}{n}$. The public key is $(n, g)$ and the private key is $(\lambda, \mu)$ where $\mu = L(g^{\lambda} \bmod n^2)^{-1} \bmod n$. ### 3.2.3 Encryption For plaintext $m \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ and randomness $r \in \mathbb{Z}_n^{*}$: $$ E(m, r) = g^{m} \cdot r^{n} \bmod n^{2} $$ ### 3.2.4 Homomorphic Addition (The Core Property) Given $E(m_1, r_1)$ and $E(m_2, r_2)$: $$ E(m_1, r_1) \cdot E(m_2, r_2) \bmod n^2 = (g^{m_1} r_1^{n}) \cdot (g^{m_2} r_2^{n}) \bmod n^2 $$ $$ = g^{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (r_1 r_2)^{n} \bmod n^2 $$ $$ = E(m_1 + m_2 \bmod n,\, r_1 r_2 \bmod n) $$ **Therefore, the cloud can compute $E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\right)$ by simply multiplying all ciphertexts:** $$ E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i\right) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} E(m_i, r_i) \bmod n^2 $$ ### 3.2.5 Decryption by the Aggregator $$ \sum m_i = L\!\left(C^{\lambda} \bmod n^2\right) \cdot \mu \bmod n $$ ### 3.2.6 Worked Numerical Toy Example Take $p = 5, q = 7 \Rightarrow n = 35, n^2 = 1225, \lambda = \text{lcm}(4, 6) = 12$. Let $g = 2$, then $L(2^{12} \bmod 1225) = L(76) = (76 - 1)/35 = 75/35 = 15/7$. Choose $g$ such that this is invertible. Let $m_1 = 10, m_2 = 15, r_1 = 3, r_2 = 4$. $$ E(10, 3) = 2^{10} \cdot 3^{35} \bmod 1225 $$ $$ 2^{10} = 1024, \quad 3^{35} \bmod 1225 = \cdots \text{(evaluable but lengthy)} $$ Product ciphertext: $$ C = E(10, 3) \cdot E(15, 4) \bmod 1225 = E(25 \bmod 35, 12) = E(25, 12) $$ Decryption yields $\sum m_i = 25$, which is **exactly** the sum of plaintexts — without the cloud ever learning 10 or 15 individually. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Numerical Trap:** When you are asked to verify the homomorphic property numerically, *always* reduce modulo $n$ at the end. Forgetting the $\bmod n$ at the final step is the most common deduction (–1 to –2 marks). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: TLS 1.3 Handshake (Cloud-Edge Authentication) ### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step (1-RTT Handshake) The TLS 1.3 protocol, mandated in **all** modern cloud APIs, executes as follows: **Round 1 (Client → Server): ClientHello** The client sends a `ClientHello` containing: - Random nonce $r_c \in \{0,1\}^{32}$ - Supported cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`) - Key share: ephemeral ECDHE public key $X_c = g^{a} \bmod p$ (or $a \cdot G$ on curve secp256r1) **Server Response: ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished** The server replies with: - `ServerHello`: chosen cipher suite + server random $r_s$ - Key share: ephemeral $X_s = g^{b} \bmod p$ - `Certificate`: X.509 chain rooted in a trusted CA - `CertificateVerify`: ECDSA signature over $\text{TranscriptHash}$ using the server's long-term key - `Finished`: HMAC of the transcript using the **server handshake traffic secret** $TS_{server}$ **Shared Secret Derivation** $$ \text{ECDHE shared} = g^{ab} \bmod p $$ $$ \text{Handshake Secret} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt} = \text{PSK or 0},\; \text{IKM} = g^{ab}) $$ $$ \text{Master Secret} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{salt} = \text{Derive-Secret}(\text{HS}, \text{"master"},\; \text{transcript}),\; \text{IKM} = 0^{32}) $$ Application traffic keys are derived: $$ TS_{server} = \text{HKDF-Expand-Label}(\text{MS},\; \text{"s ap traffic"},\; \text{transcript},\; 32) $$ **Round 2 (Client → Server): Finished + Application Data** The client sends `Finished` (HMAC of transcript under $TS_{client}$) and then application data encrypted with the derived keys. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Result:** TLS 1.3 reduced handshake to **1 RTT** (vs. 2 RTT in TLS 1.2) and removed all weak ciphers (RC4, MD5, SHA-1, static RSA key exchange). In 0-RTT mode, the client can send data on the first flight, with **replay risk** for *idempotent* requests only. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection for Cloud Workloads | Workload | Confidentiality Need | Integrity Need | Recommended Suite | Justification | |----------|---------------------|----------------|--------------------|---------------| | Object storage (DAR) | High | High | **AES-256-GCM** + KMS | AEAD, hardware-accelerated, native KMS | | TLS API endpoints | High | High | **TLS 1.3 + X25519 + AES-256-GCM** | Forward secrecy, low handshake latency | | Database TDE | High | Medium | **AES-XTS-256** | Tuned for sector-level disk encryption | | Confidential ML inference | High | High | **CKKS FHE** or **TEE (SGX)** | Compute on encrypted data | | IoT telemetry | Medium | High | **ChaCha20-Poly1305 + Ed25519** | Lightweight, no hardware AES | | Cross-region DR replication | High | High | **AES-256-GCM + external KEK** | Avoid cloud-provider lock-in | | Audit log chains | Medium | Critical | **SHA-256 Merkle + Ed25519 batch sig** | Tamper-evident, compact proofs | | Federated identity (SAML/OIDC) | High | High | **RSA-PSS / ECDSA P-256 + SHA-256** | Interoperable with all IdPs | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cloud Cryptographic Stack (Layered Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef trust fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff classDef crypto fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#ffffff classDef kmi fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000 classDef data fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000 subgraph L1["Data States (What to Protect)"] d1["Data at Rest
Object Stores, DBs, Volumes"]:::data d2["Data in Transit
TLS, IPsec, MACsec"]:::data d3["Data in Use
CPU, RAM, Enclaves"]:::data end subgraph L2["Cryptographic Primitives (How to Protect)"] c1["AES-256-GCM
Symmetric AEAD"]:::crypto c2["RSA-3072 / ECC P-384
Asymmetric"]:::crypto c3["SHA-256 / SHA-3
Hash Functions"]:::crypto c4["Paillier / CKKS FHE
Homomorphic"]:::crypto end subgraph L3["Key & Identity Management (Who Holds Keys)"] k1["Cloud HSM
FIPS 140-3 Level 3"]:::kmi k2["Cloud KMS
Envelope Encryption"]:::kmi k3["Customer BYOK
External Key Origin"]:::kmi k4["PKI / Certificate Authority"]:::kmi end subgraph L4["Trust & Compliance (Why It Is Trusted)"] t1["Remote Attestation
TEE Quote Verification"]:::trust t2["Audit Logs
Cryptographically Chained"]:::trust t3["Compliance
GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS 4.0"]:::trust end L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 c1 -.envelope wraps.-> k2 c2 -.key custody.-> k1 c4 -.key rotation.-> k3 k4 -.signs certificates.-> t1 ``` > [!NOTE] > This block diagram shows the **defense-in-depth layering** mandated by NIST SP 800-144 and adopted by KTU Module 4. Each layer assumes the *layer below it is compromised* — a core tenet of cloud security architecture. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Envelope Encryption Data Flow (Client → Cloud KMS) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef client fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 classDef cloud fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000 classDef hsm fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 A["Client Application
Plaintext Data D"]:::client B["Step 1: Generate DEK
K_DEK = CSPRNG 256-bit"]:::client C["Step 2: Encrypt Data
C_data = AES-GCM(K_DEK, D)"]:::client D["Step 3: Request KEK Wrap
HSM Unwrap Authority"]:::client E["Cloud HSM
Holds KEK (never exported)"]:::hsm F["Step 4: Wrap DEK
C_DEK = AES-Wrap(KEK, K_DEK)"]:::hsm G["Cloud Object Store
C_data + C_DEK metadata"]:::cloud H["Step 5: On Read - Unwrap
K_DEK = AES-Unwrap(KEK, C_DEK)"]:::hsm I["Step 6: Decrypt in Memory
D = AES-GCM-Decrypt(K_DEK, C_data)"]:::client A --> B --> C --> D --> E E --> F --> G G --> H H --> I ``` > [!TIP] > **Sequence in Plain English:** The *client* generates a per-object key, encrypts locally, then asks the *HSM* to wrap (encrypt) that per-object key with the master KEK. The cloud *never* sees the plaintext DEK. On read, the HSM unwraps the DEK **inside the HSM boundary**, returns it temporarily to the client (or releases plaintext via a secure channel), and the client decrypts the object. The HSM's KEK *never* leaves the hardware. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Threat Model and Counter-Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef threat fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000 classDef ctrl fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 th1["T1: External Network Attacker
Eavesdropping / MITM"]:::threat th2["T2: Malicious Cloud Insider
Privileged Admin Access"]:::threat th3["T3: Multi-Tenant Co-Resident
Side-Channel / VM Escape"]:::threat th4["T4: Stolen Cloud Credentials
API Key Leakage"]:::threat th5["T5: Data-at-Rest Theft
Stolen Disk / Snapshot"]:::threat th6["T6: Compromised Hypervisor
Host OS Takeover"]:::threat c1["TLS 1.3 + Certificate Pinning"]:::ctrl c2["BYOK + HSM + Audit Logging"]:::ctrl c3["TEE / Confidential VM + Memory Encryption"]:::ctrl c4["MFA + Short-Lived STS Tokens + IAM"]:::ctrl c5["Envelope Encryption + Per-Object DEK"]:::ctrl c6["Attestation + Signed Boot + TPM"]:::ctrl th1 -.mitigated by.-> c1 th2 -.mitigated by.-> c2 th3 -.mitigated by.-> c3 th4 -.mitigated by.-> c4 th5 -.mitigated by.-> c5 th6 -.mitigated by.-> c6 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A common exam question asks *"Map each cloud security threat to a cryptographic control."* This diagram is your one-page answer template. Memorize the six pairs above; they map directly to the **CIA Triad + Non-Repudiation** assessment rubric. ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Homomorphic Encryption Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef input fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 classDef crypto fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 p1["Plaintext m1"]:::input p2["Plaintext m2"]:::input p3["Plaintext m3"]:::input e1["Enc: c1 = E(m1)"]:::crypto e2["Enc: c2 = E(m2)"]:::crypto e3["Enc: c3 = E(m3)"]:::crypto agg["Cloud Server
Computes c1 * c2 * c3 mod n^2
Without seeing plaintext"]:::crypto d1["Dec: m_sum = D(c_sum)"]:::output res["Aggregated Result
m1 + m2 + m3"]:::output p1 --> e1 --> agg p2 --> e2 --> agg p3 --> e3 --> agg agg --> d1 --> res ``` > [!NOTE] > This is the *exact* Paillier workflow from §3.2. The cloud server **never** holds a decryption key — it only performs ciphertext-domain arithmetic. The owning client performs the only `Dec` step. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]** **Differentiate between Data-at-Rest encryption and Data-in-Use protection. Give one real-world cryptographic mechanism for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Data-at-Rest (DAR) | Data-in-Use (DIU) | |--------|---------------------|---------------------| | Definition | Static data stored on persistent media (disks, object stores, backups) | Data actively being processed in CPU, RAM, or accelerator memory | | Threat | Stolen disks, snapshots, insider disk reads | Memory dumps, hypervisor compromise, malicious co-tenant | | Example Mechanism | **AES-256-GCM** with envelope encryption via cloud KMS | **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (CKKS)** or **TEE (Intel SGX)** enclaves | | Latency Overhead | Low (offline encryption, hardware AES-NI) | High (FHE: **1000×–10000×**; TEE: <5%) | | Granularity | Per-object DEK or per-volume key | Per-instruction / per-enclave session | **Real-world deployments:** AWS S3 SSE-KMS = DAR; Microsoft Azure Confidential Computing = DIU. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **What is BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) in cloud cryptography? How does it differ from HYOK (Hold Your Own Key)?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **BYOK (Bring Your Own Key):** The customer *generates* an encryption key in their own on-premise HSM and *imports* it into the cloud provider's KMS. The cloud provider's KMS stores a *wrapped* copy. The customer can revoke access, but the key technically *resides* in the cloud KMS at some point. *(1 mark)* - **HYOK (Hold Your Own Key):** A stricter variant. The key *never* leaves the customer's hardware. The cloud provider must call back to the customer's HSM (over a secure channel) for every cryptographic operation. Latency is higher but the trust boundary is tighter. *(1 mark)* - **Practical difference:** BYOK is supported by AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, Google Cloud KMS (import-only). HYOK is rarer; examples include **Thales Luna HSM + CipherTrust** or **AWS CloudHSM with external key store** mode. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): Envelope Encryption + Cloud KMS Architecture **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice A]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **envelope encryption** scheme used in cloud storage systems. With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the **key generation, data encryption, key wrapping, storage, and decryption** phases. Why is envelope encryption preferred over encrypting data directly with a KEK? **(b)** [7 Marks] A healthcare startup wants to store **2 TB** of patient MRI images in AWS S3 with HIPAA-grade encryption. The CIO has mandated **BYOK** with **AES-256-GCM**, and audit logs must be **tamper-evident**. Design a complete cryptographic architecture covering: (i) key hierarchy, (ii) data encryption, (iii) audit log integrity, (iv) access control. Mention specific AWS / Azure / GCP services for each tier. #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Envelope Encryption **1. Why Envelope Encryption? (2 marks)** - A KEK stored in an HSM is **slow** (≈10,000 RSA-2048 ops/sec, ≈10,000 AES-256 ops/sec) compared to software AES-NI (≈5 GB/sec per core). - Encrypting a multi-GB object directly with the HSM-bound KEK is **impractical** — it bottlenecks the HSM. - Envelope encryption limits HSM usage to **one wrap operation per object** and uses a fast symmetric DEK for bulk data. **2. The Five Phases (4 marks)** | Phase | Operation | Algorithm | Output | |-------|-----------|-----------|--------| | Key Gen | $K_{DEK} \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{256}$ | CSPRNG (e.g., `/dev/urandom`, NIST SP 800-90A) | 256-bit DEK | | Data Encryption | $C_{data} = \text{AES-GCM-256}(K_{DEK}, IV, AAD, D)$ | AES-GCM (AEAD) | Ciphertext + 128-bit tag | | Key Wrap | $C_{DEK} = \text{AES-KW-256}(K_{KEK}, K_{DEK})$ | AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394) | 296-byte wrapped DEK | | Storage | Persist $C_{data}$ and $\{C_{DEK}, IV, AAD, T, \text{metadata}\}$ | Object metadata schema | S3 object + DynamoDB key map | | Decryption | Reverse: unwrap DEK → decrypt data | AES-KW-256 Unwrap → AES-GCM-256 Decrypt | Plaintext $D$ | **3. Block Diagram (1 mark — see §4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2)** **Mark Allocation Summary:** Phases 2 marks · Why preferred 2 marks · Diagram 1 mark · Algorithms named 1 mark · Mark justification 1 mark. **(Total: 7)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Healthcare Cloud Architecture **(i) Key Hierarchy (2 marks)** ``` Tier 0: External Root Key (in customer's on-prem Thales Luna HSM) | v (via dedicated HSM-to-HSM TLS tunnel) Tier 1: CMK (Customer Master Key) in AWS KMS — alias "mri-cmk-prod" | v (envelope wrap on every object PUT) Tier 2: Per-Object DEK (AES-256) — generated, used once, then wrapped | v Tier 3: AES-256-GCM encryption of S3 object body ``` **(ii) Data Encryption (2 marks)** - **S3 Object:** SSE-KMS with the CMK `mri-cmk-prod` (AES-256-GCM mode, **not** AES-256-CBC). - **Database:** RDS PostgreSQL with **TDE** (pgaudit + AWS RDS encryption at rest, KMS-backed). - **EHR API:** Mutual TLS 1.3 with client certificates issued by an internal CA in AWS Private CA. - **Cross-region DR:** S3 Cross-Region Replication with a *different* CMK in the destination region (key isolation). **(iii) Audit Log Integrity (1.5 marks)** - Enable **AWS CloudTrail** with log file validation enabled (SHA-256 + RSA-signed digest files). - Stream logs to a **central S3 bucket** with **Object Lock** (Compliance mode) — immutable for 7 years (HIPAA). - Build a **Merkle tree** over daily log batches; publish Merkle root to **Amazon QLDB** (Quantum Ledger Database) for cryptographic notarization. - Alert on any Merkle root mismatch via **CloudWatch + Lambda**. **(iv) Access Control (1.5 marks)** - **IAM Roles** (no long-lived access keys) — assume role via **AWS STS** with short-lived (≤ 1 hour) credentials. - **MFA** required for any `kms:Decrypt` call from a privileged user. - **S3 Bucket Policy** denies any unencrypted PUT (`aws:SecureTransport=true` and `s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption=aws:kms`). - **AWS IAM Access Analyzer** continuously validates least-privilege. - **KMS Key Policies** restrict `kms:Decrypt` to a specific VPC endpoint + IP allowlist + specific role ARNs. **Mark Allocation Summary:** Key hierarchy 2 · Data encryption 2 · Audit 1.5 · Access control 1.5. **(Total: 7)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. *Do not* mention just "use AES" without specifying mode (GCM vs. CBC vs. XTS) and key size. Half a mark is deducted for vague algorithm names. > 2. *Do not* skip the **MFA on `kms:Decrypt`** step — examiners specifically look for it under HIPAA questions. > 3. *Do not* write *"audit logs are stored"* without explaining the **cryptographic chain** (SHA-256, Merkle tree, or QLDB). Generic "logging" gets zero marks. > 4. *Do not* confuse **BYOK** (import) with **HYOK** (never leaves) — this is a recurring 2-mark trap. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Homomorphic Encryption + Multi-Tenant Cloud Analytics **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Choice B]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Define **Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE)**. With the Paillier cryptosystem, show step-by-step that the scheme supports **additive homomorphism** over $\mathbb{Z}_n$. Use the equations $E(m) = g^{m} r^{n} \bmod n^{2}$ and $D(c) = L(c^{\lambda} \bmod n^{2}) \cdot \mu \bmod n$, where $L(x) = (x-1)/n$. **(b)** [7 Marks] A cloud analytics service receives encrypted health metrics (heart rate, BP, glucose) from **1000 patients** via IoT wearables. The hospital wants the cloud to compute the **average** of each metric **without** decrypting individual values. Design a solution using (i) **Paillier PHE** for the sum, and (ii) **FHE (CKKS)** for the average. Discuss **performance trade-offs** and at least **two security challenges** of homomorphic computation in multi-tenant clouds. #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Paillier Additive Homomorphism **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark)** Public key $(n, g)$ with $n = p \cdot q$ where $p, q$ are large equal-length primes. Private key $(\lambda, \mu)$ where $\lambda = \text{lcm}(p-1, q-1)$ and $\mu = (L(g^{\lambda} \bmod n^2))^{-1} \bmod n$. **Step 2 — Encrypt two messages (1.5 marks)** For plaintexts $m_1, m_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_n$ and randomness $r_1, r_2 \in \mathbb{Z}_n^{*}$: $$ E(m_1) = g^{m_1} \cdot r_1^{n} \bmod n^2 $$ $$ E(m_2) = g^{m_2} \cdot r_2^{n} \bmod n^2 $$ **Step 3 — Multiply ciphertexts (2 marks)** $$ E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) = (g^{m_1} r_1^{n}) \cdot (g^{m_2} r_2^{n}) \bmod n^2 $$ $$ = g^{m_1 + m_2} \cdot (r_1 r_2)^{n} \bmod n^2 $$ **Step 4 — Identify as a valid ciphertext (1.5 marks)** By definition of Paillier encryption, the right-hand side is $E(m_1 + m_2 \bmod n,\; r_1 r_2 \bmod n)$. Therefore: $$ E(m_1) \cdot E(m_2) \bmod n^2 = E(m_1 + m_2 \bmod n) $$ This proves **additive homomorphism** over the plaintext group $(\mathbb{Z}_n, +)$. *(1 mark for the concluding statement.)* **Mark Allocation:** Setup 1 · Encryption 1.5 · Multiplication 2 · Identification 1.5 · Conclusion 1. **(Total: 7)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Cloud Health Analytics with HE **(i) Paillier PHE for the Sum (2 marks)** - Each wearable encrypts its heart rate $h_i$ as $E(h_i)$ using a hospital-issued public key $(n, g)$. - Cloud server computes: $$ S = \prod_{i=1}^{1000} E(h_i) \bmod n^2 = E\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{1000} h_i \bmod n\right) $$ - Hospital (private key holder) decrypts to recover the sum. - **Average = sum / 1000** is computed *after* decryption (the hospital multiplies by the inverse of 1000 mod n). **(ii) CKKS FHE for the Average (2 marks)** - CKKS supports **approximate arithmetic on real numbers** — perfect for floating-point sensor data. - Encrypt each $h_i$ as a CKKS ciphertext $\text{ct}_i$ with a common public key and a shared *rescale modulus chain*. - Cloud server computes the running sum **and** multiplies by the plaintext constant $1/1000$ in one go: $$ \text{ct}_{avg} = \left(\frac{1}{1000}\right) \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{1000} \text{ct}_i $$ - The hospital bootstraps and decrypts to recover the **approximate average** $\bar{h}$ within a known precision $\epsilon \approx 2^{-40}$. **(iii) Performance Trade-offs (1.5 marks)** | Aspect | Paillier (PHE) | CKKS (FHE) | |--------|----------------|------------| | Operations | Sum only | Sum, product, polynomial, activation (ReLU via degree-7 approx) | | Ciphertext size | 2× plaintext (4096 bits) | ~**20–50×** plaintext (MB-scale) | | Compute time (1000 ops) | ~10 ms | ~10–100 **seconds** | | Bootstrapping | Not needed | **Mandatory** after every ~15 multiplications | | Precision | Exact | Approximate (Bounded $\epsilon$) | **(iv) Security Challenges in Multi-Tenant Clouds (1.5 marks)** 1. **Ciphertext Expansion & Side Channels:** Large CKKS ciphertexts leak timing and memory-access patterns; co-tenant VMs in the same host can mount cross-VM cache side-channel attacks. 2. **Key Management for HE:** A single private key holder creates a *single point of failure*; threshold / distributed key generation (DKG) is needed for multi-hospital federated analytics. 3. **Verifiable Computation:** The cloud could return an *incorrect* sum; solutions require **SNARK/STARK proofs of correct homomorphic evaluation** (research frontier). 4. **Quantum Threat:** Paillier is broken by Shor's algorithm; transition to **lattice-based FHE** (BFV, BGV, CKKS) is mandatory post-2030. **Mark Allocation:** PHE 2 · FHE 2 · Trade-offs 1.5 · Security 1.5. **(Total: 7)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. *Always* show the algebraic expansion in homomorphism proofs — do not just *state* the property. Half the marks are for derivation. > 2. For CKKS questions, *name the rescale-and-bootstrapping loop* explicitly. Examiners associate ≥ 2 marks with this concept. > 3. *Never* write "FHE is slow" without giving a *quantified* number (e.g., 1000×–10000× overhead). Vague claims score zero. > 4. *Always* close the answer with a *security challenge* — pure performance answers are penalized 1–2 marks. > 5. For multi-tenant questions, *mention at least one side-channel* (cache, memory, or timing). This is a 2024-specific trend in KTU paper-setters. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Print this section and pin it above your exam desk the night before the test.** ### Core Concepts (Must Know) - [ ] **Three data states:** DAR, DIT, DIU — know one cryptographic control for each. - [ ] **Envelope Encryption** formula: $C_{DEK} = E_{KEK}(K_{DEK})$; $C_{data} = E_{DEK}(data)$. - [ ] **AES-256-GCM** is the default for DAR; **TLS 1.3** is the default for DIT; **FHE / TEE** for DIU. - [ ] **Paillier** is **additive**; **RSA/ElGamal** is **multiplicative**; **CKKS / BFV / BGV** are FHE. - [ ] **BYOK vs. HYOK** distinction; **KMS vs. HSM** separation of duties. - [ ] **TLS 1.3** is **1-RTT** (or 0-RTT for resumption); static RSA key exchange is *removed*. - [ ] **FIPS 140-3 Level 3** is the gold standard for cloud HSMs. - [ ] **Merkle-tree audit logs** = tamper-evidence; **CloudTrail log file validation** = SHA-256 + RSA-signed digests. ### Algorithms & Key Sizes (Memorize) - [ ] **AES-128 / AES-256** — symmetric, block size 128 bits. - [ ] **RSA-2048** minimum; **RSA-3072** recommended through 2030. - [ ] **ECC P-256** = ~128-bit security; **ECC P-384** = ~192-bit security. - [ ] **SHA-256** for general hashing; **SHA-3 / Keccak** as a SHA-2 alternative. - [ ] **Ed25519** for modern signatures; **ECDSA P-256** for TLS interop. ### Architecture Patterns (Recognize Instantly) - [ ] **Defense in Depth:** Perimeter + Identity + Data + Audit layers. - [ ] **Zero Trust:** No implicit trust based on network location; verify every request. - [ ] **Confidential Computing:** TEE-based attestation; memory encryption (MKTME, SEV-SNP). - [ ] **Federated Identity:** SAML 2.0 + OAuth 2.0 + OIDC over cloud IdPs (Azure AD, Okta, AWS IAM Identity Center). ### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These Marks) - [ ] ❌ Calling "encryption" without naming the **mode** (GCM vs. CBC). - [ ] ❌ Forgetting the **mod n** in HE arithmetic derivations. - [ ] ❌ Confusing **authentication** (HMAC, MAC) with **non-repudiation** (digital signatures). - [ ] ❌ Writing **"TLS"** without specifying **version** (1.2 vs. 1.3 is a 1-mark difference). - [ ] ❌ Skipping the **performance trade-off** in any cloud crypto design question. - [ ] ❌ Omitting **multi-tenancy / insider threat** in the threat model. - [ ] ❌ Failing to mention **key rotation frequency** (cloud best practice: ≤ 90 days for DEKs, ≤ 365 days for KEKs). ### Numbers to Commit to Memory (Last-Minute Cram) - [ ] AES-128 brute-force work factor: **2¹²⁸** operations. - [ ] RSA-2048 ≈ **112-bit** security level. - [ ] SHA-256 collision resistance: **2¹²⁸** operations (birthday bound). - [ ] TLS 1.3 handshake: **1 RTT** (full) / **0 RTT** (resumption). - [ ] HIPAA audit log retention: **6 years**; PCI-DSS: **1 year online + 3 months immediate**. - [ ] HSM latency budget: signing < **10 ms**; throughput > **10,000 RSA-2048/sec**. - [ ] CKKS FHE multiplication depth: **~15 levels** before mandatory **bootstrapping**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Mantra:** *State the threat → Name the algorithm → Show the math → Quantify the trade-off → Cite the standard.* This 5-step rhythm is the KTU topper's formula for Module 4. Use it on every long-answer question.

topics.title: Cryptography in IoT: Challenges and Cryptographic Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT501 - APPLIED CRYPTOGRAPHY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cryptography in IoT: Challenges and Cryptographic Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of IoT

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is formally defined by the **International Telecommunication Union (ITU-T Y.2060)** as a global infrastructure for the information society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies.

From a cryptographic perspective, the **IEEE Internet of Things Journal** defines IoT as: *"A self-configuring, adaptive, complex network that interconnects 'things' to the Internet through the use of standard communication protocols, where the 'things' have identities, physical attributes, and virtual personalities, and use intelligent interfaces for information processing within the social, environmental, and user contexts."*

## 1.2 Why Cryptography is Critical in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cryptography in IoT is **NOT** optional. By 2025–2030, more than **75 billion** IoT devices are projected to be deployed worldwide. Each device is a potential entry point for attackers, making cryptographic protection mandatory.

Cryptography in IoT serves four primary security objectives:

| Security Goal | Cryptographic Mechanism |
|---------------|------------------------|
| **Confidentiality** | Symmetric/Asymmetric Encryption (AES, ECC) |
| **Integrity** | Hash Functions (SHA-2, SHA-3, Lightweight Hash) |
| **Authentication** | Digital Signatures, MACs, Challenge-Response |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Public-Key Signatures (ECDSA, EdDSA) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Apartment Building"

Imagine a **large smart apartment building** (this is your IoT network):

- **Apartments** = IoT devices (sensors, smart meters, wearables)
- **Tenants** = Data being transmitted
- **Doorman** = Cryptographic protocols
- **Locks on doors** = Encryption keys

In a **regular** building, tenants use heavy steel doors with complex locks (heavy cryptography). But in an IoT setting, we have **millions of tiny smart mailboxes** (resource-constrained sensors) that cannot afford such heavy locks. We need **lightweight, energy-efficient locks** that still keep the letters (data) safe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** A smart ECG (electrocardiogram) wearable runs on a coin-cell battery (CR2032) rated at **~240 mAh at 3 V**. Traditional AES-128 may consume more energy than the device can sustain for a week. This is the fundamental motivation for **lightweight cryptography**.

## 1.4 Key IoT Characteristics Affecting Cryptography

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following characteristics are the **root causes** of cryptographic challenges in IoT:
> 1. **Resource-Constrained Devices** — Limited CPU, RAM (often < 10 KB), battery
> 2. **Massive Scale** — Billions of devices, cannot manually configure each
> 3. **Heterogeneity** — Different hardware, OS, protocols
> 4. **Mobility** — Devices join/leave networks dynamically
> 5. **Adversarial Physical Access** — Devices deployed in unattended locations
> 6. **Unreliable Networks** — Wireless, lossy, intermittent connectivity

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Reference Models for IoT

IoT systems are typically described using two architectural models:

### A. The 3-Layer Model (Simplified)
1. **Perception Layer** (Sensors, Actuators, RFID tags) → Generates and collects data
2. **Network Layer** (Gateways, Routers, Cloud) → Transmits and routes data
3. **Application Layer** (Smart Home, Health Monitoring, Industrial Control) → Delivers services to users

### B. The 5-Layer Model (Detailed)
1. **Perception Layer**
2. **Network Layer**
3. **Middleware Layer** (Data storage, processing, decision-making)
4. **Application Layer**
5. **Business Layer** (Manages overall system, applications, user privacy)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "where is cryptography applied in IoT?", the answer must cover **all three to five layers**, NOT just the network layer. End-to-end security demands protection at every layer.

## 2.2 Detailed Cryptographic Challenges in IoT

### Challenge 1: Resource Constraints
Most IoT devices fall into **Class 0, 1, or 2** of the RFC 7228 classification:

| Device Class | RAM | Code Size (Flash) | Cryptographic Capability |
|--------------|-----|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Class 0** | < 10 KB | < 100 KB | Cannot implement cryptography directly |
| **Class 1** | ~10 KB | ~100 KB | Can run lightweight primitives |
| **Class 2** | ~50 KB | ~250 KB | Can run optimized AES-128, ECC |
| **Class 3+** | > 50 KB | > 250 KB | Can run full TLS/SSL stacks |

### Challenge 2: Energy Consumption
For a typical sensor node operating on 3 V at 1 MHz:
- **AES-128** encryption consumes ~**0.81 μJ** per 128-bit block
- **RSA-1024** consumes ~**297 mJ** per operation
- **ECC-160** consumes ~**15.6 mJ** per operation

This shows that **asymmetric cryptography is orders of magnitude more expensive** than symmetric cryptography in IoT.

### Challenge 3: Scalability and Key Management
- **Manual key distribution** is infeasible for billions of devices.
- **Symmetric keys** require **n(n-1)/2** keys for n devices — impractical.
- **Public-key infrastructure (PKI)** is heavy for tiny devices.

### Challenge 4: Heterogeneity
A network may contain 8-bit AVR microcontrollers, 32-bit ARM Cortex-M chips, and full Linux gateways simultaneously. A single cryptographic protocol rarely fits all.

### Challenge 5: Physical Tampering
Devices deployed in the field (smart meters on poles, agricultural sensors) can be **physically captured** for side-channel analysis, fault injection, and key extraction.

### Challenge 6: Latency and Real-Time Constraints
Industrial IoT (IIoT) and vehicular IoT require sub-millisecond response. Heavy cryptographic handshakes (e.g., TLS 1.3 with full PKI) may exceed deadlines.

### Challenge 7: Standardization and Interoperability
Multiple competing standards (CoAP, MQTT, DTLS, Zigbee IP, LoRaWAN) each define their own security models, fragmenting the ecosystem.

## 2.3 Cryptographic Solutions for IoT

### A. Lightweight Block Ciphers

| Cipher | Block Size (bits) | Key Size (bits) | Structure | Notes |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|-------|
| **PRESENT** | 64 | 80 / 128 | SPN | Ultra-light, ISO/IEC 29192 |
| **CLEFIA** | 128 | 128 / 192 / 256 | Feistel (Type-2) | Sony, ISO/IEC 29192 |
| **SIMON** | 32, 48, 64, 96, 128 | 64, 72, 96, 128, 192, 256 | Feistel | NSA, hardware-friendly |
| **SPECK** | 32, 48, 64, 96, 128 | 64, 72, 96, 128, 192, 256 | ARX | NSA, software-friendly |
| **LED** | 64 | 64 / 128 | SPN | Very low gate count |
| **Piccolo** | 64 | 80 / 128 | Feistel (GFS) | Sony, low power |

### B. Lightweight Stream Ciphers

| Cipher | Key/IV Size | Target Use |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| **Grain-128** | 128 / 96 | Hardware, RFID |
| **Trivium** | 80 / 80 | Hardware, low-gate |
| **MICKEY 2.0** | 80 / 80 | Hardware, RFID tags |

### C. Lightweight Hash Functions

| Hash | Output (bits) | Internal State (bits) | Use Case |
|------|---------------|----------------------|----------|
| **PHOTON** | 64/80/128/160/224/256 | 100/144/196/256/384/512 | ISO/IEC 29192 |
| **QUARK** | 64/128/224 | 68/136/272 | RFID authentication |
| **SPONGENT** | 80/128/160/224/256 | 88/136/176/240/272/304 | Hash + MAC |
| **Lesamnta-LW** | 256 | — | ISO/IEC 29192 |

### D. Public-Key Solutions (ECC and Variants)

- **ECC-160** provides security equivalent to RSA-1024 but with much smaller keys.
- **Curve25519 / Ed25519** are popular in modern IoT protocols (e.g., used in CoAP/DTLS).
- **Identity-Based Encryption (IBE)** simplifies key management.
- **Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE)** enables fine-grained access control.

### E. Protocol-Level Solutions
- **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** for UDP-based CoAP
- **TLS-PSK** (Pre-Shared Key) mode for low-overhead authentication
- **EAP-PSK, EAP-AKA** for constrained devices
- **HMAC-based Extract-and-Expand Key Derivation Function (HKDF)**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU Part A and Part B questions.**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / Units |
|-------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Symmetric Key Count** | $N_{keys} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Keys needed for n devices in pairwise symmetric scheme |
| **Block Cipher Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{B}{C \times T_{clk}}$ | $B$ = block bits, $C$ = clock cycles, $T_{clk}$ = clock period (s) |
| **Energy per Encryption** | $E = V^2 \cdot C_{avg} \cdot N_{cycles}$ | $V$ = supply voltage (V), $C_{avg}$ = average switched capacitance (F), $N_{cycles}$ = clock cycles |
| **Security Strength (bits)** | $S = \log_2(\text{Operations to break})$ | E.g., AES-128 → 128-bit security |
| **Equivalent AES Security for RSA** | $k_{RSA} \approx 6 \times k_{ECC}$ | RSA needs ~6× the bits of ECC for same security |
| **Gate Equivalents (GE)** | $\text{GE} = \dfrac{A_{chip}}{A_{gate}}$ | Measure of area in standard-cell ASIC |
| **Birthday Attack Bound** | $N \approx 2^{n/2}$ | $n$ = hash output bits; collisions found after $2^{n/2}$ trials |
| **Brute-Force Attack Bound** | $N \approx 2^{k}$ | $k$ = key length in bits |
| **Moore's Law (Security Energy)** | $E_{sec}(t) = E_{sec}(0) \cdot 2^{-t/2}$ | Security energy halves every 2 years |
| **Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret** | $K = A^{b} \mod p = B^{a} \mod p$ | Foundation of many IoT key agreement protocols |

> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing the symmetric key formula, do not write it as `n(n-1)/2` in plain text inside a table — use the LaTeX fraction form as shown above to get full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Solutions

- **Smart Healthcare**: Lightweight authenticated encryption secures patient data from wearable ECG sensors to hospital clouds.
- **Smart Grid**: Lightweight block ciphers protect real-time electricity consumption data from tampering.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: DTLS + ECC secures Machine-to-Machine (M2M) communication in factory floors.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: ECDSA + hash chains enable secure V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) messaging.
- **Smart Agriculture**: PRESENT and SPECK protect soil-moisture sensor data over LoRaWAN.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Cost of a Single AES-128 Encryption

We compute the total energy required to encrypt one 128-bit block using a hardware AES-128 implementation.

**Given Parameters (typical 0.13 μm CMOS technology):**
- Supply voltage: $V = 1.2 \text{ V}$
- Average switched capacitance per gate per cycle: $C_{avg} = 1 \text{ fF} = 1 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F}$
- Number of gates in AES-128 core: $G = 3{,}400 \text{ GE}$
- Clock cycles per encryption: $N_{cycles} = 1{,}032$ (for one block encryption)
- Clock frequency: $f = 1 \text{ MHz}$

**Step 1: Energy per gate per cycle**

$$
E_{gate} = V^2 \cdot C_{avg}
$$

**Step 2: Substituting the values**

$$
E_{gate} = (1.2)^2 \cdot (1 \times 10^{-15})
$$

$$
E_{gate} = 1.44 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J} = 1.44 \text{ fJ}
$$

**Step 3: Total energy for one encryption (single clock cycle model)**

$$
E_{total} = E_{gate} \cdot G \cdot N_{cycles}
$$

$$
E_{total} = (1.44 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot 3{,}400 \cdot 1{,}032
$$

$$
E_{total} = 5.05 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J} \approx 5.05 \text{ nJ}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** One AES-128 block encryption consumes **~5.05 nJ** on this hardware. For a sensor node with a **CR2032 battery** (energy = 240 mAh × 3 V = 720 J), this permits approximately **1.4 × 10¹¹** encryptions — which is a *very large* number, proving that lightweight AES is feasible.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Throughput of PRESENT Cipher

**Given:**
- Block size: $B = 64$ bits
- Key size: $K = 80$ bits
- Clock cycles per block: $C = 32$ (PRESENT round function: 31 rounds + 1 for input)
- Clock frequency: $f = 100 \text{ kHz}$ (typical low-power IoT mode)

**Step 1: Clock period**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{100 \times 10^3} = 10 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2: Time for one block**

$$
T_{block} = C \times T_{clk} = 32 \times 10 \text{ μs} = 320 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3: Throughput**

$$
T_{throughput} = \frac{B}{C \cdot T_{clk}} = \frac{64}{32 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
T_{throughput} = \frac{64}{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 200{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 200 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> PRESENT at 100 kHz gives **200 kbps** throughput. This is more than sufficient for most IoT telemetry applications (typical sensor data rate: 1–10 kbps).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Symmetric Key Count Growth for IoT Network

**Given:** n IoT devices, all requiring pairwise secure channels.

**Step 1: Each device needs a unique key with every other device.**

For device $i$, the number of keys it stores = $(n-1)$.

**Step 2: Total number of distinct symmetric keys**

$$
N_{keys} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate for n = 1 million (a moderate-size smart city deployment)**

$$
N_{keys} = \frac{10^6 \cdot (10^6 - 1)}{2} \approx 5 \times 10^{11}
$$

**Step 4: Storage per device**

Each device stores $(n-1) = 999{,}999$ keys. Assuming 128-bit AES key = 16 bytes:

$$
\text{Storage} = 999{,}999 \times 16 \text{ bytes} \approx 16 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** Symmetric keys are infeasible at IoT scale. This is why **Public-Key Cryptography (ECC)** is mandatory for IoT, despite its higher computational cost — because each device only stores **one private key** (32 bytes for Curve25519).

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Security Strength Equivalence — RSA vs ECC

The security strength $s$ of an $n$-bit key is defined as the work factor in operations to break it. Equating the work factors:

$$
2.7 \cdot k_{RSA} \approx 4.9 \cdot k_{ECC}
$$

Approximately, for equivalent security:

$$
k_{RSA} \approx 6 \cdot k_{ECC}
$$

**Equivalence Table (NIST SP 800-57):**

| Symmetric (AES) | ECC | RSA / DH |
|-----------------|-----|----------|
| 80 | 160 | 1024 |
| 112 | 224 | 2048 |
| 128 | 256 | 3072 |
| 192 | 384 | 7680 |
| 256 | 512 | 15360 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a Class 1 IoT device, **AES-128 + ECC-256** provides 128-bit security. The combined key + certificate footprint is small enough to fit in flash memory.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Suitability for IoT

| Algorithm | Type | GE (Hardware) | Code Size (SW) | Energy/Bit | Suitability |
|-----------|------|---------------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| AES-128 | Block Cipher | 3,400 GE | ~ 1.5 KB | Medium | Excellent |
| PRESENT-80 | Block Cipher | 1,570 GE | ~ 1.0 KB | Very Low | Best for RFID |
| SIMON-64/128 | Block Cipher | ~ 1,200 GE | — | Very Low | Excellent |
| SPECK-64/128 | Block Cipher | ~ 1,000 GE | ~ 0.6 KB | Very Low | Excellent |
| Grain-128 | Stream Cipher | ~ 1,300 GE | — | Low | Excellent |
| Trivium | Stream Cipher | ~ 800 GE | — | Low | Excellent |
| SHA-256 | Hash | ~ 10,000 GE | ~ 2.5 KB | Medium | Acceptable |
| PHOTON-128 | Hash | ~ 1,700 GE | — | Low | Best for Class 1 |
| ECC-160 | Public-Key | ~ 12,000 GE | ~ 8 KB | High | Class 2+ only |
| RSA-1024 | Public-Key | ~ 50,000 GE | ~ 20 KB | Very High | NOT for IoT |

> [!NOTE]
> **GE = Gate Equivalents** (smaller = better). Code size in software is also critical for Class 0/1 devices.

## 4.3 Lightweight Authentication Protocol Flow (Challenge–Response using ECC)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor Node
    participant G as Gateway
    participant KDS as Key Distribution Server

    Note over S,KDS: Initial Setup: S has private key dS, public key Q_S = d_S x G

    S->>G: HELLO with Q_S
    G->>KDS: Forward HELLO
    KDS-->>G: Challenge Nonce N
    G->>S: Challenge Nonce N

    Note over S: Sign N with d_S
    S->>G: Sig_S of N
    G->>KDS: Verify Sig_S
    KDS-->>G: ACK or NACK

    Note over S,G: Session Key K = x_K x y_S x G established
    G->>S: Encrypted Session Token E_K
    S-->>G: Acknowledgement

    Note over S,G: Secure Channel Established
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**List and briefly explain any three challenges of applying traditional cryptography in IoT environments.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Resource Constraints:** Most IoT devices (Class 0, 1) have very limited RAM (< 10 KB), flash (< 100 KB), and battery power. Traditional algorithms like RSA-1024 require ~50,000 Gate Equivalents (GE) of hardware, far exceeding the capability of resource-constrained sensors.

2. **Scalability and Key Management:** IoT networks scale to millions of devices. Pairwise symmetric keys require $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ keys (≈5 × 10¹¹ for 1 million devices), which is impractical to store and distribute.

3. **Heterogeneity:** IoT networks combine 8-bit microcontrollers, 32-bit ARM chips, and Linux gateways. A single cryptographic protocol rarely fits all classes of devices, demanding multiple lightweight alternatives.

4. **Physical Security:** Devices deployed in unattended locations (smart meters, agricultural sensors) are vulnerable to physical capture, side-channel attacks, and key extraction.

> **Word limit reminder:** 3-mark questions need ~80–120 words and 3 distinct points.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between symmetric and public-key lightweight cryptographic solutions for IoT. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Symmetric Key | Public-Key |
|---------|---------------|------------|
| **Key Count** | n(n-1)/2 for n devices | Only 2n keys |
| **Computation Cost** | Low (e.g., PRESENT ~1,570 GE) | High (e.g., ECC-160 ~12,000 GE) |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower |
| **Key Distribution** | Difficult at scale | Easier |
| **Example** | PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK | ECC-256, Ed25519 |

**Example of symmetric:** PRESENT cipher (64-bit block, 80-bit key, 31 rounds, ISO/IEC 29192 standard).

**Example of public-key:** ECC-160 (provides 80-bit security equivalent to RSA-1024 with far smaller key size).

> **Word limit reminder:** 3-mark questions must contain a comparison table or 4 distinct points.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B contains a Module Internal Choice. You must answer **ONE** of the two questions provided. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Show all derivations, diagrams, and tables for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**Q. (a) Discuss in detail the major challenges of implementing traditional cryptographic algorithms in IoT environments. Illustrate with the symmetric-key scalability problem.**

**Q. (b) Explain the architecture of the PRESENT lightweight block cipher with a neat diagram. Compare PRESENT with AES-128 on the basis of hardware area, throughput, and code size.**

### Model Answer for Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**Cryptographic Challenges in IoT:**

1. **Resource Constraints (2 marks):** IoT devices are classified as Class 0, 1, 2, 3 (RFC 7228). Class 0/1 devices have < 10 KB RAM and < 100 KB flash. Heavy algorithms like RSA-2048 (~50,000 GE) cannot fit, while PRESENT (~1,570 GE) can.

2. **Energy Constraints (1 mark):** Battery-powered devices cannot afford computationally expensive operations. RSA-1024 consumes ~297 mJ per operation versus AES-128's ~5 nJ per block.

3. **Scalability (Symmetric Key Problem) (3 marks):**
   - Pairwise symmetric scheme: $N_{keys} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$
   - For n = 1 million devices: $N_{keys} \approx 5 \times 10^{11}$ keys
   - Each device stores (n − 1) = 999,999 keys
   - Storage: 999,999 × 16 bytes ≈ 16 MB per device — exceeds Class 1 capacity
   - Manual key distribution is infeasible at scale

4. **Heterogeneity and Physical Security (1 mark):** Mixed-device networks and unattended deployment expose devices to tampering and side-channel attacks.

### Model Answer for Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**PRESENT Cipher Architecture (4 marks):**

PRESENT is a 64-bit block, 80/128-bit key SPN (Substitution–Permutation Network) cipher standardized in **ISO/IEC 29192-2**.

- **31 rounds** of encryption
- Each round has three layers:
  1. **addRoundKey** — XOR with round key $K_i$ (64 bits)
  2. **sBoxLayer** — 4-bit to 4-bit S-box (16 S-boxes applied in parallel)
  3. **pLayer** — Bit permutation layer

**Block Diagram of PRESENT Round Function:**

```
Plaintext (64 bits)
        |
        v
   addRoundKey (XOR with K_1)
        |
        v
    sBoxLayer (16 parallel 4-bit S-boxes)
        |
        v
      pLayer (bit permutation)
        |
        v
   addRoundKey (XOR with K_2)
        |
        v
    sBoxLayer
        |
        v
      pLayer
        |  (repeat for 31 rounds)
        v
   addRoundKey (XOR with K_32)
        |
        v
   Ciphertext (64 bits)
```

**Comparison Table: PRESENT vs AES-128 (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PRESENT-80 | AES-128 |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **Block Size** | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| **Key Size** | 80 bits | 128 / 192 / 256 bits |
| **Rounds** | 31 | 10 (128-bit key) |
| **Structure** | SPN | SPN |
| **Hardware Area (GE)** | ~ 1,570 GE | ~ 3,400 GE |
| **Code Size (Software)** | ~ 1.0 KB | ~ 1.5 KB |
| **Throughput (100 kHz)** | 200 kbps | ~ 25 kbps |
| **Standard** | ISO/IEC 29192 | FIPS 197 |
| **Best Suited For** | RFID, Class 0/1 | Class 2+ |

> **Conclusion:** PRESENT is preferred for ultra-light devices, AES-128 is preferred when higher throughput and larger block size are needed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**Q. (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the 5-layer IoT architecture. Identify the cryptographic requirements at each layer.**

**Q. (b) Explain the Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) based key exchange for IoT. Derive the energy and storage benefits of ECC-160 over RSA-1024.**

### Model Answer for Q. (a) — 7 Marks

**5-Layer IoT Architecture Diagram (4 marks):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │    Business Layer            │  ← Governance, privacy, business models
   └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                  │
   ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
   │    Application Layer         │  ← Smart home, health, industrial
   └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                  │
   ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
   │    Middleware Layer          │  ← Storage, analytics, decision
   └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                  │
   ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
   │    Network Layer             │  ← Gateways, routing, cloud
   └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                  │
   ┌──────────────▼───────────────┐
   │    Perception Layer          │  ← Sensors, RFID, actuators
   └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Cryptographic Requirements per Layer (3 marks):**

| Layer | Cryptographic Primitive | Purpose |
|-------|------------------------|---------|
| **Perception** | Lightweight Block Cipher (PRESENT, AES-128), Lightweight MAC | Data confidentiality + integrity at the source |
| **Network** | DTLS, IPsec, ECC, ECDSA | Secure transport, key agreement, authentication |
| **Middleware** | AES-256, TLS, Hash chains (Merkle tree) | Data-at-rest protection, secure aggregation |
| **Application** | OAuth 2.0, JWT, Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE) | User authentication, fine-grained access control |
| **Business** | Blockchain, Federated Learning security, Audit logs (signed) | Trust management, regulatory compliance |

### Model Answer for Q. (b) — 7 Marks

**ECC Key Exchange for IoT (3 marks):**

The Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman (ECDH) protocol is widely used in IoT for lightweight key agreement.

- **Domain parameters:** Curve $E$ over $\mathbb{F}_p$, base point $G$ of order $n$
- **Device A** chooses random private key $d_A \in [1, n-1]$; public key $Q_A = d_A \cdot G$
- **Device B** chooses random private key $d_B \in [1, n-1]$; public key $Q_B = d_B \cdot G$
- **Shared secret:** $K = d_A \cdot Q_B = d_B \cdot Q_A$

For IoT, popular curves are **Curve25519** (key size: 32 bytes) and **secp256r1** (NIST P-256, 32 bytes).

**Storage and Energy Comparison: ECC-160 vs RSA-1024 (4 marks):**

| Parameter | ECC-160 | RSA-1024 |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Key Size** | 160 bits (20 bytes) | 1024 bits (128 bytes) |
| **Equivalent Security** | 80 bits | 80 bits |
| **Public Key Storage** | 40 bytes | 256 bytes |
| **Private Key Storage** | 20 bytes | 128 bytes |
| **Signature Size** | ~ 40 bytes | ~ 128 bytes |
| **Hardware GE** | ~ 12,000 GE | ~ 50,000 GE |
| **Energy per Operation** | ~ 15.6 mJ | ~ 297 mJ |
| **Computation Time (typical 8-bit MCU)** | ~ 1.5 s | ~ 25 s |

> **Storage Benefit:** ECC-160 requires **~6× less storage** than RSA-1024 for the same security level.
> **Energy Benefit:** ECC-160 consumes **~19× less energy** than RSA-1024 per operation.

> **Conclusion:** ECC is the de-facto public-key solution for IoT devices from Class 1 upwards.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (Verified from KTU Valuation Reports):**
> 1. **Confusing the 3-layer and 5-layer models:** Students often write "3-layer" when the question demands 5-layer. Always read carefully.
> 2. **Writing "lightweight cryptography" without giving a specific algorithm:** This costs 1–2 marks. Always name at least one cipher (PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, Grain, Trivium, PHOTON).
> 3. **Forgetting the IoT device class:** When discussing resource constraints, explicitly mention **Class 0, Class 1, Class 2** (RFC 7228). Generic answers lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Incorrect symmetric key formula:** Writing it as `n(n-1)/2` in plain text instead of $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ loses presentation marks.
> 5. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-parts:** KTU examiners expect a block diagram for full marks in design/architecture questions.
> 6. **Confusing PRESENT with AES:** Present is **64-bit block, 80/128-bit key, 31 rounds**; AES is **128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit key, 10/12/14 rounds**. Mixing them up = 1 mark deduction.
> 7. **Forgetting security equivalence table:** Always include the AES-ECC-RSA equivalence table for any ECC question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cryptography in IoT:**

- **IoT Architecture:** Know both 3-layer (Perception, Network, Application) and 5-layer (adds Middleware and Business). Be able to draw both.
- **Security Goals:** CIA + Authentication + Non-Repudiation + Privacy + Availability.
- **Device Classes (RFC 7228):** Class 0 (<10 KB RAM), Class 1 (~10 KB), Class 2 (~50 KB), Class 3+ (>50 KB).
- **Symmetric Key Count:** $N_{keys} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ — proves infeasibility of pairwise keys at scale.
- **Lightweight Block Ciphers:** PRESENT (ISO/IEC 29192), SIMON, SPECK, LED, CLEFIA.
- **Lightweight Stream Ciphers:** Grain-128, Trivium, MICKEY 2.0.
- **Lightweight Hash Functions:** PHOTON, SPONGENT, QUARK (ISO/IEC 29192-5).
- **PRESENT Details:** 64-bit block, 80/128-bit key, 31 rounds, SPN structure, ~1,570 GE hardware.
- **AES-128 vs PRESENT:** AES = 128-bit block, more secure per round; PRESENT = 64-bit block, half the area of AES.
- **ECC over RSA:** ECC-160 ≈ RSA-1024 (security), ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072, ECC-384 ≈ RSA-7680. Storage ~6× smaller; energy ~19× lower.
- **Public-Key Recommendation:** ECC-256 with Curve25519 or secp256r1 is the IoT standard.
- **Protocols:** DTLS for CoAP (UDP), TLS-PSK for low-overhead handshakes, HMAC + HKDF for key derivation.
- **Energy Model:** $E = V^2 \cdot C_{avg} \cdot N_{cycles}$. Voltage scaling is the most effective energy saver.
- **Security Bits:** AES-128 = 128-bit security; ECC-256 = 128-bit security; RSA-3072 = 128-bit security.
- **Standardization Bodies:** NIST (SP 800-57, SP 800-38A), ISO/IEC 29192 (lightweight crypto), IETF (CoAP, DTLS, RFC 7252, RFC 6347), IEEE 802.15.4.
- **Birthday Attack:** Hash output of n bits requires $\approx 2^{n/2}$ trials to find a collision. SHA-256 → ~2¹²⁸ trials.
- **Side-Channel Defenses:** Masking, hiding, constant-time implementations, and shielded hardware for IoT.
- **Real-World IoT Protocols Using ECC:** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Zigbee IP, LoRaWAN, Thread, Matter (formerly Project CHIP).
- **Future Trends:** Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) for IoT (lattice-based, code-based), Blockchain for IoT trust management, Federated Learning with Homomorphic Encryption, Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs) for key generation.
- **Common Viva Questions:** "Why is RSA not used in IoT?" "Compare PRESENT and AES." "Why is ECC preferred over RSA for IoT?" "What is the role of a lightweight hash in IoT?" "Explain the IoT device classes."
